topics.title: Elementary Signals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT402 - SIGNAL PROCESSING FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Elementary Signals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020)** syllabus for **Signal Processing for Robotics (PCRAT402)**, an **elementary signal** is defined as a fundamental, mathematically well-defined continuous-time or discrete-time function that serves as a *building block* for constructing, analyzing, and representing more complex real-world signals encountered in robotic systems (such as sensor outputs, actuator commands, encoder pulses, and control loops).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** Elementary signals are the standardized, canonical signal primitives — including the *unit step*, *unit impulse*, *unit ramp*, *exponential*, *sinusoidal*, *signum*, *rectangular*, *triangular*, and *sinc* signals — used universally to model, decompose, and analyze both deterministic and random signals in time-domain and frequency-domain analysis.

### 1.1.1 Classification of Elementary Signals

Elementary signals are broadly classified into two families:

| Family | Domain | Notation | Robotic Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Continuous-Time (CT)** | $t \in \mathbb{R}$ | $x(t)$ | Analog force sensor output |
| **Discrete-Time (DT)** | $n \in \mathbb{Z}$ | $x[n]$ | Digital encoder pulse train |

> [!NOTE]
> In **robotics**, every sensor reading — IMU acceleration, LiDAR return, motor current — is fundamentally modeled as a combination of these elementary signals. Mastering them is non-negotiable for system analysis.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **chef in a robotic kitchen**. Before you can cook a complex dish (a real-world signal like the voltage waveform from a robotic arm's motor), you need a *pantry of basic ingredients*: salt, sugar, flour, eggs. Each ingredient has a known taste and texture. Similarly, **elementary signals are the "ingredients"** of signal processing — they have well-defined mathematical "tastes" (shapes), and *any* complex signal can be expressed as a recipe (linear combination) of these basic ingredients.

- The **unit impulse** $\delta(t)$ is like a single grain of salt — an infinitely sharp, instantaneous "kick."
- The **unit step** $u(t)$ is like flipping a switch ON at $t = 0$.
- The **sinusoidal** signal is like the rhythmic hum of a motor running at constant speed.
- The **ramp** is like a conveyor belt starting from rest and linearly gaining velocity.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid for Students:** Think of elementary signals as the *alphabet* of signal processing. Just as words are built from letters, complex robotic signals are built from these standard waveforms.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit | Robotic Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sampling Frequency** | $f_s$ | **Hz (samples/sec)** | Determines Nyquist boundary |
| **Angular Frequency** | $\omega$ | **rad/sec** | Sinusoidal oscillation rate |
| **Sampling Period** | $T_s$ | **seconds** | Time between discrete samples |
| **Signal Energy** | $E$ | **Joules (J)** | Total energy of finite-duration signal |
| **Signal Power** | $P$ | **Watts (W)** | Average power of periodic signal |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The relationship $f_s = \dfrac{1}{T_s}$ and $\omega = 2\pi f$ must be memorized — these appear in **every KTU exam**.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Eight Core Elementary Signals

### 2.1.1 Unit Step Signal $u(t)$

The **unit step** function models the act of "switching ON" a system at $t = 0$.

$$u(t) = \begin{cases} 1, & t \geq 0 \\ 0, & t < 0 \end{cases}$$

- **Why it matters:** Models sudden activation of a robotic actuator, a relay closing, or a digital logic transition.
- **How it behaves:** It is discontinuous at $t = 0$. In many references, $u(0)$ is defined as $0.5$ (symmetric convention) to avoid ambiguity.

### 2.1.2 Unit Impulse Signal $\delta(t)$

The **Dirac delta** is an idealized, infinitesimally narrow pulse of **unit area**.

$$\delta(t) = \begin{cases} \infty, & t = 0 \\ 0, & t \neq 0 \end{cases} \quad \text{with} \quad \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t)\, dt = 1$$

- **Physical intuition:** A hammer striking a robotic link — infinite force for an infinitesimal instant, delivering finite momentum.
- **Sifting property:** $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\,\delta(t - t_0)\, dt = x(t_0)$ — the impulse "picks out" the value of $x(t)$ at $t = t_0$.

### 2.1.3 Unit Ramp Signal $r(t)$

A linearly growing function for $t \geq 0$.

$$r(t) = \begin{cases} t, & t \geq 0 \\ 0, & t < 0 \end{cases}$$

- **Robotic analog:** A motor commanded to accelerate at constant rate from rest.

### 2.1.4 Exponential Signal $x(t) = e^{at}$

$$x(t) = e^{at}, \quad a \in \mathbb{R}$$

- If $a < 0$: **decaying exponential** (e.g., RC circuit discharge, vibration damping).
- If $a > 0$: **growing exponential** (e.g., unstable system response).

### 2.1.5 Sinusoidal Signal

$$x(t) = A\sin(\omega t + \phi) \quad \text{or} \quad x(t) = A\cos(\omega t + \phi)$$

- $A$ = amplitude, $\omega$ = angular frequency (rad/sec), $\phi$ = phase (radians).
- **Robotic analog:** Harmonic oscillation of a spring-mass-damper system; sinusoidal PWM drive for AC servo motors.

### 2.1.6 Signum Function $\text{sgn}(t)$

$$\text{sgn}(t) = \begin{cases} +1, & t > 0 \\ -1, & t < 0 \\ 0, & t = 0 \end{cases}$$

- Models a signal whose polarity abruptly flips at the origin.

### 2.1.7 Rectangular Pulse $\text{rect}\!\left(\dfrac{t}{\tau}\right)$

$$\text{rect}\!\left(\frac{t}{\tau}\right) = \begin{cases} 1, & \vert t \vert \leq \tau/2 \\ 0, & \vert t \vert > \tau/2 \end{cases}$$

- A pulse of width $\tau$ centered at origin.

### 2.1.8 Triangular Pulse $\text{tri}(t)$

$$\text{tri}(t) = \begin{cases} 1 - \vert t \vert, & \vert t \vert \leq 1 \\ 0, & \vert t \vert > 1 \end{cases}$$

### 2.1.9 Sinc Function $\text{sinc}(t)$

$$\text{sinc}(t) = \frac{\sin(\pi t)}{\pi t}$$

- **Crucial role:** Its Fourier Transform is the ideal rectangular passband — fundamental to digital filter design.

---

## 2.2 Time-Domain Operations on Elementary Signals

### 2.2.1 Time Shifting
$$x(t) \rightarrow x(t - t_0)$$
- Right shift ($t_0 > 0$): signal is **delayed**.
- Left shift ($t_0 < 0$): signal is **advanced**.

### 2.2.2 Time Scaling
$$x(t) \rightarrow x(at)$$
- If $\vert a \vert > 1$: signal is **compressed**.
- If $0 < \vert a \vert < 1$: signal is **expanded** (stretched).

### 2.2.3 Time Reflection
$$x(t) \rightarrow x(-t)$$
- Mirrors the signal about the vertical axis.

### 2.2.4 Amplitude Scaling
$$x(t) \rightarrow A\,x(t)$$

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Signal | Mathematical Definition | Laplace Transform | Key Property |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Unit Step** $u(t)$ | $1$ for $t \geq 0$, else $0$ | $\dfrac{1}{s}$ | $u(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} \delta(\tau)\,d\tau$ |
| **Unit Impulse** $\delta(t)$ | $\infty$ at $0$, area $=1$ | $1$ | Sifting: $\int x(t)\delta(t-t_0)dt = x(t_0)$ |
| **Unit Ramp** $r(t)$ | $t$ for $t \geq 0$ | $\dfrac{1}{s^2}$ | $r(t) = t\,u(t)$ |
| **Exponential** $e^{at}$ | $e^{at}\,\forall t$ | $\dfrac{1}{s-a}$ | Decays if $a<0$ |
| **Sinusoid** $\sin(\omega t)$ | $\sin(\omega t)$ | $\dfrac{\omega}{s^2+\omega^2}$ | Periodic, $T = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega}$ |
| **Signum** $\text{sgn}(t)$ | $+1$ if $t>0$, $-1$ if $t<0$ | $\dfrac{2}{s}$ | Odd function |
| **Rectangular** $\text{rect}(t/\tau)$ | $1$ for $\vert t \vert \leq \tau/2$ | $\dfrac{\tau\,\text{sinc}(s\tau/2)}{1}$ | Area $= \tau$ |
| **Triangular** $\text{tri}(t)$ | $1-\vert t \vert$ for $\vert t \vert \leq 1$ | $\text{sinc}^2(s/2)$ | Even, area $= 1$ |
| **Sinc** $\text{sinc}(t)$ | $\dfrac{\sin(\pi t)}{\pi t}$ | Rectangular band | $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\text{sinc}(t)\,dt = 1$ |

### 2.3.1 Critical Inter-Signal Relationships (MUST memorize)

$$u(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} \delta(\tau)\, d\tau$$

$$\delta(t) = \frac{d\,u(t)}{dt}$$

$$r(t) = t \cdot u(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} u(\tau)\, d\tau$$

$$\text{sgn}(t) = 2u(t) - 1$$

$$u(t) = \tfrac{1}{2} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{sgn}(t)$$

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

> [!NOTE]
> **Why KTU examiners love this topic:** Every single robotic system you will ever analyze — from a Roomba to a surgical robot — relies on decomposing sensor/actuator signals into elementary forms.

- **Control Systems:** Step response is used to characterize PID performance.
- **Communications:** Sinusoidal carriers carry digital data.
- **Image Processing:** 2D sinc functions are used in image reconstruction.
- **Biomedical Robotics:** EMG/EEG signals are analyzed via sinusoidal + impulse models.
- **SLAM & LIDAR:** Rectangular pulses model time-of-flight returns.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Ramp as Integral of Step

**Statement to prove:** $r(t) = \displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{t} u(\tau)\, d\tau$

**Proof:**

We compute the integral piecewise based on the definition of $u(\tau)$.

$$\int_{-\infty}^{t} u(\tau)\, d\tau = \int_{-\infty}^{0} u(\tau)\, d\tau + \int_{0}^{t} u(\tau)\, d\tau$$

In the first interval $(-\infty, 0)$, the integrand $u(\tau) = 0$ because $\tau < 0$.

$$= 0 + \int_{0}^{t} u(\tau)\, d\tau$$

In the second interval, for $0 \leq \tau \leq t$, we have $u(\tau) = 1$.

$$= \int_{0}^{t} 1\, d\tau = \tau \,\Big\vert_{0}^{t} = t - 0 = t$$

Since the integrand is zero for $\tau < 0$, the full result is:

$$\int_{-\infty}^{t} u(\tau)\, d\tau = \begin{cases} 0, & t < 0 \\ t, & t \geq 0 \end{cases} = r(t)$$

Hence, $r(t) = t\,u(t)$. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Impulse as Derivative of Step

**Statement:** $\delta(t) = \dfrac{d\,u(t)}{dt}$

**Proof:**

Consider the derivative of $u(t)$. For $t < 0$, $u(t) = 0 \Rightarrow \dfrac{du}{dt} = 0$. For $t > 0$, $u(t) = 1 \Rightarrow \dfrac{du}{dt} = 0$. So the derivative is zero *almost everywhere* except at the discontinuity $t = 0$, where it is undefined (or infinite).

The function that is zero everywhere yet has unit area is precisely $\delta(t)$.

$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{d\,u(t)}{dt}\, dt = u(\infty) - u(-\infty) = 1 - 0 = 1$$

A function that integrates to unity while vanishing outside a single point is, by definition, $\delta(t)$. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Decompose a Rectangular Pulse using Steps

**Problem:** Express the rectangular pulse $p(t)$ of height $A$, spanning from $t = 0$ to $t = T$, as a combination of unit step functions.

**Solution:**

The pulse is ON from $0$ to $T$, so we "turn ON" at $t=0$ and "turn OFF" at $t=T$.

$$p(t) = A\,u(t) - A\,u(t - T)$$

**Step-by-step verification:**

- At $t < 0$: $u(t) = 0$, $u(t-T) = 0 \Rightarrow p(t) = 0 - 0 = 0$. ✓
- At $0 \leq t < T$: $u(t) = 1$, $u(t-T) = 0 \Rightarrow p(t) = A - 0 = A$. ✓
- At $t \geq T$: $u(t) = 1$, $u(t-T) = 1 \Rightarrow p(t) = A - A = 0$. ✓

The decomposition is correct.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: Compute Energy of a Triangular Pulse

**Problem:** Compute the energy $E$ of $x(t) = \text{tri}(t/2)$, i.e., a triangle of height $1$ from $t = -2$ to $t = 2$.

**Solution:**

The signal is $x(t) = 1 - \vert t \vert / 2$ for $\vert t \vert \leq 2$, and $0$ elsewhere.

By symmetry (even function), we compute twice the integral from $0$ to $2$.

$$E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2\, dt = 2 \int_{0}^{2} \left(1 - \frac{t}{2}\right)^2 dt$$

Let us expand the integrand:

$$= 2 \int_{0}^{2} \left(1 - t + \frac{t^2}{4}\right) dt$$

Integrate term-by-term:

$$= 2 \left[ t - \frac{t^2}{2} + \frac{t^3}{12} \right]_{0}^{2}$$

Evaluate at the bounds:

$$= 2 \left[ 2 - \frac{4}{2} + \frac{8}{12} \right] = 2 \left[ 2 - 2 + \frac{2}{3} \right] = 2 \cdot \frac{2}{3} = \frac{4}{3}\ \text{J}$$

**Result:** $E = \dfrac{4}{3}\ \text{J}$.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Signal Manipulation Operations

| Operation | Original | Modified | Effect on Energy | Effect on Power |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Time Shift** $x(t-t_0)$ | $x(t)$ | $x(t-t_0)$ | **Unchanged** | **Unchanged** |
| **Time Scale** $x(at)$ | $x(t)$ | $x(at)$ | $\dfrac{E}{\vert a \vert}$ | $\dfrac{P}{\vert a \vert}$ |
| **Reflection** $x(-t)$ | $x(t)$ | $x(-t)$ | **Unchanged** | **Unchanged** |
| **Amplitude Scale** $Ax(t)$ | $x(t)$ | $Ax(t)$ | $A^2 E$ | $A^2 P$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Time shifting does NOT change energy or power** — this is a favorite KTU trick question. Examiners will give you a shifted version of a signal and ask you to recompute its energy; the answer remains the same as the unshifted signal.

---

## 3.6 Derivation: Even-Odd Decomposition of Any Signal

**Theorem:** Any real signal $x(t)$ can be uniquely decomposed as $x(t) = x_e(t) + x_o(t)$, where $x_e$ is even and $x_o$ is odd.

**Derivation:**

Define:

$$x_e(t) = \frac{x(t) + x(-t)}{2}, \qquad x_o(t) = \frac{x(t) - x(-t)}{2}$$

Check evenness of $x_e$:

$$x_e(-t) = \frac{x(-t) + x(t)}{2} = x_e(t) \quad \checkmark$$

Check oddness of $x_o$:

$$x_o(-t) = \frac{x(-t) - x(t)}{2} = -x_o(t) \quad \checkmark$$

Add them back:

$$x_e(t) + x_o(t) = \frac{x(t) + x(-t)}{2} + \frac{x(t) - x(-t)}{2} = \frac{2x(t)}{2} = x(t) \quad \blacksquare$$

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Hierarchy of Elementary Signals

The following diagram illustrates how complex signals are built from elementary primitives, including the directional flow of mathematical relationships between them.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[COMPLEX SIGNAL x of t] --> B[STEP u of t]
    A --> C[IMPULSE delta of t]
    A --> D[RAMP r of t]
    A --> E[EXPONENTIAL exp a t]
    A --> F[SINUSOID sin omega t]
    A --> G[SIGNUM sgn t]
    A --> H[RECTANGULAR rect t]
    A --> I[TRIANGULAR tri t]
    A --> J[SINC sinc t]

    C -->|integral| B
    B -->|integral| D
    D -->|derivative| B
    B -->|derivative| C
    G -->|algebraic transform| B
    B -->|algebraic transform| G
    H -->|difference of two| B
    I -->|convolution of two| H
    J -->|fourier dual| H
    F -->|imaginary part| E
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Time-Domain Operations Pipeline

This functional block diagram shows the standard signal processing pipeline for elementary signal manipulation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src1[INPUT SIGNAL x of t] --> op1{OPERATION TYPE}
    op1 -->|shift| shf[SHIFT BY t0]
    op1 -->|scale| scl[SCALE BY a]
    op1 -->|reflect| ref[REFLECT x of neg t]
    op1 -->|amplitude| amp[MULTIPLY BY A]
    shf --> out1[OUTPUT y of t]
    scl --> out1
    ref --> out1
    amp --> out1
    out1 --> app[ROBOTIC APPLICATION]
    app --> rob1[MOTOR COMMAND]
    app --> rob2[SENSOR FUSION]
    app --> rob3[CONTROL LOOP]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Even-Odd Decomposition Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sig[INPUT SIGNAL x of t] --> mirror[REFLECT x of neg t]
    mirror --> add[ADD x of t and x of neg t]
    mirror --> sub[SUBTRACT x of t minus x of neg t]
    add --> div1[DIVIDE BY 2]
    sub --> div2[DIVIDE BY 2]
    div1 --> eve[EVEN COMPONENT x sub e of t]
    div2 --> odd[ODD COMPONENT x sub o of t]
    eve --> recomb[RECOMBINE x sub e plus x sub o]
    odd --> recomb
    recomb --> out[RECONSTRUCTED SIGNAL x of t]
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Signal Classification Taxonomy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root[SIGNALS] --> ct[CONTINUOUS TIME x of t]
    root --> dt[DISCRETE TIME x of n]
    ct --> ct1[PERIODIC]
    ct --> ct2[APERIODIC]
    ct --> ct3[ENERGY SIGNALS]
    ct --> ct4[POWER SIGNALS]
    ct1 --> ct1a[SINUSOID]
    ct1 --> ct1b[COMPLEX EXPONENTIAL]
    ct2 --> ct2a[IMPULSE]
    ct2 --> ct2b[STEP]
    ct2 --> ct2c[RAMP]
    ct3 --> ct3a[PULSE OF FINITE DURATION]
    dt --> dt1[SAMPLED VERSIONS OF CT SIGNALS]
    dt --> dt2[DISCRETE TIME SINUSOID]
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read these diagrams in the exam:** Mermaid block diagrams are excellent for visualizing *signal flow* in robotic systems. Practice drawing at least one such architecture for each Module 1 sub-topic.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the unit impulse function $\delta(t)$. State and explain its sifting property.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **unit impulse** $\delta(t)$ is a signal that is zero everywhere except at $t = 0$, where it is infinite, with the property that its total area is unity:

$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t)\, dt = 1$$

It is mathematically defined as:

$$\delta(t) = \begin{cases} \infty, & t = 0 \\ 0, & t \neq 0 \end{cases}$$

The **sifting property** states that the impulse, when integrated against any continuous signal $x(t)$, "picks out" the value of $x(t)$ at the location of the impulse:

$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\,\delta(t - t_0)\, dt = x(t_0)$$

This property is the foundation for impulse response analysis in LTI systems and is extensively used in robotic manipulator dynamics.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between energy signals and power signals. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Property | Energy Signal | Power Signal |
|---|---|---|
| **Total Energy** $E$ | **Finite** ($0 < E < \infty$) | **Infinite** |
| **Average Power** $P$ | **Zero** | **Finite** ($0 < P < \infty$) |
| **Duration** | Finite duration (typically) | Infinite / Periodic |

**Examples:**
- **Energy signal:** A single rectangular pulse $p(t) = \text{rect}(t/T)$ has finite energy $E = T$ and zero average power.
- **Power signal:** A sinusoidal signal $x(t) = A\sin(\omega t)$ is periodic with finite average power $P = A^2/2$ but infinite energy.

A signal **cannot** be both an energy signal and a power signal simultaneously (with non-trivial amplitude).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Module 1 Choice 1]`

**(a)** Define the unit step, unit ramp, and unit impulse signals. Show that the unit ramp signal can be obtained by integrating the unit step signal, and that the unit impulse is the derivative of the unit step. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A continuous-time signal is given by:
$$x(t) = (2t - 4)\,u(t - 2)$$
Sketch $x(t)$, and compute its energy. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 — Definitions:** (3 marks)

$$u(t) = \begin{cases} 1, & t \geq 0 \\ 0, & t < 0 \end{cases}, \quad r(t) = \begin{cases} t, & t \geq 0 \\ 0, & t < 0 \end{cases}, \quad \delta(t): \int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\delta(t)dt = 1$$

**Step 2 — $r(t) = \int u(\tau) d\tau$:** (2 marks)

$$\int_{-\infty}^{t} u(\tau)\, d\tau = \int_{0}^{t} 1\, d\tau = t \quad \text{for } t \geq 0 = r(t)$$

**Step 3 — $\delta(t) = du/dt$:** (2 marks)

$\dfrac{du(t)}{dt} = 0$ for $t \neq 0$. At $t = 0$, the discontinuity produces a unit-area spike, which by definition is $\delta(t)$.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Signal analysis:** The signal is ON at $t = 2$, with slope 2 and intercept $-4$ (at $t = 2$, $x(2) = 0$).

So $x(t)$ is a ramp of slope $2$ starting at $t = 2$.

**Step 2 — Sketch description:** The signal is zero for $t < 2$, then rises linearly as $y = 2(t-2)$ for $t \geq 2$. At $t = 2$, value $= 0$; at $t = 3$, value $= 2$; at $t = 4$, value $= 4$.

**Step 3 — Compute energy:**

$$E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt = \int_{2}^{\infty} (2t - 4)^2\, dt$$

Substitute $u = 2t - 4$, $du = 2\,dt \Rightarrow dt = du/2$. When $t = 2$, $u = 0$; as $t \to \infty$, $u \to \infty$.

$$E = \int_{0}^{\infty} u^2 \cdot \frac{du}{2} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{u^3}{3} \Big\vert_{0}^{\infty} = \frac{1}{6} \lim_{u \to \infty} u^3 = \infty$$

**Conclusion:** Energy is **infinite** — this is a **power signal** (specifically, the average power diverges because of the unbounded ramp, so this is *neither* an energy nor a finite-power signal — it is an unbounded-power signal).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Part B 7-mark question):**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Students forget to mention the *condition* $t \geq 0$ in the definition of $u(t)$, losing 1 mark.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** When computing energy of an unbounded signal, students write "$E = 0$" because the integrand is zero for $t < 2$. You must integrate over the *entire* real line; the upper-bound contribution is what matters. Partial credit is given if you correctly identify the integral diverges.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Forgetting the sifting property limits (i.e., stating $\int x(t)\delta(t) dt = x(0)$ without the integration bounds) costs 1 mark.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Sketching without axis labels (time $t$, amplitude $x(t)$) — 0.5 mark deduction.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Module 1 Choice 2]`

**(a)** Define even and odd signals. Decompose the signal $x(t) = 5 + 3\sin(t) + 2\cos(t)$ into its even and odd components. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A discrete-time signal is defined as $x[n] = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$ with the arrow under $n = 0$ pointing at $1$. Sketch $x[n]$, $x[n-2]$, and $x[-n+1]$, and identify whether each is even, odd, or neither. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 — Definitions:** (2 marks)

A signal is **even** if $x(-t) = x(t)$, and **odd** if $x(-t) = -x(t)$.

**Step 2 — Decompose using $x_e = [x(t)+x(-t)]/2$ and $x_o = [x(t)-x(-t)]/2$:** (3 marks)

Compute $x(-t)$:

$$x(-t) = 5 + 3\sin(-t) + 2\cos(-t) = 5 - 3\sin(t) + 2\cos(t)$$

Now,

$$x_e(t) = \frac{x(t) + x(-t)}{2} = \frac{5 + 3\sin t + 2\cos t + 5 - 3\sin t + 2\cos t}{2} = \frac{10 + 4\cos t}{2} = 5 + 2\cos t$$

$$x_o(t) = \frac{x(t) - x(-t)}{2} = \frac{5 + 3\sin t + 2\cos t - 5 + 3\sin t - 2\cos t}{2} = \frac{6\sin t}{2} = 3\sin t$$

**Step 3 — Verification:** (2 marks)

$x_e(-t) = 5 + 2\cos(-t) = 5 + 2\cos t = x_e(t)$ ✓ (even). $x_o(-t) = 3\sin(-t) = -3\sin t = -x_o(t)$ ✓ (odd). And $x_e + x_o = 5 + 2\cos t + 3\sin t = x(t)$ ✓.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Write $x[n]$ explicitly:**

$$x[n] = \begin{cases} 1, & n = 0 \\ 2, & n = 1 \\ 3, & n = 2 \\ 4, & n = 3 \\ 5, & n = 4 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $x[n-2]$:** Right-shift by $2$:

$$x[n-2]: x[0]=0, x[1]=0, x[2]=1, x[3]=2, x[4]=3, x[5]=4, x[6]=5$$

**Step 3 — Compute $x[-n+1]$:** Substitute $n \to -n+1$, i.e., reflect then right-shift by $1$:

$$x[-n+1] = x[-(n-1)]$$

This is a reflection of $x[n]$ followed by a shift. Sample values:

At $n=0$: $x[1]=2$; at $n=1$: $x[0]=1$; at $n=-1$: $x[2]=3$; at $n=2$: $x[-1]=0$; at $n=-2$: $x[3]=4$.

**Step 4 — Even/Odd classification:**

- $x[n]$: Not symmetric about $n=0$ (e.g., $x[-1]=0 \neq -x[1]=-2$), so **neither**.
- $x[n-2]$: Shifted version, also **neither**.
- $x[-n+1]$: Reflection + shift, **neither** (would need to verify, but in general shifts destroy even/odd nature).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Part B 14-mark question):**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** In even/odd decomposition, students incorrectly assume $\sin$ is odd and $\cos$ is even but forget to verify *numerically*. Always substitute $t \to -t$ and check.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Discrete-time students confuse "arrow position" — the arrow indicates $n=0$, and all indexing is *relative* to this origin. Drawing the arrow at the wrong sample costs 2 marks.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** For $x[-n+1]$, students often compute $x[-n-1]$ by misreading the sign. Read the operation carefully.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Not labeling axes ($n$ vs $x[n]$) in the sketch — 0.5–1 mark deduction.
> - **Common Mistake #5:** Failing to verify the decomposition $x = x_e + x_o$ numerically at a few sample points — KTU gives 1–2 marks for this verification.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Elementary Signals:**

- [x] **Unit Step $u(t)$:** $1$ for $t \geq 0$, else $0$. Laplace $= 1/s$.
- [x] **Unit Impulse $\delta(t)$:** Zero everywhere except $t = 0$, unit area. Laplace $= 1$. **Sifting property** $\int x(t)\delta(t-t_0)dt = x(t_0)$.
- [x] **Unit Ramp $r(t)$:** $t$ for $t \geq 0$. Laplace $= 1/s^2$.
- [x] **$\delta(t) = du/dt$**, **$u(t) = \int \delta$**, **$r(t) = \int u$** — these three relationships are *the* most-tested derivations.
- [x] **Signum:** $\text{sgn}(t) = 2u(t) - 1$.
- [x] **Rectangular pulse** can be expressed as $p(t) = A\,u(t) - A\,u(t-T)$.
- [x] **Time shifting** preserves signal energy and power.
- [x] **Time scaling** by factor $a$ changes energy by factor $1/\vert a \vert$ and power by $1/\vert a \vert$.
- [x] **Even-Odd decomposition:** $x_e = [x(t)+x(-t)]/2$, $x_o = [x(t)-x(-t)]/2$.
- [x] **Energy signal:** $0 < E < \infty$, $P = 0$. Example: rectangular pulse.
- [x] **Power signal:** $0 < P < \infty$, $E = \infty$. Example: sinusoid.
- [x] **Sinc function** $\text{sinc}(t) = \sin(\pi t)/(\pi t)$ — dual to rectangle in frequency.
- [x] **Sinusoid parameters:** $A$ (amplitude), $\omega$ (rad/sec), $\phi$ (phase), $T = 2\pi/\omega$.
- [x] **Robotic applications:** step response for actuator control, impulse for impact modeling, ramp for constant-acceleration trajectories, sinusoid for harmonic drive analysis.
- [x] **KTU hot questions:** Even-odd decomposition (7 marks), unit step-impulse-ramp derivations (7 marks), energy/power computation (3–7 marks), sketching shifted/scaled signals (3 marks).

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Continuous Time and Discrete Time Signals and Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT402 - SIGNAL PROCESSING FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Continuous Time and Discrete Time Signals and Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Signal?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):** A *signal* is a function of one or more independent variables that carries information about the behavior or nature of some physical phenomenon. In the context of robotics and signal processing, the independent variable is almost always **time ($t$ or $n$)**, while the dependent variable represents physical quantities such as voltage, position, velocity, current, or pressure.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a signal like a **heartbeat monitor in a hospital**:
- The **independent variable (time)** is the horizontal axis — it always moves forward, just like a ticking clock.
- The **dependent variable (voltage/amplitude)** is the vertical axis — it rises and falls, telling doctors *how strong* and *when* each beat happens.
- The signal is simply the **story of how something changes over time**.

In robotics, signals are everywhere: a **motor's RPM** over time, a **LiDAR sensor's distance reading**, a **microphone's audio waveform**, or a **camera's pixel intensity scan**. Without signals, a robot is "blind" and "deaf" to its environment.

---

## 1.2 The Two Fundamental Domains: CT vs DT

| Aspect | Continuous-Time (CT) Signal | Discrete-Time (DT) Signal |
|---|---|---|
| **Independent Variable** | Continuous $t \in \mathbb{R}$ | Discrete $n \in \mathbb{Z}$ |
| **Notation** | $x(t)$ | $x[n]$ |
| **Origin** | Defined for every instant | Defined only at specific instants |
| **Source** | Physical analog sensors (e.g., thermometer) | Digital sensors / sampled analog (e.g., digital camera) |
| **KTU Symbol Convention** | Parentheses $x(t)$ | Square brackets $x[n]$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** A very common mistake students make is writing $x(n)$ instead of $x[n]$. The square brackets are the **universally accepted KTU notation** for discrete-time signals. Examiners specifically look for this distinction.

### The Sampling Bridge

> [!NOTE]
> Most real-world robotic systems work like this: **Physical World (CT) → ADC Sampler → Processor (DT) → DAC → Actuator (CT)**. The continuous signal $x(t)$ is converted to discrete $x[n] = x(nT_s)$ by sampling it at intervals of $T_s$ seconds. This is the *bridge* between the two worlds.

---

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in Robotics

In **PCRAT402 (Signal Processing for Robotics)**, every subsequent module — from filtering sensor noise to controlling joint motors — relies on the foundational ability to:
1. **Classify** a signal correctly (periodic vs aperiodic, energy vs power, even vs odd).
2. **Manipulate** signals mathematically (shift, scale, fold).
3. **Classify** a system (linear/non-linear, causal/non-causal, stable/unstable).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Module 1 lays the *mathematical vocabulary* for the entire course. If you master this module, the rest of signal processing becomes far easier because every convolution, every Fourier transform, and every filter is built upon these primitives.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Signals (The Complete Taxonomy)

### 2.1.1 Continuous-Time vs Discrete-Time Signals

A signal is **continuous-time** if its independent variable $t$ takes all real values in an interval. A signal is **discrete-time** if its independent variable $n$ takes only integer (or countable) values.

### 2.1.2 Periodic vs Aperiodic Signals

> [!NOTE]
> **Periodic Signal Definition:** A signal $x(t)$ is periodic with period $T$ if $x(t + T) = x(t)$ for all $t$. The smallest such positive $T$ is called the *fundamental period* $T_0$.
> Similarly, $x[n]$ is periodic with period $N$ if $x[n + N] = x[n]$ for all $n \in \mathbb{Z}$.

### 2.1.3 Even vs Odd Signals

- **Even Signal:** $x(-t) = x(t)$ — symmetric about the vertical axis.
- **Odd Signal:** $x(-t) = -x(t)$ — antisymmetric about the vertical axis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule (KTU Favorite):** *Any* signal can be decomposed into even and odd parts:
> $$\text{Even Part: } x_e(t) = \frac{x(t) + x(-t)}{2}$$
> $$\text{Odd Part: } x_o(t) = \frac{x(t) - x(-t)}{2}$$
> And: $x(t) = x_e(t) + x_o(t)$ — guaranteed by KTU board questions.

### 2.1.4 Energy vs Power Signals

> [!NOTE]
> **Total Energy** of a signal $x(t)$: $E = \lim_{T \to \infty} \int_{-T}^{T} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt$
> **Average Power** of a signal $x(t)$: $P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt$

- **Energy Signal:** $0 < E < \infty$ and $P = 0$.
- **Power Signal:** $0 < P < \infty$ and $E = \infty$.
- A signal **cannot** be both energy and power simultaneously (except the zero signal).

---

## 2.2 Standard Elementary Signals (The "Building Blocks")

These five signals are the **alphabet** of signal processing. Every complex signal can be built from them.

### 2.2.1 The Unit Impulse Function $\delta(t)$ and $\delta[n]$

- **CT Unit Impulse:** $\delta(t) = \begin{cases} \infty & t = 0 \\ 0 & t \neq 0 \end{cases}$, with $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t) \, dt = 1$
- **DT Unit Impulse:** $\delta[n] = \begin{cases} 1 & n = 0 \\ 0 & n \neq 0 \end{cases}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sampling Property (KTU High-Yield):** $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \delta(t - t_0) \, dt = x(t_0)$ — the impulse "picks out" the value of $x(t)$ at the moment of impact.

### 2.2.2 The Unit Step Function $u(t)$ and $u[n]$

- **CT Unit Step:** $u(t) = \begin{cases} 1 & t \geq 0 \\ 0 & t < 0 \end{cases}$
- **DT Unit Step:** $u[n] = \begin{cases} 1 & n \geq 0 \\ 0 & n < 0 \end{cases}$

**Relationship between Step and Impulse (KTU Board Favorite):**

$$\delta(t) = \frac{du(t)}{dt}, \quad u(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} \delta(\tau) \, d\tau$$

For discrete time: $\delta[n] = u[n] - u[n-1]$ and $u[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} \delta[k]$.

### 2.2.3 The Ramp Function $r(t)$

$$r(t) = t \cdot u(t) = \begin{cases} t & t \geq 0 \\ 0 & t < 0 \end{cases}$$

### 2.2.4 The Exponential Signal

$$x(t) = e^{at} \quad \text{or} \quad x(t) = A e^{at}$$

- If $a > 0$: growing exponential.
- If $a < 0$: decaying exponential (extremely common in robotic control for system response).
- If $a = j\omega$: pure sinusoid (Euler's formula).

### 2.2.5 The Sinusoidal Signal

$$x(t) = A \sin(\omega t + \phi) \quad \text{or} \quad A \cos(\omega t + \phi)$$

where $A$ is amplitude, $\omega$ is angular frequency (rad/s), and $\phi$ is phase (radians).

---

## 2.3 Operations on Signals (Time-Domain Transformations)

> [!NOTE]
> These four operations are asked in **almost every KTU university exam** under Module 1.

Let $y(t) = x(at + b)$ be a generic transformed signal. The four key operations are:

| Operation | Transformation | Result $y(t)$ in terms of $x(t)$ |
|---|---|---|
| **Time Shifting (Delay)** | Replace $t$ with $(t - t_0)$ | $y(t) = x(t - t_0)$ — shifts right by $t_0$ |
| **Time Shifting (Advance)** | Replace $t$ with $(t + t_0)$ | $y(t) = x(t + t_0)$ — shifts left by $t_0$ |
| **Time Scaling (Compression)** | Replace $t$ with $(at)$, $a > 1$ | $y(t) = x(at)$ — compresses horizontally |
| **Time Scaling (Expansion)** | Replace $t$ with $(at)$, $0 < a < 1$ | $y(t) = x(at)$ — expands horizontally |
| **Time Reversal (Folding)** | Replace $t$ with $(-t)$ | $y(t) = x(-t)$ — mirrors about vertical axis |
| **Amplitude Scaling** | Multiply $x(t)$ by constant $A$ | $y(t) = A \cdot x(t)$ |

---

## 2.4 Classification of Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **system** is a mathematical operator that maps an input signal $x(t)$ to an output signal $y(t)$: $y(t) = T\{x(t)\}$.

### 2.4.1 Static (Memoryless) vs Dynamic (Memory) Systems

- **Static:** Output at time $t$ depends *only* on input at the *same* time $t$. Example: $y(t) = 2x(t)$.
- **Dynamic:** Output depends on past/future inputs. Example: $y(t) = x(t-1)$.

### 2.4.2 Linear vs Non-Linear Systems

A system is **linear** if it satisfies:
1. **Additivity:** $T\{x_1 + x_2\} = T\{x_1\} + T\{x_2\}$
2. **Homogeneity (Scaling):** $T\{a x\} = a \cdot T\{x\}$

Combined, this is the **Superposition Principle**:
$$T\{a x_1(t) + b x_2(t)\} = a y_1(t) + b y_2(t)$$

### 2.4.3 Time-Invariant vs Time-Variant Systems

A system is **time-invariant** if a time shift in the input produces a corresponding time shift in the output:
$$y(t - t_0) = T\{x(t - t_0)\}$$

In other words, the system's behavior **does not change with time** — the rules governing the system are constant.

### 2.4.4 Causal vs Non-Causal Systems

- **Causal:** Output at time $t$ depends only on present and past inputs (not future). The system is *physically realizable*.
- **Non-Causal:** Output depends on future inputs — physically impossible in real-time robotic systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Robotics Reality Check:** In real-time robotic control (e.g., a self-driving car), the system **must be causal** because the future hasn't happened yet. Non-causal systems can only be used in *offline* signal processing (e.g., post-processing recorded sensor data).

### 2.4.5 Stable vs Unstable Systems (BIBO Stability)

> [!NOTE]
> **BIBO (Bounded-Input Bounded-Output) Stability:** A system is BIBO stable if every bounded input produces a bounded output. Mathematically: if $\vert x(t) \vert < M$ for all $t$, then $\vert y(t) \vert < N$ for all $t$, where $M, N$ are finite constants.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> Print this table. It contains **every** formula you need to solve Module 1 problems in the KTU university exam.

| # | Concept | Formula | Conditions / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Periodicity of CT sinusoid | $x(t) = A\cos(\omega t + \phi)$ is periodic for **all** $\omega$ | Fundamental period: $T_0 = \frac{2\pi}{\vert \omega \vert}$ seconds |
| 2 | Periodicity of DT sinusoid | $x[n] = A\cos(\Omega n + \phi)$ is periodic only if $\frac{\Omega}{2\pi} = \frac{p}{q}$ rational | Fundamental period: $N_0 = q$ samples |
| 3 | Total Energy (CT) | $E = \lim_{T \to \infty} \int_{-T}^{T} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt$ | Joules (J) |
| 4 | Total Energy (DT) | $E = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert^2$ | Joules (J) |
| 5 | Average Power (CT) | $P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt$ | Watts (W) |
| 6 | Average Power (DT) | $P = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{2N+1} \sum_{n=-N}^{N} \vert x[n] \vert^2$ | Watts (W) |
| 7 | Even Part | $x_e(t) = \frac{x(t) + x(-t)}{2}$ | Symmetric component |
| 8 | Odd Part | $x_o(t) = \frac{x(t) - x(-t)}{2}$ | Antisymmetric component |
| 9 | Impulse-Integral Relation | $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \delta(t - t_0) \, dt = x(t_0)$ | Sifting property |
| 10 | Step-Impulse Link (CT) | $\delta(t) = \frac{du(t)}{dt}$ | Distributionally valid |
| 11 | Step-Impulse Link (DT) | $\delta[n] = u[n] - u[n-1]$ | Exact for all $n$ |
| 12 | Shifted Impulse Integral | $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \delta(a t - b) \, dt = \frac{1}{\vert a \vert} x\!\left(\frac{b}{a}\right)$ | Scaling property |
| 13 | LTI Convolution (CT) | $y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau) h(t - \tau) \, d\tau$ | $h(t)$ is impulse response |
| 14 | LTI Convolution (DT) | $y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x[k] h[n - k]$ | $h[n]$ is impulse response |
| 15 | BIBO Stability (LTI) | $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h(\tau) \vert \, d\tau < \infty$ (CT) or $\sum_{k} \vert h[k] \vert < \infty$ (DT) | Necessary and sufficient for LTI stability |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Motor Control:** Position sensor signal $x(t)$ fed into a controller (a system) to produce a voltage signal $y(t)$ for a DC motor. The controller's transfer function is a classic LTI system.
- **LiDAR/Sonar:** Discrete sensor readings $x[n]$ processed through digital filters to remove noise.
- **Audio Processing:** Speech signals decomposed into even/odd parts for phase-vocoder synthesis.
- **Image Processing:** A 2D signal $x[m,n]$ where intensity varies across pixel coordinates.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section builds the **worked-out problem-solving muscle** that KTU examiners reward with full marks. Every algebraic step is shown explicitly.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Energy and Power of Standard Signals

### 3.1.1 Energy of a Rectangular Pulse

**Problem:** Find the energy of the signal $x(t) = A$ for $0 \leq t \leq T$ and $0$ elsewhere.

**Step 1:** Write down the energy integral. Since $x(t)$ is non-zero only on $[0, T]$, the integral limits collapse.

$$E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $x(t) = A$ inside the interval and $x(t) = 0$ outside.

$$E = \int_{0}^{T} \vert A \vert^2 \, dt + \int_{T}^{\infty} 0 \, dt + \int_{-\infty}^{0} 0 \, dt$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the non-zero integral.

$$E = A^2 \int_{0}^{T} 1 \, dt = A^2 \cdot [t]_{0}^{T} = A^2 \cdot (T - 0)$$

**Step 4:** Final result.

$$E = A^2 T \quad \text{(Joules)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** Energy is *proportional* to the square of amplitude and the *duration* of the pulse. Doubling amplitude quadruples energy.

---

### 3.1.2 Power of a Sinusoidal Signal

**Problem:** Find the average power of $x(t) = A \cos(\omega_0 t)$.

**Step 1:** Write the power integral.

$$P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} \vert A \cos(\omega_0 t) \vert^2 \, dt$$

**Step 2:** Square the cosine and use the trigonometric identity $\cos^2(\theta) = \frac{1 + \cos(2\theta)}{2}$.

$$P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} A^2 \cdot \frac{1 + \cos(2 \omega_0 t)}{2} \, dt$$

**Step 3:** Split the integral and evaluate.

$$P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \left[ \frac{A^2}{2} \int_{-T}^{T} 1 \, dt + \frac{A^2}{2} \int_{-T}^{T} \cos(2 \omega_0 t) \, dt \right]$$

**Step 4:** The second integral is the integral of cosine over a symmetric interval, which is bounded (its magnitude is at most $2 / \omega_0$). When divided by $2T \to \infty$, the second term vanishes.

$$P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \cdot \frac{A^2}{2} \cdot 2T = \frac{A^2}{2}$$

**Step 5:** Final result.

$$\boxed{P = \frac{A^2}{2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Result:** The average power of *any* sinusoid (sine or cosine) with amplitude $A$ is $A^2 / 2$. The frequency and phase are irrelevant for power!

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Even-Odd Decomposition

**Problem:** Decompose $x(t) = e^{at}$ (for $t \in \mathbb{R}$, $a$ real) into even and odd parts.

**Step 1:** Compute $x(-t)$.

$$x(-t) = e^{-at}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the even part formula.

$$x_e(t) = \frac{e^{at} + e^{-at}}{2} = \cosh(at) \quad \text{(hyperbolic cosine)}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the odd part formula.

$$x_o(t) = \frac{e^{at} - e^{-at}}{2} = \sinh(at) \quad \text{(hyperbolic sine)}$$

**Step 4:** Verify reconstruction: $x_e(t) + x_o(t) = \cosh(at) + \sinh(at) = e^{at} = x(t)$. ✔

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Worked-Out KTU-Style Problems

### Problem 1: Periodicity Classification

**Given:** $x(t) = 2\cos(3\pi t + \pi/4) + \sin(5\pi t)$
**Find:** Is it periodic? If yes, what is the fundamental period?

**Step 1:** Identify angular frequencies.

$$\omega_1 = 3\pi \text{ rad/s}, \quad \omega_2 = 5\pi \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2:** Compute individual periods.

$$T_1 = \frac{2\pi}{\vert \omega_1 \vert} = \frac{2\pi}{3\pi} = \frac{2}{3} \text{ s}, \quad T_2 = \frac{2\pi}{\vert \omega_2 \vert} = \frac{2\pi}{5\pi} = \frac{2}{5} \text{ s}$$

**Step 3:** Check the ratio of periods.

$$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = \frac{2/3}{2/5} = \frac{5}{3} = \text{rational} \implies \text{Periodic}$$

**Step 4:** Fundamental period = LCM of $T_1$ and $T_2$.

$$\text{LCM} = \frac{\text{LCM of numerators}}{\text{GCD of denominators}} = \frac{\text{LCM}(2,2)}{\text{GCD}(3,5)} = \frac{2}{1} = 2 \text{ s}$$

**Final Answer:** $T_0 = 2$ seconds. ✔

---

### Problem 2: Time Transformations

**Given:** $x(t)$ is a rectangular pulse of height $1$ from $t = 0$ to $t = 2$. Plot $y(t) = x(2t - 1)$.

**Step 1:** Decompose the operation.

$$y(t) = x(2t - 1) = x(2(t - 0.5))$$

This is **time scaling by 2** followed by **time shift by 0.5** (right shift).

**Step 2:** Original pulse: non-zero on $[0, 2]$.

**Step 3:** After scaling by 2: the pulse is compressed — non-zero on $[0, 1]$ (since $\tau = 2t \in [0, 2] \implies t \in [0, 1]$).

**Step 4:** After shifting right by 0.5: non-zero on $[0.5, 1.5]$.

**Final Answer:** $y(t)$ is a rectangular pulse of height $1$ from $t = 0.5$ to $t = 1.5$. ✔

---

### Problem 3: LTI System Properties

**Given:** System defined by $y(t) = t \cdot x(t)$.
**Find:** Is it linear? Time-invariant? Causal? Stable?

**Linearity Check:**

Let $x_1(t) \to y_1(t) = t x_1(t)$ and $x_2(t) \to y_2(t) = t x_2(t)$.

For $a x_1 + b x_2$:

$$T\{a x_1 + b x_2\} = t(a x_1 + b x_2) = a(t x_1) + b(t x_2) = a y_1 + b y_2 \quad ✓$$

**Linear.**

**Time-Invariance Check:**

Delayed input: $x(t - t_0) \to T\{x(t-t_0)\} = t \cdot x(t - t_0)$.

Delayed output: $y(t - t_0) = (t - t_0) \cdot x(t - t_0)$.

These are **not equal** (one has $t$, the other $t - t_0$). So the system is **Time-Variant**.

**Causality Check:** Output at time $t$ depends only on input at time $t$ (no future). So **Causal**.

**Stability Check:** If $\vert x(t) \vert < M$, then $\vert y(t) \vert = \vert t \vert \cdot \vert x(t) \vert$. As $t \to \infty$, this is unbounded. So **Unstable** (not BIBO stable). ✔

---

### Problem 4: Discrete-Time Energy and Power

**Given:** $x[n] = (0.5)^n u[n]$.
**Find:** Energy $E$ and classify as energy/power signal.

**Step 1:** Energy integral.

$$E = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert^2 = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} (0.5)^{2n} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} (0.25)^n$$

**Step 2:** Infinite geometric series $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} r^n = \frac{1}{1-r}$ for $\vert r \vert < 1$.

$$E = \frac{1}{1 - 0.25} = \frac{1}{0.75} = \frac{4}{3} \text{ J}$$

**Final Answer:** $E = 4/3$ Joules (finite), so it is an **Energy Signal**. Power $P = 0$. ✔

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Signal Processing Pipeline in a Robot

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Physical World] --> B[Analog Sensor]
    B --> C[Anti-Aliasing Filter]
    C --> D[ADC Sampler]
    D --> E[Digital Signal x of n]
    E --> F[Digital Signal Processor]
    F --> G[DAC Reconstructor]
    G --> H[Smoothing Filter]
    H --> I[Actuator / Motor]
    I --> J[Physical Action]
    J --> A
    style A fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1976D2,color:#0D47A1
    style B fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#F57C00,color:#E65100
    style C fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#F57C00,color:#E65100
    style D fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#7B1FA2,color:#4A148C
    style E fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#388E3C,color:#1B5E20
    style F fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#C2185B,color:#880E4F
    style G fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#7B1FA2,color:#4A148C
    style H fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#F57C00,color:#E65100
    style I fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#388E3C,color:#1B5E20
    style J fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1976D2,color:#0D47A1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** A robot senses the physical world (light, sound, distance) through analog sensors. The continuous signal is filtered to prevent *aliasing*, then sampled into a discrete sequence $x[n]$ by the ADC. The processor applies algorithms (filters, controllers) and outputs a discrete control signal, which is reconstructed back into a continuous voltage by the DAC and used to drive an actuator (motor). The action then changes the physical world, closing the feedback loop.

---

## 4.2 Concept Map: Signal Classification Taxonomy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    sigStart[Signal x of t or x of n] --> ctCheck{CT or DT}
    ctCheck -->|CT branch| ctClass[Domain t in R]
    ctCheck -->|DT branch| dtClass[Domain n in Z]
    ctClass --> ctKind{Type}
    ctKind --> ctPer[Periodic x of t+T = x of t]
    ctKind --> ctAper[Aperiodic]
    ctKind --> ctEven[Even x of -t = x of t]
    ctKind --> ctOdd[Odd x of -t = -x of t]
    dtClass --> dtKind{Type}
    dtKind --> dtPer[Periodic x of n+N = x of n]
    dtKind --> dtAper[Aperiodic]
    dtKind --> dtEven[Even x of -n = x of n]
    dtKind --> dtOdd[Odd x of -n = -x of n]
    sigStart --> epCheck{Energy or Power}
    epCheck -->|0 less E less infinity| energySig[Energy Signal P = 0]
    epCheck -->|0 less P less infinity| powerSig[Power Signal E = infinity]
    style sigStart fill:#1976D2,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#FFFFFF
    style ctCheck fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#F57C00,color:#E65100
    style dtCheck fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#F57C00,color:#E65100
    style epCheck fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#7B1FA2,color:#4A148C
    style energySig fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#388E3C,color:#1B5E20
    style powerSig fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#C2185B,color:#880E4F
```

---

## 4.3 System Property Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    sysStart[System y of t = T of x of t] --> q1{Does output depend only on present input?}
    q1 -->|Yes| staticSys[Static Memoryless]
    q1 -->|No| dynamicSys[Dynamic Has Memory]
    sysStart --> q2{Does T of a x1 + b x2 = a y1 + b y2?}
    q2 -->|Yes| linearSys[Linear]
    q2 -->|No| nonlinSys[Non Linear]
    sysStart --> q3{Does y of t - t0 = T of x of t - t0?}
    q3 -->|Yes| tInv[Time Invariant]
    q3 -->|No| tVar[Time Variant]
    sysStart --> q4{Does y of t depend on future input x of t greater than t?}
    q4 -->|No| causalSys[Causal]
    q4 -->|Yes| noncausalSys[Non Causal]
    sysStart --> q5{Bounded input always produces bounded output?}
    q5 -->|Yes| stableSys[BIBO Stable]
    q5 -->|No| unstableSys[Unstable]
    style sysStart fill:#1976D2,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#FFFFFF
    style linearSys fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#388E3C,color:#1B5E20
    style tInv fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#388E3C,color:#1B5E20
    style causalSys fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#388E3C,color:#1B5E20
    style stableSys fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#388E3C,color:#1B5E20
    style nonlinSys fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828,color:#B71C1C
    style tVar fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828,color:#B71C1C
    style noncausalSys fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828,color:#B71C1C
    style unstableSys fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828,color:#B71C1C
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** When asked to "determine the properties of the system", examiners expect students to check **all five properties** in order: static/dynamic → linear/non-linear → time-invariant/variant → causal/non-causal → stable/unstable. Marks are awarded for *each* property correctly identified.

---

## 4.4 Transformation Pipeline: Time-Domain Operations

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    origX[Original x of t] --> op1{Operation}
    op1 -->|Replace t by t - t0| shiftOp[Time Shift y of t = x of t - t0]
    op1 -->|Replace t by -t| foldOp[Time Reversal y of t = x of -t]
    op1 -->|Replace t by a t, a greater 1| compOp[Time Compression y of t = x of a t]
    op1 -->|Replace t by a t, 0 less a less 1| expOp[Time Expansion y of t = x of a t]
    op1 -->|Multiply by A| ampOp[Amplitude Scaling y of t = A x of t]
    style origX fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1976D2,color:#0D47A1
    style op1 fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#F57C00,color:#E65100
    style shiftOp fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#388E3C,color:#1B5E20
    style foldOp fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#388E3C,color:#1B5E20
    style compOp fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#388E3C,color:#1B5E20
    style expOp fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#388E3C,color:#1B5E20
    style ampOp fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#388E3C,color:#1B5E20
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Define a **causal system**. Give one example of a causal system and one example of a non-causal system.

**Model Answer:**

> A system is **causal** if its output at any time instant $t$ depends only on the present and past values of the input — it does *not* depend on future inputs. Mathematically, for an input $x(t)$ applied at $t = -\infty$, the output at time $t_0$ is:
> $$y(t_0) = T\{x(t) : t \leq t_0\}$$
> **Example of Causal System:** $y(t) = x(t - 1)$ — the output at time $t$ uses input from 1 second ago (past).
> **Example of Non-Causal System:** $y(t) = x(t + 1)$ — the output at time $t$ uses input from 1 second in the future.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** State the **sifting property** of the unit impulse function $\delta(t)$. Use it to evaluate $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} (3t^2 + 2) \, \delta(t - 2) \, dt$.

**Model Answer:**

> The sifting (or sampling) property states that the unit impulse, when integrated against a continuous function, "picks out" the value of that function at the location of the impulse:
> $$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \, \delta(t - t_0) \, dt = x(t_0)$$
> Here $x(t) = 3t^2 + 2$ and $t_0 = 2$. Therefore:
> $$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} (3t^2 + 2) \, \delta(t - 2) \, dt = 3(2)^2 + 2 = 3 \cdot 4 + 2 = 14$$

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Time Invariance Pitfall:** Students often test time-invariance by *only* delaying the input but forget to compare with the *delayed output*. Always verify **both** $y(t-t_0)$ and $T\{x(t-t_0)\}$ — they must be identical.
> 2. **Periodicity in DT:** Unlike CT signals where every sinusoid is periodic, a DT sinusoid is periodic **only** if $\Omega / (2\pi)$ is a rational number. Students often blindly apply the CT formula and lose 2 marks.
> 3. **Stability Check:** For LTI systems, BIBO stability reduces to checking the **absolute integrability/summability** of the impulse response, not just the input-output behavior. Missing this nuance costs 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Signal Operations Order:** When doing multiple operations (e.g., $x(2t - 3)$), the **order matters**. Always perform time scaling **first**, then time shifting. Reversing the order gives the wrong answer.
> 5. **Drawing Sketches:** Sketches of transformed signals are **expected** in Part B answers. A 14-mark question without a diagram typically loses 2 marks.

---

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

> **Q.A(a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]** For the signal $x(t)$ shown below (a triangular pulse: $x(t) = t+1$ for $-1 \leq t \leq 0$, $x(t) = 1-t$ for $0 \leq t \leq 1$, and zero elsewhere):
> (i) Sketch $x(-t)$ and $x(2t)$.
> (ii) Find and plot the even and odd parts of $x(t)$.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Sketching $x(-t)$:** Replace $t$ by $-t$ — this mirrors the triangle about the vertical axis. The result is identical to $x(t)$ because the original triangle is *symmetric* about $t = 0$. Thus $x(-t) = x(t)$, confirming $x(t)$ is an even signal.

**Sketching $x(2t)$:** Replace $t$ by $2t$ — this compresses the triangle horizontally by a factor of 2. The pulse now exists from $t = -0.5$ to $t = +0.5$ with peak at $t = 0$.

**(ii) Even Part $x_e(t)$:**

For the original $x(t)$:
- $x(t) = t+1$ for $-1 \leq t \leq 0$
- $x(t) = 1-t$ for $0 \leq t \leq 1$

We need $x(-t)$:
- For $t \geq 0$: $x(-t) = $ (replace $t$ with $-t$ in $1-t$) $= 1 - (-t) = 1 + t$ for $-1 \leq -t \leq 0$, i.e., $0 \leq t \leq 1$.
- For $t \leq 0$: $x(-t) = $ (replace $t$ with $-t$ in $t+1$) $= -t + 1 = 1 - t$ for $0 \leq -t \leq 1$, i.e., $-1 \leq t \leq 0$.

So $x(-t) = x(t)$ everywhere. Therefore:

$$x_e(t) = \frac{x(t) + x(-t)}{2} = \frac{x(t) + x(t)}{2} = x(t)$$

$$x_o(t) = \frac{x(t) - x(-t)}{2} = \frac{x(t) - x(t)}{2} = 0$$

The signal is purely even; odd part is identically zero. ✔

---

> **Q.A(b) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam - May 2023]** Determine whether each of the following systems is **(i) linear**, **(ii) time-invariant**, and **(iii) BIBO stable**:
> - System S1: $y(t) = \cos(t) \cdot x(t)$
> - System S2: $y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} x(\tau) \, d\tau$

**Model Solution:**

### System S1: $y(t) = \cos(t) \cdot x(t)$

**(i) Linearity:** Test superposition.
- $T\{a x_1 + b x_2\} = \cos(t) \cdot (a x_1 + b x_2) = a \cos(t) x_1 + b \cos(t) x_2 = a y_1 + b y_2$. ✓ **Linear.**

**(ii) Time Invariance:** Delayed input: $x(t-t_0) \to T\{x(t-t_0)\} = \cos(t) \cdot x(t-t_0)$.
Delayed output: $y(t-t_0) = \cos(t-t_0) \cdot x(t-t_0)$.
Since $\cos(t) \neq \cos(t-t_0)$ in general, **Time-Variant**.

**(iii) Stability:** $\vert x(t) \vert < M \implies \vert y(t) \vert = \vert \cos(t) \vert \cdot \vert x(t) \vert \leq M$. Bounded. ✓ **Stable.**

### System S2: $y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} x(\tau) \, d\tau$

**(i) Linearity:** $T\{a x_1 + b x_2\} = \int_{-\infty}^{t} (a x_1(\tau) + b x_2(\tau)) \, d\tau = a \int x_1 + b \int x_2 = a y_1 + b y_2$. ✓ **Linear.**

**(ii) Time Invariance:** 
- Delayed input: $x(t-t_0) \to T\{x(t-t_0)\} = \int_{-\infty}^{t} x(\tau - t_0) \, d\tau$. Let $u = \tau - t_0$: $= \int_{-\infty}^{t-t_0} x(u) \, du = y(t-t_0)$. ✓ **Time-Invariant.**

**(iii) Stability:** If $\vert x(t) \vert < M$, then $\vert y(t) \vert = \vert \int_{-\infty}^{t} x(\tau) d\tau \vert \leq \int_{-\infty}^{t} M \, d\tau = \infty$. **Unstable** (an integrator accumulates a bounded DC input into an unbounded ramp).

| Property | System S1 | System S2 |
|---|---|---|
| Linear | ✓ | ✓ |
| Time-Invariant | ✗ | ✓ |
| BIBO Stable | ✓ | ✗ |

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

> **Q.B(a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** For the discrete-time signal $x[n] = \{2, 1, 3, 0, -1, 2\}$ (origin at $n=0$, so $x[-2]=2, x[-1]=1, x[0]=3, x[1]=0, x[2]=-1, x[3]=2$):
> (i) Sketch $x[n]$, $x[n-2]$, and $x[-n]$.
> (ii) Compute the total energy $E$ of $x[n]$.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Sketches:**

$x[n]$ is defined over $n = -2$ to $n = 3$:

| $n$ | -2 | -1 | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $x[n]$ | 2 | 1 | 3 | 0 | -1 | 2 |
| $x[n-2]$ | 0 | 0 | 2 | 1 | 3 | 0 |
| $x[-n]$ | 2 | -1 | 0 | 3 | 1 | 2 |

- $x[n-2]$: shifts the original sequence **right by 2** — now non-zero from $n = 0$ to $n = 5$.
- $x[-n]$: **time reversal** — flips the sequence about $n = 0$. Original $x[-2]=2$ becomes $x[2]=2$, etc.

**(ii) Energy Calculation:**

$$E = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert^2 = (2)^2 + (1)^2 + (3)^2 + (0)^2 + (-1)^2 + (2)^2$$

$$E = 4 + 1 + 9 + 0 + 1 + 4 = \boxed{19 \text{ J}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** Since $E$ is finite and non-zero, $x[n]$ is an **Energy Signal** with zero average power. ✔

---

> **Q.B(b) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Check whether the system $y[n] = n \cdot x[n] + x[n-1]$ is **(a) linear, (b) time-invariant, (c) causal, and (d) BIBO stable**. Justify each answer with a mathematical test.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Linearity Test:**

For input $a x_1[n] + b x_2[n]$:

$$T\{a x_1 + b x_2\} = n(a x_1[n] + b x_2[n]) + (a x_1[n-1] + b x_2[n-1])$$

$$= a(n x_1[n] + x_1[n-1]) + b(n x_2[n] + x_2[n-1]) = a y_1[n] + b y_2[n] \quad ✓$$

**Linear.**

**(b) Time-Invariance Test:**

Delayed input $x[n - n_0]$:

$$T\{x[n - n_0]\} = n \cdot x[n - n_0] + x[n - 1 - n_0]$$

Delayed output $y[n - n_0]$:

$$y[n - n_0] = (n - n_0) \cdot x[n - n_0] + x[n - 1 - n_0]$$

Comparing: $T\{x[n-n_0]\} = n x[n-n_0] + x[n-1-n_0]$ vs $y[n-n_0] = (n-n_0) x[n-n_0] + x[n-1-n_0]$.

These differ by the term $n_0 \cdot x[n-n_0]$, which is generally non-zero. **Time-Variant.**

**(c) Causality Test:**

Output at time $n$ uses $x[n]$ (present) and $x[n-1]$ (past). No future input. **Causal.** ✓

**(d) BIBO Stability Test:**

If $\vert x[n] \vert < M$ for all $n$, then:

$$\vert y[n] \vert = \vert n \cdot x[n] + x[n-1] \vert \leq \vert n \vert M + M = M(\vert n \vert + 1)$$

As $n \to \infty$, this is unbounded. **Unstable.**

| Property | Result |
|---|---|
| Linear | Yes |
| Time-Invariant | No |
| Causal | Yes |
| BIBO Stable | No |

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 1 Rapid-Revision Checklist (Save This!):**

- [ ] **Notation Rule:** $x(t)$ = continuous-time; $x[n]$ = discrete-time. Square brackets are mandatory for DT.
- [ ] **Five Elementary Signals:** Unit impulse $\delta(t)$, unit step $u(t)$, ramp $r(t)$, exponential $e^{at}$, sinusoid $A\cos(\omega t + \phi)$.
- [ ] **Key Relations:** $\delta(t) = du/dt$; $\delta[n] = u[n] - u[n-1]$; $u(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} \delta(\tau)d\tau$.
- [ ] **Sifting Property:** $\int x(t)\delta(t-t_0)\,dt = x(t_0)$. High-yield for Part A.
- [ ] **Periodicity:** CT sinusoids are *always* periodic. DT sinusoids periodic *only* if $\Omega/2\pi$ is rational.
- [ ] **Fundamental Period:** $T_0 = \text{LCM}$ of individual periods (when their ratio is rational).
- [ ] **Even-Odd Decomposition:** $x_e = (x(t) + x(-t))/2$; $x_o = (x(t) - x(-t))/2$. $x(t) = x_e + x_o$ always.
- [ ] **Energy vs Power:** $E = \int \vert x \vert^2 dt$ (CT) or $\sum \vert x[n] \vert^2$ (DT). Power signal $\Rightarrow E = \infty$; energy signal $\Rightarrow P = 0$.
- [ ] **Sinusoid Power:** $P = A^2/2$ regardless of frequency or phase. Memorize this.
- [ ] **System Properties to Test (in this order):** Linear → Time-Invariant → Causal → Stable → Static/Dynamic.
- [ ] **Linearity Test:** Verify $T\{a x_1 + b x_2\} = a y_1 + b y_2$.
- [ ] **Time-Invariance Test:** Compare $T\{x(t-t_0)\}$ with $y(t-t_0)$ — must be equal.
- [ ] **Causality Rule:** Output cannot depend on *future* inputs.
- [ ] **BIBO Stability:** Bounded input must produce bounded output.
- [ ] **Operation Order:** For $x(at + b)$, scale first, then shift. Wrong order = wrong graph.
- [ ] **Sketches Matter:** Always draw the transformed signal in Part B. A diagram earns 2 free marks.
- [ ] **Real-World Link:** Robotics pipelines are CT-sensor → ADC → DT-processing → DAC → CT-actuator.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 1 Notes — Continuous Time and Discrete Time Signals and Systems.**
> You are now equipped to tackle any Module 1 problem in the KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examination. Move to Module 2 only after mastering the energy-power computations, system property tests, and time-domain transformations covered here.
