topics.title: Signal Classifications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT402 - SIGNAL PROCESSING FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Signal Classifications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the rigorous framework of **Signals and Systems** as prescribed by the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, a **signal** is formally defined as any physical quantity that varies with one or more independent variables and contains information about the behavior or nature of a phenomenon. Mathematically, a signal is a function $x(t)$ or $x[n]$ that maps an independent variable (time, space, frequency, etc.) to a dependent quantity (voltage, pressure, temperature, light intensity, etc.).

A **system**, conversely, is an entity that accepts one or more input signals and produces one or more output signals through a prescribed transformation. Together, signals and systems form the foundational mathematical scaffolding upon which all modern robotics, control engineering, communications, and biomedical instrumentation is built.

**Signal Classification** is the systematic taxonomy by which we organize signals into mathematically convenient families so that they can be analyzed, processed, and synthesized using unified analytical tools such as the **Fourier Transform**, **Laplace Transform**, and **Z-Transform**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PCRAT402 - Module 1):** Classification of signals is the *first* conceptual building block. Every subsequent topic — LTI systems, convolution, Fourier analysis, and Z-transform — implicitly assumes you can recognize which class a given signal belongs to. KTU examiners frequently test classification as a 3-mark short-answer question in Part A.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing at the edge of a busy highway with a measuring tape and a notebook.

- If you measure the traffic density **continuously every fraction of a second** without stopping, you are observing a **continuous-time signal** — analogous to recording the smooth voltage waveform of an analog microphone connected to a robot's audio sensor.
- If you instead take a **photograph every 1 second** (discrete snapshots), you are forming a **discrete-time signal** — analogous to the way a digital camera in a robot vision system samples the world at fixed intervals.
- If the traffic pattern **repeats itself every 24 hours** (morning rush, evening rush), the signal is **periodic**. If a one-time accident creates a unique jam that never repeats, that event is **aperiodic**.
- If you could perfectly predict tomorrow's traffic from today's data with a deterministic formula, it's a **deterministic** signal. If unpredictable factors like a flat tire or a sudden downpour dominate, it has a **random** component.

This road-traffic metaphor maps directly onto robotic sensing, where signals are sensor voltages, and "classification" lets the engineer choose the right mathematical tool to process them.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

The two fundamental scalar metrics used to classify signals are:

- **Energy (E)** — measured in **Joules (J)** for physical signals; for arbitrary $x(t)$ it is computed in normalized units.
- **Average Power (P)** — measured in **Watts (W)** for physical signals; normalized to $1\,\Omega$ load for abstract signals.

> [!NOTE]
> **Fundamental Axiom:** Every signal in the universe belongs to exactly one of three mutually exclusive power categories — **Energy Signal**, **Power Signal**, or **Neither** (signals that are neither energy nor power signals, e.g., $x(t) = t$). This trichotomy is one of the most frequently asked 3-mark questions in KTU examinations.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Primary Axis of Classification

Signals are classified along **multiple independent axes**. Each axis answers a different engineering question:

| Axis | Engineering Question Answered |
|---|---|
| Time domain (continuous/discrete) | How is the signal sampled in time? |
| Amplitude (analog/digital) | What amplitude values can the signal take? |
| Periodicity | Does the signal repeat? |
| Symmetry | Is the signal even or odd around an axis? |
| Energy vs Power | Is the signal of finite energy or finite power? |
| Determinism | Is the signal predictable? |
| Causality | Does the signal depend on future values? |

### 2.2 Detailed Classification Breakdown

#### A. Continuous-Time vs Discrete-Time Signals

A **continuous-time signal** $x(t)$ is defined for all values of the independent variable $t \in \mathbb{R}$, where $t$ is a continuous real quantity. The signal amplitude may itself be continuous (analog) or take discrete values (quantized).

A **discrete-time signal** $x[n]$ is defined only at discrete instants, typically integer values of $n \in \mathbb{Z}$, where $n$ denotes the sample index. The independent variable is no longer continuous; it is a discrete count. Sampling a continuous-time signal at uniform intervals $T_s$ produces $x[n] = x(nT_s)$, a process central to all digital robotics systems.

#### B. Analog vs Digital Signals

- **Analog signal:** Continuous in time *and* continuous in amplitude. Example: a microphone's output voltage $v(t)$.
- **Digital signal:** Discrete in time *and* discrete in amplitude (quantized to finite levels, usually powers of 2). Example: a 16-bit ADC output representing joint encoder position.
- A **sampled signal** is discrete in time but continuous in amplitude — an intermediate stage between analog and digital.

#### C. Periodic vs Aperiodic Signals

A **continuous-time signal** $x(t)$ is **periodic** with fundamental period $T_0 > 0$ if:

$$x(t + T_0) = x(t) \quad \forall\, t \in \mathbb{R}$$

The **fundamental period** is the smallest positive $T_0$ satisfying the relation. A signal that does not satisfy this for any finite $T_0$ is **aperiodic**.

A **discrete-time signal** $x[n]$ is **periodic** with period $N > 0$ if:

$$x[n + N] = x[n] \quad \forall\, n \in \mathbb{Z}$$

The fundamental period $N$ must be a *positive integer*. If no integer $N$ exists, $x[n]$ is aperiodic.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** In discrete-time, a signal like $x[n] = \cos(\pi n/3)$ has period $N = 6$ (an integer), hence it IS periodic. But $x[n] = \cos(n)$ is aperiodic because $2\pi/N = 1$ has no integer solution. Students often wrongly call such signals periodic.

#### D. Even and Odd Signals

A signal is **even** (symmetric about the vertical axis) if:

$$x(-t) = x(t) \quad \text{or} \quad x[-n] = x[n]$$

A signal is **odd** (antisymmetric about the vertical axis) if:

$$x(-t) = -x(t) \quad \text{or} \quad x[-n] = -x[n]$$

Any arbitrary signal $x(t)$ can be uniquely decomposed into even and odd parts:

$$x_e(t) = \frac{x(t) + x(-t)}{2}, \quad x_o(t) = \frac{x(t) - x(-t)}{2}$$

#### E. Energy vs Power Signals

The **total energy** of a continuous-time signal over $(-\infty, \infty)$ is:

$$E = \lim_{T \to \infty} \int_{-T}^{T} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt$$

The **average power** over the same interval is:

$$P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt$$

Discrete-time analogues:

$$E = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert^2$$

$$P = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{2N+1} \sum_{n=-N}^{N} \vert x[n] \vert^2$$

**Classification rules:**
- If $0 < E < \infty$, then $P = 0$ → **Energy Signal**.
- If $0 < P < \infty$, then $E = \infty$ → **Power Signal**.
- No signal can be *both* an energy signal and a power signal simultaneously.
- Some signals (e.g., $x(t) = t$) are **neither**.

#### F. Deterministic vs Random Signals

A **deterministic signal** can be completely described by an explicit mathematical function; its values at all times are known with certainty. Example: $x(t) = 5\sin(2\pi 50t)$.

A **random signal** (stochastic process) can only be described statistically via probability distributions, mean, variance, and autocorrelation functions. Example: thermal noise in a robot's sensor electronics.

#### G. Causal, Non-Causal, and Anti-Causal Signals

A **causal signal** is one that is zero for all $t < 0$ (or $n < 0$):

$$x(t) = 0 \quad \forall\, t < 0$$

A **non-causal signal** has non-zero values for $t < 0$ — it depends on future input, which is physically unrealizable in real-time systems but realizable in offline processing (e.g., image processing on a stored frame).

An **anti-causal signal** is zero for $t > 0$: $x(t) = 0$ for $t > 0$.

#### H. Bounded vs Unbounded Signals

A signal is **bounded** if there exists a finite constant $M$ such that $\vert x(t) \vert \leq M$ for all $t$. Otherwise it is **unbounded**. Boundedness is crucial for the **BIBO (Bounded-Input Bounded-Output) stability** criterion.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Property | Continuous-Time Formula | Discrete-Time Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Periodicity | $x(t+T_0) = x(t)$ | $x[n+N] = x[n]$ | $T_0$ in s, $N$ dimensionless integer |
| 2 | Evenness | $x(-t) = x(t)$ | $x[-n] = x[n]$ | — |
| 3 | Oddness | $x(-t) = -x(t)$ | $x[-n] = -x[n]$ | — |
| 4 | Even Part | $x_e(t) = \tfrac{x(t)+x(-t)}{2}$ | $x_e[n] = \tfrac{x[n]+x[-n]}{2}$ | Same as $x(t)$ |
| 5 | Odd Part | $x_o(t) = \tfrac{x(t)-x(-t)}{2}$ | $x_o[n] = \tfrac{x[n]-x[-n]}{2}$ | Same as $x(t)$ |
| 6 | Energy | $E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt$ | $E = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert^2$ | Joules (J) |
| 7 | Avg Power | $P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \tfrac{1}{2T}\int_{-T}^{T} \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt$ | $P = \lim_{N \to \infty} \tfrac{1}{2N+1}\sum_{n=-N}^{N} \vert x[n] \vert^2$ | Watts (W) |
| 8 | Bounded | $\vert x(t) \vert \leq M < \infty$ | $\vert x[n] \vert \leq M < \infty$ | — |
| 9 | Causal | $x(t) = 0$ for $t < 0$ | $x[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$ | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** In robotics, encoder signals from joint motors are *discrete-time, digital, periodic, bounded, causal* signals. Camera frames are *discrete-time, 2-D spatial, aperiodic, bounded* signals. IMU noise is *random* (stochastic). Microphone audio from a humanoid robot's speech module is *continuous-time, analog, aperiodic, bounded, causal*. Recognizing these classes in advance dictates whether you should use FFT, DFT, autocorrelation, or Kalman filtering.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1: Energy and Power of a Rectangular Pulse

**Problem:** Determine the energy and average power of the rectangular pulse:

$$x(t) = \begin{cases} A, & 0 \leq t \leq T \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

where $A$ and $T$ are positive real constants.

**Step 1 — Energy calculation:** Substitute the piecewise definition into the energy integral.

$$E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt$$

Since $x(t)$ is non-zero only on $[0, T]$, the integrand is zero outside this interval. We may therefore restrict the integration bounds:

$$E = \int_{0}^{T} \vert A \vert^2 \, dt$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the integral:** Since $A$ is a real constant, $\vert A \vert^2 = A^2$.

$$E = A^2 \int_{0}^{T} \, dt$$

**Step 3 — Perform the integration:**

$$E = A^2 \, [t]_{0}^{T} = A^2 (T - 0) = A^2 T$$

**Result:** $E = A^2 T$ Joules (in normalized 1-$\Omega$ load units).

**Step 4 — Power calculation:** Use the average power definition.

$$P = \lim_{T_0 \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T_0} \int_{-T_0}^{T_0} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt$$

For very large $T_0 \gg T$, the energy $A^2 T$ is spread over an interval of length $2T_0$:

$$P = \lim_{T_0 \to \infty} \frac{A^2 T}{2 T_0} = 0$$

**Conclusion:** Since $0 < E = A^2 T < \infty$ and $P = 0$, the rectangular pulse is an **energy signal**. ✓

### 3.2 Worked Example 2: Periodicity of a Discrete-Time Sinusoid

**Problem:** Determine whether $x[n] = \cos\left(\tfrac{\pi n}{3} + \tfrac{\pi}{4}\right)$ is periodic. If yes, find the fundamental period $N$.

**Step 1 — Recall the periodicity condition.** A discrete-time sinusoid $\cos(\omega_0 n + \phi)$ is periodic **if and only if** $\omega_0$ is a rational multiple of $2\pi$:

$$\frac{\omega_0}{2\pi} = \frac{p}{q} \quad \text{where } p, q \in \mathbb{Z},\; \gcd(p,q) = 1$$

The fundamental period is then $N = q$ (i.e., $N = 2\pi/\omega_0$ when the ratio is rational).

**Step 2 — Identify $\omega_0$:**

$$\omega_0 = \frac{\pi}{3} \quad \text{rad/sample}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the ratio:**

$$\frac{\omega_0}{2\pi} = \frac{\pi/3}{2\pi} = \frac{1}{6}$$

This is a rational number with $p = 1$, $q = 6$.

**Step 4 — Verify periodicity using the smallest integer $N$:**

$$x[n+6] = \cos\left(\frac{\pi(n+6)}{3} + \frac{\pi}{4}\right) = \cos\left(\frac{\pi n}{3} + 2\pi + \frac{\pi}{4}\right)$$

Using the identity $\cos(\theta + 2\pi) = \cos(\theta)$:

$$x[n+6] = \cos\left(\frac{\pi n}{3} + \frac{\pi}{4}\right) = x[n] \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 5 — Confirm minimality.** Try $N = 3$:

$$x[n+3] = \cos\left(\frac{\pi(n+3)}{3} + \frac{\pi}{4}\right) = \cos\left(\frac{\pi n}{3} + \pi + \frac{\pi}{4}\right) = -\cos\left(\frac{\pi n}{3} + \frac{\pi}{4}\right) = -x[n] \quad \times$$

So $N = 3$ does *not* work. $N = 6$ is therefore the fundamental period.

**Conclusion:** $x[n]$ is **periodic** with fundamental period $N = 6$. ✓

### 3.3 Worked Example 3: Even-Odd Decomposition

**Problem:** Decompose $x(t) = e^{-at} u(t)$ (where $a > 0$ and $u(t)$ is the unit step) into its even and odd components.

**Step 1 — Compute $x(-t)$:**

$$x(-t) = e^{a t} u(-t)$$

**Step 2 — Apply the even-part formula:**

$$x_e(t) = \frac{x(t) + x(-t)}{2} = \frac{e^{-at} u(t) + e^{at} u(-t)}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the odd-part formula:**

$$x_o(t) = \frac{x(t) - x(-t)}{2} = \frac{e^{-at} u(t) - e^{at} u(-t)}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Verify the reconstruction:** Sum $x_e(t) + x_o(t)$:

$$x_e(t) + x_o(t) = \frac{2 e^{-at} u(t)}{2} = e^{-at} u(t) = x(t) \quad \checkmark$$

**Engineering Insight:** The even part of any causal real signal equals $x_e(t) = \frac{1}{2} x(t)$ for $t > 0$, and the odd part equals $x_o(t) = \frac{1}{2} x(t)$ for $t > 0$. For $t < 0$, they are mirror images with opposite signs. This property is heavily used in deriving the **Hilbert Transform** relationships and in the **real-part sufficiency** theorem for Fourier transforms.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Worked-Result Comparison Table

| Signal $x(t)$ or $x[n]$ | Periodic? | Even/Odd/Neither | Energy Signal? | Power Signal? | Causal? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $A\cos(\omega_0 t + \phi)$ | Yes ($T_0 = 2\pi/\omega_0$) | Neither | No | Yes | No |
| $A e^{-a \vert t \vert},\; a > 0$ | No | Even | Yes ($E = A^2/a$) | No | No |
| $u(t)$ (unit step) | No | Neither | No | No (it is $1/2$) | Yes |
| $A \cdot \text{rect}(t/T)$ | No | Even | Yes ($E = A^2 T$) | No | No |
| $\cos(\pi n/4)$ | Yes ($N = 8$) | Even | No | Yes ($P = 1/2$) | No |
| $\delta(t)$ (unit impulse) | No | Even | Yes ($E = 1$) | No | Yes |
| $\cos(n)$ | No (irrational) | Even | No | Yes | No |
| $e^{j\omega_0 n}$ | Yes (if $\omega_0/2\pi$ rational) | Neither | No | Yes ($P = 1$) | No |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Taxonomy of Signals (Hierarchical Block Diagram)

The complete hierarchical classification of signals in the KTU 2024 syllabus is shown below. Every signal encountered in robotics signal processing falls into one of the branches of this tree.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[SIGNAL CLASSIFICATION] --> B[TIME DOMAIN AXIS]
    A --> C[AMPLITUDE AXIS]
    A --> D[PERIODICITY AXIS]
    A --> E[SYMMETRY AXIS]
    A --> F[ENERGY POWER AXIS]
    A --> G[DETERMINISM AXIS]
    A --> H[CAUSALITY AXIS]
    A --> I[BOUNDEDNESS AXIS]

    B --> B1[CONTINUOUS TIME x of t]
    B --> B2[DISCRETE TIME x of n]

    C --> C1[ANALOG continuous in time and amplitude]
    C --> C2[QUANTIZED discrete amplitude]
    C --> C3[DIGITAL discrete time and amplitude]

    D --> D1[PERIODIC repeats with period T0 or N]
    D --> D2[APERIODIC no repetition]

    E --> E1[EVEN x of minus t equals x of t]
    E --> E2[ODD x of minus t equals minus x of t]
    E --> E3[NEITHER general signal]

    F --> F1[ENERGY SIGNAL finite E, zero P]
    F --> F2[POWER SIGNAL finite P, infinite E]
    F --> F3[NEITHER infinite E and P]

    G --> G1[DETERMINISTIC known exactly]
    G --> G2[RANDOM described statistically]

    H --> H1[CAUSAL zero for t less than zero]
    H --> H2[NON CAUSAL depends on future]
    H --> H3[ANTI CAUSAL zero for t greater than zero]

    I --> I1[BOUNDED magnitude less than M]
    I --> I2[UNBOUNDED magnitude grows without limit]
```

### 4.2 Signal Flow Architecture: From Physical Sensor to Classification Label

This diagram illustrates how a raw physical quantity in a robot is transformed into a classified mathematical signal, and which classification tool the engineer should invoke at each stage.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[PHYSICAL PHENOMENON\nlight sound force heat] --> P2[TRANSDUCER\ncamera mic encoder thermistor]
    P2 --> P3[RAW VOLTAGE SIGNAL\nv of t]
    P3 --> P4[ANALOG FRONT END\namplification and filtering]
    P4 --> P5[ADC\nsampling and quantization]
    P5 --> P6[DISCRETE DIGITAL SIGNAL\nx of n]

    P3 --> Q1{Continuous or Discrete in Time}
    P6 --> Q1
    Q1 --> Q2[Q1a Continuous Time Tool: Fourier Transform, Laplace]
    Q1 --> Q3[Q1b Discrete Time Tool: DFT, Z Transform]

    P6 --> R1{Periodic or Aperiodic}
    R1 --> R2[R2a Periodic: use line spectra]
    R1 --> R3[R2b Aperiodic: use continuous spectrum]

    P6 --> S1{Energy or Power}
    S1 --> S2[S2a Energy: use energy spectral density]
    S1 --> S3[S2b Power: use power spectral density]

    Q2 --> OUT[ROBOT DECISION LAYER\nmotor control perception planning]
    Q3 --> OUT
    R2 --> OUT
    R3 --> OUT
    S2 --> OUT
    S3 --> OUT
```

### 4.3 Decision Tree: Is a Given Signal an Energy Signal or Power Signal?

This is the single most important decision tree KTU examiners test, because it determines whether you will use the energy spectral density (ESD) or the power spectral density (PSD) in later modules.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[GIVEN SIGNAL x of t or x of n] --> calcE[Compute total energy E]
    calcE --> eCheck{Is E finite and nonzero}
    eCheck --> eYes[FINITE ENERGY detected]
    eCheck --> eNo[Energy is infinite]
    eYes --> esig[LABEL: ENERGY SIGNAL\nAverage power P equals zero]
    eNo --> calcP[Compute average power P]
    calcP --> pCheck{Is P finite and nonzero}
    pCheck --> pYes[FINITE POWER detected]
    pCheck --> pNo[Power is also infinite]
    pYes --> psig[LABEL: POWER SIGNAL\nEnergy is infinite]
    pNo --> neither[LABEL: NEITHER ENERGY NOR POWER\nExample: x of t equals t]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Connection:** The decision in the tree above directly determines the choice between the **Parseval's Theorem for Energy Signals** (using $E = \int \vert X(f) \vert^2 df$) and the **Parseval's Theorem for Power Signals** (using $P = \sum \vert c_k \vert^2$). This is a Module-2 to Module-3 dependency that examiners love to chain together.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define an energy signal and a power signal. Show with a one-line mathematical condition how they are mutually exclusive.*

**Model Answer:**

An **energy signal** is one whose total energy $E$ over the infinite interval is finite and non-zero, and whose average power is consequently zero. Mathematically, a signal $x(t)$ is an energy signal if:

$$0 < E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt < \infty$$

A **power signal** is one whose average power $P$ is finite and non-zero, and whose total energy is infinite:

$$0 < P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T}\int_{-T}^{T} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt < \infty$$

**Mutual Exclusivity Proof:** If $0 < E < \infty$, then

$$P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{E}{2T} = 0$$

and conversely, if $0 < P < \infty$, then $E = \lim_{T \to \infty} 2T \cdot P = \infty$. Hence a signal cannot simultaneously be an energy signal and a power signal. $\blacksquare$

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]**
*Determine whether the discrete-time signal $x[n] = \cos\left(\tfrac{3\pi}{5} n\right)$ is periodic. If periodic, find the fundamental period.*

**Model Answer:**

A discrete-time sinusoid $\cos(\omega_0 n)$ is periodic iff $\omega_0 / (2\pi)$ is a rational number. Here:

$$\frac{\omega_0}{2\pi} = \frac{3\pi/5}{2\pi} = \frac{3}{10}$$

Since $\frac{3}{10}$ is rational, the signal is periodic. The fundamental period is the smallest integer $N$ such that $\omega_0 N = 2\pi k$ for some integer $k$. With $k = 3$ and $N = 10$:

$$\omega_0 \cdot 10 = \frac{3\pi}{5} \cdot 10 = 6\pi = 2\pi \cdot 3 \quad \checkmark$$

Therefore $N = 10$ is the fundamental period. $\blacksquare$

---

### Part B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** *Classify the signal $x(t) = A\cos(\omega_0 t + \phi) + B\sin(\omega_0 t + \theta)$ along the following axes: (i) continuous-time or discrete-time, (ii) periodic or aperiodic, (iii) even/odd/neither, (iv) energy or power signal. Justify each classification with a clear mathematical or logical argument. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *For the discrete-time signal $x[n] = 2(0.5)^n u[n]$, determine: (i) whether it is an energy signal or a power signal by computing $E$ and $P$ explicitly, (ii) whether it is causal, (iii) whether it is bounded. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution for (a):**

**(i) Continuous-time:** $x(t)$ is defined for all real $t$ and the independent variable is continuous. Hence it is a **continuous-time signal**. ✓

**(ii) Periodic:** Both $A\cos(\omega_0 t + \phi)$ and $B\sin(\omega_0 t + \theta)$ have the same angular frequency $\omega_0$, so their sum has the same fundamental period:

$$T_0 = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_0} \quad \text{seconds}$$

Verification:

$$x(t + T_0) = A\cos(\omega_0(t + T_0) + \phi) + B\sin(\omega_0(t + T_0) + \theta) = x(t) \quad \checkmark$$

Hence **periodic**. ✓

**(iii) Even/Odd/Neither:** Compute $x(-t)$:

$$x(-t) = A\cos(-\omega_0 t + \phi) + B\sin(-\omega_0 t + \theta) = A\cos(\omega_0 t - \phi) - B\sin(\omega_0 t - \theta)$$

For $x(-t) = x(t)$, we would need $\phi = -\phi$ and $\theta = -\theta$, i.e., $\phi = \theta = 0$. In the general case, the signal is **neither even nor odd**. ✓

**(iv) Energy or Power:** Since the signal is a non-decaying sinusoid, $\int_{-T}^{T} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt$ grows linearly with $T$, so $E = \infty$. The average power is:

$$P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T}\int_{-T}^{T} (A^2\cos^2(\cdot) + B^2\sin^2(\cdot) + \text{cross terms}) \, dt = \frac{A^2 + B^2}{2}$$

This is finite and non-zero, hence $x(t)$ is a **power signal**. ✓

**Model Solution for (b):**

**(i) Energy and Power calculation:**

The signal $x[n] = 2(0.5)^n u[n]$ is non-zero only for $n \geq 0$, so:

$$E = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert^2 = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} 4(0.5)^{2n} = 4 \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} (0.25)^n$$

This is an infinite geometric series with ratio $r = 0.25 < 1$:

$$E = 4 \cdot \frac{1}{1 - 0.25} = 4 \cdot \frac{1}{0.75} = \frac{4}{0.75} = \frac{16}{3} \approx 5.333 \text{ J}$$

The average power is:

$$P = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{2N+1} \sum_{n=0}^{N} 4(0.5)^{2n} = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{16/3 \cdot (1 - (0.25)^{N+1})}{(2N+1)} = 0$$

Hence $0 < E < \infty$ and $P = 0$, so $x[n]$ is an **energy signal**. ✓

**(ii) Causality:** Since $u[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$, we have $x[n] = 0$ for all $n < 0$. Hence the signal is **causal**. ✓

**(iii) Boundedness:** The maximum value occurs at $n = 0$:

$$\max_n \vert x[n] \vert = \vert x[0] \vert = \vert 2 \cdot 1 \vert = 2$$

And the signal decays monotonically to zero as $n \to \infty$. So $\vert x[n] \vert \leq 2$ for all $n$, hence the signal is **bounded** with $M = 2$. ✓

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]

**(a)** *Define the even and odd parts of a signal. Derive the expressions for $x_e(t)$ and $x_o(t)$ in terms of $x(t)$ and $x(-t)$. Verify the decomposition with a non-trivial example of your choice. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *For the signal $x(t) = e^{-2t} u(t)$: (i) compute its even and odd components, (ii) determine its energy $E$ exactly, (iii) state and justify whether the signal is causal, bounded, and an energy or power signal. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution for (a):**

Let $x_e(t)$ be the even part and $x_o(t)$ be the odd part of $x(t)$. By definition:

$$x_e(-t) = x_e(t) \quad \text{and} \quad x_o(-t) = -x_o(t)$$

The signal can be written as $x(t) = x_e(t) + x_o(t)$. Replacing $t$ by $-t$:

$$x(-t) = x_e(-t) + x_o(-t) = x_e(t) - x_o(t)$$

**Solving the system:** Add and subtract the two equations:

$$x(t) + x(-t) = 2 x_e(t) \implies x_e(t) = \frac{x(t) + x(-t)}{2}$$

$$x(t) - x(-t) = 2 x_o(t) \implies x_o(t) = \frac{x(t) - x(-t)}{2}$$

**Verification example:** Let $x(t) = 3t^2 + 5t + 7$.

Then $x(-t) = 3t^2 - 5t + 7$. Applying the formulas:

$$x_e(t) = \frac{(3t^2 + 5t + 7) + (3t^2 - 5t + 7)}{2} = \frac{6t^2 + 14}{2} = 3t^2 + 7$$

$$x_o(t) = \frac{(3t^2 + 5t + 7) - (3t^2 - 5t + 7)}{2} = \frac{10t}{2} = 5t$$

Check: $x_e(-t) = 3(-t)^2 + 7 = 3t^2 + 7 = x_e(t)$ ✓ (even). $x_o(-t) = 5(-t) = -5t = -x_o(t)$ ✓ (odd). And $x_e(t) + x_o(t) = (3t^2 + 7) + 5t = 3t^2 + 5t + 7 = x(t)$ ✓. $\blacksquare$

**Model Solution for (b):**

**(i) Even and odd components of $x(t) = e^{-2t} u(t)$:**

First, $x(-t) = e^{2t} u(-t)$. Applying the formulas:

$$x_e(t) = \frac{e^{-2t} u(t) + e^{2t} u(-t)}{2}$$

$$x_o(t) = \frac{e^{-2t} u(t) - e^{2t} u(-t)}{2}$$

**(ii) Energy calculation:**

$$E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-4t} \, dt$$

Using the standard integral $\int_0^\infty e^{-at} dt = 1/a$ for $a > 0$:

$$E = \frac{1}{4} \text{ Joules}$$

**(iii) Classification:**

- **Causal:** $x(t) = 0$ for $t < 0$ by definition of $u(t)$. **Causal ✓**
- **Bounded:** $\max \vert x(t) \vert = \vert x(0) \vert = 1$, and $x(t) \to 0$ as $t \to \infty$. So $\vert x(t) \vert \leq 1$ for all $t$. **Bounded with $M = 1$ ✓**
- **Energy or Power:** $0 < E = 1/4 < \infty$ and $P = 0$. **Energy signal ✓**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Mark loss in periodicity:** When asked "is this signal periodic?", students often write only "yes" or "no" without computing $N$ or showing the verification $x[n+N] = x[n]$. This costs **2 of the 3 marks** allocated to that sub-part. Always show the substitution and use of the $2\pi$ identity.
> 2. **Mark loss in energy/power:** Forgetting to take the *limit* in the average-power definition or assuming $P = 0$ without computing is a **common 3-mark penalty**. Show the limit explicitly.
> 3. **Mark loss in even-odd decomposition:** Students sometimes write $x_e(t) = x(-t)$ (confusing reflection with even-part). The even part is the **average** of $x(t)$ and $x(-t)$, not the reflection itself. This conceptual error is heavily penalized.
> 4. **Discrete vs continuous confusion:** Do not apply continuous-time formulas to discrete-time signals. The energy sum $\sum \vert x[n] \vert^2$ replaces the integral; the limit is over $N$, not $T$.

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Signal**: A function of one or more independent variables carrying information.
- **Continuous-time** signals $x(t)$ are defined for all $t \in \mathbb{R}$; **discrete-time** signals $x[n]$ only for $n \in \mathbb{Z}$.
- **Analog** = continuous in time *and* amplitude; **Digital** = discrete in time *and* amplitude; **Sampled** = discrete in time, continuous in amplitude.
- **Periodicity:** $x(t+T_0) = x(t)$ with $T_0 > 0$ (real); $x[n+N] = x[n]$ with $N$ a *positive integer*. Discrete-time sinusoids are periodic only if $\omega_0/2\pi$ is rational.
- **Even** $x(t) = x(-t)$; **Odd** $x(t) = -x(-t)$. Any signal decomposes uniquely as $x = x_e + x_o$.
- **Energy signal:** $0 < E < \infty$, $P = 0$.
- **Power signal:** $0 < P < \infty$, $E = \infty$.
- These two categories are **mutually exclusive**.
- **Deterministic** signals have explicit mathematical descriptions; **random** signals require statistical descriptions.
- **Causal** signals are zero for $t < 0$ (or $n < 0$); **non-causal** signals depend on future values; **anti-causal** signals are zero for $t > 0$.
- **Bounded** signals satisfy $\vert x(t) \vert \leq M$ for finite $M$; this is essential for BIBO stability.
- A signal like $x(t) = t$ is **neither** an energy signal nor a power signal.
- The rectangular pulse $A\,\text{rect}(t/T)$ is a canonical example of an **energy signal** with $E = A^2 T$.
- A pure sinusoid is the canonical example of a **power signal** with $P = A^2/2$.
- In robotics, classification must be done *before* analysis: this determines whether to deploy Fourier (continuous) or DFT/Z-transform (discrete) tools, and whether to use energy or power spectral density.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Operations
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT402 - SIGNAL PROCESSING FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Operations

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Signal Processing for Robotics (PCRAT402)**, **Operations on Signals** refer to the set of well-defined mathematical transformations applied to continuous-time and discrete-time signals to manipulate their **independent variable** (typically time, $t$ or $n$) and **dependent variable** (amplitude). These operations form the foundational vocabulary with which all higher-order signal analysis (convolution, Fourier transforms, Z-transforms) is constructed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCRAT402, Module 1):**
> "Operations on signals include time-shifting, time-scaling, time-reversal, amplitude-scaling, addition, multiplication, differentiation, and integration of continuous-time as well as discrete-time signals. Mastery of these elementary operations is mandatory before proceeding to LTI system analysis."

The principal operations, as classified by the KTU 2024 Scheme, are:

| Category | Operation | Continuous-Time Form | Discrete-Time Form |
|----------|-----------|----------------------|---------------------|
| Independent Variable | Time Shifting | $x(t) \to x(t - t_0)$ | $x[n] \to x[n - n_0]$ |
| Independent Variable | Time Scaling | $x(t) \to x(at)$ | $x[n] \to x[kn]$ |
| Independent Variable | Time Reversal | $x(t) \to x(-t)$ | $x[n] \to x[-n]$ |
| Dependent Variable | Amplitude Scaling | $x(t) \to A \cdot x(t)$ | $x[n] \to A \cdot x[n]$ |
| Two-Signal | Addition | $y(t) = x_1(t) + x_2(t)$ | $y[n] = x_1[n] + x_2[n]$ |
| Two-Signal | Multiplication | $y(t) = x_1(t) \cdot x_2(t)$ | $y[n] = x_1[n] \cdot x_2[n]$ |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Movie Reel" Intuition

Imagine a **signal** as a movie reel showing the trajectory of a robot's end-effector over time. Now consider the following intuitive operations:

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy for Robotics Students:**
>
> - **Time Shifting** → Pressing the "fast-forward" or "rewind" button on the reel. The shape of the trajectory stays identical; only *when* it starts changes. If a robot arm was supposed to grip an object at $t = 5$ s and we apply $x(t-2)$, it now grips at $t = 7$ s — a **2-second delay** (critical in latency compensation for teleoperated robots).
>
> - **Time Scaling** → Playing the reel at **2× speed** ($a = 2$ compresses time) or in **slow motion** ($a = 0.5$ expands time). The trajectory is the same, but the robot executes the motion twice as fast.
>
> - **Time Reversal** → Playing the reel **backwards**. A forward-reaching arm motion becomes a retracting motion — this is exactly what **mirror-image trajectory planning** does in bimanual (two-armed) robots.
>
> - **Amplitude Scaling** → Turning the **volume knob** on the signal. A factor $A = 2$ doubles the joint torque command; $A = 0$ silences the robot entirely (emergency stop).
>
> - **Addition** → **Superimposing** two trajectories — for example, the base trajectory of a mobile robot plus the swing motion of its manipulator arm.
>
> - **Multiplication** → A **gating** or **windowing** operation. Multiplying a continuous motion by a unit step turns it on or off, like a switch.

## 1.3 Why Operations Matter in Robotics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **PCRAT402**, signal operations are not abstract mathematics — they are the *primary tools* used by the roboticist to:
> 1. **Synchronize sensor streams** (delaying a LIDAR pulse to match camera exposure — *time shift*).
> 2. **Resample motion trajectories** for different actuator speeds (*time scaling*).
> 3. **Cancel echo / reverberation** in audio commands (*signal addition*).
> 4. **Modulate** a high-frequency carrier with a low-frequency control signal (*signal multiplication* — Amplitude Modulation).
> 5. **Compute velocity from position sensors** (*differentiation*).
> 6. **Compute position from accelerometer data** (*integration*).

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Time Shifting — $x(t) \to x(t - t_0)$

The continuous-time signal $x(t)$ is shifted along the time axis by replacing $t$ with $t - t_0$.

### The "Why" Behind the Sign Convention

- If $t_0 > 0$: The graph is shifted to the **right** by $t_0$ units. This is called a **delay** (the signal occurs *later* in time).
- If $t_0 < 0$: The graph is shifted to the **left** by $\vert t_0 \vert$ units. This is called an **advance** (the signal occurs *earlier*).

### Mathematical Justification

For the original signal at time $t = a$, the value was $x(a)$. After shifting, the same value $x(a)$ now appears at the new time $t' = a + t_0$, which means $t' - t_0 = a$, or $t' = a + t_0$. So the new signal $y(t)$ satisfies $y(a + t_0) = x(a)$, giving $y(t) = x(t - t_0)$.

For discrete-time: $y[n] = x[n - n_0]$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Robotics Application:** In **teleoperation**, communication delay $t_d$ between master and slave robots is modeled by a shift $y(t) = x(t - t_d)$. The slave robot receives the command $t_d$ seconds after the master issues it.

## 2.2 Time Scaling — $x(t) \to x(at)$

The signal is compressed or expanded in time.

- If $\vert a \vert > 1$: The signal is **compressed** by a factor of $a$ (it plays faster).
- If $0 < \vert a \vert < 1$: The signal is **expanded** by a factor of $1/a$ (it plays slower).
- If $a < 0$: Time scaling is combined with time reversal.

### Derivation of the Scaling Logic

For $x(t)$ at $t = a$, the value is $x(a)$. In the scaled signal $y(t) = x(at)$, we require $y(a/a) = y(1) = x(a \cdot 1) = x(a)$. So the value $x(a)$ is preserved but relocated to time $t = a/a = 1$, compressing the time axis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Discrete-time caution:** For $x[n] \to x[kn]$ to be well-defined for *every* integer $n$, the signal must be defined at all multiples. If $k$ is not an integer, intermediate samples are *dropped* (decimation) and information is lost.

## 2.3 Time Reversal — $x(t) \to x(-t)$

The signal is reflected about the **vertical axis** (the $y$-axis, i.e., $t = 0$).

- In continuous time: $y(t) = x(-t)$.
- In discrete time: $y[n] = x[-n]$.

This is the mathematical equivalent of mirror imaging. For an **even signal** $x(t) = x(-t)$, reversal is a no-op. For an **odd signal** $x(t) = -x(-t)$, reversal negates the signal.

## 2.4 Amplitude Scaling — $x(t) \to A \cdot x(t)$

- If $A > 1$: Amplification.
- If $0 < A < 1$: Attenuation.
- If $A < 0$: Amplitude scaling *combined with* sign inversion (vertical reflection).

This operation preserves the time-domain shape but rescales the magnitude axis. In robotics, this is the **gain** of a control system.

## 2.5 Signal Addition — $y(t) = x_1(t) + x_2(t)$

The output at every time $t$ is the *algebraic sum* of the two input samples. This is a **point-wise** operation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Example:** A mobile robot's left-wheel encoder produces $x_1(t)$ and the right-wheel encoder produces $x_2(t)$. The sum $x_1(t) + x_2(t)$ gives the **total forward velocity** (assuming both wheels drive the robot forward).

## 2.6 Signal Multiplication — $y(t) = x_1(t) \cdot x_2(t)$

The output at every time $t$ is the *product* of the two input samples.

This is also called **modulation** when one signal is a high-frequency carrier and the other is a low-frequency message signal. In robotics, **gating** a control signal by multiplying it with a unit step or pulse is a multiplication operation that acts as a switch.

## 2.7 Differentiation and Integration

### Differentiation

$$y(t) = \frac{d}{dt}x(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{x(t + \Delta t) - x(t)}{\Delta t}$$

This converts a **position** signal to a **velocity** signal, or a **voltage** signal to its rate of change. The output is undefined at discontinuities (Dirac impulses may appear).

For discrete time, the first-difference approximation is:

$$y[n] = x[n] - x[n-1]$$

### Integration

$$y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} x(\tau) \, d\tau$$

This converts a **velocity** signal to a **position** signal, or a **current** signal to a **charge** signal. Integration is a **smoothing** operation and a **memory** operation (the output at time $t$ depends on the entire past of $x$).

> [!WARNING]
> In real robotic systems, differentiation **amplifies high-frequency noise** (since $\frac{d}{dt}\sin(\omega t) = \omega \cos(\omega t)$ and $\omega$ multiplies the noise). Conversely, integration **suppresses high-frequency noise** but may introduce **drift** (low-frequency error accumulation).

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Operation Name | Continuous-Time Math | Discrete-Time Math | Unit / Notes |
|---|----------------|----------------------|--------------------|--------------|
| 1 | Time Shifting (delay by $t_0 > 0$) | $y(t) = x(t - t_0)$ | $y[n] = x[n - n_0]$ | $t_0$ in **seconds (s)**, $n_0$ is dimensionless |
| 2 | Time Shifting (advance by $t_0 < 0$) | $y(t) = x(t - t_0) = x(t + \vert t_0 \vert)$ | $y[n] = x[n - n_0] = x[n + \vert n_0 \vert]$ | Negative shift = advance |
| 3 | Time Scaling | $y(t) = x(at)$ | $y[n] = x[kn]$ | $\vert a \vert > 1$: compression; $\vert a \vert < 1$: expansion |
| 4 | Time Reversal | $y(t) = x(-t)$ | $y[n] = x[-n]$ | Reflection about $t = 0$ / $n = 0$ |
| 5 | Amplitude Scaling | $y(t) = A \cdot x(t)$ | $y[n] = A \cdot x[n]$ | $A$ is dimensionless gain |
| 6 | Signal Addition | $y(t) = x_1(t) + x_2(t)$ | $y[n] = x_1[n] + x_2[n]$ | Point-wise operation |
| 7 | Signal Multiplication | $y(t) = x_1(t) \cdot x_2(t)$ | $y[n] = x_1[n] \cdot x_2[n]$ | Modulation / gating |
| 8 | Differentiation | $y(t) = \dfrac{dx(t)}{dt}$ | $y[n] = x[n] - x[n-1]$ | First-difference in DT |
| 9 | Integration | $y(t) = \displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{t} x(\tau) \, d\tau$ | $y[n] = y[n-1] + x[n]$ | Accumulator in DT |
| 10 | Energy of Signal | $E = \displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt$ | $E = \displaystyle\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert^2$ | Energy is in **joules (J)** if $x$ is a voltage/current across/through a **1 Ω** resistor |

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - In **LIDAR signal processing**, the *time-of-flight* $t_0$ of a reflected pulse is extracted via matched filtering, which is essentially a time-shifted correlation.
> - In **motor control**, the *PWM duty cycle* is amplitude-scaled and the *gating frequency* determines the time-scaling factor.
> - In **speech recognition for human-robot interaction**, signals are pre-emphasized (high-pass differentiation) to boost high-frequency formants.
> - In **sensor fusion**, signals from IMU, GPS, and wheel encoders are added (and weighted) to produce a more accurate position estimate.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example 1: Combined Time Shifting and Scaling

**Problem (KTU-style):**
A continuous-time signal is defined as:

$$x(t) = \begin{cases} t + 1, & -1 \le t \le 1 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

Sketch and obtain the mathematical expression for $y(t) = x(-2t + 3)$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Decompose the transformation**

The transformation $x(-2t + 3)$ involves three operations in sequence. We must apply them in the **correct order**, working from the innermost substitution outward (or, equivalently, from the original variable to the new variable).

The general rule is: *the operations closest to the variable $t$ are applied first to the original signal*.

Rewrite: $y(t) = x(-2t + 3) = x(-2(t - 1.5))$.

The factor $1.5$ comes from $-2t + 3 = -2(t - 1.5)$.

So we identify the operations as:
- **Operation 1 (innermost):** Time shift — replace $t$ by $t - 1.5$ (shift right by 1.5).
- **Operation 2 (middle):** Time scale — multiply the inside by 2 (compress by factor 2 in time).
- **Operation 3 (outermost):** Time reversal — apply the negative sign (reflect about $t = 0$).

**Step 2: Apply Operation 1 — Time Shift by $+1.5$**

Define $x_1(t) = x(t - 1.5)$.

The original signal $x(t)$ is non-zero on $-1 \le t \le 1$. The shifted signal $x_1(t)$ is non-zero on $-1 \le t - 1.5 \le 1$, which gives $0.5 \le t \le 2.5$.

$$x_1(t) = \begin{cases} (t - 1.5) + 1 = t - 0.5, & 0.5 \le t \le 2.5 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 3: Apply Operation 2 — Time Scale by factor 2 (compress)**

Define $x_2(t) = x_1(2t)$.

The signal $x_1(t)$ is non-zero on $0.5 \le t \le 2.5$. The compressed signal $x_2(t)$ is non-zero on $0.5 \le 2t \le 2.5$, which gives $0.25 \le t \le 1.25$.

$$x_2(t) = \begin{cases} (2t - 0.5), & 0.25 \le t \le 1.25 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 4: Apply Operation 3 — Time Reversal**

Define $y(t) = x_2(-t)$.

The signal $x_2(t)$ is non-zero on $0.25 \le t \le 1.25$. The reversed signal $y(t)$ is non-zero on $0.25 \le -t \le 1.25$, which gives $-1.25 \le t \le -0.25$.

To get the expression, substitute $-t$ for $t$ in $x_2$:

$$y(t) = \begin{cases} 2(-t) - 0.5 = -2t - 0.5, & -1.25 \le t \le -0.25 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 5: Verification by direct substitution**

Original: $x(t)$ is non-zero for $t \in [-1, 1]$ with value $t + 1$.

We want $y(t) = x(-2t + 3)$ to be non-zero. The argument $-2t + 3$ must lie in $[-1, 1]$:

$$-1 \le -2t + 3 \le 1$$

$$-4 \le -2t \le -2$$

$$1 \le t \le 2$$

Wait — this contradicts Step 4! Let me re-examine the algebraic decomposition.

**Re-examination (Step 5 corrected):**

Rewrite $-2t + 3$ as $-2(t - 1.5) = -2t + 3$. ✓ Algebra is correct.

But the non-zero condition for the original is $-1 \le \text{argument} \le 1$. Setting the argument $\tau = -2t + 3$:

$$-1 \le -2t + 3 \le 1$$

Subtract 3:

$$-4 \le -2t \le -2$$

Divide by $-2$ (flip inequalities):

$$2 \ge t \ge 1$$

i.e., $1 \le t \le 2$. The signal $y(t)$ should be non-zero on $[1, 2]$, not $[-1.25, -0.25]$.

**Where did Step 4 go wrong?**

The decomposition order must be reconsidered. The rule is: **the algebraic operation is performed on the variable $t$ directly**. The transformation $x(-2t + 3)$ can be visualized as follows:

1. Start with $x(t)$.
2. Apply $t \to t + 1.5$: shift left by 1.5. (Note: $+1.5$ inside shifts left, not right! Because $x(t + 1.5)$ means the value at $t=0$ is $x(1.5)$, which used to be at $t = 1.5$ in the original. So the graph shifts **left**.)
3. Apply $t \to 2t$: compress by 2.
4. Apply $t \to -t$: reflect.

**Corrected Step 2: Apply Operation 1 — Replace $t$ by $t + 1.5$ (i.e., $x(t + 1.5)$, shift LEFT by 1.5)**

Non-zero when $-1 \le t + 1.5 \le 1$, i.e., $-2.5 \le t \le -0.5$.

$$x_1(t) = (t + 1.5) + 1 = t + 2.5, \quad -2.5 \le t \le -0.5$$

**Corrected Step 3: Apply Operation 2 — Compress by 2: $x_2(t) = x_1(2t)$**

Non-zero when $-2.5 \le 2t \le -0.5$, i.e., $-1.25 \le t \le -0.25$.

$$x_2(t) = (2t) + 2.5 = 2t + 2.5, \quad -1.25 \le t \le -0.25$$

**Corrected Step 4: Apply Operation 3 — Reflect: $y(t) = x_2(-t)$**

Non-zero when $-1.25 \le -t \le -0.25$, i.e., $0.25 \le t \le 1.25$.

$$y(t) = 2(-t) + 2.5 = -2t + 2.5, \quad 0.25 \le t \le 1.25$$

**Step 6: Verification by direct substitution (again)**

$\tau = -2t + 3$. Need $-1 \le \tau \le 1$.

We got $1 \le t \le 2$ in Step 5. But Step 4 (corrected) gives $0.25 \le t \le 1.25$. **Still a contradiction!**

This means **Step 5 (direct substitution) is wrong** because the non-zero condition is correctly $-1 \le \tau \le 1$, giving $1 \le t \le 2$, but the *value* of $y(t)$ should be $\tau + 1 = (-2t + 3) + 1 = -2t + 4$. Let me recheck by direct substitution.

$$y(t) = x(-2t + 3) = (-2t + 3) + 1 = -2t + 4$$

For $1 \le t \le 2$: $y(1) = -2 + 4 = 2$, $y(2) = -4 + 4 = 0$.

The corrected Step 4 gives $y(t) = -2t + 2.5$ for $0.25 \le t \le 1.25$. The values at the endpoints: $y(0.25) = -0.5 + 2.5 = 2$ and $y(1.25) = -2.5 + 2.5 = 0$. So the values match at the endpoints, but the **intervals differ**!

The error is in **Step 5's domain calculation**. Let me redo it carefully.

Non-zero condition: $-1 \le -2t + 3 \le 1$

Subtract 3: $-4 \le -2t \le -2$

Divide by $-2$, **flipping** the inequalities: $2 \ge t \ge 1$

So $1 \le t \le 2$. ✓

But the corrected Step 4 gives domain $[0.25, 1.25]$. So there is **still an error in the corrected step-by-step**.

The fundamental error: the *order* of operations should be applied from **innermost to outermost** in the algebraic expression. In $x(-2t + 3)$, the operations on the variable $t$ are:
- Multiply by $-2$ (innermost).
- Add 3 (outermost).

OR equivalently, factor as $x(-2(t - 1.5))$, which gives:
- Subtract 1.5 from $t$ (i.e., $t - 1.5$): shift right by 1.5.
- Multiply by 2: compress.
- Multiply by $-1$: reflect.

So the correct order of operations is: **shift right by 1.5, then compress by 2, then reflect.**

Let me redo with this order.

**Step 2 (final):** $x_1(t) = x(t - 1.5)$ — shift right by 1.5.

Domain: $-1 \le t - 1.5 \le 1 \Rightarrow 0.5 \le t \le 2.5$.

Value: $x_1(t) = (t - 1.5) + 1 = t - 0.5$, for $0.5 \le t \le 2.5$.

**Step 3 (final):** $x_2(t) = x_1(2t)$ — compress by 2.

Domain: $0.5 \le 2t \le 2.5 \Rightarrow 0.25 \le t \le 1.25$.

Value: $x_2(t) = (2t) - 0.5 = 2t - 0.5$, for $0.25 \le t \le 1.25$.

**Step 4 (final):** $y(t) = x_2(-t)$ — reflect.

Domain: $0.25 \le -t \le 1.25 \Rightarrow -1.25 \le t \le -0.25$.

Value: $y(t) = 2(-t) - 0.5 = -2t - 0.5$, for $-1.25 \le t \le -0.25$.

**Direct substitution (final):** Domain $1 \le t \le 2$, value $-2t + 4$.

**These still don't match.** There must be a **sign convention** discrepancy.

The rule for the order of operations on the variable: When the expression is $x(at + b)$, the operations are applied in this order to the original signal:

1. First, **shift** by $-b/a$ (i.e., $t \to t - (-b/a) = t + b/a$ inside the function).
2. Then, **scale** by $a$.
3. Then, **reverse** if $a < 0$.

Wait — let me reconsider using a known example. Oppenheim's standard convention is:

If $y(t) = x(at + b)$:
- First shift $x$ by $b$ (to get $x(t + b)$, which is a shift to the LEFT by $|b|$).
- Then scale by $a$ (to get $x(at + b)$).
- Then reverse if $a < 0$.

For $y(t) = x(-2t + 3) = x(-2(t - 1.5))$:

**Oppenheim convention:**
1. Shift $x$ by $+3$: $x_1(t) = x(t + 3)$. Domain: $-1 \le t + 3 \le 1 \Rightarrow -4 \le t \le -2$. Value: $(t+3) + 1 = t + 4$.
2. Scale by $-2$: $x_2(t) = x_1(-2t) = x(-2t + 3) = y(t)$. Domain: $-4 \le -2t \le -2 \Rightarrow 1 \le t \le 2$. Value: $-2t + 4$.

**Now the domain matches:** $1 \le t \le 2$, value $-2t + 4$. ✓

So the Oppenheim convention is correct, and the value of the signal at $t = 1$ is $y(1) = 2$, and at $t = 2$ is $y(2) = 0$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip — Operation Order Convention:**
> When given $y(t) = x(at + b)$:
> 1. First, replace $t$ with $t + b$ in $x$: this gives a **shift** of $-b$ (left by $b$ if $b > 0$).
> 2. Then, replace the argument with $at$ (scale by $a$).
> 3. If $a < 0$, the scaling step inherently includes reversal.

**Final Answer for Example 1:**

$$\boxed{y(t) = \begin{cases} -2t + 4, & 1 \le t \le 2 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}}$$

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example 2: Differentiation of a Piecewise Signal

**Problem:**
Given:

$$x(t) = \begin{cases} 0, & t < -1 \\ t + 1, & -1 \le t < 1 \\ 2, & t \ge 1 \end{cases}$$

Find $y(t) = \dfrac{dx(t)}{dt}$.

### Solution

**Step 1: Identify the segments where $x(t)$ is differentiable.**

- For $t < -1$: $x(t) = 0$ (constant), so $\frac{dx}{dt} = 0$.
- For $-1 \le t < 1$: $x(t) = t + 1$ (linear, slope = 1), so $\frac{dx}{dt} = 1$.
- For $t \ge 1$: $x(t) = 2$ (constant), so $\frac{dx}{dt} = 0$.

**Step 2: Account for the discontinuities.**

The signal $x(t)$ has **jump discontinuities** at $t = -1$ and $t = 1$.

- At $t = -1$: $x(-1^-) = 0$, $x(-1^+) = 0$. The signal is **continuous** at $t = -1$ (both sides equal 0). No impulse.
- At $t = 1$: $x(1^-) = 1 + 1 = 2$, $x(1^+) = 2$. The signal is **continuous** at $t = 1$. No impulse.

**Step 3: Write the final answer.**

$$y(t) = \frac{dx(t)}{dt} = \begin{cases} 0, & t < -1 \\ 1, & -1 < t < 1 \\ 0, & t > 1 \end{cases}$$

In compact form, using the rectangular function:

$$y(t) = \text{rect}\left(\frac{t}{2}\right) = \begin{cases} 1, & \vert t \vert < 1 \\ 0, & \vert t \vert > 1 \end{cases}$$

## 3.3 Exhaustive Worked Example 3: Energy of a Shifted Signal

**Problem:**
A signal $x(t)$ has energy $E_x = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt = 10$ J. Find the energy of $y(t) = x(t - 5) + 2 \cdot x(3t)$.

### Solution

**Step 1: Energy of the shifted component.**

Let $y_1(t) = x(t - 5)$.

Substitute $\tau = t - 5$, so $t = \tau + 5$ and $dt = d\tau$. When $t \to -\infty$, $\tau \to -\infty$; when $t \to \infty$, $\tau \to \infty$.

$$E_1 = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert y_1(t) \vert^2 \, dt = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(\tau) \vert^2 \, d\tau = 10 \text{ J}$$

(Time shifting does not change energy — it only translates the time axis.)

**Step 2: Energy of the scaled-and-shifted component.**

Let $y_2(t) = 2 \cdot x(3t)$.

Substitute $\tau = 3t$, so $t = \tau/3$ and $dt = d\tau/3$.

$$E_2 = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert y_2(t) \vert^2 \, dt = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert 2 x(\tau) \vert^2 \frac{d\tau}{3} = \frac{4}{3} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(\tau) \vert^2 \, d\tau = \frac{40}{3} \text{ J}$$

**Step 3: Total energy is NOT the sum, because the two components overlap in time.**

Since $x(t - 5)$ and $2 \cdot x(3t)$ may have overlapping supports, the total energy is:

$$E_y = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t - 5) + 2x(3t) \vert^2 \, dt = E_1 + E_2 + 2 \cdot \text{Cross-term}$$

The cross-term is:

$$C = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t - 5) \cdot 2x(3t) \, dt = 2 \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau) \cdot x(3\tau + 15) \, d\tau$$

This requires knowledge of the **autocorrelation** of $x(t)$ and is generally not computable without the explicit form of $x(t)$. **For KTU exam purposes, unless told the signals are non-overlapping or orthogonal, you must include the cross-term.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Energy Rule:**
> $$\int \vert x_1 + x_2 \vert^2 = \int \vert x_1 \vert^2 + \int \vert x_2 \vert^2 + 2 \int x_1 x_2$$
> Energy is **NOT** simply additive unless $x_1$ and $x_2$ are **orthogonal** (zero cross-correlation).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Operation Properties

| Property | Time Shift | Time Scale | Time Reverse | Amp. Scale | Add | Multiply | Differentiate | Integrate |
|----------|------------|------------|--------------|------------|-----|----------|---------------|-----------|
| Linearity | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Causality preserved | ✓ (delay only) | ✓ | ✗ (in general) | ✓ | Depends | Depends | ✗ (output may lead input) | ✓ |
| Energy invariant? | ✓ | ✗ (changes by $\frac{1}{\vert a \vert}$) | ✓ | ✗ (scales by $A^2$) | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ (not even defined for all $x$) | ✗ |
| Period invariant? | ✓ | ✓ (period scales) | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ (sum of periods) | ✗ (LCM) | ✓ | ✗ (may become aperiodic) |
| Even/Odd preserved? | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ (swaps them) | ✗ (if $A<0$, swaps) | If both even, sum is even; if both odd, sum is odd | Product is even if both same, odd if different | Odd if $x$ is even, even if $x$ is odd | Even if $x$ is odd, odd if $x$ is even |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Signal Operations as Robotic Processing Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Signal x of t]:::inputNode
    B1[Time Shifter t minus t0]:::opNode
    B2[Time Scaler at]:::opNode
    B3[Time Reverser negative t]:::opNode
    B4[Amplitude Scaler A]:::opNode
    B5[Adder plus x2 of t]:::opNode
    B6[Multiplier times x2 of t]:::opNode
    B7[Differentiator d by dt]:::opNode
    B8[Integrator integral dt]:::opNode
    Z[Output Signal y of t]:::outputNode

    A --> B1 --> B2 --> B3 --> B4 --> B5 --> B6 --> B7 --> B8 --> Z

    classDef inputNode fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1976D2,stroke-width:2px,color:#0D47A1
    classDef opNode fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#F57C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#E65100
    classDef outputNode fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#388E3C,stroke-width:2px,color:#1B5E20
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Teleoperation Latency Compensation Using Time Shift

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MasterSide[Master Robot Side]
        M1[Operator Hand Motion m of t]:::signal
        M2[Master Encoder]:::sensor
    end

    subgraph ChannelDelay[Communication Channel]
        C1[Delay t_d seconds]:::delay
    end

    subgraph SlaveSide[Slave Robot Side]
        S1[Time Advance Predictor negative t_d]:::predictor
        S2[Slave Actuator]:::actuator
        S3[End Effector Motion s of t]:::signal
    end

    M1 --> M2 --> C1 --> S1 --> S2 --> S3
    S3 -.Feedback.-> C1

    classDef signal fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0277BD,stroke-width:2px,color:#01579B
    classDef sensor fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#FFA000,stroke-width:2px,color:#E65100
    classDef delay fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828,stroke-width:2px,color:#B71C1C
    classDef predictor fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A,stroke-width:2px,color:#4A148C
    classDef actuator fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#1B5E20
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of the diagram:** The operator's hand motion $m(t)$ is encoded, transmitted through a channel with delay $t_d$, received by the slave, and then a predictor applies a time advance of $-t_d$ (i.e., a shift in the opposite direction) so that the slave's end-effector $s(t)$ moves in *synchronization* with the master, compensating for the round-trip latency.

```mermaid
flowchart TB

    Root --> R1[Independent Variable Manipulations]:::branch

    R1 --> R1a[Time Shifting x of t minus t0]:::leaf
    R1 --> R1b[Time Scaling x of at]:::leaf
    R1 --> R1c[Time Reversal x of negative t]:::leaf

    R2 --> R2a[Amplitude Scaling A times x of t]:::leaf
    R2 --> R2b[DC Offset Addition x of t plus C]:::leaf

    R3 --> R3a[Signal Addition x1 plus x2]:::leaf
```

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> ReceiveTransformation
    ReceiveTransformation: Receive y of t equals x of A t plus B
    ParseExpression: Parse the inner expression A t plus B
    IdentifyOps: Identify the operations
    ApplyShift: Apply Time Shift by B by A
    ApplyScale: Apply Time Scale by A
    CheckReverse: Is A negative
    ApplyReverse: Apply Time Reversal
    OutputResult: Final Output y of t

    ReceiveTransformation --> ParseExpression
    ParseExpression --> IdentifyOps
    IdentifyOps --> ApplyShift

    CheckReverse --> OutputResult: A greater than zero
```
