# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Assessment Pattern (ESE — End Semester Examination):**
> - **Part A:** 3-mark short-answer questions (typically 5 questions to answer out of 7, but module-wise questions are usually 1–2 per module).
> - **Part B:** 14-mark long-answer questions with **internal choice** (a student answers EITHER Question A OR Question B, but not both).
> - Each Part B question has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**.
> - **Cognitive levels** tested: Remember (L1), Understand (L2), Apply (L3), Analyze (L4), Evaluate (L5).

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**Define signal reversal. State what happens to (i) an even signal, and (ii) an odd signal, when reversed.**

**Model Answer:**

> Signal reversal (or **time reversal**) of a continuous-time signal $x(t)$ produces a new signal $y(t) = x(-t)$. Geometrically, this corresponds to reflecting the original signal about the vertical axis ($t = 0$).
>
> **(i) Even Signal:** An even signal satisfies $x(t) = x(-t)$ for all $t$. Therefore, applying reversal yields $y(t) = x(-t) = x(t)$. The signal is **unchanged** by reversal.
>
> **(ii) Odd Signal:** An odd signal satisfies $x(t) = -x(-t)$ for all $t$. Therefore, applying reversal yields $y(t) = x(-t) = -x(t)$. The signal is **negated** (sign-inverted) by reversal, but the magnitude plot is preserved.

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary]**

**A discrete-time signal is given by $x[n] = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ with $n = 0, 1, 2, 3$. Sketch $y[n] = x[2 - n]$.**

**Model Answer:**

> The transformation $x[2 - n]$ can be decomposed as $x[-(n - 2)]$: first shift right by 2, then reverse. We construct the table:
>
> | $n$ | $2 - n$ | $x[2 - n]$ |
> |-----|---------|-------------|
> | $-1$ | $3$ | $4$ |
> | $0$ | $2$ | $3$ |
> | $1$ | $1$ | $2$ |
> | $2$ | $0$ | $1$ |
> | $3$ | $-1$ | $0$ (out of support) |
>
> So $y[n] = \{4, 3, 2, 1\}$ for $n = -1, 0, 1, 2$.
>
> **Sketch (stem plot):**
>
> | n | -1 | 0 | 1 | 2 |
> |---|----|----|----|----|
> | y[n] | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 |
>
> The signal is a reverse-ramp decreasing from 4 to 1 over $n \in [-1, 2]$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

A continuous-time signal is shown in the figure as a triangular pulse:

$$x(t) = \begin{cases} 1 - \vert t \vert, & \vert t \vert \le 1 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**(i)** Sketch $y_1(t) = x(t - 2)$. **(ii)** Sketch $y_2(t) = x(2t)$. **(iii)** Sketch $y_3(t) = x(-t + 1)$. State the effect of each operation in one sentence.

**Model Solution:**

**(i)** $y_1(t) = x(t - 2)$ is a **right shift by 2 units** (a **delay** of 2 seconds).

The triangular pulse, originally centered at $t = 0$ with support $[-1, 1]$, is now centered at $t = 2$ with support $[1, 3]$.

$$y_1(t) = \begin{cases} 1 - \vert t - 2 \vert, & 1 \le t \le 3 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**(ii)** $y_2(t) = x(2t)$ is a **time compression by factor 2**.

The support $[-1, 1]$ becomes $[-0.5, 0.5]$, and the peak amplitude is still 1 (at $t = 0$).

$$y_2(t) = \begin{cases} 1 - \vert 2t \vert = 1 - 2 \vert t \vert, & \vert t \vert \le 0.5 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**(iii)** $y_3(t) = x(-t + 1) = x(-(t - 1))$ is a **right shift by 1 followed by reversal** (or equivalently, a left shift by 1 after reversal).

Using the Oppenheim convention: first apply $t \to t + 1$ to get $x(t + 1)$, then apply $t \to -t$ to get $x(-t + 1)$. Support: $-1 \le -t + 1 \le 1 \Rightarrow 0 \le t \le 2$.

$$y_3(t) = \begin{cases} 1 - \vert 1 - t \vert, & 0 \le t \le 2 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Summary sentence:** (i) Time shift preserves the shape but relocates it along the time axis. (ii) Time scaling preserves the shape but compresses or expands the time axis. (iii) Reversal reflects the signal about $t = 0$.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**(i)** For the signal $x(t) = e^{-2t} u(t)$ (where $u(t)$ is the unit step), compute the **energy** $E_x$. **(ii)** If $y(t) = x(t) + x(-t)$, find the energy $E_y$ of the new signal and verify if the energy is conserved.

**Model Solution:**

**(i)** Energy of $x(t)$:

$$E_x = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-4t} \, dt = \left[ \frac{e^{-4t}}{-4} \right]_{0}^{\infty} = 0 - \left( \frac{-1}{4} \right) = \frac{1}{4} \text{ J}$$

**(ii)** Construction of $y(t) = x(t) + x(-t)$:

- $x(t) = e^{-2t} u(t)$: non-zero only for $t \ge 0$, with value $e^{-2t}$.
- $x(-t) = e^{-2(-t)} u(-t) = e^{2t} u(-t)$: non-zero only for $t \le 0$, with value $e^{2t}$.

Therefore:

$$y(t) = \begin{cases} e^{2t}, & t \le 0 \\ e^{-2t}, & t > 0 \end{cases}$$

This is an **even function** of $t$ (symmetric about $t = 0$).

**Energy of $y(t)$:**

$$E_y = \int_{-\infty}^{0} e^{4t} \, dt + \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-4t} \, dt$$

For the first integral: $\int_{-\infty}^{0} e^{4t} \, dt = \left[ \frac{e^{4t}}{4} \right]_{-\infty}^{0} = \frac{1}{4} - 0 = \frac{1}{4}$.

For the second integral: $\int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-4t} \, dt = \frac{1}{4}$ (as computed in part (i)).

$$E_y = \frac{1}{4} + \frac{1}{4} = \frac{1}{2} \text{ J}$$

**Verification of energy conservation:**

The total energy of $x(t)$ alone was $E_x = \frac{1}{4}$ J. Adding $x(-t)$ introduces an additional $\frac{1}{4}$ J of energy. Since the supports of $x(t)$ and $x(-t)$ do NOT overlap (one is on $t \ge 0$, the other on $t \le 0$), the cross-term integral $\int x(t) x(-t) \, dt$ is zero. Therefore, energy is **additive** in this special case:

$$E_y = E_{x(t)} + E_{x(-t)} + 0 = \frac{1}{4} + \frac{1}{4} = \frac{1}{2} \text{ J} \quad \checkmark$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part B-b):**
> Students often write "energy is conserved because the operation is just a shift" — this is **incorrect**. Energy is conserved for *individual* operations (shift, reverse), but when you **add two distinct signals**, the total energy includes the cross-term. In this problem, the cross-term happens to be zero due to non-overlapping supports, but you MUST explicitly state this reasoning to earn full marks.

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Internal Choice to Question A]**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Differentiate between the following pairs (with mathematical justification):**
**(i)** Continuous-time vs. discrete-time signals.
**(ii)** Even vs. odd signals.
**(iii)** Periodic vs. aperiodic signals.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Continuous-Time vs. Discrete-Time Signals:**

| Property | Continuous-Time (CT) | Discrete-Time (DT) |
|----------|----------------------|--------------------|
| Independent variable | Continuous, $t \in \mathbb{R}$ | Discrete, $n \in \mathbb{Z}$ |
| Notation | $x(t)$ | $x[n]$ |
| Defined for | All real $t$ | Only integer $n$ |
| Energy integral | $E = \int \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt$ | $E = \sum \vert x[n] \vert^2$ |
| Example | $x(t) = \sin(\pi t)$ for $t \in \mathbb{R}$ | $x[n] = \sin(\pi n / 4)$ for $n \in \mathbb{Z}$ |
| Storage in digital systems | Must be sampled (lossy) | Inherently digital |

**(ii) Even vs. Odd Signals:**

A signal is **even** if $x(t) = x(-t)$ for all $t$ (or $x[n] = x[-n]$). Examples: $\cos(\omega t)$, $t^2$.

A signal is **odd** if $x(t) = -x(-t)$ for all $t$. Examples: $\sin(\omega t)$, $t^3$, $t$.

**Mathematical justification:** Any signal $x(t)$ can be uniquely decomposed as:

$$x(t) = x_e(t) + x_o(t)$$

where $x_e(t) = \frac{x(t) + x(-t)}{2}$ is the even part and $x_o(t) = \frac{x(t) - x(-t)}{2}$ is the odd part.

**(iii) Periodic vs. Aperiodic Signals:**

A CT signal is periodic if there exists $T > 0$ such that $x(t + T) = x(t)$ for all $t$. The smallest such $T$ is the **fundamental period**.

A DT signal is periodic if there exists $N > 0$ such that $x[n + N] = x[n]$ for all $n$.

Example: $\cos(\pi t)$ has period $T = 2$ s, while $\cos(\pi n)$ is aperiodic (because $\pi$ is not a rational multiple of $2\pi$).

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

A discrete-time signal is defined as:

$$x[n] = \begin{cases} n, & 0 \le n \le 4 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Compute and sketch:** **(i)** $y_1[n] = 2x[n] - x[n-2]$. **(ii)** $y_2[n] = x[n] \cdot u[n-2]$, where $u$ is the unit step. **(iii)** Find the energy of $y_1[n]$.

**Model Solution:**

**Setup table for $x[n]$:**

| $n$ | $< 0$ | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | $> 4$ |
|-----|-------|---|---|---|---|---|--------|
| $x[n]$ | 0 | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 0 |
| $x[n-2]$ | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 2 | (3 for $n=5$, 4 for $n=6$) |

**(i)** $y_1[n] = 2x[n] - x[n-2]$:

| $n$ | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | $\ge 7$ |
|-----|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|----------|
| $2x[n]$ | 0 | 2 | 4 | 6 | 8 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| $x[n-2]$ | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 0 |
| $y_1[n]$ | 0 | 2 | 4 | 5 | 6 | $-3$ | $-4$ | 0 |

Sketch: A stem plot with values 0, 2, 4, 5, 6, -3, -4 at $n = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6$.

**(ii)** $y_2[n] = x[n] \cdot u[n-2]$:

The unit step $u[n-2] = 1$ for $n \ge 2$ and $0$ otherwise. So this operation **gates** the signal, turning it on only for $n \ge 2$.

$$y_2[n] = \begin{cases} n, & 2 \le n \le 4 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

Sketch: A stem plot with values 2, 3, 4 at $n = 2, 3, 4$.

**(iii)** Energy of $y_1[n]$:

$$E_{y_1} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert y_1[n] \vert^2 = 0^2 + 2^2 + 4^2 + 5^2 + 6^2 + (-3)^2 + (-4)^2$$

$$= 0 + 4 + 16 + 25 + 36 + 9 + 16 = 106 \text{ (dimensionless, in signal-units}^2 \text{)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part B-b):**
> **(1)** A common mistake is forgetting to compute the values of $x[n-2]$ for $n = 5$ and $n = 6$ (where $n-2 = 3$ and $n-2 = 4$). Many students stop the table at $n = 4$ and lose 2 marks.
> **(2)** Another common mistake is computing energy as $2^2 + 4^2 + ...$ without including the negative values — sign does not matter for energy, but students sometimes write only positive terms by mistake.
> **(3)** For the multiplication $y_2[n] = x[n] \cdot u[n-2]$, students often confuse this with a *shift* operation. Multiplication by a unit step is a **gating** (windowing) operation, not a shift.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Operations on Signals" (Module 1, PCRAT402):**

- [ ] **Time Shifting:** $y(t) = x(t - t_0)$ → right shift for $t_0 > 0$ (delay), left shift for $t_0 < 0$ (advance). The shape is preserved; only the time-origin changes.
- [ ] **Time Scaling:** $y(t) = x(at)$ → $\vert a \vert > 1$ compresses, $\vert a \vert < 1$ expands. In discrete time, scaling by a non-integer $k$ causes **decimation** (information loss).
- [ ] **Time Reversal:** $y(t) = x(-t)$ → reflection about the $y$-axis. Even signals are invariant; odd signals are negated.
- [ ] **Amplitude Scaling:** $y(t) = A \cdot x(t)$ → vertical stretch ($A > 1$), compress ($0 < A < 1$), or flip-and-stretch ($A < 0$). Energy scales by $A^2$.
- [ ] **Signal Addition:** Point-wise sum; energy is additive **only if** the cross-correlation integral is zero (signals are orthogonal or non-overlapping in time).
- [ ] **Signal Multiplication:** Point-wise product; used for modulation, gating, and windowing.
- [ ] **Order of Operations (Oppenheim Convention):** For $y(t) = x(at + b)$: (1) shift by $b$, (2) scale by $a$, (3) reverse if $a < 0$. Apply on the **original signal** in this exact sequence.
- [ ] **Differentiation:** Converts position → velocity. **Amplifies** high-frequency noise. In CT, it may produce Dirac impulses at discontinuities.
- [ ] **Integration:** Converts velocity → position. **Smooths** high-frequency noise but can introduce **drift**. Output at time $t$ depends on the **entire past** of $x$.
- [ ] **Energy Invariance:** Time shifting and time reversal **do not** change the energy of a signal. Time scaling by $a$ divides the energy by $\vert a \vert$. Amplitude scaling by $A$ multiplies energy by $A^2$.
- [ ] **Even/Odd Decomposition:** Every signal $x(t)$ can be uniquely written as $x(t) = \frac{x(t)+x(-t)}{2} + \frac{x(t)-x(-t)}{2}$ = (even part) + (odd part).
- [ ] **Discrete-Time Shift Indexing:** $x[n - n_0]$ with $n_0 > 0$ is a **right shift** (delay) in the stem plot.
- [ ] **Robotics Context — Master–Slave Delay:** Modeled as $y(t) = x(t - t_d)$, a pure time shift.
- [ ] **Robotics Context — Gain Control:** Modeled as $y(t) = K \cdot x(t)$, a pure amplitude scale.
- [ ] **Robotics Context — Gating a Command:** Modeled as $y(t) = x(t) \cdot u(t - t_g)$, a multiplication by a unit step that turns the command on at $t = t_g$.
- [ ] **Robotics Context — Encoder Sampling:** Time scaling of a continuous signal by an integer $N$ produces a sampled version at $N \times$ the rate.

> [!WARNING]
> **Final KTU Exam Warning:**
> 1. Always **state the domain (support)** explicitly when sketching transformed signals. Marks are deducted for missing support intervals.
> 2. Always **state the order of operations** when given a combined transformation. Marks are deducted for ambiguous derivations.
> 3. Always include the **Dirac impulse term** in differentiation if the original signal has jump discontinuities.
> 4. Always verify the **sign convention** (delay vs. advance) by checking the values at endpoints.
> 5. For energy calculations involving **sums of signals**, do not forget the cross-term unless orthogonality is established.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Linearity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT402 - SIGNAL PROCESSING FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Linearity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Linearity in Signals & Systems):**
> A system $T\{\cdot\}$ is said to be **linear** if and only if it satisfies the **principle of superposition**, which is the combination of two independent properties: **Additivity** (also called Superposition) and **Homogeneity** (also called Scaling).

Mathematically, a system $y(t) = T\{x(t)\}$ is linear if and only if for all inputs $x_1(t)$ and $x_2(t)$, and for all scalars $\alpha$ and $\beta$:

$$T\{\alpha \cdot x_1(t) + \beta \cdot x_2(t)\} = \alpha \cdot T\{x_1(t)\} + \beta \cdot T\{x_2(t)\}$$

This single equation is the **complete mathematical definition** of linearity in continuous-time signals. The corresponding discrete-time form is:

$$T\{\alpha \cdot x_1[n] + \beta \cdot x_2[n]\} = \alpha \cdot T\{x_1[n]\} + \beta \cdot T\{x_2[n]\}$$

## 1.2 The Two Pillars of Linearity

| Property | Mathematical Form | Plain English Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Additivity** | $T\{x_1(t) + x_2(t)\} = T\{x_1(t)\} + T\{x_2(t)\}$ | Inputting two signals together gives the same output as adding their individual outputs. |
| **Homogeneity (Scaling)** | $T\{\alpha \cdot x(t)\} = \alpha \cdot T\{x(t)\}$ | Amplifying the input by a constant $k$ amplifies the output by the *same* constant $k$. |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Honest Vending Machine":**
> Imagine a vending machine that accepts coins and dispenses a drink.
> - If you put in **two coins one after the other**, you get **two drinks** (Additivity).
> - If you put in a **coin of double value**, you get exactly **twice the drink** (Homogeneity).
>
> A "linear" vending machine never gives you a free bonus, never steals a drink, and never changes its rules based on the day of the week. The output is **always strictly proportional** to the input.
>
> Now imagine a "non-linear" vending machine: you put in ₹20, it gives you one drink. You put in two ₹10 coins (total ₹20), it gives you **two drinks** — this is a *bonus* and it breaks linearity.

In robotics, linearity matters because it allows engineers to:
- **Decompose** complex robot sensor signals (e.g., LiDAR, IMU) into simple parts.
- **Analyze** each part independently and **recombine** the results — a massive simplification.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!TIP]
> - **No physical constants** (like $c$ or $g$) are required for the abstract definition of linearity.
> - The system is defined purely by its **input-output mapping** $T\{\cdot\}$.
> - The scalars $\alpha, \beta \in \mathbb{R}$ (or $\mathbb{C}$ for complex signals) are dimensionless.

## 1.5 Why Linearity is the "Holy Grail" of Signal Processing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the KTU 2024 Scheme for **Signal Processing for Robotics (PCRAT402)**, linearity is the **very first concept** in Module 1 because almost every tool you will use in this course — **Fourier Transform, Laplace Transform, Z-Transform, Convolution, LTI systems** — assumes or *requires* the system to be linear. Without linearity, these tools are **invalid**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Breaking Down the Linearity Test

A system $y(t) = T\{x(t)\}$ is tested for linearity using the **3-Step Master Test**:

**Step 1 — Apply combined input:**
Feed the system with a linear combination: $x_{combined}(t) = \alpha \, x_1(t) + \beta \, x_2(t)$.
Record output: $y_{combined}(t) = T\{\alpha \, x_1(t) + \beta \, x_2(t)\}$.

**Step 2 — Apply individual inputs:**
Compute $y_1(t) = T\{x_1(t)\}$ and $y_2(t) = T\{x_2(t)\}$ independently.

**Step 3 — Form the linear combination of outputs:**
Build the RHS: $y_{RHS}(t) = \alpha \, y_1(t) + \beta \, y_2(t)$.

**Step 4 — Compare:**
If $y_{combined}(t) \equiv y_{RHS}(t)$ for **all** $t$ and for **all** choices of $x_1, x_2, \alpha, \beta$ → the system is **LINEAR**.
If even **one counterexample** is found → the system is **NON-LINEAR**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind the Test:**
> The reason this works is that a linear system is, by definition, a **linear operator** in the mathematical sense. Linear operators preserve vector space structure. The input space is a vector space, the output space is a vector space, and $T$ is a homomorphism between them.

## 2.2 Common Pitfalls in Linearity Testing

| Common Mistake | Why It Is Wrong | Correct Approach |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Testing with only $\alpha = 1, \beta = 1$ | This only tests additivity, not homogeneity. | Test with **arbitrary** $\alpha$ and $\beta$. |
| Assuming "linear" means "straight line graph" | Many non-linear systems also have straight line portions. | Apply the **superposition test** formally. |
| Confusing linearity with **time-invariance** | A system can be linear but time-varying. | They are **independent** properties. |
| Forgetting the constant term | A system with a DC offset (e.g., $y = 2x + 5$) is non-linear. | The output for **zero input** must be **zero** (zero-in/zero-out). |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Zero-In / Zero-Out Property (Corollary of Linearity):**
> For a linear system: if $x(t) = 0$, then $y(t) = 0$.
> Proof: Set $\alpha = 0$ in the homogeneity test:
> $$T\{0 \cdot x(t)\} = 0 \cdot T\{x(t)\} = 0$$
> This is a **quick sanity check** for linearity. If the system produces a non-zero output for zero input, it is **immediately non-linear**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Property | Mathematical Expression | Condition for Linearity |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Additivity** | $T\{x_1 + x_2\} = T\{x_1\} + T\{x_2\}$ | Must hold for all valid $x_1, x_2$ |
| 2 | **Homogeneity** | $T\{\alpha x\} = \alpha T\{x\}$ | Must hold for all scalars $\alpha$ |
| 3 | **Combined (Superposition)** | $T\{\alpha x_1 + \beta x_2\} = \alpha T\{x_1\} + \beta T\{x_2\}$ | The master test equation |
| 4 | **Zero-In/Zero-Out** | $T\{0\} = 0$ | Necessary (but not sufficient) |
| 5 | **Continuous-time form** | $T\{\alpha x_1(t) + \beta x_2(t)\} = \alpha y_1(t) + \beta y_2(t)$ | Robot sensors, analog signals |
| 6 | **Discrete-time form** | $T\{\alpha x_1[n] + \beta x_2[n]\} = \alpha y_1[n] + \beta y_2[n]$ | Sampled robot data, DSP |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In robotics and signal processing, linearity is essential for:

1. **LiDAR Signal Processing** — Linear time-invariant (LTI) operations allow simple filtering (low-pass, high-pass) to clean sensor noise.
2. **Motor Control** — A linear motor model $V = K \cdot \omega$ allows the use of classical control theory (PID, lead-lag compensators).
3. **Audio/Speech Processing** — Microphone pre-amplifiers must be linear to avoid harmonic distortion.
4. **Image Processing in Computer Vision** — Linear filters (Sobel, Gaussian) are used for edge detection and smoothing.
5. **Kalman Filtering** — The Kalman filter is mathematically valid **only** for linear system models; the Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) is used as a workaround for non-linear systems.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Worked Example 1 — A Linear System

**Problem:** Test the system $y(t) = 3 \cdot x(t)$ for linearity.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Apply the combined input.
Let $x_{combined}(t) = \alpha \, x_1(t) + \beta \, x_2(t)$.

Then:
$$y_{combined}(t) = 3 \cdot x_{combined}(t)$$

Substituting the combined input:
$$y_{combined}(t) = 3 \cdot \big(\alpha \, x_1(t) + \beta \, x_2(t)\big)$$

**Step 2:** Distribute the constant $3$ over the sum.
$$y_{combined}(t) = 3\alpha \, x_1(t) + 3\beta \, x_2(t)$$

**Step 3:** Now compute the individual outputs and form the RHS.
$$y_1(t) = 3 \cdot x_1(t)$$
$$y_2(t) = 3 \cdot x_2(t)$$

RHS:
$$y_{RHS}(t) = \alpha \cdot y_1(t) + \beta \cdot y_2(t)$$

Substituting:
$$y_{RHS}(t) = \alpha \cdot 3 x_1(t) + \beta \cdot 3 x_2(t)$$

**Step 4:** Rearrange to compare with the LHS.
$$y_{RHS}(t) = 3\alpha \, x_1(t) + 3\beta \, x_2(t)$$

**Step 5:** Compare $y_{combined}(t)$ and $y_{RHS}(t)$:
$$y_{combined}(t) = 3\alpha \, x_1(t) + 3\beta \, x_2(t)$$
$$y_{RHS}(t) = 3\alpha \, x_1(t) + 3\beta \, x_2(t)$$

Since $y_{combined}(t) \equiv y_{RHS}(t)$ for **all** $\alpha, \beta, x_1(t), x_2(t)$, the system $y(t) = 3x(t)$ is **LINEAR**. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Detailed Worked Example 2 — A Non-Linear System

**Problem:** Test the system $y(t) = x^2(t)$ for linearity.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Apply the combined input.
$$y_{combined}(t) = \big(\alpha x_1(t) + \beta x_2(t)\big)^2$$

**Step 2:** Expand the square.
$$y_{combined}(t) = \alpha^2 x_1^2(t) + 2\alpha\beta \, x_1(t)x_2(t) + \beta^2 x_2^2(t)$$

**Step 3:** Compute individual outputs.
$$y_1(t) = x_1^2(t)$$
$$y_2(t) = x_2^2(t)$$

RHS:
$$y_{RHS}(t) = \alpha x_1^2(t) + \beta x_2^2(t)$$

**Step 4:** Compare LHS and RHS.
$$y_{combined}(t) = \alpha^2 x_1^2(t) + 2\alpha\beta \, x_1(t)x_2(t) + \beta^2 x_2^2(t)$$
$$y_{RHS}(t) = \alpha x_1^2(t) + \beta x_2^2(t)$$

The two expressions are **not equal** in general. The extra cross-term $2\alpha\beta \, x_1(t)x_2(t)$ and the squared coefficients $\alpha^2, \beta^2$ are the proof of **non-linearity**. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Detailed Worked Example 3 — The DC Offset Trap

**Problem:** Test the system $y(t) = 2x(t) + 5$ for linearity.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Apply the combined input.
$$y_{combined}(t) = 2\big(\alpha x_1(t) + \beta x_2(t)\big) + 5$$
$$y_{combined}(t) = 2\alpha x_1(t) + 2\beta x_2(t) + 5$$

**Step 2:** Compute individual outputs.
$$y_1(t) = 2 x_1(t) + 5$$
$$y_2(t) = 2 x_2(t) + 5$$

RHS:
$$y_{RHS}(t) = \alpha \big(2 x_1(t) + 5\big) + \beta \big(2 x_2(t) + 5\big)$$
$$y_{RHS}(t) = 2\alpha x_1(t) + 5\alpha + 2\beta x_2(t) + 5\beta$$
$$y_{RHS}(t) = 2\alpha x_1(t) + 2\beta x_2(t) + 5(\alpha + \beta)$$

**Step 3:** Compare.
LHS: $2\alpha x_1(t) + 2\beta x_2(t) + 5$
RHS: $2\alpha x_1(t) + 2\beta x_2(t) + 5(\alpha + \beta)$

These are equal **only if** $5 = 5(\alpha + \beta)$, i.e., $\alpha + \beta = 1$, which is not generally true. Hence the system is **NON-LINEAR**.

**Sanity check with zero-in/zero-out:** $y(0) = 2(0) + 5 = 5 \neq 0$. The system fails the zero-in/zero-out property. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.4 Detailed Worked Example 4 — A Robot Actuator System

**Problem:** A DC motor in a robot arm is modeled by $y(t) = K_v \cdot \frac{dx(t)}{dt}$ where $K_v$ is a constant (back-EMF constant). Test for linearity.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Apply the combined input.
$$y_{combined}(t) = K_v \cdot \frac{d}{dt}\big(\alpha x_1(t) + \beta x_2(t)\big)$$

**Step 2:** Use linearity of the derivative operator.
$$y_{combined}(t) = K_v \left(\alpha \frac{dx_1(t)}{dt} + \beta \frac{dx_2(t)}{dt}\right)$$

**Step 3:** Distribute $K_v$.
$$y_{combined}(t) = \alpha K_v \frac{dx_1(t)}{dt} + \beta K_v \frac{dx_2(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 4:** Identify the individual outputs.
$$y_1(t) = K_v \frac{dx_1(t)}{dt}, \quad y_2(t) = K_v \frac{dx_2(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 5:** Form the RHS.
$$y_{RHS}(t) = \alpha y_1(t) + \beta y_2(t) = \alpha K_v \frac{dx_1(t)}{dt} + \beta K_v \frac{dx_2(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 6:** Compare with Step 3.
LHS $\equiv$ RHS ✓. The system is **LINEAR**. $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> **Robotics Insight:** This is why the **differential drive model** of a robot works so well — differentiation is a linear operation, so the entire kinematic model remains linear and tractable using linear algebra.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Quick Classification of Common Systems

| System Equation | Linear? | Reason |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| $y(t) = K \cdot x(t)$ | ✅ YES | Pure scaling — both properties hold trivially. |
| $y(t) = \frac{dx(t)}{dt}$ | ✅ YES | Differentiation is a linear operator. |
| $y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} x(\tau) \, d\tau$ | ✅ YES | Integration is a linear operator. |
| $y(t) = a \cdot x(t) + b$ | ❌ NO | The constant $b$ violates zero-in/zero-out. |
| $y(t) = x^2(t)$ | ❌ NO | Squaring creates cross-terms. |
| $y(t) = \cos(x(t))$ | ❌ NO | Non-linear trigonometric function. |
| $y(t) = \vert x(t) \vert$ | ❌ NO | Absolute value is non-linear (kink at origin). |
| $y(t) = x(t) \cdot x(t-1)$ | ❌ NO | Product of signals is non-linear. |
| $y[n] = x[n] + 2x[n-1]$ | ✅ YES | Linear combination of shifted inputs. |
| $y[n] = x[n]^2$ | ❌ NO | Squaring in discrete-time is also non-linear. |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Linearity Test Procedure — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: System Definition y = T x] --> B[Choose two arbitrary inputs x1 t and x2 t]
    B --> C[Choose arbitrary scalars alpha and beta]
    C --> D[Step 1: Compute LHS y combined = T of alpha x1 + beta x2]
    D --> E[Step 2: Compute y1 = T of x1 and y2 = T of x2]
    E --> F[Step 3: Compute RHS = alpha y1 + beta y2]
    F --> G{Is LHS equal to RHS for all choices?}
    G -->|Yes| H[System is LINEAR]
    G -->|No| I[System is NON LINEAR]
    H --> J[Apply Fourier, Laplace, Z Transform tools]
    I --> K[Use EKF, Lyapunov, or Volterra methods]
```

## 4.2 Additivity vs Homogeneity — Side-by-Side Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph AD["ADDITIVITY TEST"]
        A1[x1 t] --> A2[System T]
        A3[x2 t] --> A2
        A2 --> A4[LHS: T of x1 plus x2]
        A1 --> A5[T]
        A3 --> A6[T]
        A5 --> A7[y1]
        A6 --> A8[y2]
        A7 --> A9[Sum: y1 plus y2]
        A8 --> A9
        A4 --> A10{LHS equals RHS?}
        A9 --> A10
    end

    subgraph HM["HOMOGENEITY TEST"]
        H1[x t] --> H2[Multiply by alpha]
        H2 --> H3[System T]
        H3 --> H4[LHS: T of alpha x]
        H1 --> H5[System T]
        H5 --> H6[y]
        H6 --> H7[Multiply by alpha]
        H7 --> H8[RHS: alpha y]
        H4 --> H9{LHS equals RHS?}
        H8 --> H9
    end
```

## 4.3 Linearity in the Robotics Signal Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Robot Sensor: LiDAR] --> S2[Amplifier: y = Kx LINEAR]
    S2 --> S3[ADC Sampler: y = x nT LINEAR]
    S3 --> S4[Filter: FIR y = sum h k x n-k LINEAR]
    S4 --> S5[Decision: Threshold y equals 1 if x greater than T]
    S5 --> S6{Linear Block?}
    S6 -->|Yes| S7[Use Fourier Z Transform LTI Theory]
    S6 -->|No| S8[Requires Non Linear Analysis]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** The above block diagrams illustrate the **functional architecture** of the linearity test and a real-world robotics signal chain. Almost every block in a typical robot perception pipeline is **linear by design**, which is why linearity is the most important prerequisite concept in this course.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
**Q:** Define linearity for a continuous-time system. State the two properties a system must satisfy to be classified as linear.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A continuous-time system $y(t) = T\{x(t)\}$ is said to be **linear** if it satisfies the principle of superposition for all inputs $x_1(t), x_2(t)$ and all scalars $\alpha, \beta$:
$$T\{\alpha x_1(t) + \beta x_2(t)\} = \alpha T\{x_1(t)\} + \beta T\{x_2(t)\}$$
The two constituent properties are:
1. **Additivity:** $T\{x_1(t) + x_2(t)\} = T\{x_1(t)\} + T\{x_2(t)\}$
2. **Homogeneity (Scaling):** $T\{\alpha x(t)\} = \alpha T\{x(t)\}$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write "linear means the graph is a straight line" — this is **wrong** and will fetch **0 marks**. Always quote the superposition equation.

---

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**Q:** Without performing the full test, explain how the **zero-in/zero-out property** can be used as a quick check for linearity.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
For any linear system, if the input is identically zero, the output must also be identically zero. This follows from the homogeneity property with $\alpha = 0$:
$$T\{0 \cdot x(t)\} = 0 \cdot T\{x(t)\} = 0$$
Hence, if we feed a system $x(t) = 0$ and obtain a non-zero output, the system is **definitely non-linear**. However, the converse is not true — a system can pass the zero-in/zero-out test and still be non-linear, so this is only a **necessary** condition, not sufficient.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often incorrectly claim this is a sufficient condition. Remember: it is **necessary but NOT sufficient** for linearity. (1 mark lost if this distinction is missed.)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

**Q:** Test the following systems for linearity. Justify your answer mathematically in each case.
**(a)** $y(t) = 5 \cos(x(t))$ — Test for linearity. (7 marks)
**(b)** $y(t) = 2x(t) + 3\frac{dx(t)}{dt}$ — Test for linearity. (7 marks)

---

**Solution to (a):**
**Step 1:** Let $x_{combined}(t) = \alpha x_1(t) + \beta x_2(t)$.
$$y_{combined}(t) = 5\cos(\alpha x_1(t) + \beta x_2(t))$$

**Step 2:** Individual outputs:
$$y_1(t) = 5\cos(x_1(t)), \quad y_2(t) = 5\cos(x_2(t))$$

**Step 3:** RHS:
$$y_{RHS}(t) = 5\alpha\cos(x_1(t)) + 5\beta\cos(x_2(t))$$

**Step 4:** Compare. The cosine of a sum is **not equal** to the sum of cosines (the well-known $\cos(A+B) \neq \cos A + \cos B$ identity). Also, the cosine of a scaled argument $\cos(\alpha x)$ is not equal to $\alpha \cos(x)$ in general.

**Counterexample:** Let $x_1(t) = t$, $x_2(t) = 0$, $\alpha = 1, \beta = 0$. LHS = $5\cos(t)$. RHS = $5\cos(t) + 0$ — this matches, but it is just a single case.

Let $x_1(t) = 0, x_2(t) = \pi/2, \alpha = 0, \beta = 1$. LHS = $5\cos(\pi/2) = 0$. RHS = $0 + 5\cos(\pi/2) = 0$. Still matches.

A decisive counterexample: $x_1(t) = \pi/2, x_2(t) = \pi/2, \alpha = 1, \beta = 1$. LHS = $5\cos(\pi) = -5$. RHS = $5\cos(\pi/2) + 5\cos(\pi/2) = 0 + 0 = 0$. Since $-5 \neq 0$, the system is **NON-LINEAR**. **(7 marks)** $\blacksquare$

---

**Solution to (b):**
**Step 1:** Let $x_{combined}(t) = \alpha x_1(t) + \beta x_2(t)$.
$$y_{combined}(t) = 2(\alpha x_1(t) + \beta x_2(t)) + 3 \frac{d}{dt}(\alpha x_1(t) + \beta x_2(t))$$

**Step 2:** Use linearity of differentiation.
$$y_{combined}(t) = 2\alpha x_1(t) + 2\beta x_2(t) + 3\alpha \frac{dx_1(t)}{dt} + 3\beta \frac{dx_2(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 3:** Group terms by $x_1$ and $x_2$.
$$y_{combined}(t) = \alpha \left(2x_1(t) + 3\frac{dx_1(t)}{dt}\right) + \beta \left(2x_2(t) + 3\frac{dx_2(t)}{dt}\right)$$

**Step 4:** Identify individual outputs.
$$y_1(t) = 2x_1(t) + 3\frac{dx_1(t)}{dt}, \quad y_2(t) = 2x_2(t) + 3\frac{dx_2(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 5:** Form RHS.
$$y_{RHS}(t) = \alpha y_1(t) + \beta y_2(t) = \alpha \left(2x_1(t) + 3\frac{dx_1(t)}{dt}\right) + \beta \left(2x_2(t) + 3\frac{dx_2(t)}{dt}\right)$$

**Step 6:** Compare with Step 3. They are **identical**. Hence the system is **LINEAR**. **(7 marks)** $\blacksquare$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to invoke the **linearity of the derivative operator** explicitly. Always state that $\frac{d}{dt}(\alpha x_1 + \beta x_2) = \alpha \frac{dx_1}{dt} + \beta \frac{dx_2}{dt}$ because differentiation itself is a linear operation. **2 marks** lost if this justification is missing.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**Q:** Answer the following with detailed mathematical proof.
**(a)** Prove that the system $y(t) = x^2(t) + x(t-1)$ is non-linear. (7 marks)
**(b)** Show that the discrete-time system $y[n] = 3x[n] - 2x[n-2]$ is linear. (7 marks)

---

**Solution to (a):**
**Step 1:** Apply the combined input.
$$y_{combined}(t) = (\alpha x_1(t) + \beta x_2(t))^2 + (\alpha x_1(t-1) + \beta x_2(t-1))$$

**Step 2:** Expand the square.
$$y_{combined}(t) = \alpha^2 x_1^2(t) + 2\alpha\beta x_1(t)x_2(t) + \beta^2 x_2^2(t) + \alpha x_1(t-1) + \beta x_2(t-1)$$

**Step 3:** Compute individual outputs.
$$y_1(t) = x_1^2(t) + x_1(t-1), \quad y_2(t) = x_2^2(t) + x_2(t-1)$$

**Step 4:** Form the RHS.
$$y_{RHS}(t) = \alpha(x_1^2(t) + x_1(t-1)) + \beta(x_2^2(t) + x_2(t-1))$$
$$y_{RHS}(t) = \alpha x_1^2(t) + \alpha x_1(t-1) + \beta x_2^2(t) + \beta x_2(t-1)$$

**Step 5:** Compare. The LHS contains the extra cross-term $2\alpha\beta x_1(t)x_2(t)$ and squared coefficients $\alpha^2, \beta^2$ that the RHS does not have. Hence the system is **NON-LINEAR**. **(7 marks)** $\blacksquare$

---

**Solution to (b):**
**Step 1:** Apply the combined input.
$$y_{combined}[n] = 3(\alpha x_1[n] + \beta x_2[n]) - 2(\alpha x_1[n-2] + \beta x_2[n-2])$$

**Step 2:** Distribute.
$$y_{combined}[n] = 3\alpha x_1[n] + 3\beta x_2[n] - 2\alpha x_1[n-2] - 2\beta x_2[n-2]$$

**Step 3:** Group by $\alpha$ and $\beta$.
$$y_{combined}[n] = \alpha(3x_1[n] - 2x_1[n-2]) + \beta(3x_2[n] - 2x_2[n-2])$$

**Step 4:** Identify individual outputs.
$$y_1[n] = 3x_1[n] - 2x_1[n-2], \quad y_2[n] = 3x_2[n] - 2x_2[n-2]$$

**Step 5:** Form RHS.
$$y_{RHS}[n] = \alpha y_1[n] + \beta y_2[n] = \alpha(3x_1[n] - 2x_1[n-2]) + \beta(3x_2[n] - 2x_2[n-2])$$

**Step 6:** Compare with Step 3. They are **identical**. Hence the system is **LINEAR**. **(7 marks)** $\blacksquare$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** In discrete-time systems, students often confuse $x[n-2]$ (a shift) with non-linearity. A pure shift in time **preserves** linearity — only operations like squaring, products, or thresholding break it. **1 mark** lost if the shift operation is incorrectly flagged as non-linear.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Linearity**

- [x] **Linearity = Additivity + Homogeneity** (the superposition principle).
- [x] Master equation: $T\{\alpha x_1 + \beta x_2\} = \alpha T\{x_1\} + \beta T\{x_2\}$ — must hold for **all** inputs and **all** scalars.
- [x] **Zero-in/zero-out** ($T\{0\} = 0$) is a **necessary but not sufficient** quick check.
- [x] **Differentiation and integration are linear operations** — systems built from them are linear.
- [x] **Squaring, products of signals, absolute value, trigonometric functions, thresholds** → all non-linear.
- [x] A system $y = ax + b$ is **non-linear** if $b \neq 0$ (DC offset trap).
- [x] **Linearity ≠ Time-Invariance** — they are independent properties; LTI requires both.
- [x] In KTU 2024 Scheme PCRAT402, linearity is the **gateway** to Fourier, Laplace, and Z-transforms.
- [x] Robot sensors and actuators (LiDAR, encoders, DC motors) are usually modeled as **linear** systems.
- [x] **Always test with general $\alpha, \beta$** — testing only with $\alpha = \beta = 1$ only checks additivity, not homogeneity.
- [x] **Counterexample method** is a valid proof of non-linearity: one failure of superposition is enough.
- [x] Discrete-time shift $x[n-k]$ is a **linear** operation.
- [x] Product of two signals $x_1(t) \cdot x_2(t)$ is a **non-linear** operation (creates cross-terms).

---
**End of Notes — Linearity | Module 1 | PCRAT402**

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 1

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🎯 Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Stability of LTI Systems

### Formal Definition (BIBO Stability)
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **BIBO Stability (Bounded-Input Bounded-Output):** A continuous-time LTI system is said to be **BIBO stable** if and only if for every *bounded* input signal $x(t)$ (i.e., $\vert x(t) \vert \leq M_x < \infty$ for all $t$), the corresponding output $y(t)$ is also bounded (i.e., $\vert y(t) \vert \leq M_y < \infty$ for all $t$).

In the discrete-time domain, the same definition applies: every bounded input sequence $x[n]$ must produce a bounded output sequence $y[n]$.

### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy — "The Bouncy Castle"
Imagine a spring-loaded platform in a bouncy castle:
- **Stable System** → A child jumps on it, the platform settles back to its rest position without ever flying off into the sky or sinking through the floor. *Energy is dissipated, motion remains bounded.*
- **Unstable System** → The same jump causes the platform to oscillate with ever-increasing amplitude until it physically breaks. *Energy grows without bound — the output is unbounded even for a tiny input.*
- **Marginally Stable** → The platform oscillates forever at constant amplitude, never dying out and never exploding. *Bounded, but on the edge.*

This is exactly how a BIBO stable system behaves in response to a bounded input: the response never runs away to infinity.

### Physical Significance in Robotics
- In a **robotic arm controller**, an unstable transfer function can cause the motor to spin out of control, potentially destroying the hardware.
- In **signal processing for sensors** (LIDAR, IMU), unstable filters introduce runaway DC drift, corrupting all downstream data fusion.

---

## 1.2 Causality of LTI Systems

### Formal Definition
> [!NOTE]
> **Causal System:** An LTI system is called *causal* (or *non-anticipatory*) if the output $y(t)$ at any instant $t = t_0$ depends **only** on the values of the input $x(t)$ for $t \leq t_0$ (i.e., present and past inputs). It does not depend on any future input values $x(t)$ for $t > t_0$.
>
> Mathematically: $y(t) = \mathcal{T}\{x(\tau), \, \tau \leq t\}$

For discrete-time: $y[n]$ depends only on $x[k]$ for $k \leq n$.

### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy — "The Weather Forecast Machine"
- **Causal System** → A home thermostat. It reads the *current* room temperature to decide whether to turn the heater ON/OFF. It cannot peek at tomorrow's temperature to make today's decision.
- **Non-Causal System** → A weather prediction model that uses *future* satellite data to estimate today's storm trajectory (offline processing where delay is acceptable). It "cheats" by looking ahead.
- **Anti-Causal System** → A post-mortem analyzer that only uses future timestamps relative to a chosen reference — useful in some biomedical signal reconstructions but impossible to implement in real-time hardware.

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Point:** *All real-time physical systems* (robot controllers, autopilots, audio amplifiers) **must be causal** because the future has not yet occurred. Non-causal systems are permissible only in offline (batch) processing.

---

## 1.3 Why These Two Properties Matter Together

In **Signal Processing for Robotics**, every filter, controller, or estimator you design must be checked for both:
1. **Stability** — will the system blow up?
2. **Causality** — can it run in real-time on a physical robot?

A non-causal but stable filter is fine for offline data analysis (e.g., post-processing recorded LIDAR scans), while an unstable causal filter is useless regardless of how elegant its design is.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📚 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Stability Criteria — The Three Conditions

### A. Continuous-Time (CT) LTI System — Impulse Response Condition
An LTI system with impulse response $h(t)$ is BIBO stable **if and only if** the impulse response is **absolutely integrable**:

$$\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert h(t) \vert \, dt < \infty$$

If the integral diverges (equals $\infty$), the system is **not BIBO stable**.

### B. Continuous-Time (CT) LTI System — Transfer Function / Pole Condition
For a rational transfer function $H(s) = \frac{N(s)}{D(s)}$:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **BIBO Stability (CT):** A causal LTI system is BIBO stable **if and only if all poles of $H(s)$ lie strictly in the left-half of the s-plane**, i.e., the real part of every pole satisfies $\text{Re}(s_p) < 0$.

**Pole classification:**
- All poles in **LHP** (strict $\text{Re}(s_p) < 0$) → **Asymptotically Stable**
- Simple poles on **$j\omega$ axis** (no LHP/RHP poles) → **Marginally Stable** (BIBO unstable in strict sense, output is bounded but not absolutely integrable)
- **Any** pole in RHP ($\text{Re}(s_p) > 0$) or repeated poles on $j\omega$ axis → **Unstable**

### C. Discrete-Time (DT) LTI System — Pole Condition
For a rational $H(z) = \frac{N(z)}{D(z)}$:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **BIBO Stability (DT):** A causal LTI system is BIBO stable **if and only if all poles of $H(z)$ lie strictly inside the unit circle** in the z-plane, i.e., $\vert z_p \vert < 1$ for every pole.

- All poles $\vert z_p \vert < 1$ → **Asymptotically Stable**
- Simple poles on unit circle, none outside → **Marginally Stable**
- Any pole $\vert z_p \vert > 1$ or repeated poles on unit circle → **Unstable**

---

## 2.2 Causality Criteria — The Time-Domain Test

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Causality Condition (CT):** An LTI system is causal **if and only if** $h(t) = 0$ for all $t < 0$. The impulse response must be *right-sided* (non-zero only for $t \geq 0$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Causality Condition (DT):** $h[n] = 0$ for all $n < 0$. The impulse response is a *right-sided* sequence.

**Classification by impulse response support:**
- $h(t) \neq 0$ only for $t \geq 0$ → **Causal**
- $h(t) \neq 0$ only for $t \leq 0$ → **Anti-Causal**
- $h(t)$ non-zero on both sides → **Non-Causal**

---

## 2.3 Region of Convergence (ROC) — The Bridge Between Stability & Causality

For the Laplace transform $H(s) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} h(t) e^{-st} dt$ and the Z-transform $H(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} h[n] z^{-n}$:

| Property | ROC Condition (CT) | ROC Condition (DT) |
|---|---|---|
| **Stability** | ROC must **include the $j\omega$ axis** ($\text{Re}(s) = 0$) | ROC must **include the unit circle** ($\vert z \vert = 1$) |
| **Causality** (right-sided signal) | ROC is a right-half plane: $\text{Re}(s) > \text{Re}(s_{p,\max})$ | ROC is exterior of circle: $\vert z \vert > \vert z_{p,\max} \vert$ |
| **Anti-Causality** (left-sided) | ROC is a left-half plane: $\text{Re}(s) < \text{Re}(s_{p,\min})$ | ROC is interior of circle: $\vert z \vert < \vert z_{p,\min} \vert$ |
| **Two-sided signal** | ROC is a vertical strip (no poles) | ROC is an annular ring (no poles) |

### The Key Insight
> [!NOTE]
> **For a causal + stable system (CT):** All poles in LHP **AND** ROC is $\text{Re}(s) > \text{Re}(s_{p,\max})$. The ROC extending to $+\infty$ in $\text{Re}(s)$ automatically **includes the $j\omega$ axis** because the rightmost pole is already in the LHP. Stability and causality are *simultaneously satisfied*.
>
> **Counter-example:** If ROC is $\text{Re}(s) < -2$ (left-half plane), the system is **stable** (ROC includes $j\omega$ axis) but **anti-causal** (left-sided signal).

---

## 2.4 📋 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Condition | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | BIBO Stability (CT) | $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h(t) \vert \, dt < \infty$ | Direct check from impulse response |
| 2 | BIBO Stability (DT) | $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h[n] \vert < \infty$ | Direct check from impulse sequence |
| 3 | Pole Stability (CT) | All poles satisfy $\text{Re}(s_p) < 0$ | Quick check from transfer function |
| 4 | Pole Stability (DT) | All poles satisfy $\vert z_p \vert < 1$ | Quick check from transfer function |
| 5 | Causality (CT) | $h(t) = 0$ for $t < 0$ | Right-sided impulse response |
| 6 | Causality (DT) | $h[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$ | Right-sided impulse sequence |
| 7 | ROC for Causal Stability (CT) | $\text{Re}(s) > \max\{\text{Re}(s_p)\}$ and $j\omega$ axis inside ROC | Uniquely determines a causal stable system |
| 8 | ROC for Causal Stability (DT) | $\vert z \vert > \max\{\vert z_p \vert\}$ and unit circle inside ROC | Uniquely determines a causal stable DT system |
| 9 | Output Bound | $\vert y(t) \vert \leq \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h(\tau) \vert \, \vert x(t-\tau) \vert \, d\tau \leq M_x \int \vert h(\tau) \vert d\tau$ | Derives BIBO from convolution |
| 10 | Stability + Causality of $H(s) = \frac{1}{s-a}$ | Pole $s = a$; stable iff $a < 0$ | First-order system template |

---

## 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Robot Joint Controllers (PID):** The closed-loop transfer function must have all closed-loop poles in the LHP; otherwise the joint accelerates unboundedly.
- **Digital Filters in DSP for Sensors:** An IIR filter with a pole outside the unit circle will overflow the microcontroller's accumulator and produce NaN/Inf.
- **Kalman Filter (State Estimation):** The prediction error covariance matrix must remain bounded — equivalent to requiring stability of the state transition matrix's eigenvalues.
- **Adaptive Filters in Robotics (LMS, RLS):** The step-size parameter is bounded precisely by the stability conditions of the underlying difference equation.

# 🧮 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of BIBO Stability from the Convolution Integral

We want to prove rigorously that a CT-LTI system is BIBO stable if and only if $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h(t) \vert \, dt < \infty$.

### Proof (Sufficient Condition): If the integral is finite, the output is bounded.

**Step 1** — Write the output via convolution:
The output of an LTI system is the convolution of the input with the impulse response:

$$y(t) = (h * x)(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} h(\tau) \, x(t - \tau) \, d\tau$$

**Step 2** — Take the absolute value and apply the triangle inequality:

$$\vert y(t) \vert = \left\vert \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} h(\tau) \, x(t - \tau) \, d\tau \right\vert \leq \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert h(\tau) \vert \, \vert x(t - \tau) \vert \, d\tau$$

*Reasoning:* The triangle inequality for integrals states that the absolute value of an integral is at most the integral of the absolute value.

**Step 3** — Use the bounded-input assumption. Let $\vert x(t) \vert \leq M_x < \infty$ for all $t$. Then $\vert x(t-\tau) \vert \leq M_x$ for all $\tau$. Substitute:

$$\vert y(t) \vert \leq \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert h(\tau) \vert \, M_x \, d\tau = M_x \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert h(\tau) \vert \, d\tau$$

**Step 4** — Define the finite integral:

Let $M_h = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert h(\tau) \vert \, d\tau$, assumed finite ($M_h < \infty$). Then:

$$\vert y(t) \vert \leq M_x \cdot M_h < \infty \quad \text{for all } t$$

*Conclusion:* The output is uniformly bounded by the product $M_x M_h$. Hence the system is BIBO stable.

### Proof (Necessary Condition): If the system is BIBO stable, the integral is finite.

**Step 5** — Construct a worst-case bounded input. Suppose $M_h = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h(\tau) \vert \, d\tau = \infty$. Choose the bounded input:

$$x(t) = \text{sgn}\{h(-t)\} = \begin{cases} +1 & \text{if } h(-t) > 0 \\ -1 & \text{if } h(-t) < 0 \\ 0 & \text{if } h(-t) = 0 \end{cases}$$

This input satisfies $\vert x(t) \vert \leq 1$ (bounded), and the resulting output is:

$$y(0) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} h(\tau) \, x(-\tau) \, d\tau = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert h(\tau) \vert \, d\tau = \infty$$

*Conclusion:* A bounded input produces an unbounded output, violating BIBO stability. Hence $M_h < \infty$ is necessary. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 1: First-Order CT System

**Problem:** Determine the stability and causality of the system $H(s) = \frac{1}{s+3}$.

**Step 1** — Find the impulse response via inverse Laplace transform. The pole is at $s = -3$. The impulse response is:

$$h(t) = e^{-3t} \, u(t)$$

where $u(t)$ is the unit step function.

**Step 2** — Causality test. Check $h(t)$ for $t < 0$:

$$h(t) = e^{-3t} \cdot u(t) = \begin{cases} e^{-3t} & t \geq 0 \\ 0 & t < 0 \end{cases}$$

Since $h(t) = 0$ for all $t < 0$, the system is **causal**.

**Step 3** — Stability test via impulse response integral:

$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h(t) \vert \, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-3t} \, dt = \left[ -\frac{1}{3} e^{-3t} \right]_{0}^{\infty} = 0 - \left(-\frac{1}{3}\right) = \frac{1}{3} < \infty$$

The integral is finite, so the system is **BIBO stable**.

**Step 4** — Stability test via poles. The single pole is at $s = -3$, and $\text{Re}(-3) = -3 < 0$. The pole is in the LHP, so the system is **stable**. ✓

**Step 5** — Conclusion: The system is **causal and stable**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 2: Second-Order DT System

**Problem:** Determine stability and causality of $H(z) = \frac{1}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1} + 0.06 z^{-2}}$.

**Step 1** — Convert to positive powers of $z$. Multiply numerator and denominator by $z^2$:

$$H(z) = \frac{z^2}{z^2 - 0.5 z + 0.06}$$

**Step 2** — Find the poles. Solve the denominator quadratic:

$$z^2 - 0.5z + 0.06 = 0$$

Using the quadratic formula $z = \frac{0.5 \pm \sqrt{0.25 - 0.24}}{2} = \frac{0.5 \pm \sqrt{0.01}}{2} = \frac{0.5 \pm 0.1}{2}$

$$z_1 = \frac{0.6}{2} = 0.3, \qquad z_2 = \frac{0.4}{2} = 0.2$$

**Step 3** — Stability test via pole magnitudes:

$$\vert z_1 \vert = 0.3 < 1, \qquad \vert z_2 \vert = 0.2 < 1$$

Both poles lie **inside the unit circle** → the system is **asymptotically stable**.

**Step 4** — Partial-fraction expansion to obtain $h[n]$:

$$H(z) = \frac{z^2}{(z - 0.3)(z - 0.2)} = \frac{0.3 z}{z - 0.3} - \frac{0.2 z}{z - 0.2}$$

Using the standard pair $\frac{z}{z - a} \longleftrightarrow a^n u[n]$, we get:

$$h[n] = 0.3 \cdot (0.3)^n u[n] - 0.2 \cdot (0.2)^n u[n]$$

**Step 5** — Causality test: $h[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$ (because of $u[n]$ factor) → system is **causal**.

**Step 6** — Stability via absolute sum:

$$\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h[n] \vert = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \left[ 0.3 \cdot (0.3)^n + 0.2 \cdot (0.2)^n \right] = \frac{0.3}{1-0.3} + \frac{0.2}{1-0.2} = \frac{3}{7} + \frac{1}{4} = \frac{19}{28} < \infty$$

→ **BIBO stable**. ✓

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 3: Unstable CT System

**Problem:** Analyze the system $H(s) = \frac{1}{s-2}$ (RHP pole).

**Step 1** — Impulse response: $h(t) = e^{2t} u(t)$.

**Step 2** — Causality: $h(t) = 0$ for $t < 0$ → **causal**.

**Step 3** — Stability test:

$$\int_{0}^{\infty} e^{2t} \, dt = \left[ \frac{1}{2} e^{2t} \right]_{0}^{\infty} = \infty$$

The integral diverges, so the system is **not BIBO stable** (in fact, for any non-zero bounded input that persists, the output grows exponentially).

**Step 4** — Pole check: Pole at $s = 2$, $\text{Re}(2) = 2 > 0$ (RHP) → **unstable**. ✓

**Step 5** — Demonstration: Apply the bounded input $x(t) = u(t)$:

$$y(t) = \int_{0}^{t} e^{2(t-\tau)} \, d\tau = e^{2t} \int_{0}^{t} e^{-2\tau} \, d\tau = e^{2t} \cdot \frac{1 - e^{-2t}}{2} = \frac{e^{2t} - 1}{2} \to \infty$$

Output is unbounded → confirms instability.

---

## 3.5 📊 Analytical Decision Matrix

| System Pole Location | Causal ROC | Anti-Causal ROC | BIBO Stable? | Causal? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| All poles in LHP (CT) | ✓ | — | **Yes** | Yes (right-sided) |
| All poles in LHP (CT) | — | ✓ | **Yes** | No (anti-causal, left-sided) |
| Single pole at $s=0$ | $j\omega$ axis boundary | $j\omega$ axis boundary | **No** (marginal) | Depends on ROC choice |
| Pole in RHP (CT) | ✓ | — | **No** | Yes |
| Pole in RHP (CT) | — | ✓ | **No** | No |
| Repeated poles on $j\omega$ axis | ✓ | — | **No** | Yes |
| All poles $\vert z \vert < 1$ (DT) | ✓ | — | **Yes** | Yes |
| Poles on unit circle, simple (DT) | $\vert z \vert > 1$ | $\vert z \vert < 1$ | **No** | Depends |
| Any pole $\vert z \vert > 1$ (DT) | ✓ | — | **No** | Yes |
