# 📝 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]`
**Define BIBO stability for a continuous-time LTI system. State the necessary and sufficient condition on the impulse response for BIBO stability.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **BIBO Stability Definition:** A continuous-time LTI system is BIBO (Bounded-Input Bounded-Output) stable if every bounded input signal $x(t)$ (with $\vert x(t) \vert \leq M_x < \infty$ for all $t$) produces a bounded output $y(t)$ (with $\vert y(t) \vert \leq M_y < \infty$ for all $t$).
>
> **Necessary and Sufficient Condition:** A CT-LTI system with impulse response $h(t)$ is BIBO stable if and only if the impulse response is **absolutely integrable**:
> $$\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert h(t) \vert \, dt < \infty$$

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]`
**What is a causal system? How do you test causality of an LTI system from its impulse response?**

**Model Answer:**

> A **causal system** is one whose output at any time $t$ depends only on the present and past values of the input, not on any future input values. For an LTI system with impulse response $h(t)$, the system is causal if and only if:
> $$h(t) = 0 \quad \text{for all } t < 0$$
> i.e., the impulse response is a *right-sided* function. Equivalently, in discrete time, $h[n] = 0$ for all $n < 0$.

---

## 📕 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 1]`

**(a)** For the LTI system described by the transfer function:
$$H(s) = \frac{2s + 6}{s^2 + 5s + 6}$$
**(i)** Determine the poles and zeros of the system. **(3 marks)**
**(ii)** Test the system for BIBO stability using the pole-location criterion. **(2 marks)**
**(iii)** Find the impulse response $h(t)$ and verify stability using the absolute integrability condition. **(2 marks)**

**(b)** A discrete-time LTI system has transfer function:
$$H(z) = \frac{1}{1 - 1.5z^{-1} + 0.5z^{-2}}$$
**(i)** Find the poles of the system and check whether the system is BIBO stable. **(3 marks)**
**(ii)** Determine whether the system is causal. Justify using the impulse response. **(2 marks)**
**(iii)** If a bounded input $x[n] = 2$ (for all $n$) is applied, comment on the boundedness of the output. **(2 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A**

#### Part (a)(i): Poles and Zeros
Factor numerator: $2s + 6 = 2(s + 3)$ → **Zero at $s = -3$**
Factor denominator: $s^2 + 5s + 6 = (s+2)(s+3)$ → **Poles at $s = -2$ and $s = -3$**

#### Part (a)(ii): Stability by Pole Location
Both poles have $\text{Re}(s_p) = -2$ and $-3$, both strictly less than 0. Hence both poles lie in the **LHP** → the system is **BIBO stable**.

#### Part (a)(iii): Impulse Response and Absolute Integrability
Perform partial-fraction expansion:

$$H(s) = \frac{2(s+3)}{(s+2)(s+3)} = \frac{2}{s+2}$$

(since the $(s+3)$ factor cancels). Inverse Laplace transform:

$$h(t) = 2 e^{-2t} u(t)$$

Absolute integrability test:

$$\int_{0}^{\infty} \vert 2 e^{-2t} \vert \, dt = 2 \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-2t} \, dt = 2 \cdot \frac{1}{2} = 1 < \infty$$

Since the integral is finite, the system is **BIBO stable** ✓ (matches pole-location result).

---

#### Part (b)(i): Poles and Stability of DT System
Multiply by $z^2$:

$$H(z) = \frac{z^2}{z^2 - 1.5z + 0.5}$$

Solve $z^2 - 1.5z + 0.5 = 0$:

$$z = \frac{1.5 \pm \sqrt{2.25 - 2}}{2} = \frac{1.5 \pm 0.5}{2}$$

Poles: $z_1 = 1.0$ and $z_2 = 0.5$.

Magnitudes: $\vert z_1 \vert = 1.0$ (on the unit circle) and $\vert z_2 \vert = 0.5$ (inside).

**Stability check:** Pole $z_1 = 1$ lies **on the unit circle**. With a simple pole on the unit circle, the system is **not BIBO stable** (it is at best marginally stable).

#### Part (b)(ii): Causality
We can write:

$$H(z) = \frac{z^2}{(z-1)(z-0.5)} = \frac{A \cdot z}{z-1} + \frac{B \cdot z}{z-0.5}$$

Solving: $A = 2, B = -1$ (by partial fractions). So:

$$H(z) = \frac{2z}{z-1} - \frac{z}{z-0.5}$$

Using $\frac{z}{z-a} \longleftrightarrow a^n u[n]$:

$$h[n] = 2(1)^n u[n] - (0.5)^n u[n] = (2 - (0.5)^n) u[n]$$

Since $h[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$, the system is **causal**.

#### Part (b)(iii): Response to DC Input
Input $x[n] = 2$ for all $n$. Using Z-domain: $X(z) = \frac{2}{1 - z^{-1}}$ (with appropriate ROC).

The DC gain is $H(z)\big\vert_{z=1} = \frac{1}{1 - 1.5 + 0.5} = \frac{1}{0} = \infty$.

The DC gain is **infinite** — meaning a bounded constant input produces a **linearly growing unbounded output**. This is the textbook signature of marginal instability (pole on unit circle at $z=1$). Confirms **not BIBO stable**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 1]`

**(a)** Consider the system $h(t) = e^{-t} u(t-1)$ (a delayed exponential).
**(i)** Test whether the system is causal. **(3 marks)**
**(ii)** Test BIBO stability using the impulse response integral. **(2 marks)**
**(iii)** Discuss how this system relates to a robot actuator that introduces a 1-second delay. **(2 marks)**

**(b)** A continuous-time LTI system has the impulse response:
$$h(t) = e^{-2t} u(t) + e^{3t} u(-t-1)$$
**(i)** Determine whether the system is causal. **(3 marks)**
**(ii)** Determine whether the system is BIBO stable. Justify using both time-domain and frequency-domain arguments. **(2 marks)**
**(iii)** Identify the Region of Convergence (ROC) and explain its relation to stability and causality. **(2 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B**

#### Part (a)(i): Causality of $h(t) = e^{-t} u(t-1)$
$u(t-1) = 0$ for $t < 1$. So $h(t) = 0$ for $t < 1$, in particular for all $t < 0$.

Since $h(t) = 0$ for $t < 0$, the system **is causal** ✓.

#### Part (a)(ii): Stability
$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h(t) \vert \, dt = \int_{1}^{\infty} e^{-t} \, dt = e^{-1} < \infty$$

The integral is finite, so the system is **BIBO stable** ✓.

#### Part (a)(iii): Robotics Interpretation
This impulse response models a **first-order robotic actuator with a 1-second transport delay** (e.g., a long pneumatic line, a chemical reaction delay, or a slow sensor). The system is realizable in real-time (causal) and well-behaved (stable), but the 1-second lag degrades control loop bandwidth. In **PCRAT402 context**, such delays critically limit the achievable PID loop gain before instability emerges.

---

#### Part (b)(i): Causality
$h(t) = e^{-2t} u(t) + e^{3t} u(-t-1)$

First term is non-zero for $t \geq 0$; second term is non-zero for $t \leq -1$. Hence $h(t) \neq 0$ for $t < 0$ (specifically, $t \leq -1$).

**The system is non-causal** (it has a left-sided component).

#### Part (b)(ii): Stability
Compute the absolute integral:

$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h(t) \vert \, dt = \int_{-\infty}^{-1} e^{3t} \, dt + \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-2t} \, dt$$

- First integral: $\int_{-\infty}^{-1} e^{3t} dt = \frac{e^{-3}}{3} < \infty$ (converges as $t \to -\infty$)
- Second integral: $\frac{1}{2} < \infty$

Total: $\frac{e^{-3}}{3} + \frac{1}{2} < \infty$, so the system **is BIBO stable**.

**Frequency-domain check:** The Laplace transform has poles at $s = -2$ and $s = +3$. Wait — the pole at $s = 3$ is in the RHP!

**Reconciling:** The ROC is the *vertical strip* $-2 < \text{Re}(s) < 3$. This strip **includes the $j\omega$ axis** ($\text{Re}(s) = 0$ lies in $(-2, 3)$), so the system is BIBO stable. The ROC is a strip because the signal is two-sided, **not** because the pole at $s=3$ is "allowed" — the pole is in RHP, but stability is determined by ROC, not pole location alone when the system is non-causal.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Insight:** For **non-causal** systems, the simple rule "all poles in LHP ⇒ stable" is **not sufficient**. You must check the ROC directly. A non-causal system can be stable even with RHP poles, as long as the ROC includes the $j\omega$ axis.

#### Part (b)(iii): ROC Analysis
**ROC = $-2 < \text{Re}(s) < 3$** (the strip between the two poles, containing no poles itself).

- **Stability:** The $j\omega$ axis ($\text{Re}(s) = 0$) lies inside the strip $-2 < 0 < 3$ → ROC includes $j\omega$ axis → **stable**.
- **Causality:** ROC is a vertical strip (not a right-half plane extending to $+\infty$) → the signal is **two-sided**, not right-sided → system is **non-causal**.

This example beautifully illustrates that **ROC** is the single most informative descriptor tying together stability, causality, and time-domain support.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls** (where KTU examiners deduct 2-3 marks per question):
>
> 1. **Confusing marginal stability with BIBO stability.** A simple pole on the $j\omega$ axis (CT) or unit circle (DT) gives **bounded but not absolutely summable** outputs for some bounded inputs (e.g., $x(t) = \sin(\omega_0 t)$). Strict BIBO stability requires the pole to be *strictly* in LHP / inside unit circle.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to specify the ROC** when discussing causality. A transfer function alone does NOT uniquely determine a system; two systems with the same $H(s)$ but different ROCs can have opposite causality and stability properties.
>
> 3. **Applying the "all poles in LHP" rule to non-causal systems.** This rule is valid only for *causal* LTI systems. For non-causal systems, you must check ROC.
>
> 4. **Treating repeated poles on the $j\omega$ axis as marginally stable.** They are *unstable* (e.g., $H(s) = \frac{1}{s^2}$ produces a ramp output for a step input).
>
> 5. **In discrete time, confusing "pole inside unit circle" with "pole on unit circle is OK."** It is **not** OK for BIBO stability.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🔑 **BIBO Stability (CT):** $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h(t) \vert \, dt < \infty$. For rational systems, **all poles in LHP** (assuming causality).
- 🔑 **BIBO Stability (DT):** $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h[n] \vert < \infty$. For rational systems, **all poles inside the unit circle** (assuming causality).
- 🔑 **Causality:** $h(t) = 0$ for $t < 0$ (CT); $h[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$ (DT). All real-time systems **must be causal**.
- 🔑 **Marginal Stability:** Simple poles on $j\omega$ axis (CT) or unit circle (DT) → bounded but not BIBO stable.
- 🔑 **Unstable (CT):** Any RHP pole OR repeated $j\omega$-axis pole.
- 🔑 **Unstable (DT):** Any pole $\vert z \vert > 1$ OR repeated unit-circle pole.
- 🔑 **ROC for Causal Stable CT:** $\text{Re}(s) > \max\{\text{Re}(s_p)\}$ and includes $j\omega$ axis.
- 🔑 **ROC for Causal Stable DT:** $\vert z \vert > \max\{\vert z_p \vert\}$ and includes unit circle.
- 🔑 **ROC ties everything together:** A causal signal ↔ right-sided ROC; an anti-causal signal ↔ left-sided ROC; a two-sided signal ↔ annular/vertical-strip ROC. Stability ↔ ROC includes the $j\omega$ axis (CT) or unit circle (DT).
- 🔑 **Real-time robotics rule:** Every filter and controller you deploy must satisfy **both** BIBO stability **and** causality.
- 🔑 **For non-causal systems:** "Pole in LHP" is **not** sufficient for stability — must check ROC.
- 🔑 **Standard template:** $H(s) = \frac{1}{s-a}$ is causal and stable iff $a < 0$; $H(z) = \frac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}}$ is causal and stable iff $\vert a \vert < 1$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: LTI Systems - Definition and Examples, Convolution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT402 - SIGNAL PROCESSING FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** LTI Systems - Definition and Examples, Convolution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of an LTI System

A **Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) System** is a continuous-time (or discrete-time) system $T\{.\}$ that simultaneously satisfies two fundamental properties:

1. **Linearity** — The system obeys the **Superposition Principle**, which is the combination of the **Additivity Property** and the **Homogeneity (Scaling) Property**.
2. **Time-Invariance** — The behaviour and characteristics of the system do **not change with time**; a shift in the input signal results in an identical shift in the output signal.

Mathematically, an LTI system $T\{.\}$ transforms an input signal $x(t)$ into an output signal $y(t)$ as:

$$y(t) = T\{x(t)\}$$

For **any arbitrary constants** $a$ and $b$, and **any arbitrary time shifts** $t_0$:

$$\boxed{T\{a \cdot x_1(t) + b \cdot x_2(t)\} = a \cdot T\{x_1(t)\} + b \cdot T\{x_2(t)\}}$$

$$\boxed{T\{x(t - t_0)\} = y(t - t_0)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In robotics, LTI systems form the **mathematical backbone** for modelling DC motors, robotic arm dynamics (in simplified form), sensor filters, and controller transfer functions. Almost every classical control and signal-processing operation assumes LTI behaviour as a foundational idealisation.

## 1.2 The Two Pillars of LTI Behaviour

### Pillar 1 — Linearity (Superposition)
Linearity itself is composed of two sub-properties:

| Sub-Property | Mathematical Statement | Plain-English Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Homogeneity (Scaling)** | $T\{a \cdot x(t)\} = a \cdot T\{x(t)\}$ | If you scale the input by $a$, the output also gets scaled by exactly $a$ |
| **Additivity** | $T\{x_1(t) + x_2(t)\} = T\{x_1(t)\} + T\{x_2(t)\}$ | The response to a sum of inputs equals the sum of individual responses |

### Pillar 2 — Time-Invariance
$$T\{x(t - t_0)\} = y(t - t_0)$$

The system's **internal parameters (gain, delay, coefficients)** do not drift or change with time. The same input, applied today or applied 5 seconds later, produces the same output shape.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Coffee Machine" of Signals**
>
> Imagine a **smart coffee vending machine**. You press a button specifying the strength of coffee, and it dispenses a cup.
>
> - **Linearity** is like a **proportional recipe**: If you ask for "double the coffee strength", the machine gives double the coffee powder, double the water, and ultimately a cup with double the intensity. It never suddenly adds sugar when you only asked for more coffee.
> - **Time-Invariance** is like a **machine that never wears out or recalibrates**: If the same button is pressed at 9 AM, 12 noon, or 6 PM, you get exactly the same cup. The machine's "personality" does not change with time.
>
> In signal processing, an LTI system is precisely this kind of *predictable, well-behaved, recipe-following transformation*. Any deviation (e.g., a non-linear amplifier that clips, or a circuit whose gain drifts with temperature) violates the LTI assumption.

## 1.4 Standard Examples of LTI Systems

| # | System | Input-Output Relation | LTI? | Why / Why Not |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Ideal Integrator | $y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} x(\tau)\, d\tau$ | ✅ Yes | Linear integration + constant limits |
| 2 | Ideal Differentiator | $y(t) = \dfrac{dx(t)}{dt}$ | ✅ Yes | Linear differentiation operator |
| 3 | Pure Time Delay | $y(t) = x(t - t_0)$ | ✅ Yes | Linear shift operator |
| 4 | RC Low-Pass Filter | $RC\,\dot{y}(t) + y(t) = x(t)$ | ✅ Yes | Constant coefficients $\Rightarrow$ LTI |
| 5 | Square-Law Device | $y(t) = x^2(t)$ | ❌ No | Squaring is a non-linear operation |
| 6 | Time-Varying Gain | $y(t) = t \cdot x(t)$ | ❌ No | The gain $t$ changes with time |
| 7 | Saturating Amplifier | $y(t) = \mathrm{sat}\{x(t)\}$ | ❌ No | Saturation is non-linear |

## 1.5 Why LTI Systems Are Central to Robotics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **PCRAT402 — Signal Processing for Robotics**, LTI systems are the default model for:
> - **DC Motor position/velocity control** (transfer function of armature-controlled motor)
> - **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU) filtering** (low-pass filters for accelerometer noise)
> - **LiDAR / Camera signal smoothing**
> - **State-space plant models** in linearised robotic manipulator dynamics
>
> Mastering LTI is the **gateway to understanding all of classical control theory** applied in robotics.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Test Procedure for Verifying LTI Behaviour

To test whether a system $y(t) = T\{x(t)\}$ is LTI, the KTU examiner expects the following **two-step verification procedure**:

### Step 1 — Test Linearity (Two Sub-Tests)

**Test 1A — Homogeneity Test:**
Apply input $a \cdot x(t)$ where $a$ is any arbitrary constant (commonly $a = 2$ or $a = 3$).
- Compute LHS: $T\{a \cdot x(t)\}$
- Compute RHS: $a \cdot T\{x(t)\}$
- If LHS $=$ RHS, homogeneity holds.

**Test 1B — Additivity Test:**
Apply inputs $x_1(t)$ and $x_2(t)$ separately, then together.
- Compute LHS: $T\{x_1(t) + x_2(t)\}$
- Compute RHS: $T\{x_1(t)\} + T\{x_2(t)\}$
- If LHS $=$ RHS, additivity holds.

If **both sub-tests pass**, the system is linear.

### Step 2 — Test Time-Invariance

1. Apply $x(t)$ and obtain $y(t) = T\{x(t)\}$.
2. Apply a shifted input $x_1(t) = x(t - t_0)$.
3. Compute $y_1(t) = T\{x_1(t)\} = T\{x(t - t_0)\}$.
4. Compute the time-shifted original output: $y(t - t_0)$.
5. If $y_1(t) = y(t - t_0)$ for **all** $t_0$, the system is time-invariant.

If **both linearity and time-invariance** hold, the system is **LTI**.

## 2.2 The Convolution Operation — Why It Exists

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Insight:** Any LTI system is **completely characterised** by its response to a single special input — the **unit impulse $\delta(t)$**. The output of an LTI system to *any* arbitrary input $x(t)$ can be computed as the **convolution** of the input with the system's **impulse response $h(t)$**.

This is the celebrated **LTI System Theorem** that bridges input–output behaviour with the impulse response.

## 2.3 Definition of Convolution

### Continuous-Time Convolution (Convolution Integral)

The convolution of two continuous-time signals $x(t)$ and $h(t)$ is defined as:

$$\boxed{y(t) = x(t) * h(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau) \cdot h(t - \tau)\, d\tau}$$

where $\tau$ is a **dummy integration variable** and $t$ is the parameter that produces the output at time $t$.

### Discrete-Time Convolution (Convolution Sum)

For sampled signals $x[n]$ and $h[n]$:

$$\boxed{y[n] = x[n] * h[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} x[k] \cdot h[n - k]}$$

## 2.4 The Convolution Procedure — Four Steps

| Step | Operation | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Step 1 — Folding** | Replace $h(\tau)$ with $h(-\tau)$ | Mirror the second signal about the vertical axis |
| **Step 2 — Shifting** | Replace $h(-\tau)$ with $h(t - \tau)$ | Slide the folded signal by $t$ units along the time axis |
| **Step 3 — Multiplication** | Compute $x(\tau) \cdot h(t - \tau)$ | Point-wise product of the two overlapping signals |
| **Step 4 — Integration / Summation** | Integrate (or sum) the product | Area under the product gives output value at that $t$ |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Property | Mathematical Statement | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | LTI Output via Convolution | $y(t) = x(t) * h(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau)\, h(t - \tau)\, d\tau$ | Predicting output of any LTI filter |
| 2 | Discrete Convolution | $y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} x[k]\, h[n-k]$ | Digital filter implementation in robots |
| 3 | Commutative Property | $x(t) * h(t) = h(t) * x(t)$ | Computational flexibility |
| 4 | Associative Property | $[x(t) * h_1(t)] * h_2(t) = x(t) * [h_1(t) * h_2(t)]$ | Cascading LTI systems |
| 5 | Distributive Property | $x(t) * [h_1(t) + h_2(t)] = x(t) * h_1(t) + x(t) * h_2(t)$ | Parallel LTI systems |
| 6 | Identity Element | $x(t) * \delta(t) = x(t)$ | Impulse = system identity |
| 7 | Convolution with Shift | $x(t) * \delta(t - t_0) = x(t - t_0)$ | Time-delay implementation |
| 8 | Width Property | $\text{width}\{x * h\} = \text{width}\{x\} + \text{width}\{h\}$ | Predicting support of output |
| 9 | Area Property | $\text{Area}\{x * h\} = \text{Area}\{x\} \cdot \text{Area}\{h\}$ | DC-gain analysis |
| 10 | Differentiation Property | $\dfrac{d}{dt}[x(t) * h(t)] = \dfrac{dx(t)}{dt} * h(t) = x(t) * \dfrac{dh(t)}{dt}$ | Differentiator-LTI interchange |
| 11 | Causality Condition | System is causal $\iff$ $h(t) = 0$ for $t < 0$ | Real-time filter design |
| 12 | Stability Condition | System is BIBO stable $\iff$ $\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert h(\tau) \vert\, d\tau < \infty$ | Sensor filter stability |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Marks are awarded for **writing the convolution integral limits correctly** ($\tau$ from $-\infty$ to $+\infty$, with the substitution $h(t-\tau)$ flipped and shifted). Even a sign error in the shift will cost marks.

## 2.6 Graphical Interpretation of Convolution

> [!NOTE]
> Convolution is essentially a **"sliding weighted average"**:
> - The folded-and-shifted $h(t - \tau)$ acts as a **sliding window** that travels across $x(\tau)$.
> - At every position $t$, the area of the overlap (i.e., the integral of the product) gives $y(t)$.
> - When the window is *entirely outside* the support of $x(\tau)$, the output is zero.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Robotics:** A low-pass filter's impulse response convolved with a noisy encoder signal produces a clean velocity estimate.
- **Audio:** An acoustic room's impulse response convolved with a clean voice signal simulates reverberation.
- **Image Processing:** A Gaussian kernel convolved with a 2-D image produces blurring (used in vision pre-processing for object detection).
- **Communication:** Channel impulse response convolved with transmitted bits determines the received distorted signal.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why Any LTI System Output Is the Convolution of Input with Impulse Response

**Starting Premise:** Any arbitrary signal $x(t)$ can be expressed as a continuum of weighted, shifted impulses using the **Sifting Property** of the Dirac delta:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x(t) &= \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau)\, \delta(t - \tau)\, d\tau
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **decomposition** of $x(t)$ into a sum (integral) of infinitesimally thin impulses, each of strength $x(\tau)$ located at time $\tau$.

**Step 1 — Pass the decomposed input through the LTI system $T\{.\}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y(t) &= T\{x(t)\} \\
     &= T\!\left\{\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau)\, \delta(t - \tau)\, d\tau \right\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Use Linearity (Homogeneity + Additivity) to move $T\{.\}$ inside the integral:**

Since $T\{.\}$ is linear, the operator can be brought inside the integral and applied term-by-term. The constant $x(\tau)$ is pulled out via homogeneity:

$$
\begin{aligned}
y(t) &= \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau)\, T\{\delta(t - \tau)\}\, d\tau
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Use Time-Invariance to relate $T\{\delta(t - \tau)\}$ to the impulse response:**

By definition, the **impulse response** is:
$$h(t) = T\{\delta(t)\}$$

By the time-invariance property, shifting the input by $\tau$ shifts the output by the same $\tau$:

$$T\{\delta(t - \tau)\} = h(t - \tau)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back to obtain the convolution integral:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\therefore\; y(t) &= \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau)\, h(t - \tau)\, d\tau \\
                  &= x(t) * h(t)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Q.E.D.** This is the most fundamental derivation in signal processing: **the entire input–output behaviour of any LTI system is completely specified by its impulse response $h(t)$**, and the output is the convolution of the input with $h(t)$.

## 3.2 Worked-Out Analytical Example: Convolution of Two Rectangular Pulses

> Let $x(t) = \text{rect}\!\left(\dfrac{t}{2}\right)$, a rectangle of width **2** centred at the origin (i.e., amplitude 1 on $[-1, 1]$).
> Let $h(t) = \text{rect}\!\left(\dfrac{t}{2}\right)$, identical rectangle.
> Compute $y(t) = x(t) * h(t)$.

### Step 1 — Write the convolution integral

$$
\begin{aligned}
y(t) &= \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau)\, h(t - \tau)\, d\tau
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Identify the support of $x(\tau)$

$$x(\tau) = 1 \quad \text{for } -1 \le \tau \le 1, \quad 0 \text{ otherwise}$$

### Step 3 — Identify the support of $h(t - \tau)$

$h(t - \tau) = 1$ when $-1 \le t - \tau \le 1$, i.e., when $t - 1 \le \tau \le t + 1$.

### Step 4 — Determine the overlap region

The product $x(\tau)\,h(t - \tau) = 1$ only when **both** conditions hold simultaneously:

$$-1 \le \tau \le 1 \quad \text{AND} \quad t - 1 \le \tau \le t + 1$$

The integration limits are therefore:

$$\max(-1, t - 1) \le \tau \le \min(1, t + 1)$$

### Step 5 — Identify the three distinct regions of $t$

**Case A — No overlap (output = 0):**

When $t + 1 < -1$, i.e., $t < -2$: the two rectangles do not overlap. So:
$$y(t) = 0, \quad t < -2$$

**Case B — Partial overlap (rising region):**

When $-2 \le t \le 0$: the lower limit is $-1$ and the upper limit is $t + 1$. So:

$$
\begin{aligned}
y(t) &= \int_{-1}^{t+1} 1 \cdot 1\, d\tau \\
     &= \big[\tau\big]_{-1}^{t+1} \\
     &= (t + 1) - (-1) \\
     &= t + 2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Case C — Partial overlap (falling region):**

When $0 \le t \le 2$: the lower limit is $t - 1$ and the upper limit is $+1$. So:

$$
\begin{aligned}
y(t) &= \int_{t-1}^{1} 1 \cdot 1\, d\tau \\
     &= \big[\tau\big]_{t-1}^{1} \\
     &= 1 - (t - 1) \\
     &= 2 - t
\end{aligned}
$$

**Case D — No overlap (output = 0):**

When $t - 1 > 1$, i.e., $t > 2$: no overlap.
$$y(t) = 0, \quad t > 2$$

### Step 6 — Final Assembled Result

$$
\boxed{
y(t) =
\begin{cases}
0, & t < -2 \\
t + 2, & -2 \le t \le 0 \\
2 - t, & 0 \le t \le 2 \\
0, & t > 2
\end{cases}
}
$$

This is the famous **triangular pulse** — the convolution of two rectangles always yields a triangle (a foundational result used in digital filter design and smoothing).

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification via Width Property:** Width of rectangle = 2, so width of convolution = $2 + 2 = 4$. Indeed, the triangle spans from $t = -2$ to $t = 2$, i.e., width $= 4$. ✓
> **Verification via Area Property:** Area of triangle = $\frac{1}{2} \cdot \text{base} \cdot \text{height} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 4 \cdot 2 = 4$. Area of each rectangle = 2, so predicted area = $2 \times 2 = 4$. ✓

## 3.3 Discrete Convolution Worked Example

Let $x[n] = \{1, 2, 3\}$ (i.e., $x[0]=1,\ x[1]=2,\ x[2]=3$) and $h[n] = \{1, 1\}$ ($h[0]=1,\ h[1]=1$). Compute $y[n] = x[n] * h[n]$.

### Step 1 — Determine output length

Output length $= N_x + N_h - 1 = 3 + 2 - 1 = 4$, so $y[n]$ has 4 samples: $n = 0, 1, 2, 3$.

### Step 2 — Apply the convolution sum $y[n] = \sum_{k} x[k]\,h[n-k]$

**For $n = 0$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y[0] &= \sum_{k=0}^{2} x[k]\, h[-k] \\
     &= x[0]\,h[0] + x[1]\,h[-1] + x[2]\,h[-2] \\
     &= (1)(1) + (2)(0) + (3)(0) \\
     &= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**For $n = 1$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y[1] &= \sum_{k=0}^{2} x[k]\, h[1-k] \\
     &= x[0]\,h[1] + x[1]\,h[0] + x[2]\,h[-1] \\
     &= (1)(1) + (2)(1) + (3)(0) \\
     &= 1 + 2 = 3
\end{aligned}
$$

**For $n = 2$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y[2] &= \sum_{k=0}^{2} x[k]\, h[2-k] \\
     &= x[0]\,h[2] + x[1]\,h[1] + x[2]\,h[0] \\
     &= (1)(0) + (2)(1) + (3)(1) \\
     &= 0 + 2 + 3 = 5
\end{aligned}
$$

**For $n = 3$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y[3] &= \sum_{k=0}^{2} x[k]\, h[3-k] \\
     &= x[0]\,h[3] + x[1]\,h[2] + x[2]\,h[1] \\
     &= (1)(0) + (2)(0) + (3)(1) \\
     &= 3
\end{aligned}
$$

### Final Result

$$\boxed{y[n] = \{1, 3, 5, 3\}}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — "What Test Should I Apply?"

| Given Scenario | Test to Apply | Expected Result if LTI |
|---|---|---|
| System involves squaring/cubing $x(t)$ | Linearity test (Additivity) | Will FAIL — non-linear |
| System has $t$ multiplying $x(t)$ | Time-invariance test | Will FAIL — time-varying |
| System has constant coefficients | Both tests | Will PASS — LTI |
| Need output of LTI system for arbitrary input | Use convolution with $h(t)$ | $y(t) = x(t) * h(t)$ |
| Need to find $h(t)$ of a system | Apply $\delta(t)$ as input | Output is $h(t)$ by definition |
| Need BIBO stability of an LTI system | Integrate $\vert h(t) \vert$ | Finite $\Rightarrow$ stable |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 LTI System Functional Block Diagram

This diagram illustrates how an LTI system is conceptualised as a black-box transformation from input signal to output signal, with the impulse response acting as its complete "fingerprint".

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["INPUT x of t"] --> B["LTI SYSTEM T"]
    B --> C["OUTPUT y of t"]
    B -.-> D["CHARACTERISED BY h of t"]
    D -.-> E["Impulse Response of System"]
    E -.-> B
    F["DELTA t Input"] --> G["T applied to Delta"]
    G --> H["OUTPUT IS h of t"]
```

## 4.2 Convolution Process Flow — Four-Step Schematic

This diagram breaks down the four mechanical operations of the convolution integral in the order students must execute them during the examination.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["STEP 1 FOLDING h tau becomes h minus tau"]
    S2["STEP 2 SHIFTING h minus tau becomes h t minus tau"]
    S3["STEP 3 MULTIPLICATION x tau times h t minus tau"]
    S4["STEP 4 INTEGRATION Integral from minus infinity to plus infinity"]
    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
    S3 --> S4
    S4 --> R["OUTPUT y of t equals x t convolve h t"]
```

## 4.3 LTI System Test Decision Tree

This decision tree guides the KTU student in verifying whether a given system is LTI, by applying the homogeneity, additivity, and time-shift tests in sequence.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P["GIVEN SYSTEM T open brace x close brace equals y"]
    P --> Q1["LINEARITY TEST"]
    Q1 --> Q1A["Homogeneity T open brace a x close brace equals a T open brace x close brace"]
    Q1A --> Q1B["Additivity T open brace x1 plus x2 close brace equals T open brace x1 close brace plus T open brace x2 close brace"]
    Q1B --> Q2{"Both Sub Tests Pass"}
    Q2 -- NO --> NL["SYSTEM IS NON LINEAR"]
    Q2 -- YES --> Q3["TIME INVARIANCE TEST"]
    Q3 --> Q4["Compute T open brace x of t minus t0 close brace"]
    Q4 --> Q5["Compare with y of t minus t0"]
    Q5 --> Q6{"Equal for All t0"}
    Q6 -- NO --> NTI["SYSTEM IS TIME VARYING"]
    Q6 -- YES --> LTI["SYSTEM IS LTI"]
```

## 4.4 LTI Cascaded / Parallel Architectures (using Convolution Properties)

This diagram visualises how convolution's associative and distributive properties permit the combination of multiple LTI blocks.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CAS["CASCADE ARCHITECTURE ASSOCIATIVE"]
        direction LR
        X1["x t"] --> H1["h1 t"]
        H1 --> H2["h2 t"]
        H2 --> Y1["y t equals x t convolve h1 t convolve h2 t"]
    end
    subgraph PAR["PARALLEL ARCHITECTURE DISTRIBUTIVE"]
        direction LR
        X2["x t"] --> P1["h1 t"]
        X2 --> P2["h2 t"]
        P1 --> SUM["plus"]
        P2 --> SUM
        SUM --> Y2["y t equals x t convolve h1 plus h2 t"]
    end
```

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture: LTI System in a Robotic Control Loop

> [!NOTE]
> Since the topic is mathematical in nature, the following mermaid diagram maps the **functional flow of signals** through a real robotic LTI system — specifically, a DC motor with encoder feedback.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    REF["Reference Command u t"] --> ERR["Subtraction Node"]
    FB["Encoder Signal y t"] --> ERR
    ERR --> CTRL["PI Controller hC t"]
    CTRL --> MOT["DC Motor Plant hP t"]
    MOT --> OUT["Motor Speed Output y t"]
    OUT --> SENS["Sensor Filter hF t"]
    SENS --> FB
    CTRL -.->|Impulse response| IC["hC t"]
    MOT -.->|Impulse response| IP["hP t"]
    SENS -.->|Impulse response| IF["hF t"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each block ($h_C$, $h_P$, $h_F$) is an LTI system with its own impulse response. The total closed-loop behaviour is obtained by convolving all impulse responses in the cascade. This is why **LTI + Convolution** is foundational to all of robotic control engineering.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, May 2024]
**Define an LTI system. State any two examples of LTI systems used in robotics.**

**Model Answer:**

A system $T\{.\}$ is said to be **Linear Time-Invariant (LTI)** if it satisfies two properties:
1. **Linearity:** $T\{a \cdot x_1(t) + b \cdot x_2(t)\} = a \cdot T\{x_1(t)\} + b \cdot T\{x_2(t)\}$ (where $a$, $b$ are constants).
2. **Time-Invariance:** $T\{x(t - t_0)\} = y(t - t_0)$ (where $t_0$ is any arbitrary shift).

**Two examples of LTI systems in robotics:**
- **DC Motor Position Control:** $J \ddot{\theta}(t) + b \dot{\theta}(t) = \tau(t)$ — modelled as a second-order LTI plant.
- **RC Low-Pass Filter for Sensor Noise:** $RC \cdot \dot{y}(t) + y(t) = x(t)$ — a first-order LTI system used to denoise encoder signals.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for linearity definition, 1 mark for time-invariance definition, 1 mark for two examples.)*

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Dec 2023]
**State and explain the commutative and associative properties of convolution.**

**Model Answer:**

**Commutative Property:**
$$x(t) * h(t) = h(t) * x(t)$$

This means the order of the two signals being convolved does not change the output. Mathematically:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x(t) * h(t) &= \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau)\, h(t - \tau)\, d\tau \\
            &= \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} h(\tau)\, x(t - \tau)\, d\tau \\
            &= h(t) * x(t)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering utility:** The commutative property allows us to choose whichever signal is easier to fold-and-shift graphically.

**Associative Property:**
$$[x(t) * h_1(t)] * h_2(t) = x(t) * [h_1(t) * h_2(t)]$$

**Engineering utility:** When cascading multiple LTI systems (e.g., controller + plant + filter), the total impulse response is the convolution of the individual impulse responses, and the grouping order does not matter.

*(3 marks: 1 mark commutative statement, 1 mark associative statement, 1 mark engineering utility.)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 1 Choice A]

**(a)** Test whether the system $y(t) = t \cdot x(t)$ is **(i)** linear and **(ii)** time-invariant. Justify your answer mathematically. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the convolution $y(t) = x(t) * h(t)$ where:
$$x(t) = e^{-t}\, u(t), \qquad h(t) = u(t)$$

Sketch $y(t)$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — Linearity and Time-Invariance Test

**Step 1 — Test for Linearity:**

We need to check whether $T\{a x_1(t) + b x_2(t)\} = a\, T\{x_1(t)\} + b\, T\{x_2(t)\}$.

**LHS Computation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T\{a x_1(t) + b x_2(t)\} &= t \cdot [a x_1(t) + b x_2(t)] \\
                         &= a\, t\, x_1(t) + b\, t\, x_2(t)
\end{aligned}
$$

**RHS Computation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
a\, T\{x_1(t)\} + b\, T\{x_2(t)\} &= a\,[t \cdot x_1(t)] + b\,[t \cdot x_2(t)] \\
                                  &= a\, t\, x_1(t) + b\, t\, x_2(t)
\end{aligned}
$$

Since **LHS = RHS** for all $a$, $b$, the system is **linear**. ✓

**Step 2 — Test for Time-Invariance:**

Let $x_1(t) = x(t - t_0)$. Compute the response:

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_1(t) &= T\{x_1(t)\} = T\{x(t - t_0)\} = t \cdot x(t - t_0)
\end{aligned}
$$

Now compute the time-shifted original output:

$$
\begin{aligned}
y(t - t_0) &= (t - t_0) \cdot x(t - t_0)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparison:** For time-invariance, we need $y_1(t) = y(t - t_0)$:

$$
t \cdot x(t - t_0) \;\neq\; (t - t_0) \cdot x(t - t_0)
$$

These are **not equal** (the $t_0$ difference persists). Hence the system is **NOT time-invariant**. ✗

**Conclusion:** The system $y(t) = t \cdot x(t)$ is **Linear but Time-Variant**, hence **NOT LTI**.

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — Convolution of Exponential and Unit Step

**Step 1 — Set up the convolution integral:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y(t) &= x(t) * h(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau)\, h(t - \tau)\, d\tau \\
     &= \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} e^{-\tau}\, u(\tau)\, u(t - \tau)\, d\tau
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Determine the integration limits:**

$u(\tau) = 1$ requires $\tau \ge 0$.
$u(t - \tau) = 1$ requires $\tau \le t$.

So both step functions are simultaneously 1 only when $0 \le \tau \le t$. **But** this is only meaningful when $t \ge 0$ (otherwise the upper limit is below the lower limit, giving zero).

So we have **two cases**:

**Case 1: $t < 0$:** $y(t) = 0$.

**Case 2: $t \ge 0$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y(t) &= \int_{0}^{t} e^{-\tau} \cdot 1 \cdot 1\, d\tau \\
     &= \int_{0}^{t} e^{-\tau}\, d\tau \\
     &= \big[-e^{-\tau}\big]_{0}^{t} \\
     &= -e^{-t} - (-e^{0}) \\
     &= 1 - e^{-t}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Final assembled result:**

$$
\boxed{
y(t) =
\begin{cases}
0, & t < 0 \\
1 - e^{-t}, & t \ge 0
\end{cases}
}
$$

**Step 4 — Sketch description:**

For $t < 0$, $y(t) = 0$ along the time axis. At $t = 0$, $y(0) = 0$. As $t$ increases, $y(t)$ rises exponentially and asymptotically approaches the value $1$ as $t \to +\infty$. The curve is a classic **rising exponential** starting at the origin and saturating at $y = 1$. This is the classic step response of a first-order RC low-pass filter.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 1 Choice B]

**(a)** With the help of neat mathematical steps, derive the convolution integral $y(t) = x(t) * h(t)$ for an LTI system, starting from the sifting property of the Dirac delta. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the system $y(t) = 3\,x(t) + 2$, check if the system is LTI. Compute its output for the input $x(t) = \sin(\omega t)$ and comment on whether the system is BIBO stable. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — Derivation of Convolution Integral

**Step 1 — Express input as a superposition of shifted impulses using the sifting property:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x(t) &= \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau)\, \delta(t - \tau)\, d\tau
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Pass the decomposed input through the LTI system:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y(t) &= T\{x(t)\} = T\!\left\{\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau)\, \delta(t - \tau)\, d\tau\right\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Linearity (homogeneity + additivity) to bring $T\{.\}$ inside the integral:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y(t) &= \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau)\, T\{\delta(t - \tau)\}\, d\tau
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Time-Invariance:**

Since the system is time-invariant, shifting the input by $\tau$ shifts the output by the same $\tau$:

$$
T\{\delta(t - \tau)\} = h(t - \tau)
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute to obtain the convolution integral:**

$$
\boxed{y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau)\, h(t - \tau)\, d\tau = x(t) * h(t)}
$$

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — Test of LTI Status and BIBO Stability of $y(t) = 3 x(t) + 2$

**Step 1 — Test Linearity:**

Apply the additivity test with inputs $x_1(t)$ and $x_2(t)$:

**LHS:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T\{x_1(t) + x_2(t)\} &= 3\,[x_1(t) + x_2(t)] + 2 = 3 x_1(t) + 3 x_2(t) + 2
\end{aligned}
$$

**RHS:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T\{x_1(t)\} + T\{x_2(t)\} &= [3 x_1(t) + 2] + [3 x_2(t) + 2] = 3 x_1(t) + 3 x_2(t) + 4
\end{aligned}
$$

Since **LHS $\neq$ RHS** (the constant offset $2$ does not obey additivity), the system is **NOT linear**. ✗

Hence the system is **NOT LTI**.

**Step 2 — Compute output for $x(t) = \sin(\omega t)$:**

$$
y(t) = 3 \sin(\omega t) + 2
$$

**Step 3 — BIBO Stability Check:**

A system is **BIBO stable** if every bounded input produces a bounded output.

- Input: $x(t) = \sin(\omega t)$ is **bounded** ($\vert \sin(\omega t) \vert \le 1$).
- Output: $y(t) = 3 \sin(\omega t) + 2$ is **bounded** ($\vert y(t) \vert \le 5$).

For **any** bounded input $\vert x(t) \vert \le M$, the output is $\vert y(t) \vert = \vert 3 x(t) + 2 \vert \le 3M + 2$, which is finite.

Hence the system is **BIBO Stable** ✓ — but this is *despite* it being non-linear and non-LTI. BIBO stability is a separate property from LTI.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Conflating "Linear" with "LTI":** A system can be linear but time-varying (or vice versa). Always test *both* properties before declaring a system LTI. *(−2 marks if missed)*
> 2. **Forgetting the constant offset breaks linearity:** $y(t) = 3 x(t) + 2$ has a constant "bias" of $2$. This **alone** violates linearity. Many students incorrectly label such systems as LTI.
> 3. **Sign error in convolution folding:** $h(t - \tau)$ — students often write $h(\tau - t)$, which produces a different (incorrect) result. Always flip $h$ first to get $h(-\tau)$, *then* shift to get $h(t - \tau)$.
> 4. **Integration limits:** When both signals are causal ($u(t)$ present), the lower limit is **0**, not $-\infty$. Marks are deducted for $\pm\infty$ limits in causal cases.
> 5. **Forgetting to check both cases of $t$:** In convolution of causal signals, you **must** split into $t < 0$ and $t \ge 0$ cases. Missing one case is a $-1$ to $-2$ mark penalty.
> 6. **Stating only LTI but skipping BIBO:** A 7-mark question on a system expects both LTI test *and* BIBO test. Skipping one half costs 3–4 marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **LTI = Linear + Time-Invariant** — two independent properties that must *both* be satisfied.
- [x] **Linearity** = Homogeneity + Additivity (the Superposition Principle).
- [x] **Time-Invariance** = A shift in input produces an identical shift in output.
- [x] **Impulse Response $h(t)$** completely characterises any LTI system.
- [x] **Convolution Integral:** $y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau)\, h(t - \tau)\, d\tau$.
- [x] **Discrete Convolution Sum:** $y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} x[k]\, h[n-k]$.
- [x] **Commutative, Associative, Distributive** — three core algebraic properties of convolution.
- [x] **Identity element** of convolution is the Dirac delta: $x(t) * \delta(t) = x(t)$.
- [x] **Width Property:** width of output = sum of widths of the two inputs being convolved.
- [x] **Convolution of two rectangles** always yields a **triangle** (key for filter smoothing).
- [x] **Convolution of $e^{-at} u(t)$ with $u(t)$** yields $\dfrac{1 - e^{-at}}{a} u(t)$ — a classic result.
- [x] **Causal system** ⇒ $h(t) = 0$ for $t < 0$.
- [x] **BIBO stable LTI system** ⇒ $\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert h(\tau) \vert\, d\tau < \infty$.
- [x] **LTI systems in robotics:** DC motor model, RC filters, encoder smoothing, IMU low-pass filtering.
- [x] **Robotics relevance:** Sensor noise reduction, controller design, kinematic signal flow, and feedback-loop stability all rely on LTI + convolution foundations.
- [x] **Exam mantra:** Always state the *definition* first, then the *test*, then the *math*, then the *conclusion* — examiners reward structured answers.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Correlation and Orthogonality of Signals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT402 - SIGNAL PROCESSING FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Correlation and Orthogonality of Signals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Correlation

**Correlation** is a mathematical measure that quantifies the degree of similarity between two signals as a function of the time-lag (shift) applied to one of them. In the context of robotics, correlation is the backbone of signal matching, template matching for object recognition, sonar/radar echo detection, SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping), and even DNA/sequence matching in biological robotics applications.

There are two principal forms:
- **Auto-correlation**: Measures the similarity of a signal with a **time-shifted version of itself**.
- **Cross-correlation**: Measures the similarity between **two different signals** as one is shifted in time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCRAT402 / Module 1)**
> Correlation is treated as an *energy-domain operation* (not a transform like the Fourier series). It is directly applicable to *finite-energy deterministic signals* used in robotic sensors (ultrasonic, LIDAR pulses, IR bursts).

---

## 1.2 What Does "Orthogonality" of Signals Mean?

Two signals $x_1(t)$ and $x_2(t)$ are said to be **orthogonal** over an interval $[t_1, t_2]$ if their inner product (the integral of their product) over that interval equals **zero**.

Mathematically:
$$\int_{t_1}^{t_2} x_1(t)\, x_2^{*}(t)\, dt = 0$$

where $x_2^{*}(t)$ denotes the complex conjugate of $x_2(t)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Expected Answer Style):**
> Two real-energy signals $x_1(t)$ and $x_2(t)$ are orthogonal over the interval $[a, b]$ if
> $$\int_{a}^{b} x_1(t)\, x_2(t)\, dt = 0$$
> Orthogonal signals carry **mutually exclusive information** — knowing one tells you nothing about the other over the specified interval. They are the signal-processing analogue of perpendicular vectors in $\mathbb{R}^n$.

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

### 🧠 Analogy 1 — Orthogonality = Two Comrades Who Do Not Interfere
Imagine two electricians, **A** and **B**, working on completely independent tasks on a construction site during the same shift. At the end of the day, their *productivity contributions do not overlap* — the work done by A is completely separate from B's. The mathematical "product" (work overlap) integrated over the shift = 0. This is precisely what orthogonality means in signals: the two signals do not "interact" or share information over the chosen interval.

### 🧠 Analogy 2 — Correlation = Sliding One Fingerprint Over Another
Imagine you have two ink fingerprints on transparent sheets, and you slide one slowly over the other. The moment the patterns align, the dark-ink overlap (similarity) reaches its **maximum**. The shift at which this maximum occurs tells you the *time delay* between the two signals. This is exactly what **cross-correlation** computes — and it is the same principle used in **pulse-echo sonar** of a robot mapping a wall.

---

## 1.4 Why Robotics Engineers Study This

- **Object recognition**: Comparing a live camera/LIDAR signal to stored templates (cross-correlation peak detection).
- **Echo ranging**: Measuring the time lag between transmitted and received ultrasonic pulses (auto-correlation).
- **Modulation**: Sinusoidal carriers of different frequencies are *mutually orthogonal* — the foundation of **FDM (Frequency Division Multiplexing)** in robotic communication buses (CAN, EtherCAT).
- **Filter design**: Orthogonality underpins optimal filter theory (Wiener, matched filters).
- **Sensor fusion**: Decorrelated sensor streams simplify Kalman-filter state estimation.

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Pearl:** Always state the *interval* when claiming orthogonality. Two signals may be orthogonal on $[0, 1]$ but not on $[0, 2]$.

---

## 1.5 Key Metrics at a Glance

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Definition |
|---|---|---|
| Inner product of two signals | $\langle x, y \rangle$ | $\int_{a}^{b} x(t)y^{*}(t)\, dt$ |
| Energy of a signal | $E_x$ | $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^{2}\, dt$ |
| Auto-correlation | $R_{xx}(\tau)$ | $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)x^{*}(t-\tau)\, dt$ |
| Cross-correlation | $R_{xy}(\tau)$ | $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)y^{*}(t-\tau)\, dt$ |

> All limits default to **$(-\infty, \infty)$** for energy signals unless the problem explicitly restricts to an interval.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Orthogonal Signals — Operational Theory

Two continuous-time signals $x_1(t)$ and $x_2(t)$ are **orthogonal** over the interval $[a, b]$ if and only if:

$$\int_{a}^{b} x_1(t)\, x_2^{*}(t)\, dt = 0$$

### Why "Why" Matters for KTU:
- **Why orthogonality?** It is the foundation of representing signals as a sum of basis functions (think Fourier series — sine and cosine form an orthogonal set).
- **Why the conjugate?** Real signals are self-conjugate, but most KTU problems expect you to handle complex exponentials $e^{j\omega t}$ too.

### Generalised Orthogonal Set
A set of signals $\{ \phi_1(t), \phi_2(t), \dots, \phi_N(t) \}$ is orthogonal over $[a,b]$ if:

$$\int_{a}^{b} \phi_i(t)\, \phi_j^{*}(t)\, dt = \begin{cases} 0, & i \neq j \\ E_i, & i = j \end{cases}$$

If additionally $E_i = 1$ for all $i$, the set is called **orthonormal**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Orthogonal vs Orthonormal (Board-Favourite Trap Question):**
> - Orthogonal ⇒ inner product = 0 for $i \neq j$ (no constraint on diagonal).
> - Orthonormal ⇒ orthogonal **AND** each signal has unit energy.

---

## 2.2 Auto-Correlation $R_{xx}(\tau)$ — Definition and Properties

### 2.2.1 Definition (for energy signals)
$$R_{xx}(\tau) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\, x^{*}(t - \tau)\, dt = \langle x(t), x(t-\tau) \rangle$$

### 2.2.2 Properties
1. **Even symmetry**: $R_{xx}(\tau) = R_{xx}^{*}(-\tau)$
2. **Maximum at zero lag**: $\vert R_{xx}(\tau) \vert \leq R_{xx}(0) = E_x$ (the total signal energy).
3. **Real for real signals**: $R_{xx}(\tau) \in \mathbb{R}$ if $x(t)$ is real.
4. **Riemann-Lebesgue**: $R_{xx}(\tau) \to 0$ as $\vert \tau \vert \to \infty$ for most physical signals.

### 2.2.3 "Why These Properties?"
- **(1)** arises because shifting one copy left vs right simply swaps the role of variables.
- **(2)** follows from the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality: $|\langle x, y \rangle|^2 \leq \langle x, x\rangle\langle y, y\rangle$, with $y = x(t-\tau)$ giving $E_x^2$.
- **(4)** is intuitive — far-apart shifted copies of a finite-energy signal have negligible overlap.

### 2.2.4 Relation to Energy Spectral Density
By the **Wiener–Khinchin theorem**:
$$R_{xx}(\tau) \;\xleftrightarrow{\;\mathcal{F}\;}\; S_{xx}(\omega) = \vert X(\omega) \vert^{2}$$

where $S_{xx}(\omega)$ is the Energy Spectral Density (ESD). This bridge is essential when designing **matched filters** in robotics — peak SNR occurs at the auto-correlation peak.

---

## 2.3 Cross-Correlation $R_{xy}(\tau)$ — Definition and Properties

### 2.3.1 Definition
$$R_{xy}(\tau) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\, y^{*}(t - \tau)\, dt = \langle x(t), y(t-\tau) \rangle$$

Some texts (and KTU!) use the **alternative convention** with a **positive shift** on $y$:
$$R_{xy}(\tau) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\, y^{*}(t + \tau)\, dt$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Convention Trap:** Always verify the sign convention used in your textbook/teacher's notes. KTU typically adopts the **first form** $R_{xy}(\tau)=\int x(t)y^{*}(t-\tau)dt$, but the answer key for older papers sometimes uses the second. **Write the formula you adopt in your answer sheet.**

### 2.3.2 Properties
1. $R_{xy}(\tau) = R_{yx}^{*}(-\tau)$ — conjugate symmetry.
2. $\vert R_{xy}(\tau) \vert \leq \sqrt{E_x E_y}$ — Cauchy–Schwarz bound.
3. **Not necessarily even** (unlike auto-correlation).
4. If $x(t)$ and $y(t)$ are orthogonal, $R_{xy}(\tau) = 0$ for **every** $\tau$.
5. $R_{xy}(\tau) = 0 \;\forall \tau$ implies $x(t)$ and $y(t)$ have **no overlap** (decorrelated).

### 2.3.3 Peak Detection Use in Robotics
If $y(t) = x(t - t_0) + n(t)$ (delayed signal + noise), then $R_{xy}(\tau)$ peaks at $\tau = t_0$. This is **time-delay estimation** — used for:
- Ultrasonic range finding (echo time-of-flight)
- LIDAR pulse matching
- Acoustic source localisation

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Property | Mathematical Statement | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Orthogonality | $\int_{a}^{b} x_1(t)x_2^{*}(t)\, dt = 0$ | Proving orthogonality |
| 2 | Orthonormal | $\int_{a}^{b} \phi_i(t)\phi_j^{*}(t)\, dt = \delta_{ij}$ | Normalising basis sets |
| 3 | Energy of $x(t)$ | $E_x = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\vert x(t)\vert^{2}\, dt$ | Checking unit-normalisation |
| 4 | Auto-correlation (energy) | $R_{xx}(\tau)=\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}x(t)x^{*}(t-\tau)dt$ | Self-similarity detection |
| 5 | Auto-correlation peak | $R_{xx}(0) = E_x$ (maximum) | Energy computation |
| 6 | Even symmetry | $R_{xx}(\tau)=R_{xx}^{*}(-\tau)$ | Simplification |
| 7 | Cross-correlation | $R_{xy}(\tau)=\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}x(t)y^{*}(t-\tau)dt$ | Time-delay estimation |
| 8 | Cross-symmetry | $R_{xy}(\tau)=R_{yx}^{*}(-\tau)$ | Converting between forms |
| 9 | Cauchy–Schwarz bound | $\vert R_{xy}(\tau)\vert \leq \sqrt{E_x E_y}$ | Bounding correlation |
| 10 | Wiener–Khinchin | $R_{xx}(\tau)\leftrightarrow S_{xx}(\omega)=\vert X(\omega)\vert^{2}$ | Spectral analysis link |
| 11 | Orthogonality of sinusoids | $\int_{0}^{T}\cos(n\omega_0 t)\cos(m\omega_0 t)\,dt = 0$ for $n\neq m$ | Fourier series proofs |
| 12 | Power correlation (periodic) | $R_{xx}(\tau) = \dfrac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T}x(t)x^{*}(t-\tau)dt$ | Periodic signals |

> [!TIP]
> The value **$R_{xx}(0) = E_x$** is asked almost every semester — commit it to memory.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Matched filtering in robotics**: The output SNR-maximising filter has impulse response $h(t) = x(T - t)$, and its output is precisely $R_{xx}(\tau)$.
- **Fourier Series coefficients**: Computed as projections onto orthogonal sinusoidal basis.
- **Channel estimation**: Cross-correlating known training sequences with received signals in robotic wireless links.
- **Optical flow / stereo vision**: Block matching between left/right camera frames uses normalised cross-correlation (NCC).
- **ECG/EMG denoising**: Auto-correlation identifies the periodicity of cardiac/muscular cycles in wearable robotic exoskeletons.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation 1 — Proving Orthogonality of $\sin$ and $\cos$ over $[0, 2\pi]$

**Problem:** Show that $x_1(t) = \sin(t)$ and $x_2(t) = \cos(t)$ are orthogonal over $[0, 2\pi]$.

**Step 1 — Write the inner product.**
$$I = \int_{0}^{2\pi} x_1(t)\, x_2(t)\, dt = \int_{0}^{2\pi} \sin(t)\cos(t)\, dt$$

**Step 2 — Apply the trigonometric identity.**
Using $2\sin A \cos A = \sin(2A)$:
$$\sin(t)\cos(t) = \frac{1}{2}\sin(2t)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and integrate.**
$$I = \int_{0}^{2\pi} \frac{1}{2}\sin(2t)\, dt = \frac{1}{2}\left[-\frac{\cos(2t)}{2}\right]_{0}^{2\pi}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the limits.**
$$I = -\frac{1}{4}\bigl[\cos(4\pi) - \cos(0)\bigr] = -\frac{1}{4}[1 - 1] = 0$$

**Step 5 — Conclude.**
Since the integral is **zero**, $\sin(t)$ and $\cos(t)$ are orthogonal over $[0, 2\pi]$. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation 2 — Proving Orthogonality of Complex Exponentials

**Problem:** Show that $\phi_n(t) = e^{j n \omega_0 t}$ and $\phi_m(t) = e^{j m \omega_0 t}$ are orthogonal over $[0, T]$ where $T = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega_0}$.

**Step 1 — Write the inner product.**
$$I_{nm} = \int_{0}^{T} e^{j n \omega_0 t}\left(e^{j m \omega_0 t}\right)^{*}\, dt = \int_{0}^{T} e^{j n \omega_0 t}\, e^{-j m \omega_0 t}\, dt$$

**Step 2 — Combine the exponentials.**
$$I_{nm} = \int_{0}^{T} e^{j (n-m)\omega_0 t}\, dt$$

**Step 3 — Case (a): $n = m$.**
$$I_{nn} = \int_{0}^{T} e^{j(0)t}\, dt = \int_{0}^{T} 1\, dt = T$$
So the energy $E_n = T$.

**Step 4 — Case (b): $n \neq m$.**
$$I_{nm} = \left[\frac{e^{j(n-m)\omega_0 t}}{j(n-m)\omega_0}\right]_{0}^{T} = \frac{1}{j(n-m)\omega_0}\left[e^{j(n-m)\omega_0 T} - 1\right]$$

Substituting $\omega_0 T = 2\pi$:
$$e^{j(n-m)\cdot 2\pi} = \cos(2\pi(n-m)) + j\sin(2\pi(n-m)) = 1$$

Therefore:
$$I_{nm} = \frac{1 - 1}{j(n-m)\omega_0} = 0$$

**Step 5 — Conclude.**
$$\int_{0}^{T} e^{j n \omega_0 t}\, e^{-j m \omega_0 t}\, dt = T \cdot \delta_{nm}$$

This is the **fundamental orthogonality relation** that underpins the Discrete-Time Fourier Series (DTFS). $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Worked Derivation 3 — Computing Auto-Correlation of a Rectangular Pulse

**Problem:** Compute $R_{xx}(\tau)$ for $x(t) = \text{rect}\!\left(\dfrac{t}{T}\right)$, i.e., a unit-amplitude pulse of width $T$ centred at the origin.

**Step 1 — Define the signal explicitly.**
$$x(t) = \begin{cases} 1, & -T/2 \leq t \leq T/2 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 2 — Write the auto-correlation integral.**
$$R_{xx}(\tau) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\, x(t - \tau)\, dt$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and overlap.**
$x(t) = 1$ on $[-T/2,\, T/2]$, and $x(t-\tau) = 1$ on $[-T/2 + \tau,\, T/2 + \tau]$. The overlap is non-empty only when:
$$\vert \tau \vert \leq T$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate.**
For $\vert \tau \vert \leq T$:
$$R_{xx}(\tau) = \int_{-T/2}^{T/2 - \tau} 1\cdot 1\, dt = T - \vert \tau \vert$$

(Symmetric about $\tau = 0$; the upper limit becomes $T/2 - \tau$ for $\tau \geq 0$ and a mirror image holds for $\tau < 0$.)

**Step 5 — Write the final closed form.**
$$R_{xx}(\tau) = \begin{cases} T - \vert \tau \vert, & \vert \tau \vert \leq T \\ 0, & \vert \tau \vert > T \end{cases}$$

This is a **triangular pulse** of peak $T$ at $\tau = 0$. $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** $R_{xx}(0) = T = \int \vert x(t) \vert^{2} dt = E_x$ ✓ (peak equals energy, confirming property 2).

---

## 3.4 Worked Derivation 4 — Cauchy–Schwarz Bound on Cross-Correlation

**Problem:** Prove that $\vert R_{xy}(\tau)\vert^{2} \leq E_x \cdot E_y$ for any two energy signals.

**Step 1 — Form the energy of a linear combination.**
$$E_\alpha = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\left\vert \alpha\, x(t) + y(t-\tau) \right\vert^{2}dt \geq 0$$

for any real constant $\alpha$.

**Step 2 — Expand the squared magnitude.**
$$\vert \alpha x(t) + y(t-\tau) \vert^{2} = \alpha^{2}\vert x(t)\vert^{2} + 2\alpha \operatorname{Re}\{x(t)y^{*}(t-\tau)\} + \vert y(t-\tau)\vert^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Integrate.**
$$E_\alpha = \alpha^{2} E_x + 2\alpha \operatorname{Re}\{R_{xy}(\tau)\} + E_y \geq 0$$

**Step 4 — Apply the discriminant condition** ($\alpha^{2} E_x + 2\alpha \operatorname{Re}\{R_{xy}\} + E_y \geq 0$ for all $\alpha$).

For a quadratic $a\alpha^{2} + b\alpha + c \geq 0\ \forall \alpha$, we need discriminant $b^{2} - 4ac \leq 0$:
$$4[\operatorname{Re}\{R_{xy}(\tau)\}]^{2} - 4 E_x E_y \leq 0$$

**Step 5 — Conclude.**
$$\vert R_{xy}(\tau) \vert^{2} \leq E_x E_y \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \vert R_{xy}(\tau)\vert \leq \sqrt{E_x E_y}\ \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Cross-Correlation of Common Signal Pairs

| $x(t)$ | $y(t)$ | $R_{xy}(\tau)$ | Peak Lag |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\delta(t)$ | $\delta(t-t_0)$ | $\delta(\tau - t_0)$ | $\tau = t_0$ |
| Rectangular pulse $T$ | Same pulse delayed by $t_0$ | Triangular pulse, peak at $t_0$ | $\tau = t_0$ |
| $\cos(\omega_0 t)$ | $\cos(\omega_0(t-t_0))$ | $\dfrac{T}{2}\cos(\omega_0\tau + \phi)$ | Periodic |
| White noise $n(t)$ | $n(t-t_0) + $ same noise | Sharp peak at $\tau = t_0$ | $\tau = t_0$ |
| $e^{j\omega_0 t}$ | $e^{j\omega_1 t}$ ($\omega_0 \neq \omega_1$) | $0$ (orthogonal) | None |

> [!TIP]
> If two sinusoids have **different frequencies**, they are orthogonal over the fundamental period. This is why **FDM** (Frequency Division Multiplexing) works.

---

## 3.6 Practice Numerical Example

**Problem:** Compute $R_{xy}(\tau)$ for $x(t) = e^{-at}u(t)$ and $y(t) = e^{-bt}u(t)$ where $a,b > 0$.

**Step 1 — Write the cross-correlation (assume $a \neq b$).**
$$R_{xy}(\tau) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-at}u(t)\, e^{-b(t-\tau)}u(t-\tau)\, dt$$

**Step 2 — Simplify.** Both exponentials exist only when $t \geq 0$ and $t - \tau \geq 0$, i.e., $t \geq \max(0,\tau)$.
$$R_{xy}(\tau) = \int_{\max(0,\tau)}^{\infty} e^{-at}\, e^{-bt}\, e^{b\tau}\, dt = e^{b\tau}\int_{\max(0,\tau)}^{\infty} e^{-(a+b)t}\, dt$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate.**
$$R_{xy}(\tau) = \frac{e^{b\tau}}{a+b}\left[e^{-(a+b)\max(0,\tau)}\right]$$

**Step 4 — Two cases.**
$$R_{xy}(\tau) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{e^{-a\tau}}{a+b}, & \tau \geq 0 \\[6pt] \dfrac{e^{b\tau}}{a+b}, & \tau < 0 \end{cases}$$

**Step 5 — Verify peak.** Maximum occurs at $\tau = 0$: $R_{xy}(0) = \dfrac{1}{a+b}$. $\blacksquare$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Cross-Correlation Engine for Robotic Echo Ranging

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Tx Pulse Generator"] --> nodeB["Ultrasonic Transducer"]
    nodeB -->|"Acoustic Wave"| nodeC["Environment Obstacle"]
    nodeC -->|"Reflected Echo"| nodeD["Rx Transducer"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Amplifier and Filter"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["ADC Sampler"]
    nodeA --> nodeG["Reference Signal Buffer"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["Cross Correlator Rxy tau"]
    nodeF --> nodeH
    nodeH -->|"Peak Detection"| nodeI["Time Delay Estimator"]
    nodeI -->|"Distance = v times tau over 2"| nodeJ["Robot Range Output"]
```

**Interpretation:** The cross-correlator slides the stored transmitted pulse (`x(t)`) against the received echo (`y(t)`). The lag $\tau$ at which $R_{xy}(\tau)$ peaks equals the **round-trip time of flight**. The robot converts this to distance using $d = \dfrac{v_{sound} \cdot \tau}{2}$.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Conceptual Map — Orthogonality vs Correlation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Signal Relationship Concepts"] --> nodeB["Orthogonality"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["Correlation"]
    nodeB --> nodeD["Inner Product equals Zero"]
    nodeB --> nodeE["No Information Overlap"]
    nodeB --> nodeF["Sine and Cosine over Full Period"]
    nodeC --> nodeG["Auto Correlation Rxx tau"]
    nodeC --> nodeH["Cross Correlation Rxy tau"]
    nodeG --> nodeI["Maximum at tau equals Zero"]
    nodeG --> nodeJ["Equals Energy at Origin"]
    nodeH --> nodeK["Peak indicates Time Delay"]
    nodeH --> nodeL["Bounded by Cauchy Schwarz"]
    nodeF --> nodeM["Basis of Fourier Series"]
    nodeK --> nodeN["Used in Sonar and LIDAR"]
    nodeL --> nodeO["Used in Matched Filtering"]
```

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Matched Filter Realisation Using Auto-Correlation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Input x(t) plus Noise n(t)"] --> nodeB["Matched Filter h(t) equals x of T minus t"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Convolution y(t) equals Rx of t plus noise cross term"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Sample at t equals T"]
    nodeD -->|"Maximum SNR Decision"| nodeE["Detector Output"]
```

**Why this matters in robotics:** A matched filter is mathematically equivalent to computing the auto-correlation of the known transmit pulse against the noisy received signal. It maximises the SNR at the decision instant — critical for robust detection in low-SNR environments (deep-sea AUVs, dusty mining robots, far-field LIDAR).

---

## 4.4 Time-Domain Schematic — Auto-Correlation of a Rectangular Pulse

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["x(t) Rectangular Pulse Width T"] -->|"Shift by tau"| nodeB["x of t minus tau"]
    nodeA -->|"Multiply"| nodeC["Product x(t) times x of t minus tau"]
    nodeB -->|"Multiply"| nodeC
    nodeC -->|"Integrate over t"| nodeD["Rxx tau equals T minus absolute tau"]
    nodeD -->|"For absolute tau less than T"| nodeE["Triangular Pulse Peak T"]
    nodeD -->|"For absolute tau greater than T"| nodeF["Zero"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The triangular auto-correlation is the **time-domain signature** of all rectangular pulses. Engineers recognise this shape on an oscilloscope as "I just auto-correlated a rect pulse".

---

## 4.5 Functional Architecture — Robotic SLAM Block Using Correlation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["LIDAR Scan Stream"] --> nodeB["Preprocessor and Denoiser"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Reference Map Tile Store"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Normalised Cross Correlator"]
    nodeB --> nodeD
    nodeD -->|"Peak Position and Confidence"| nodeE["Pose Estimator EKF"]
    nodeE -->|"Robot Pose Update"| nodeF["Occupancy Grid Map Builder"]
    nodeF --> nodeC
    nodeF --> nodeG["SLAM Output to Motion Planner"]
```

**Interpretation:** This shows how cross-correlation feeds the **pose estimator** loop in modern SLAM stacks. Each scan tile is correlated with stored reference tiles; the peak indicates loop-closure or scan-match correction.
