# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]`

**Q: Define orthogonal signals. Show that $\sin(2\pi t)$ and $\cos(2\pi t)$ are orthogonal over the interval $[0, 1]$.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Two signals $x_1(t)$ and $x_2(t)$ are orthogonal over $[a, b]$ if $\displaystyle\int_{a}^{b} x_1(t)\, x_2^{*}(t)\, dt = 0$.

**Proof:**
$$I = \int_{0}^{1} \sin(2\pi t)\cos(2\pi t)\, dt = \frac{1}{2}\int_{0}^{1}\sin(4\pi t)\, dt$$
$$= \frac{1}{2}\left[-\frac{\cos(4\pi t)}{4\pi}\right]_{0}^{1} = -\frac{1}{8\pi}\bigl[\cos(4\pi) - \cos(0)\bigr] = -\frac{1}{8\pi}[1 - 1] = 0$$

Since the inner product equals zero, the two signals are orthogonal over $[0, 1]$. $\blacksquare$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to specify the **interval**. Without an interval, orthogonality is meaningless. **Always mention $[a,b]$**.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Series II]`

**Q: Define the auto-correlation function $R_{xx}(\tau)$ of an energy signal. List any four of its properties.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** For an energy signal $x(t)$, the auto-correlation function is:
$$R_{xx}(\tau) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\, x^{*}(t-\tau)\, dt$$

**Four Properties:**
1. $R_{xx}(0) = E_x$ (maximum value equals signal energy).
2. $R_{xx}(\tau) = R_{xx}^{*}(-\tau)$ (conjugate-even symmetry).
3. $\vert R_{xx}(\tau)\vert \leq R_{xx}(0)$ (peak at origin).
4. $R_{xx}(\tau) \to 0$ as $\vert\tau\vert \to \infty$ for most physical energy signals (Riemann–Lebesgue property).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Do **not** confuse "auto-correlation" with "cross-correlation." Many students write $R_{xy}$ by mistake and lose the entire 3 marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question B-A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Series I]`

**(a)** [7 Marks] For the two energy signals $x_1(t)$ and $x_2(t)$ shown, verify whether they form an orthogonal set on $[0, 2]$:
- $x_1(t) = 1$, for $0 \leq t \leq 1$ and $0$ otherwise.
- $x_2(t) = 1$, for $1 \leq t \leq 2$ and $0$ otherwise.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Compute the auto-correlation function $R_{xx}(\tau)$ of the signal $x(t) = e^{-t}u(t)$ and plot its variation with $\tau$. State three properties satisfied by this $R_{xx}(\tau)$.

---

### **Model Answer for B-A (a)**

**Step 1 — State the orthogonality condition.**
We need to verify $\displaystyle\int_{0}^{2} x_1(t)\, x_2(t)\, dt = 0$.

**Step 2 — Identify the support (non-zero regions).**
- $x_1(t) = 1$ on $[0, 1]$
- $x_2(t) = 1$ on $[1, 2]$

**Step 3 — Compute the inner product.**
$$\int_{0}^{2} x_1(t)\, x_2(t)\, dt = \int_{0}^{1} 1 \cdot 0\, dt + \int_{1}^{1} 1\cdot 1\, dt + \int_{1}^{2} 0\cdot 1\, dt$$

Strictly, $x_1(t)\, x_2(t) = 1$ **only at the single point** $t = 1$ (a set of measure zero).

Therefore:
$$\int_{0}^{2} x_1(t)\, x_2(t)\, dt = 0$$

**Step 4 — Conclude.**
The two rectangular pulses, being non-overlapping, are orthogonal over $[0, 2]$. $\blacksquare$

---

### **Model Answer for B-A (b)**

**Step 1 — Write the auto-correlation integral.**
$$R_{xx}(\tau) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-t}u(t)\cdot e^{-(t-\tau)}u(t-\tau)\, dt$$

**Step 2 — Simplify the integration limits.**
Both signals are non-zero only when $t \geq 0$ AND $t - \tau \geq 0$, i.e., $t \geq \max(0, \tau)$.

**Step 3 — Evaluate, case 1: $\tau \geq 0$.**
$$R_{xx}(\tau) = \int_{\tau}^{\infty} e^{-t}\cdot e^{-(t-\tau)}\, dt = e^{\tau}\int_{\tau}^{\infty} e^{-2t}\, dt$$
$$= e^{\tau}\left[\frac{e^{-2t}}{-2}\right]_{\tau}^{\infty} = \frac{e^{\tau}\cdot e^{-2\tau}}{2} = \frac{e^{-\tau}}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate, case 2: $\tau < 0$.**
$$R_{xx}(\tau) = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-t}\cdot e^{-(t-\tau)}\, dt = e^{\tau}\int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-2t}\, dt = e^{\tau}\cdot\frac{1}{2} = \frac{e^{\tau}}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Combine.**
$$R_{xx}(\tau) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{e^{-\tau}}{2}, & \tau \geq 0 \\[8pt] \dfrac{e^{\tau}}{2}, & \tau < 0 \end{cases} = \frac{1}{2}e^{-\vert\tau\vert}$$

This is a **two-sided decaying exponential** with peak value $R_{xx}(0) = \dfrac{1}{2}$.

**Step 6 — Three properties:**
1. **Maximum at origin:** $R_{xx}(0) = 1/2 = E_x$ ✓ (since $E_x = \int_0^\infty e^{-2t}dt = 1/2$).
2. **Even symmetry:** $R_{xx}(\tau) = R_{xx}(-\tau) = \frac{1}{2}e^{-\vert\tau\vert}$ ✓.
3. **Decays to zero:** $\lim_{\vert\tau\vert\to\infty} R_{xx}(\tau) = 0$ ✓.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students forget to handle the $\tau \geq 0$ and $\tau < 0$ cases **separately** because the lower integration limit changes. **Always split the analysis at $\tau = 0$.**

---

### **Question B-B (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Series III]`

**(a)** [7 Marks] For two signals $x(t) = t$ and $y(t) = 1$ defined on the interval $[-1, 1]$, determine whether they are orthogonal. Compute their inner product and cross-correlation function $R_{xy}(\tau)$.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Using the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality, derive the upper bound for $\vert R_{xy}(\tau)\vert$ for two energy signals. Mention its significance in matched filter design.

---

### **Model Answer for B-B (a)**

**Step 1 — Compute the inner product (orthogonality check).**
$$I = \int_{-1}^{1} t \cdot 1\, dt = \left[\frac{t^{2}}{2}\right]_{-1}^{1} = \frac{1}{2} - \frac{1}{2} = 0$$

**Conclusion:** $x(t) = t$ and $y(t) = 1$ **are orthogonal** on $[-1, 1]$.

**Step 2 — Compute the cross-correlation.**
$$R_{xy}(\tau) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\, y^{*}(t-\tau)\, dt$$

Substituting $x(t) = t$ and $y(t-\tau) = 1$ (constant):
$$R_{xy}(\tau) = \int_{-1}^{1} t\, dt = 0$$

So $R_{xy}(\tau) = 0$ for all $\tau$, consistent with the orthogonality conclusion.

**Step 3 — Cross-correlation in the general case (no shift version).**
If we instead treat $y(t) = 1$ on $[0, 1]$ and $x(t) = t$ on $[0, 1]$ with $R_{xy}(\tau) = \int x(t) y(t-\tau) dt$:
- For $\tau \in [0, 1]$: $R_{xy}(\tau) = \int_{\tau}^{1} t \cdot 1 \, dt = \frac{1-\tau^{2}}{2}$.
- For $\tau < 0$: $R_{xy}(\tau) = \int_{0}^{1} t \, dt = \frac{1}{2}$.
- For $\tau > 1$: $R_{xy}(\tau) = 0$.

(Choose the case specified by your exam question.)

---

### **Model Answer for B-B (b)**

**Step 1 — State Cauchy–Schwarz.**
For two energy signals $x(t)$ and $y(t)$:
$$\left\vert \int x(t)\, y^{*}(t)\, dt \right\vert^{2} \leq \int \vert x(t)\vert^{2}\, dt \cdot \int \vert y(t)\vert^{2}\, dt$$

**Step 2 — Apply to cross-correlation.**
With $x(t)$ and $y(t-\tau)$:
$$\vert R_{xy}(\tau)\vert^{2} \leq E_x \cdot E_y \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \vert R_{xy}(\tau)\vert \leq \sqrt{E_x E_y}$$

**Step 3 — Derivation (worked through in Section 3.4 above).**

**Step 4 — Significance in matched filter design.**
- The matched filter maximises output SNR at $t = T$ equal to $2E_x / N_0$ where $N_0$ is noise PSD.
- The output peak value $\vert R_{xy}(\tau)\vert \leq \sqrt{E_x E_y}$ bounds the maximum detection signal.
- It guarantees **no false detection beyond the bound**, enabling constant false-alarm rate (CFAR) thresholding in robotic sensors.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** When asked for the bound, write the **square** version too: $\vert R_{xy}(\tau)\vert^{2} \leq E_x E_y$. Examiners often award a separate mark for the squared form.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 1: Correlation & Orthogonality)**

- **Orthogonality condition:** Always integrate the product over the **stated interval**; result must equal **zero**.
- **Inner product notation:** $\langle x, y \rangle = \int x(t)y^{*}(t) dt$ — this is the bedrock formula.
- **Orthogonal ≠ orthonormal.** Orthonormal additionally requires each signal to have unit energy.
- **Auto-correlation at zero lag** gives the **signal energy**: $R_{xx}(0) = E_x$.
- **Auto-correlation is even**: $R_{xx}(\tau) = R_{xx}(-\tau)$ for real signals.
- **Cross-correlation is NOT even.** Use $R_{xy}(\tau) = R_{yx}^{*}(-\tau)$.
- **Peak of cross-correlation** indicates the **time delay** between two similar signals — the foundation of sonar/LIDAR ranging.
- **Cauchy–Schwarz bound:** $\vert R_{xy}(\tau)\vert \leq \sqrt{E_x E_y}$ — must be remembered verbatim.
- **Wiener–Khinchin theorem** links $R_{xx}(\tau) \leftrightarrow \vert X(\omega)\vert^{2}$ — essential for spectral analysis.
- **Convention trap:** Verify whether your course uses $y(t-\tau)$ or $y(t+\tau)$ in the definition.
- **Sinusoidal orthogonality** (Fourier series): $\int_0^T \cos(n\omega_0 t)\cos(m\omega_0 t)dt = 0$ for $n \neq m$ — most-frequently tested.
- **Decay property:** $R_{xx}(\tau) \to 0$ as $\vert\tau\vert \to \infty$ for finite-energy signals.
- **Energy vs Power** correlation: For *periodic* signals use the **time-average** form: $R_{xx}(\tau) = \dfrac{1}{T}\int_0^T x(t)x^{*}(t-\tau)\, dt$.
- **Robotic applications** to remember: ultrasonic ranging, LIDAR scan matching, matched filter detection, SLAM pose correction, FDM communication buses.
- **Always split the correlation integral** at $\tau = 0$ (or wherever the support changes).
- **Unit / sanity check:** If you compute $R_{xx}(\tau)$, plug in $\tau = 0$ and confirm you get $\int \vert x(t)\vert^{2} dt$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Frequency Domain Representation - Continuous Time Fourier Series
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT402 - SIGNAL PROCESSING FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Frequency Domain Representation - Continuous Time Fourier Series

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Continuous Time Fourier Series (CTFS)** is a frequency-domain mathematical representation of a periodic, continuous-time signal $x(t)$ that satisfies the **Dirichlet conditions**. It decomposes the signal into a weighted sum of harmonically related complex exponentials (or sines and cosines), thereby translating the signal from the time domain into its constituent frequency components.

A signal $x(t)$ is **periodic with fundamental period $T_0$** if and only if:
$$x(t) = x(t + T_0) \quad \forall\, t \in \mathbb{R}$$

The **fundamental angular frequency** (in rad/s) is defined as:
$$\omega_0 = \frac{2\pi}{T_0} = 2\pi f_0$$

where $f_0 = 1/T_0$ is the **fundamental frequency in Hz**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Continuous Time Fourier Series is the foundational tool for analyzing all periodic signals encountered in robotics — motor control waveforms, sensor excitation signals, PWM outputs, and vibration analysis. Mastery of this topic is essential for KTU ESE and is a direct carry-over into Z-Transform and DFT modules.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Prism of a Robotic Engineer

Imagine you are holding a prism that splits white light into its constituent rainbow colors. **Fourier Series is the mathematical prism for signals.**

- **Time Domain** $x(t)$: the white light (a complex, mixed waveform, e.g., a noisy current drawn by a robotic arm's motor).
- **Fourier Series** $X_k$: the rainbow (a set of pure, single-frequency sinusoids — like isolating the 50 Hz fundamental, the 150 Hz third harmonic, etc.).
- **Fourier Coefficients** $a_k, b_k$ (or $X_k$): the *intensity* of each color in the rainbow.

Just as an artist can re-mix the rainbow to reproduce the original white light, we can **sum the harmonics** to perfectly reconstruct the original time-domain waveform. This is the essence of Fourier decomposition and synthesis.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A periodic signal of period $T_0$ contains energy **only** at discrete frequencies $f = kf_0$ (harmonics of the fundamental). It is *not* a continuous spectrum — that is the Fourier Transform. This discrete-versus-continuous distinction is heavily tested in KTU exams.

### 1.3 Dirichlet Conditions (Existence Theorem)

For a periodic signal $x(t)$ to possess a valid Fourier Series representation, it must satisfy the following three conditions over any single period:

1. **Absolute Integrability:** $\int_{T_0} \vert x(t) \vert \, dt < \infty$ (the signal must be finite-energy over one period).
2. **Finite Number of Discontinuities:** $x(t)$ must have at most a finite number of finite discontinuities (jumps) within a period.
3. **Finite Number of Extrema:** $x(t)$ must have at most a finite number of maxima and minima within a period.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All physically realizable signals in robotics (bounded voltages, finite currents, real mechanical displacements) **always** satisfy the Dirichlet conditions. So the Fourier Series is, for all practical engineering purposes, **guaranteed to exist**.

### 1.4 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics (Bolded)

- **Fundamental Period:** $T_0$ (seconds)
- **Fundamental Frequency:** $f_0 = 1/T_0$ (Hertz, **Hz**)
- **Fundamental Angular Frequency:** $\omega_0 = 2\pi/T_0$ (radians/second, **rad/s**)
- **k-th Harmonic Frequency:** $k f_0$ or $k \omega_0$ (dimensionless integer $k \in \mathbb{Z}$)
- **k-th Fourier Coefficient:** $X_k$ or $C_k$ (complex-valued, dimensionless or signal-units)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Equivalent Forms of CTFS

A periodic signal $x(t)$ with period $T_0$ can be represented in three equivalent Fourier Series forms. Mastery of the conversions between them is the single most-tested skill in KTU 2024 ESE.

#### Form 1: Trigonometric (Real) Fourier Series

$$x(t) = a_0 + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\left[a_n \cos(n\omega_0 t) + b_n \sin(n\omega_0 t)\right]$$

where the **DC component** and **Fourier coefficients** are:

$$a_0 = \frac{1}{T_0}\int_{t_0}^{t_0+T_0} x(t)\, dt$$

$$a_n = \frac{2}{T_0}\int_{t_0}^{t_0+T_0} x(t)\cos(n\omega_0 t)\, dt$$

$$b_n = \frac{2}{T_0}\int_{t_0}^{t_0+T_0} x(t)\sin(n\omega_0 t)\, dt$$

#### Form 2: Compact Trigonometric Form (Amplitude–Phase)

$$x(t) = C_0 + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} C_n \cos\!\left(n\omega_0 t + \theta_n\right)$$

with the conversion relations:
$$C_0 = a_0, \quad C_n = \sqrt{a_n^2 + b_n^2}, \quad \theta_n = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{-b_n}{a_n}\right)$$

#### Form 3: Complex Exponential (Most General) Fourier Series

$$x(t) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} X_k\, e^{\,j k \omega_0 t}$$

with the **synthesis equation** for coefficients:
$$X_k = \frac{1}{T_0}\int_{T_0} x(t)\, e^{-j k \omega_0 t}\, dt$$

> [!NOTE]
> The complex exponential form is the **most elegant** and is the form used in the **Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT)** and **Z-Transform** modules. Always use this form unless a KTU question explicitly asks for the trigonometric form.

### 2.2 Why the Three Forms? — A Logical Breakdown

1. **Why DC component $a_0$?** The average value of $x(t)$ over one period is constant and represents the zero-frequency (DC) content.
2. **Why cosines and sines?** They are **orthogonal** over a period:
   $$\int_{T_0} \cos(m\omega_0 t)\cos(n\omega_0 t)\, dt = 0 \quad \text{for } m \neq n$$
   Orthogonality guarantees that each coefficient $a_n$ or $b_n$ can be computed *independently* of all others.
3. **Why complex exponentials?** Euler's formula $e^{j\theta} = \cos\theta + j\sin\theta$ unifies the sine and cosine into a single rotating phasor. This compact form is essential for **system analysis** because $e^{j k \omega_0 t}$ is the **eigenfunction** of any Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) system.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Range / Domain** |
|---|---|---|
| Fundamental frequency | $f_0 = 1/T_0$ | $f_0 \in \mathbb{R}^+$ |
| Fundamental angular freq. | $\omega_0 = 2\pi/T_0$ | $\omega_0 \in \mathbb{R}^+$ |
| DC component (trig form) | $a_0 = (1/T_0)\int_{T_0} x(t)\, dt$ | $k = 0$ |
| Cosine coefficient | $a_n = (2/T_0)\int_{T_0} x(t)\cos(n\omega_0 t)\, dt$ | $n \geq 1$ |
| Sine coefficient | $b_n = (2/T_0)\int_{T_0} x(t)\sin(n\omega_0 t)\, dt$ | $n \geq 1$ |
| Complex coefficient | $X_k = (1/T_0)\int_{T_0} x(t)\, e^{-jk\omega_0 t}\, dt$ | $k \in \mathbb{Z}$ |
| Amplitude spectrum | $C_n = \sqrt{a_n^2 + b_n^2}$ | $n \geq 1$ |
| Phase spectrum | $\theta_n = -\tan^{-1}(b_n / a_n)$ | $n \geq 1$ |
| Trig to exponential | $X_0 = a_0; \quad X_k = (a_k - j b_k)/2$ | $k > 0$ |
| Trig to exponential (neg.) | $X_{-k} = X_k^*$ (conjugate symmetric) | $k < 0$ |
| Exp. to trig | $a_k = X_k + X_{-k}; \quad b_k = j(X_k - X_{-k})$ | $k \geq 1$ |
| Parseval's Power Theorem | $(1/T_0)\int_{T_0} \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert X_k \vert^2$ | Total power equality |
| RMS value | $x_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{\sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert X_k \vert^2}$ | — |
| Harmonic freq. | $f_k = k f_0$ | $k \in \mathbb{Z}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Avoidance:** Forgetting the factor of $1/T_0$ in $X_k$ is the **#1 most common error** in Fourier Series problems. The integral itself is over one period, and you **must** divide by $T_0$. Also, for the trigonometric form, $a_n$ and $b_n$ have an extra factor of $2/T_0$ (because we sum over $n \geq 1$ only, while the complex form covers both positive and negative $k$).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility in Robotics

- **Motor Torque Ripple Analysis:** Brushless DC (BLDC) motors and stepper motors draw **non-sinusoidal periodic currents**. Fourier Series reveals the dominant harmonics, allowing engineers to design filters to reduce Electromagnetic Interference (EMI).
- **PWM Spectrum:** Pulse-Width Modulation (PWM) signals used in robotic servo control are square waves. The Fourier Series of a square wave dictates the **harmonic content**, which is critical for EMI compliance.
- **Sensor Calibration:** Many position encoders (e.g., optical encoders, resolvers) produce periodic waveforms with known harmonics. By analyzing the harmonic content, calibration errors and mechanical misalignment can be detected.
- **Vibration & Structural Analysis:** Robotic arm vibrations are inherently periodic. Fourier Series decomposes the vibration into modal frequencies, enabling targeted damping.

### 2.5 Symmetry Properties (Critical Shortcuts for KTU)

| **Symmetry of $x(t)$** | **Effect on Coefficients** | **Visible Frequencies** |
|---|---|---|
| Even: $x(-t) = x(t)$ | $b_n = 0 \;\; \forall n$ | DC + Cosines only |
| Odd: $x(-t) = -x(t)$ | $a_n = 0 \;\; \forall n$ (and $a_0=0$) | Sines only |
| Half-wave (HWS): $x(t \pm T_0/2) = -x(t)$ | $a_n = b_n = 0$ for even $n$ | Odd harmonics only |
| Quarter-wave (Even + HWS) | Only $a_n$ survives for odd $n$ | DC + Odd Cosines |
| Quarter-wave (Odd + HWS) | Only $b_n$ survives for odd $n$ | Odd Sines only |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pro Tip:** Always check the symmetry of $x(t)$ **before** integrating. This single check can reduce your computation time by 50% and is the fastest way to score full marks in Part A.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From the Synthesis Equation to the Coefficients

We want to derive the **analysis equation** for $X_k$ from the synthesis equation:
$$x(t) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} X_k\, e^{jk\omega_0 t}$$

**Step 1 — Multiply both sides by $e^{-jm\omega_0 t}$** (an arbitrary harmonic of the same period):
$$x(t)\, e^{-jm\omega_0 t} = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} X_k\, e^{jk\omega_0 t}\, e^{-jm\omega_0 t}$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange the exponential:**
$$x(t)\, e^{-jm\omega_0 t} = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} X_k\, e^{j(k-m)\omega_0 t}$$

**Step 3 — Integrate both sides over one period $T_0$:**
$$\int_{T_0} x(t)\, e^{-jm\omega_0 t}\, dt = \int_{T_0} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} X_k\, e^{j(k-m)\omega_0 t}\, dt$$

**Step 4 — Exchange integration and summation (justified by uniform convergence, guaranteed by Dirichlet conditions):**
$$\int_{T_0} x(t)\, e^{-jm\omega_0 t}\, dt = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} X_k \int_{T_0} e^{j(k-m)\omega_0 t}\, dt$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the inner integral. Using the orthogonality property of complex exponentials:**

$$\int_{T_0} e^{j(k-m)\omega_0 t}\, dt = \begin{cases} T_0 & \text{if } k = m \\ 0 & \text{if } k \neq m \end{cases}$$

This is the **orthogonality identity** — the cornerstone of Fourier analysis. We prove it below.

**Step 6 — Proof of orthogonality:** For $k \neq m$,
$$\int_{0}^{T_0} e^{j(k-m)\omega_0 t}\, dt = \left[ \frac{e^{j(k-m)\omega_0 t}}{j(k-m)\omega_0} \right]_{0}^{T_0} = \frac{e^{j(k-m)2\pi} - 1}{j(k-m)\omega_0}$$

Since $k - m$ is an integer, $e^{j(k-m)2\pi} = 1$, so the numerator is **zero**. For $k = m$, the integrand is $e^0 = 1$, so the integral is simply $T_0$.

**Step 7 — Apply orthogonality. Only the term $k = m$ survives in the sum:**
$$\int_{T_0} x(t)\, e^{-jm\omega_0 t}\, dt = X_m \cdot T_0$$

**Step 8 — Solve for $X_m$:**
$$X_m = \frac{1}{T_0} \int_{T_0} x(t)\, e^{-jm\omega_0 t}\, dt$$

Replacing $m$ with $k$ for general notation:
$$\boxed{X_k = \frac{1}{T_0} \int_{T_0} x(t)\, e^{-jk\omega_0 t}\, dt}$$

This is the **Fourier Series Analysis Equation** (CTFS). The derivation is **complete**.

### 3.2 Derivation: Relationship Between Trigonometric and Exponential Coefficients

Starting from the complex exponential form:
$$x(t) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} X_k\, e^{jk\omega_0 t}$$

**Step 1 — Expand $X_k$ using Euler's formula and the orthogonality-derived $a_n, b_n$:**

We define:
$$X_k = \frac{a_k - j b_k}{2} \quad \text{for } k > 0$$

and verify this by computing $X_k$ directly:
$$X_k = \frac{1}{T_0} \int_{T_0} x(t)\, e^{-jk\omega_0 t}\, dt$$

**Step 2 — Split the exponential into real and imaginary parts:**
$$e^{-jk\omega_0 t} = \cos(k\omega_0 t) - j\sin(k\omega_0 t)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the analysis equation:**
$$X_k = \frac{1}{T_0} \int_{T_0} x(t)\left[\cos(k\omega_0 t) - j\sin(k\omega_0 t)\right] dt$$

**Step 4 — Distribute the integration:**
$$X_k = \frac{1}{T_0} \int_{T_0} x(t)\cos(k\omega_0 t)\, dt - \frac{j}{T_0} \int_{T_0} x(t)\sin(k\omega_0 t)\, dt$$

**Step 5 — Recognize the trigonometric coefficients (note: trigonometric uses $2/T_0$, not $1/T_0$):**

Recall $a_k = (2/T_0) \int_{T_0} x(t)\cos(k\omega_0 t)\, dt$. So the first integral equals $a_k/2$.
Recall $b_k = (2/T_0) \int_{T_0} x(t)\sin(k\omega_0 t)\, dt$. So the second integral equals $b_k/2$.

**Step 6 — Substitute:**
$$X_k = \frac{a_k}{2} - j \frac{b_k}{2} = \frac{1}{2}(a_k - j b_k)$$

$$\boxed{X_k = \frac{a_k - j b_k}{2}, \quad k > 0}$$

**Step 7 — Negative indices:** By similar algebra (using $e^{+jk\omega_0 t}$ in Euler expansion):
$$X_{-k} = \frac{1}{2}(a_k + j b_k) = X_k^*$$

This proves the **conjugate symmetry** of $X_k$ for real-valued signals, which is a heavily tested KTU property.

### 3.3 Derivation: Parseval's Power Theorem

**Statement:** The average power of a periodic signal equals the sum of the squared magnitudes of its Fourier coefficients.

**Step 1 — Compute the average power:**
$$P_{\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{T_0} \int_{T_0} \vert x(t) \vert^2\, dt$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the synthesis expansion $x(t) = \sum_{k} X_k e^{jk\omega_0 t}$:**
$$P_{\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{T_0} \int_{T_0} x(t) \cdot x^*(t)\, dt = \frac{1}{T_0} \int_{T_0} x(t) \left[\sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} X_k e^{jk\omega_0 t}\right]^* dt$$

**Step 3 — Apply complex conjugate (since $X_k$ is in general complex):**
$$P_{\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{T_0} \int_{T_0} x(t) \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} X_k^* e^{-jk\omega_0 t}\, dt$$

**Step 4 — Swap sum and integral:**
$$P_{\text{avg}} = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} X_k^* \cdot \frac{1}{T_0} \int_{T_0} x(t) e^{-jk\omega_0 t}\, dt$$

**Step 5 — Recognize the analysis equation in the integral. The integral equals $X_k$:**
$$P_{\text{avg}} = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} X_k^* \cdot X_k = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert X_k \vert^2$$

**Step 6 — Final result:**
$$\boxed{\frac{1}{T_0} \int_{T_0} \vert x(t) \vert^2\, dt = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert X_k \vert^2}$$

This is **Parseval's Theorem** for the CTFS, and is the frequency-domain statement of **energy conservation** — a cornerstone of signal processing and KTU-tested in Module 2.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Square Wave (Canonical KTU Problem)

Consider the **periodic square wave**:
$$x(t) = \begin{cases} +1, & 0 \leq t < T_0/2 \\ -1, & T_0/2 \leq t < T_0 \end{cases}$$

with period $T_0$ and $\omega_0 = 2\pi/T_0$.

**Step 1 — Compute the DC component $X_0$:**
$$X_0 = \frac{1}{T_0} \int_{0}^{T_0} x(t)\, dt = \frac{1}{T_0}\left[\int_{0}^{T_0/2} (1)\, dt + \int_{T_0/2}^{T_0} (-1)\, dt \right]$$

$$= \frac{1}{T_0}\left[\frac{T_0}{2} - \frac{T_0}{2}\right] = 0$$

This makes physical sense — the square wave is *zero-mean* (equal positive and negative halves).

**Step 2 — Compute $X_k$ for $k \neq 0$:**
$$X_k = \frac{1}{T_0} \int_{0}^{T_0/2} (1) e^{-jk\omega_0 t}\, dt + \frac{1}{T_0} \int_{T_0/2}^{T_0} (-1) e^{-jk\omega_0 t}\, dt$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the first integral:**
$$I_1 = \frac{1}{T_0} \left[\frac{e^{-jk\omega_0 t}}{-jk\omega_0}\right]_{0}^{T_0/2} = \frac{1}{T_0} \cdot \frac{1 - e^{-jk\pi}}{jk\omega_0}$$

Since $k$ is an integer, $e^{-jk\pi} = (-1)^k$:
$$I_1 = \frac{1 - (-1)^k}{jk \cdot 2\pi} = \frac{1 - (-1)^k}{j 2\pi k}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the second integral:**
$$I_2 = -\frac{1}{T_0} \left[\frac{e^{-jk\omega_0 t}}{-jk\omega_0}\right]_{T_0/2}^{T_0} = -\frac{1}{T_0} \cdot \frac{e^{-jk 2\pi} - e^{-jk\pi}}{-jk\omega_0}$$

$$= -\frac{1 - (-1)^k}{-j 2\pi k} = \frac{1 - (-1)^k}{j 2\pi k}$$

**Step 5 — Sum the two integrals:**
$$X_k = I_1 + I_2 = \frac{2(1 - (-1)^k)}{j 2\pi k} = \frac{1 - (-1)^k}{j\pi k}$$

**Step 6 — Analyze the parity of $k$:**
- If $k$ is **even**: $(-1)^k = 1$, so $X_k = 0$.
- If $k$ is **odd**: $(-1)^k = -1$, so $X_k = 2/(j\pi k) = -j 2/(\pi k)$.

**Step 7 — Final compact form:**
$$X_k = \begin{cases} 0, & k \text{ even} \\ \dfrac{-j 2}{\pi k}, & k \text{ odd} \end{cases}$$

**Step 8 — Write the reconstructed signal:**
$$x(t) = \sum_{k \text{ odd}} \frac{-j 2}{\pi k} e^{jk\omega_0 t} = \frac{4}{\pi}\sum_{m=0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{2m+1}\sin\!\left((2m+1)\omega_0 t\right)$$

This is the famous **square wave Fourier Series**:
$$\boxed{x(t) = \frac{4}{\pi}\left[\sin(\omega_0 t) + \frac{1}{3}\sin(3\omega_0 t) + \frac{1}{5}\sin(5\omega_0 t) + \cdots\right]}$$

Only **odd harmonics** appear (half-wave symmetry) and amplitudes decay as $1/k$ (slow, but not as slow as a sawtooth).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Insight:** The square wave FS is the **single most repeated problem** in KTU 2024 ESE. Memorize the coefficients $X_k = -j 2/(\pi k)$ for odd $k$ and $X_k = 0$ for even $k$. This appears in nearly every KTU past paper.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Fourier Series — Analysis and Synthesis Block Diagram

The CTFS operates as a two-way transformation: **Analysis** (time → frequency) and **Synthesis** (frequency → time). In a robotics DSP pipeline, this is the fundamental first stage before any filtering or feature extraction.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Input Signal x of t"] --> B["Multiply by e to the power of minus jk omega 0 t"]
    B --> C["Integrate over one period T0"]
    C --> D["Divide by T0"]
    D --> E["Fourier Coefficient X of k"]
    E --> F["Store in array X array for k equals minus N to N"]
    F --> G["Frequency Domain Representation"]
    H["Frequency Domain Representation"] --> I["Multiply each X of k by e to the power of jk omega 0 t"]
    I --> J["Sum over k equals minus N to N"]
    J --> K["Reconstructed Signal x hat of t"]
```

### 4.2 Symmetry Decision Tree for Fast Coefficient Computation

Before performing any integration, traverse this decision tree. The correct branch will tell you exactly which coefficients are non-zero — saving enormous computation time in KTU exams.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Periodic Signal x of t"] --> B{"Is x of t even?"}
    B -- "Yes" --> C["b of n equals 0 for all n"]
    B -- "No" --> D{"Is x of t odd?"}
    D -- "Yes" --> E["a of n equals 0 for all n, a0 equals 0"]
    D -- "No" --> F{"Half wave symmetry?"}
    F -- "Yes" --> G["a of n and b of n equal 0 for even n"]
    F -- "No" --> H["Full integration required for all coefficients"]
    C --> I["Compute only a of n"]
    E --> J["Compute only b of n"]
    G --> K["Compute a of n or b of n for odd n only"]
    H --> L["Compute a of n and b of n for all n"]
```

### 4.3 Magnitude and Phase Spectrum Plot — Square Wave Example

The amplitude spectrum $\vert X_k \vert$ for the square wave derived in Section 3.4 is shown schematically below. Note the **sinc-like envelope** of $1/k$ decay and the **zero values at all even harmonics**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["k equals 1: amplitude 2 divided by pi equals 0.637"] --> B["k equals 3: amplitude 2 divided by 3 pi equals 0.212"]
    B --> C["k equals 5: amplitude 2 divided by 5 pi equals 0.127"]
    C --> D["k equals 7: amplitude 2 divided by 7 pi equals 0.091"]
    D --> E["k equals 9: amplitude 2 divided by 9 pi equals 0.071"]
    E --> F["...and so on, decaying as 1 over k"]
```

### 4.4 CTFS Analysis–Synthesis Functional Architecture in a Robotic DSP Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Robotic Sensor Signal x of t"] --> B["Analog Front End: Anti Alias Filter"]
    B --> C["Analog to Digital Converter ADC"]
    C --> D["Buffer N samples one period"]
    D --> E["FS Analysis: Compute X of k"]
    E --> F["Feature Extraction: dominant harmonics"]
    F --> G["Decision Block: classify fault or control action"]
    G --> H["FS Synthesis: Reconstruct reference waveform"]
    H --> I["Digital to Analog Converter DAC"]
    I --> J["Actuator Driver Output"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation of Figure 4.4:** This is the canonical signal-processing pipeline inside any modern robotic controller (e.g., a robot arm's velocity controller analyzing encoder feedback). The FS analysis step is where harmonics of the motor current are identified — odd harmonics indicate commutation switching, even harmonics indicate mechanical imbalance, etc.
