- ⭐ **Sampled Spectrum Formula:** Memorize $X_s(f) = (1/T_s) \sum_k X(f - kf_s)$ — this is tested directly.
- ⭐ **Three Sampling Types:** Impulse (theoretical), Natural (pulse train multiplication), Flat-Top (Sample-and-Hold).
- ⭐ **Aliasing is IRREVERSIBLE:** Once it occurs in sampling, no DSP can fix it. Always use AAF.
- ⭐ **Aliasing Formula:** $f_{alias} = \mid f_{in} - k f_s \mid$ — choose $k$ to make result in $[0, f_s/2]$.
- ⭐ **Anti-Aliasing Filter Placement:** MUST be **analog** and **before** the ADC (cannot be digital).
- ⭐ **Reconstruction:** Ideal LPF with gain $T_s$ and cutoff $f_s/2$ — followed by sinc-interpolation.
- ⭐ **Aperture Effect:** Flat-top sampling has $\text{sinc}(f\tau)$ distortion — must be equalized.
- ⭐ **Natural Sampling Coefficients:** $C_k = (\tau/T_s)\text{sinc}(kf_s\tau)$.
- ⭐ **Whittaker-Shannon Formula:** $x(t) = \sum_n x(nT_s) \text{sinc}[(t-nT_s)/T_s]$ — full marks for writing this.
- ⭐ **Oversampling (OSR > 1)** relaxes anti-aliasing filter design requirements and improves SNR.
- ⭐ **Robotics Context:** Always relate sampling rate to your sensor/actuator bandwidth (Nyquist = 2× bandwidth).
- ⭐ **Common Exam Trap:** When $f_s = 2f_m$ (exactly Nyquist), the guard band is zero — any practical filter non-ideality causes aliasing. Real systems use $f_s \gg 2f_m$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Aliasing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT402 - SIGNAL PROCESSING FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Aliasing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Aliasing** is the phenomenon in signal processing where a continuous-time (analog) signal is reconstructed incorrectly from its samples because the sampling frequency is **less than twice the highest frequency component** present in the original signal. This results in high-frequency components "masquerading" as lower-frequency components in the reconstructed signal, causing irreversible loss of information.

Mathematically, if a signal $x(t)$ contains a frequency component $f_m$ and is sampled at a sampling frequency $f_s$, then **aliasing occurs** when:

$$f_s < 2 f_m$$

The minimum acceptable sampling rate is known as the **Nyquist Rate** ($f_N$), and twice the maximum signal frequency is called the **Nyquist Frequency**.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Wagon Wheel" Effect

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Spinning Wheel in Movies**
>
> Imagine watching a Western movie where a stagecoach wheel rotates clockwise. If the camera captures **fewer than 2 frames per spoke-passing**, your brain interprets the wheel as rotating **counter-clockwise** (or standing still) — even though it is physically spinning forward. This perceptual illusion is **aliasing in the visual domain**.
>
> In signal processing, the same thing happens: a high-frequency sine wave, when sampled too slowly, "looks like" a much slower sine wave to the reconstruction system. The high frequency is **disguised (aliased)** as a lower frequency.

| Real-World Example | Domain | Cause of Aliasing |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Spinning wheels in films | Video | Frame rate < 2 × wheel rotation |
| Audio "whine" downsampling | Audio | $f_s < 2 f_{max}$ in track |
| Moiré patterns in photos | Imaging | Pixel grid vs. fine detail |
| Robotic LiDAR false returns | Robotics | Sensor sampling < scene change rate |

---

### 1.3 Why Aliasing Matters in Robotics

In robotics, sensors such as **encoders, LiDAR, IMUs, and cameras** all sample continuous physical phenomena. If the sampling strategy is poorly designed:
- A robot's wheel encoder may under-report rotation speed.
- A vision system may misinterpret a fast-moving conveyor belt.
- A digital controller may chase a **phantom frequency** that doesn't physically exist in the plant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Aliasing is a **foundational concept** for the design of digital control systems, ADC front-ends, and discrete-time filters used in robotic perception and actuation loops.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem (Foundation)

The **Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem** states that a bandlimited continuous-time signal $x(t)$ with maximum frequency $f_{max}$ can be **perfectly reconstructed** from its samples $x[n] = x(nT_s)$ **if and only if** the sampling frequency satisfies:

$$f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$$

Where:
- $f_s$ = Sampling frequency (Hz) = $1/T_s$
- $T_s$ = Sampling period (seconds)
- $f_{max}$ = Highest frequency component in the original signal

When this condition is **violated**, aliasing occurs and the original signal **cannot** be reconstructed from its samples.

---

### 2.2 Operational Breakdown — The "Why" and "How"

**Step 1 — Sampling in Time Domain**
Multiplication of $x(t)$ by an impulse train $p(t)$ of period $T_s$.

**Step 2 — Effect in Frequency Domain**
Convolution of $X(f)$ with another impulse train in frequency, producing **replicas of the spectrum** centered at multiples of $f_s$:

$$X_s(f) = f_s \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - k f_s)$$

**Step 3 — Critical Check**
If the spectral replicas **overlap** (i.e., $f_s < 2 f_{max}$), the overlapping region creates a **distorted, non-separable spectrum**.

**Step 4 — Reconstruction Failure**
A low-pass filter (theoretically ideal) can no longer isolate the baseband copy of $X(f)$ → **aliasing is irreversible**.

---

### 2.3 The Aliased Frequency Formula

> [!NOTE]
> When a true frequency $f$ is sampled at $f_s$ with $f_s < 2f$, the **apparent (aliased) frequency** $f_a$ observed after reconstruction is given by:

$$f_a = \left| f - N \cdot f_s \right|$$

Where $N = \text{round}\!\left( \dfrac{f}{f_s} \right)$ is the integer that brings $f_a$ into the **principal Nyquist interval** $\left[ 0, \dfrac{f_s}{2} \right]$.

**Equivalent folded form:**

$$f_a = \left| (f \mod f_s) - f_s \right| \quad \text{if folded, else} \quad f_a = (f \mod f_s)$$

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for KTU Board Examinations**

| # | Concept | Formula | Description |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Nyquist Rate | $f_N = 2 f_{max}$ | Minimum sampling rate to avoid aliasing |
| 2 | Sampling Frequency | $f_s = \dfrac{1}{T_s}$ | Inverse of sampling period |
| 3 | Nyquist Frequency | $f_{Nyq} = \dfrac{f_s}{2}$ | Highest uniquely representable frequency |
| 4 | Sampled Spectrum | $X_s(f) = f_s \displaystyle\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - k f_s)$ | Spectral replication |
| 5 | Aliased Frequency | $f_a = \left\vert f - N f_s \right\vert$ | Folded-back frequency |
| 6 | Reconstruction (Ideal) | $x(t) = \displaystyle\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] \cdot \text{sinc}\!\left( \dfrac{t - nT_s}{T_s} \right)$ | Whittaker–Shannon interpolation |
| 7 | Anti-Aliasing Filter | Bandlimit $x(t)$ to $f_c < f_s/2$ **before** sampling | Pre-emptive cure |
| 8 | Aliasing Condition | $f_s < 2 f_{max}$ | Defines the regime of distortion |

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | Application of Anti-Aliasing Principle |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Robotics** | Encoder decimation, IMU sampling rate selection |
| **Audio Engineering** | 44.1 kHz CD rate $\rightarrow$ aliases nothing audible (< 22.05 kHz) |
| **Medical Imaging** | MRI/MRA bandlimiting before ADC |
| **Telecommunications** | Bandpass sampling in software-defined radio |
| **Computer Vision** | Moiré suppression via optical low-pass filters |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Spectral Form of the Sampled Signal

**Starting Point:** Let the continuous-time signal $x(t)$ be sampled by an ideal impulse train:

$$p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - n T_s)$$

**Step 1 — Express the sampled signal:**

$$x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(n T_s) \, \delta(t - n T_s)$$

This means each sample becomes a weighted Dirac impulse located at $t = nT_s$.

**Step 2 — Take the Continuous-Time Fourier Transform (CTFT) of both sides.**  
Using the **multiplication property** of the CTFT: multiplication in time $\Longleftrightarrow$ convolution in frequency:

$$X_s(f) = X(f) * P(f)$$

**Step 3 — Compute the CTFT of the impulse train.**  
The Fourier transform of a periodic impulse train in time is another periodic impulse train in frequency, with impulses separated by $f_s$ and scaled by $1/T_s = f_s$:

$$P(f) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(f - k f_s) = f_s \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(f - k f_s)$$

**Step 4 — Apply the convolution.**  
Convolution with a shifted impulse simply shifts the function:

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_s(f) &= X(f) * \left[ f_s \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(f - k f_s) \right] \\[6pt]
X_s(f) &= f_s \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - k f_s)
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The spectrum of the sampled signal is a **periodic repetition** of the original spectrum, centered at every integer multiple of $f_s$, and scaled in amplitude by $f_s$.

**Step 5 — Condition for No Aliasing.**  
For perfect reconstruction, an ideal low-pass filter with cutoff $f_c = f_s / 2$ must be able to extract the **central replica** ($k=0$) cleanly. This requires the **spectral copies not to overlap**, i.e.:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{max} &< \frac{f_s}{2} \\[4pt]
\therefore \quad f_s &> 2 f_{max}
\end{aligned}
$$

If $f_s \leq 2 f_{max}$, the adjacent spectral copies **overlap**, producing an irreversible corruption called aliasing.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example: Aliased Frequency Calculation

> **Problem:** A pure sinusoid $x(t) = \cos(2\pi \cdot 370 \cdot t)$ is sampled at $f_s = 400$ Hz. Determine whether aliasing occurs and find the apparent frequency.

**Step 1 — Identify the signal frequency:**  
$f = 370$ Hz.

**Step 2 — Compute the Nyquist Rate:**  
$f_N = 2 f = 2 \times 370 = 740$ Hz.

**Step 3 — Compare to sampling frequency:**  
$f_s = 400$ Hz $< 740$ Hz $= f_N$.  
**→ Aliasing occurs.**

**Step 4 — Compute the aliased frequency $f_a$:**  
First, normalize $f$ by $f_s$:

$$N = \text{round}\!\left( \frac{370}{400} \right) = \text{round}(0.925) = 1$$

Then apply the aliasing formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_a &= \left| f - N f_s \right| \\[4pt]
    &= \left| 370 - (1)(400) \right| \\[4pt]
    &= \left| 370 - 400 \right| \\[4pt]
    &= 30 \; \text{Hz}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** A true 370 Hz signal, sampled at 400 Hz, **appears** as a 30 Hz sinusoid after reconstruction. The high frequency has been **folded** into the baseband.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: When Does Aliasing Happen?

| Regime | Condition | Behaviour | Recovery Possible? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :---: |
| **Oversampling** | $f_s > 2 f_{max}$ | Clean spectral copies, no overlap | ✅ Yes |
| **Critical Sampling** | $f_s = 2 f_{max}$ | Copies just touch at $f_s/2$ | ✅ Yes (theoretical) |
| **Undersampling (Aliasing)** | $f_s < 2 f_{max}$ | Spectral overlap, distortion | ❌ No |
| **Extreme Undersampling** | $f_s \ll f_{max}$ | Severe folding, noise-like | ❌ No |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Sampling System (with Anti-Aliasing)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Analog Input x of t] --> B[Anti-Aliasing LPF]
    B --> C[ADC Sampler at f sub s]
    C --> D[Digital Samples x of n]
    D --> E[Digital Signal Processor]
    E --> F[DAC Reconstructor]
    F --> G[Reconstruction LPF]
    G --> H[Analog Output y of t]
    subgraph GUARD[BANDLIMITING STAGE]
    B
    end
    subgraph SAMPLESTAGE[SAMPLING STAGE]
    C
    end
```

> **Read this diagram left-to-right:**  
> The **Anti-Aliasing LPF** is the **only** hardware/software defense against aliasing. It must bandlimit the analog input so that no frequency above $f_s/2$ reaches the ADC. Once the ADC samples, the analog anti-aliasing stage can no longer help.

---

### 4.2 Spectral Visualization — The Effect of Sampling Rate

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph A[Continuous Spectrum X of f]
    A1[Single lobe centered at 0]
    end
    subgraph B[Over-Sampled at f sub s equals 5 x f sub max]
    B1[Replicas at k=0, ±1, ±2]
    B2[Wide gaps — no overlap]
    end
    subgraph C[Critically Sampled at f sub s equals 2 x f sub max]
    C1[Replicas touching at f sub s slash 2]
    end
    subgraph D[Under-Sampled at f sub s less than 2 x f sub max]
    D1[Replicas overlap]
    D2[IRREVERSIBLE ALIASING]
    end
    A --> B
    A --> C
    A --> D
```

> **Visual takeaway:** As $f_s$ decreases, the spectral replicas move closer together. When the distance $f_s$ becomes smaller than the bandwidth $2 f_{max}$, the copies **collide** — this collision **is** aliasing.

---

### 4.3 Aliasing as Spectral "Folding"

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BELOW[Below f sub s slash 2]
    P1[True frequency f is between f sub s slash 2 and f sub s]
    end
    subgraph ABOVE[Above f sub s slash 2]
    P2[Mirror image f sub a folds down into baseband]
    end
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3[Observer sees f sub a as the true signal]
```

> **Concept:** Every frequency $f$ above $f_s/2$ has a "mirror" twin below $f_s/2$. The reconstruction process cannot tell them apart — both are valid interpolations of the same sample stream.

---

### 4.4 Robotics-Oriented Sampling Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Physical World: Robot Joint Motion] --> S2[Encoder Output: Continuous Angle]
    S2 --> S3[Anti-Aliasing Filter at f sub c]
    S3 --> S4[Microcontroller ADC or Timer Capture at f sub s]
    S4 --> S5[Discrete Samples theta of n]
    S5 --> S6[Digital Control Algorithm]
    S6 --> S7[Motor Command PWM]
    S7 --> S8[Actuator Response]
    S8 --> S1
```

> **KTU Note:** In a **digital PID controller for a robotic arm**, if the sampling rate of the encoder is too low, the controller will act on **aliased position data**, leading to **instability, jitter, or hunting oscillations**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]`
**Define aliasing. State the Nyquist criterion to avoid it.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Aliasing (1 Mark):** Aliasing is the phenomenon in which a continuous-time signal, when sampled at a rate lower than twice its highest frequency component, is reconstructed as a distorted signal in which high-frequency components appear as spurious lower-frequency components.
>
> **Nyquist Criterion (2 Marks):** To avoid aliasing, the sampling frequency $f_s$ must be at least twice the maximum frequency $f_{max}$ present in the signal, i.e., $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$. This minimum rate is called the **Nyquist Rate**.

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]`
**What is an anti-aliasing filter? Why is it placed before the ADC?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 Mark):** An anti-aliasing filter is a low-pass filter placed at the input of an analog-to-digital converter (ADC) to bandlimit the incoming analog signal so that no frequency component above $f_s/2$ reaches the sampler.
>
> **Reason for Placement (2 Marks):** Once the signal is sampled, the spectral content above $f_s/2$ folds back into the baseband and becomes inseparable from the true low-frequency content. Since the folding happens **at the sampling instant**, the only way to prevent aliasing is to remove high-frequency content **before** the ADC. Post-sampling filtering cannot undo aliasing.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the spectrum of a sampled signal $X_s(f)$ in terms of the original spectrum $X(f)$ and the sampling frequency $f_s$. Clearly state the condition for no aliasing. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A signal $x(t) = 5 \cos(200\pi t) + 3 \cos(500\pi t)$ is sampled at $f_s = 400$ Hz. Determine whether aliasing occurs. If yes, find the aliased frequencies. **(7 Marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution to (a)** — **7 Marks**

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Let the continuous-time signal be $x(t)$ and the sampling impulse train be $p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - nT_s)$, where $T_s = 1/f_s$.

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** The sampled signal is:

$$x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s) \, \delta(t - nT_s)$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Taking the CTFT and using the time-multiplication property (multiplication in time $\Leftrightarrow$ convolution in frequency):

$$X_s(f) = X(f) * P(f)$$

**Step 4 (2 Marks):** The CTFT of the impulse train is:

$$P(f) = f_s \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(f - k f_s)$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** Substituting and using the sifting property of impulses:

$$X_s(f) = f_s \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - k f_s)$$

**Step 6 (1 Mark):** **No-aliasing condition:** For non-overlapping spectral replicas, $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$, where $f_{max}$ is the highest frequency in $X(f)$.

---

##### **Model Solution to (b)** — **7 Marks**

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Identify the signal frequencies:

- Component 1: $200\pi t \Rightarrow f_1 = 100$ Hz
- Component 2: $500\pi t \Rightarrow f_2 = 250$ Hz

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Maximum signal frequency:

$$f_{max} = \max(100, 250) = 250 \; \text{Hz}$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Compute the Nyquist Rate:

$$f_N = 2 f_{max} = 2 \times 250 = 500 \; \text{Hz}$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Compare:

$$f_s = 400 \; \text{Hz} \; < \; 500 \; \text{Hz} = f_N \quad \Rightarrow \quad \textbf{Aliasing occurs.}$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** $f_1 = 100$ Hz $< f_s/2 = 200$ Hz, so **no aliasing** for this component. $f_a^{(1)} = 100$ Hz.

**Step 6 (2 Marks):** For $f_2 = 250$ Hz, compute $N$:

$$N = \text{round}\!\left( \frac{250}{400} \right) = \text{round}(0.625) = 1$$

$$f_a^{(2)} = \left| 250 - (1)(400) \right| = \left| -150 \right| = 150 \; \text{Hz}$$

**Result (1 Mark):** The 100 Hz component is preserved; the 250 Hz component appears as **150 Hz** in the reconstructed signal.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model — Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of aliasing with a suitable real-world example. Discuss the role of an anti-aliasing filter in a digital signal acquisition system. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A pure tone at 7 kHz is sampled at 6 kHz. Compute the apparent (aliased) frequency. Repeat the calculation if the sampling rate is changed to 10 kHz. Comment on the results. **(7 Marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution to (a)** — **7 Marks**

**Step 1 (2 Marks):** **Concept of Aliasing.** Aliasing is the distortion effect in which two distinct continuous signals become indistinguishable after sampling. It occurs when the sampling rate is below the Nyquist Rate. Mathematically, aliasing arises from spectral overlap of the periodically replicated spectra of the sampled signal.

**Step 2 (2 Marks):** **Real-world Example — Spinning Wheel in Cinema.** A wheel rotating at, say, 25 revolutions per second is filmed at 24 frames per second. The wheel appears to rotate at 1 revolution per second in the *reverse* direction. Here, the human visual system (acting as the reconstruction filter) misinterprets a high-frequency motion as a low-frequency one — this is aliasing in the temporal/visual domain.

**Step 3 (1.5 Marks):** **Role of Anti-Aliasing Filter.** An anti-aliasing filter is a low-pass analog filter placed **before** the ADC. It restricts the input signal bandwidth to $f_s/2$, ensuring that no frequency above the Nyquist frequency can fold back into the baseband. Typical designs use Butterworth or Chebyshev topologies with cutoff set to $0.45 f_s$ to $0.5 f_s$.

**Step 4 (1.5 Marks):** **Why placement matters.** Once the ADC samples, the analog input is already "sliced" into discrete points. High frequencies above $f_s/2$ that are still present at the sampling instant will fold instantly. Post-sampling digital filtering cannot separate the aliased component from the genuine baseband content because both now occupy the same frequency range.

---

##### **Model Solution to (b)** — **7 Marks**

**Case 1 — $f_s = 6$ kHz:**

- Signal: $f = 7$ kHz
- Nyquist Rate: $f_N = 2 \times 7 = 14$ kHz
- Since $6 < 14$ → **aliasing occurs**.

Compute $N$:

$$N = \text{round}\!\left( \frac{7}{6} \right) = \text{round}(1.167) = 1$$

$$f_a = \left| 7 - (1)(6) \right| = 1 \; \text{kHz}$$

> The 7 kHz tone is **heard** (or reconstructed) as a 1 kHz tone.

---

**Case 2 — $f_s = 10$ kHz:**

- Nyquist Rate: $f_N = 14$ kHz
- Since $10 < 14$ → **aliasing still occurs** (insufficient).

Compute $N$:

$$N = \text{round}\!\left( \frac{7}{10} \right) = \text{round}(0.7) = 1$$

$$f_a = \left| 7 - (1)(10) \right| = 3 \; \text{kHz}$$

> The 7 kHz tone now appears as 3 kHz.

---

**Comment (1 Mark):** In both cases aliasing is present (since $f_s < 14$ kHz), but the folded frequency differs based on $f_s$. The lower the sampling rate, the more aggressive the spectral folding. To recover the 7 kHz tone faithfully, the sampling rate must be raised to at least **14 kHz** (Nyquist Rate), and in practice, ≥ 1.5 × Nyquist = 21 kHz with an anti-aliasing filter.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2.** Students often confuse the Nyquist Rate ($2 f_{max}$) with the Nyquist Frequency ($f_s / 2$). These are *two different things* — read the question carefully.
> 2. **Skipping the aliasing check.** Before computing the aliased frequency, **always** verify whether aliasing actually occurs. If $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$, write "No aliasing — component is preserved at $f$ Hz."
> 3. **Round-off error in $N$.** Use `round()`, not `floor()`, when computing $N$ in the aliasing formula. Using `floor` gives the wrong folded frequency for signals close to multiples of $f_s/2$.
> 4. **Forgetting units.** Always attach Hz (or kHz, MHz) to frequencies — KTU evaluators deduct marks for unit-less numerical answers.
> 5. **Missing the anti-aliasing filter discussion.** Whenever a question involves ADC or sampling, **always mention** the role of the anti-aliasing LPF. Examiners award bonus marks for proactive system-level thinking.
> 6. **Confusing aliasing with quantization.** Aliasing is a **time-domain / frequency-domain** phenomenon; quantization is an **amplitude-domain** phenomenon. Do not mix them up in definitions.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**
>
> - **Aliasing** = irreversible distortion caused by sampling below the Nyquist Rate.
> - **Nyquist Rate** $f_N = 2 f_{max}$ — minimum sampling rate to avoid aliasing.
> - **Nyquist Frequency** $f_{Nyq} = f_s / 2$ — highest uniquely representable frequency.
> - **Sampled spectrum:** $X_s(f) = f_s \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - k f_s)$.
> - **Aliased frequency formula:** $f_a = \left\vert f - N f_s \right\vert$, where $N = \text{round}(f/f_s)$.
> - **Anti-aliasing filter** is an analog LPF placed **before** the ADC; cutoff $\leq f_s/2$.
> - **Reconstruction (Whittaker–Shannon):** uses sinc-interpolation — works only if no aliasing occurred.
> - **Real-world examples:** Wagon-wheel effect, Moiré patterns, audio whine, robotic encoder errors.
> - **In robotics:** Critical for encoder decimation, IMU sampling, vision frame rates, digital control loops.
> - **Aliasing is irreversible** — no amount of post-processing can recover lost information.
> - **CTFT convolution property** is the key mathematical engine behind the spectral replication derivation.
> - **Always verify** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ before claiming a sampled signal is faithfully recoverable.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Z Transform
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT402 - SIGNAL PROCESSING FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Z Transform

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Z-Transform** is a mathematical tool that converts a discrete-time signal $x(n)$ into a complex frequency-domain representation $X(z)$, where $z$ is a complex variable. It serves as the **discrete-time counterpart of the Laplace Transform** and is the primary analytical instrument for solving difference equations, designing digital filters, and analyzing discrete-time control systems — a foundational requirement in modern robotic signal processing pipelines.

The **Bilateral (Two-Sided) Z-Transform** of a discrete-time sequence $x(n)$ is formally defined as:

$$X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(n) \, z^{-n}$$

where $z$ is a complex variable expressed in polar form as $z = r e^{j\omega}$, with $r$ being the magnitude and $\omega$ the angular frequency in radians.

The **Unilateral (One-Sided) Z-Transform** is defined for causal sequences where $n \geq 0$:

$$X(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} x(n) \, z^{-n}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** For the PCRAT402 course, emphasis is placed on the **unilateral Z-transform** because real-time robotic systems are inherently causal — the controller cannot access future sensor readings. However, the bilateral form is essential for understanding the **Region of Convergence (ROC)**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Robot's Memory Lens"

Imagine a **robot arm equipped with a camera** that records the position of a moving object at discrete time intervals $n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$. Each position $x(n)$ is a number stored in the robot's memory.

- The **time-domain signal** $x(n)$ is like watching the object's position unfold moment by moment — you see "now," but you cannot easily see the *pattern*.
- The **Z-transform** is like placing a special **weighted magnifying lens** over the entire recorded history. Each memory $x(n)$ is multiplied by a complex weight $z^{-n}$, which acts as a tunable filter. As you rotate the lens (change $z$), different hidden patterns emerge — the **average behavior**, the **growth rate**, the **oscillation frequency**, and the **stability margins**.

In robotics, this "lens" is what allows a digital controller to:
1. Predict where the arm will be 5 samples from now (forecasting).
2. Determine whether the control loop will **blow up** (instability) or **settle** (stability).
3. Design filters that clean noisy sensor data from LIDAR or IMU inputs.

### 1.3 The Region of Convergence (ROC) — Where the Transform "Lives"

> [!NOTE]
> The ROC is the set of all values of $z$ in the complex plane for which the Z-transform summation **converges** to a finite value. It is *not* just a mathematical detail — it is the **single most important diagnostic** for determining system stability.

**Key ROC Rules for KTU Examinations:**

| Sequence Type | ROC Boundary |
|---|---|
| **Finite-duration** (causal) | Entire z-plane except $z = 0$ |
| **Finite-duration** (anti-causal) | Entire z-plane except $z = \infty$ |
| **Right-sided** (causal) $x(n) = 0, n<0$ | Exterior of a circle $\vert z \vert > R_1$ |
| **Left-sided** (anti-causal) $x(n) = 0, n \geq 0$ | Interior of a circle $\vert z \vert < R_2$ |
| **Two-sided** | Annular region $R_1 < \vert z \vert < R_2$ |
| **Stable system** (causal) | ROC **must include** the unit circle $\vert z \vert = 1$ |

Where $R_1$ and $R_2$ are real positive constants determined by the poles of $X(z)$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — Why Z-Transform Works in Robotics

The Z-transform can be understood as a sequence of logical operations that map a discrete signal into a **rational polynomial** in $z^{-1}$ (or $z$):

- **Step 1 — Sampling Indexing:** The discrete sequence $x(n)$ is enumerated at integer sample indices. In a robot, this corresponds to ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter) samples arriving every $T$ seconds.
- **Step 2 — Complex Weighting:** Each sample $x(n)$ is multiplied by $z^{-n}$. The factor $z^{-1}$ represents a **unit delay** of one sample period — physically, this is the delay between the controller's command and the next sensor reading.
- **Step 3 — Summation:** All weighted samples are summed. The Z-transform is essentially a **generalized Fourier series** with an exponential decay/growth envelope $r^{-n}$ (where $r = \vert z \vert$) that ensures convergence.
- **Step 4 — Convergence Testing:** Determine for which values of $\vert z \vert$ the geometric series converges. This defines the **ROC**.
- **Step 5 — Pole-Zero Mapping:** Factor the resulting polynomial to find **poles** (denominator roots) and **zeros** (numerator roots). Pole locations relative to the unit circle dictate stability.

### 2.2 Properties of the Z-Transform — KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a **high-yield, exam-critical** summary. Memorize the formulas, but more importantly, understand *why* each property exists physically (e.g., time shift = memory delay in a digital controller).

| Property | Time Domain | Z-Domain | ROC Modification |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Linearity** | $a\,x(n) + b\,y(n)$ | $a\,X(z) + b\,Y(z)$ | $\text{ROC} \supseteq \text{ROC}_x \cap \text{ROC}_y$ |
| **Time Shifting** | $x(n - k)$ | $z^{-k}\,X(z)$ | Same as $X(z)$, may add/remove $z=0$ or $z=\infty$ |
| **Time Reversal** | $x(-n)$ | $X(1/z)$ | Inverted: $1/R_2 < \vert z \vert < 1/R_1$ |
| **Multiplication by $a^n$** | $a^n x(n)$ | $X(z/a)$ | Scaled: $\vert a \vert R_1 < \vert z \vert < \vert a \vert R_2$ |
| **Multiplication by $n$** | $n\,x(n)$ | $-z \dfrac{dX(z)}{dz}$ | Same as $X(z)$ |
| **Convolution** | $x(n) * h(n)$ | $X(z) \cdot H(z)$ | $\text{ROC} \supseteq \text{ROC}_x \cap \text{ROC}_h$ |
| **Initial Value Theorem** | $x(0) = \lim_{z \to \infty} X(z)$ | Applies to causal sequences | — |
| **Final Value Theorem** | $\lim_{n \to \infty} x(n) = \lim_{z \to 1} (z-1) X(z)$ | Valid only if poles of $(z-1)X(z)$ are strictly **inside** the unit circle | — |
| **Accumulation** | $\sum_{k=0}^{n} x(k)$ | $\dfrac{z}{z-1} X(z)$ | $\text{ROC} \supseteq \text{ROC}_x \cap \vert z \vert > 1$ |

### 2.3 Standard Z-Transform Pairs — Master Reference Table

| $x(n)$ | $X(z)$ | ROC |
|---|---|---|
| $\delta(n)$ (unit impulse) | $1$ | All $z$ |
| $u(n)$ (unit step) | $\dfrac{1}{1 - z^{-1}} = \dfrac{z}{z-1}$ | $\vert z \vert > 1$ |
| $a^n\,u(n)$ (causal exponential) | $\dfrac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}} = \dfrac{z}{z-a}$ | $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$ |
| $-a^n\,u(-n-1)$ (anti-causal exponential) | $\dfrac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}} = \dfrac{z}{z-a}$ | $\vert z \vert < \vert a \vert$ |
| $n\,a^n\,u(n)$ | $\dfrac{a z^{-1}}{(1 - a z^{-1})^2}$ | $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$ |
| $a^{\vert n \vert}$ (two-sided) | $\dfrac{1 - a^2}{(1 - a z^{-1})(1 - a z)}$ | $a < \vert z \vert < 1/a$ |
| $\sin(\omega_0 n)\,u(n)$ | $\dfrac{z \sin(\omega_0)}{z^2 - 2z\cos(\omega_0) + 1}$ | $\vert z \vert > 1$ |
| $\cos(\omega_0 n)\,u(n)$ | $\dfrac{z(z - \cos(\omega_0))}{z^2 - 2z\cos(\omega_0) + 1}$ | $\vert z \vert > 1$ |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Digital Filter Design:** The Z-transform converts difference equations (the heart of any digital filter) into algebraic expressions. An FIR filter's frequency response is obtained by evaluating $H(e^{j\omega})$ — a special case of $H(z)$ on the unit circle.
- **Robotic Stability Analysis:** A discrete-time control loop (e.g., a PID controller running on a robot's microcontroller) is stable **if and only if all poles of its closed-loop transfer function lie strictly inside the unit circle** in the z-plane.
- **State-Space Modeling:** Modern robot localization (e.g., Kalman filters for SLAM) relies on Z-domain state-space matrices $A$, $B$, $C$, $D$ to predict and update system states.
- **Spectral Analysis:** Computing the Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (DTFT) is equivalent to evaluating the Z-transform on the unit circle: $\text{DTFT}\{x(n)\} = X(z)\big\vert_{z = e^{j\omega}}$.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Z-Transform of the Causal Exponential $a^n u(n)$

We start from the formal definition of the unilateral Z-transform and derive the most fundamental transform pair used in robotics (decaying/oscillating control responses).

**Step 1 — Write the definition with the given sequence.**

By the definition of the unilateral Z-transform:

$$X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(n)\, z^{-n} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a^n u(n)\, z^{-n}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the unit step property.**

Since $u(n) = 1$ for $n \geq 0$ and $u(n) = 0$ for $n < 0$, the summation reduces to:

$$X(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a^n z^{-n}$$

**Step 3 — Combine the bases.**

Recognize that $a^n z^{-n} = (a z^{-1})^n$:

$$X(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} (a z^{-1})^n$$

**Step 4 — Apply the geometric series formula.**

The sum $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} r^n$ converges to $\dfrac{1}{1-r}$ only when $\vert r \vert < 1$. Substituting $r = a z^{-1}$:

$$X(z) = \frac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}} = \frac{z}{z - a}$$

**Step 5 — Determine the ROC.**

Convergence requires $\vert a z^{-1} \vert < 1 \Rightarrow \vert a \vert < \vert z \vert$. Hence:

$$\boxed{\text{ROC}: \vert z \vert > \vert a \vert, \qquad X(z) = \frac{z}{z - a}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Meaning in Robotics:** If a robot's velocity error decays as $e(n) = 0.9^n$, then $a = 0.9$ and the system is stable because the pole at $z = 0.9$ lies **inside** the unit circle.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Z-Transform of $n\,a^n u(n)$ (Using the Differentiation Property)

This pair is critical for analyzing **ramp responses** of robotic actuators (e.g., a motor's velocity ramp-up).

**Step 1 — Start with the known transform.**

We already know from the previous derivation that:

$$a^n u(n) \;\longleftrightarrow\; \frac{z}{z - a}, \quad \vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$$

**Step 2 — Apply the multiplication-by-$n$ property.**

The property states: $n\,x(n) \;\longleftrightarrow\; -z\,\dfrac{dX(z)}{dz}$

**Step 3 — Differentiate $X(z)$ with respect to $z$.**

$$X(z) = \frac{z}{z - a} = z(z-a)^{-1}$$

Using the product rule:

$$\frac{dX(z)}{dz} = (z-a)^{-1} + z \cdot (-1)(z-a)^{-2} \cdot 1 = \frac{1}{z-a} - \frac{z}{(z-a)^2}$$

**Step 4 — Combine into a single fraction.**

$$\frac{dX(z)}{dz} = \frac{(z-a) - z}{(z-a)^2} = \frac{-a}{(z-a)^2}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the property formula.**

$$Z\{n\,a^n u(n)\} = -z \cdot \frac{-a}{(z-a)^2} = \frac{a z}{(z-a)^2}$$

$$\boxed{Z\{n\,a^n u(n)\} = \frac{a z}{(z-a)^2}, \quad \text{ROC}: \vert z \vert > \vert a \vert}$$

---

### 3.3 Exhaustive Worked Example: Inverse Z-Transform via Partial Fractions

**Problem:** Find the inverse Z-transform of:

$$X(z) = \frac{z^2 + z}{(z-1)(z-0.5)}, \quad \text{ROC}: \vert z \vert > 1$$

**Step 1 — Express $X(z)/z$ for partial fraction expansion.**

To use the standard partial-fraction table, divide $X(z)$ by $z$:

$$\frac{X(z)}{z} = \frac{z^2 + z}{z(z-1)(z-0.5)} = \frac{z + 1}{(z-1)(z-0.5)}$$

**Step 2 — Set up the partial fraction decomposition.**

$$\frac{X(z)}{z} = \frac{A}{z-1} + \frac{B}{z-0.5}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $A$ using the cover-up method.**

$$A = \left.\frac{z+1}{z-0.5}\right\vert_{z=1} = \frac{1+1}{1-0.5} = \frac{2}{0.5} = 4$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $B$ using the cover-up method.**

$$B = \left.\frac{z+1}{z-1}\right\vert_{z=0.5} = \frac{0.5+1}{0.5-1} = \frac{1.5}{-0.5} = -3$$

**Step 5 — Reconstruct $X(z)/z$.**

$$\frac{X(z)}{z} = \frac{4}{z-1} + \frac{-3}{z-0.5}$$

**Step 6 — Multiply through by $z$ to get $X(z)$.**

$$X(z) = \frac{4z}{z-1} - \frac{3z}{z-0.5}$$

**Step 7 — Apply the ROC to select the correct inverse pair.**

Since the ROC is $\vert z \vert > 1$, both terms correspond to **right-sided (causal)** sequences (the outermost pole magnitude is 1, so we are exterior to both poles). Using the pair $a^n u(n) \;\longleftrightarrow\; \dfrac{z}{z-a}$:

- $\dfrac{4z}{z-1} \;\longleftrightarrow\; 4 \cdot (1)^n u(n) = 4\,u(n)$
- $\dfrac{-3z}{z-0.5} \;\longleftrightarrow\; -3 \cdot (0.5)^n u(n)$

**Step 8 — Write the final inverse Z-transform.**

$$\boxed{x(n) = \left[4 - 3\,(0.5)^n\right] u(n)}$$

**Verification at $n = 0$:** $x(0) = 4 - 3 = 1$. Using initial value theorem: $\lim_{z \to \infty} X(z) = \lim_{z \to \infty} \dfrac{z^2 + z}{z^2 - 1.5z + 0.5} = 1$. ✓

**Verification at $n = 1$:** $x(1) = 4 - 1.5 = 2.5$. Long division check: $X(z) = 1 + 2.5 z^{-1} + \ldots$ ✓

---

### 3.4 Analytical Stability Test for a Robotic Control Loop

Consider a discrete-time robotic position controller with the closed-loop transfer function:

$$H(z) = \frac{0.5 z}{(z-0.8)(z-0.3)}$$

**Stability Determination:**
- Poles: $z_1 = 0.8$, $z_2 = 0.3$.
- Both magnitudes satisfy $\vert z \vert < 1$ (inside the unit circle).
- **Conclusion:** The system is **BIBO stable** (Bounded-Input Bounded-Output). Any bounded disturbance to the robot's position will decay to zero.

**Pole-Zero Interpretation in Robotics:**

| Pole Location | Physical Behavior |
|---|---|
| $\vert z \vert < 1$ (inside unit circle) | Stable, decaying response |
| $\vert z \vert = 1$ (on unit circle) | Marginally stable (sustained oscillation) |
| $\vert z \vert > 1$ (outside unit circle) | **Unstable** — robot arm oscillates violently or diverges |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Z-Transform in a Robotic Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Reference Trajectory r of n] --> B[Summing Junction]
    C[Sensor Feedback y of n] --> B
    B --> D[Error Signal e of n]
    D --> E["Digital Controller D z"]
    E --> F[Control Command u of n]
    F --> G["Robot Plant G z"]
    G --> H[Actuator Output y of n]
    H --> I[Z Domain Analysis]
    I --> J{Poles inside unit circle}
    J -->|Yes| K[Stable Operation]
    J -->|No| L[Instability Alarm]
    M[Z Transform Engine] --> I
    D -.-> M
    G -.-> M
    H -.-> M
```

### 4.2 Process Flow: Computing the Z-Transform of a Robotic Sensor Signal

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Continuous Time Signal x of t] --> P2[Analog to Digital Converter ADC]
    P2 --> P3[Discrete Sequence x of n]
    P3 --> P4[Multiply by z power -n]
    P4 --> P5[Sum over n from 0 to infinity]
    P5 --> P6{Series Converges}
    P6 -->|Yes| P7[Complex Function X of z]
    P6 -->|No| P8[ROC is empty transform does not exist]
    P7 --> P9[Factor into Poles and Zeros]
    P9 --> P10[Map to z Plane]
    P10 --> P11{All poles inside unit circle}
    P11 -->|Yes| P12[System is Stable]
    P11 -->|No| P13[System is Unstable redesign controller]
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Mapping: Time Domain vs Z-Domain in Robotics

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TD[Time Domain]
        A1[Joint Position x of n] --> A2[Velocity v of n]
        A2 --> A3[Acceleration a of n]
    end
    subgraph ZD[Z Domain]
        B1[X of z] --> B2[dX of z by dz]
        B2 --> B3[Second derivative wrt z]
    end
    A1 -.Z transform.-> B1
    A2 -.Differentiation property.-> B2
    A3 -.Higher order property.-> B3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The left side represents the **physical robotic variables** sampled at discrete instants. The right side represents their **mathematical transforms**. The dashed arrows indicate the direction of the Z-transform operator. The unit circle $\vert z \vert = 1$ in the z-plane is the **stability boundary** — analogous to the imaginary axis $j\omega$ in continuous-time systems.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the Z-transform of a discrete-time sequence $x(n)$. What is the significance of the Region of Convergence (ROC)?**

**Model Answer:**

The Z-transform of a discrete-time sequence $x(n)$ is defined as:

$$X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(n) z^{-n}$$

where $z$ is a complex variable.

**Significance of ROC:**
1. The ROC specifies the range of $z$-values for which the Z-transform **converges** to a finite value.
2. It determines whether the system is **stable** — a causal LTI system is stable if and only if its ROC includes the unit circle $\vert z \vert = 1$.
3. Two different sequences can have the **same $X(z)$ expression** but different ROCs (e.g., $a^n u(n)$ and $-a^n u(-n-1)$ both transform to $\dfrac{z}{z-a}$). The ROC uniquely identifies the sequence.
4. It is always bounded by the **poles** of $X(z)$.

**Marks Distribution:** Definition – 1 mark, ROC significance – 2 marks.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State and prove the time-shifting property of the Z-transform.**

**Model Answer:**

**Statement:** If $X(z) = Z\{x(n)\}$ with ROC $R$, then $Z\{x(n-k)\} = z^{-k} X(z)$, with ROC $R$ (except possibly $z = 0$ or $z = \infty$).

**Proof:**

$$Z\{x(n-k)\} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(n-k) z^{-n}$$

Let $m = n - k$, so $n = m + k$ and $dz = 1$:

$$= \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} x(m) z^{-(m+k)} = z^{-k} \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} x(m) z^{-m} = z^{-k} X(z)$$

**Physical Meaning:** A delay of $k$ samples in the time domain (e.g., a 3-sample processing lag in a robot's vision pipeline) corresponds to multiplication by $z^{-3}$ in the Z-domain.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q3(a). [KTU University Exam – June 2024] — 7 Marks**
**Find the Z-transform of the sequence $x(n) = n\,u(n)$. Also determine its ROC.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

We know that $u(n) \;\longleftrightarrow\; \dfrac{z}{z-1}$ with ROC $\vert z \vert > 1$.

Using the property: $n\,x(n) \;\longleftrightarrow\; -z\,\dfrac{dX(z)}{dz}$

$$\frac{d}{dz}\left(\frac{z}{z-1}\right) = \frac{(z-1) \cdot 1 - z \cdot 1}{(z-1)^2} = \frac{-1}{(z-1)^2}$$

Therefore:

$$Z\{n\,u(n)\} = -z \cdot \frac{-1}{(z-1)^2} = \frac{z}{(z-1)^2}$$

**ROC:** $\vert z \vert > 1$ (the pole at $z = 1$ has magnitude 1, so the ROC is the exterior region just beyond it).

$$\boxed{X(z) = \frac{z}{(z-1)^2}, \quad \text{ROC}: \vert z \vert > 1}$$

**Q3(b). [KTU University Exam – June 2024] — 7 Marks**
**Determine the inverse Z-transform of $X(z) = \dfrac{z}{(z-1)(z-2)}$, ROC: $\vert z \vert > 2$.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Express $X(z)/z$:**

$$\frac{X(z)}{z} = \frac{1}{(z-1)(z-2)}$$

**Step 2 — Partial fraction decomposition:**

$$\frac{1}{(z-1)(z-2)} = \frac{A}{z-1} + \frac{B}{z-2}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $A$ and $B$:**

$$A = \left.\frac{1}{z-2}\right\vert_{z=1} = \frac{1}{-1} = -1$$

$$B = \left.\frac{1}{z-1}\right\vert_{z=2} = \frac{1}{1} = 1$$

**Step 4 — Reconstruct $X(z)$:**

$$X(z) = -\frac{z}{z-1} + \frac{z}{z-2}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the ROC ($\vert z \vert > 2$) to select causal inverse pairs:**

Since the ROC is exterior to both poles, both terms correspond to right-sided sequences:

$$x(n) = \left[-1 \cdot (1)^n + 1 \cdot (2)^n\right] u(n) = \left(2^n - 1\right) u(n)$$

**Verification at $n = 0$:** $x(0) = 1 - 1 = 0$. Initial Value Theorem: $\lim_{z \to \infty} \dfrac{z}{z^2 - 3z + 2} = 0$. ✓

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q4(a). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] — 7 Marks**
**Find the Z-transform of $x(n) = (0.5)^n \cos\left(\dfrac{\pi n}{2}\right) u(n)$. Specify the ROC.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Express the cosine using Euler's formula:**

$$\cos\left(\frac{\pi n}{2}\right) = \frac{e^{j\pi n/2} + e^{-j\pi n/2}}{2}$$

Therefore:

$$x(n) = \frac{1}{2}\left[(0.5 e^{j\pi/2})^n + (0.5 e^{-j\pi/2})^n\right] u(n)$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Z-transform to each term separately:**

We use the pair $a^n u(n) \;\longleftrightarrow\; \dfrac{z}{z-a}$ with ROC $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$.

- For $(0.5 e^{j\pi/2})^n$: $a_1 = 0.5j$, so $\vert a_1 \vert = 0.5$.
- For $(0.5 e^{-j\pi/2})^n$: $a_2 = -0.5j$, so $\vert a_2 \vert = 0.5$.

$$X(z) = \frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{z}{z - 0.5j} + \frac{z}{z + 0.5j}\right]$$

**Step 3 — Combine the two fractions over a common denominator:**

$$X(z) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{z(z + 0.5j) + z(z - 0.5j)}{(z - 0.5j)(z + 0.5j)} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{2z^2}{z^2 + 0.25}$$

$$\boxed{X(z) = \frac{z^2}{z^2 + 0.25}, \quad \text{ROC}: \vert z \vert > 0.5}$$

**Q4(b). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] — 7 Marks**
**A discrete-time LTI system is described by the difference equation:**
$$y(n) - 0.5\,y(n-1) = x(n) + 0.4\,x(n-1)$$
**Find the transfer function $H(z)$ and determine whether the system is stable.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Take the unilateral Z-transform of both sides (assuming zero initial conditions):**

$$Y(z) - 0.5\,z^{-1} Y(z) = X(z) + 0.4\,z^{-1} X(z)$$

**Step 2 — Factor out $Y(z)$ and $X(z)$:**

$$Y(z)\,(1 - 0.5 z^{-1}) = X(z)\,(1 + 0.4 z^{-1})$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the transfer function $H(z) = Y(z)/X(z)$:**

$$H(z) = \frac{1 + 0.4 z^{-1}}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}} = \frac{z + 0.4}{z - 0.5}$$

**Step 4 — Identify the pole:**

The pole is at $z = 0.5$. Since $\vert 0.5 \vert = 0.5 < 1$, the pole lies **inside the unit circle**.

**Step 5 — Stability conclusion:**

The ROC is $\vert z \vert > 0.5$, which **includes the unit circle** $\vert z \vert = 1$. Therefore, the system is **stable**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting to include the ROC** when stating a Z-transform. The ROC is **mandatory**; without it, the answer is incomplete (lose 1 mark).
> 2. **Confusing causality with stability.** A causal system can be unstable (e.g., pole at $z = 1.5$). Conversely, an anti-causal system can be stable. Always check **both** the ROC and the pole locations.
> 3. **Arithmetic errors in the cover-up method** for partial fractions. Always cross-verify by recomposing: $A(z - 0.5) + B(z - 1)$ must equal the original numerator.
> 4. **Applying the Final Value Theorem without checking stability first.** The theorem is **only valid** if all poles of $(z-1)X(z)$ are strictly inside the unit circle. Applying it to an unstable system gives a meaningless finite number — full marks deducted.
> 5. **Sign errors in the differentiation property.** The correct formula is $Z\{n x(n)\} = -z\,dX(z)/dz$. A missing negative sign is one of the most common errors in KTU scripts.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** $X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(n) z^{-n}$. Unilateral version starts at $n = 0$.
- **ROC is mandatory** in every answer; never omit it. ROC is bounded by poles.
- **Stability rule:** For a **causal** LTI system, the system is stable **if and only if all poles lie strictly inside the unit circle** ($\vert z \vert < 1$).
- **Time shift:** $x(n-k) \leftrightarrow z^{-k} X(z)$ — represents sample delay in digital systems.
- **Convolution:** $x(n) * h(n) \leftrightarrow X(z) H(z)$ — foundation of digital filter design.
- **Differentiation property:** $n x(n) \leftrightarrow -z\,dX(z)/dz$ — used to find $n$-multiplied sequences.
- **Initial Value Theorem:** $x(0) = \lim_{z \to \infty} X(z)$ — quick sanity check for causal sequences.
- **Final Value Theorem:** $\lim_{n \to \infty} x(n) = \lim_{z \to 1} (z-1) X(z)$ — applies **only if** $(z-1)X(z)$ has all poles inside the unit circle.
- **Standard pairs to memorize:** $\delta(n) \leftrightarrow 1$, $u(n) \leftrightarrow \frac{z}{z-1}$, $a^n u(n) \leftrightarrow \frac{z}{z-a}$.
- **Inverse Z-transform methods:** Long division (for short sequences), Partial fractions (most common in KTU), and Residue method (for annular ROCs).
- **Same $X(z)$, different ROC = different signal.** Example: $\frac{z}{z-a}$ with $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$ gives $a^n u(n)$, but with $\vert z \vert < \vert a \vert$ gives $-a^n u(-n-1)$.
- **Pole-zero plot:** Zeros are marked with $\circ$, poles with $\times$. The unit circle $\vert z \vert = 1$ is the **stability boundary**.
- **Robotics application:** Every digital PID controller, Kalman filter, and discrete Fourier analyzer in a robot ultimately reduces to manipulating poles and zeros in the z-plane.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inverse Z Transform
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCRAT402 - SIGNAL PROCESSING FOR ROBOTICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Inverse Z Transform # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Inverse Z-Transform** is a mathematical recovery operator that converts a function defined in the complex Z-domain, $X(z)$, back into its corresponding discrete-time sequence, $x[n]$ (where $n \in \mathbb{Z}$). Formally, it is the inverse operation of the Z-transform, defined by the contour integral: $$x[n] = \frac{1}{2\pi j} \oint_C X(z) \, z^{n-1} \, dz$$ where $C$ is a closed contour encircling the origin and lying entirely within the **Region of Convergence (ROC)** of $X(z)$, traversed in the counter-clockwise direction. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The inverse Z-transform is essential in robotics for reconstructing discrete control commands (joint angles, velocities) from their pole-zero representations, particularly in z-plane controller design. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition Imagine a **musical recording studio**: - The **Z-Transform** is like a **mastering engineer** who compresses a song (the time-domain signal $x[n]$) into a compact, encoded format (the Z-domain function $X(z)$) that describes the song's "frequency fingerprint." - The **Inverse Z-Transform** is the **playback system** that takes that encoded fingerprint and *reconstructs* the original audio waveform, sample by sample. In robotics, the *encoding* often happens when we design digital controllers. The inverse Z-transform lets us determine what **actual sequence of motor voltages or actuator signals** we need to send at each time step, given the mathematical transfer function in the Z-domain. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** The Z-transform is **not unique** without its ROC. Two different time-domain signals can have the same $X(z)$ but different ROCs. Therefore, the ROC is *part of the complete specification* of the inverse Z-transform. ## 1.3 Standard Mathematical Notation | Symbol | Meaning | | :--- | :--- | | $x[n]$ | Discrete-time signal (output of inverse Z-transform) | | $X(z)$ | Z-domain representation of $x[n]$ | | $\mathcal{Z}^{-1}\{X(z)\}$ | The inverse Z-transform operator | | ROC | Region of Convergence in the Z-plane | ## 1.4 Physical Constants & Conventions - The Z-plane is a complex plane with **real part** $\sigma$ and **imaginary part** $j\Omega$. - The unit circle $\vert z \vert = 1$ corresponds to the frequency response of the system. - All sequences are assumed to be **causal** unless otherwise specified by the ROC. - **Causal signals** have ROCs that extend **outward** from the outermost pole. - **Anti-causal signals** have ROCs that extend **inward** from the innermost pole. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Why Is the Inverse Z-Transform Needed? In robotic control systems, transfer functions are typically designed in the Z-domain because: 1. **Stability analysis** becomes visual: a system is stable if all poles lie *inside* the unit circle. 2. **Convolution in time** becomes **multiplication in Z-domain**, simplifying controller design. 3. **Difference equations** (which describe discrete robotic behavior) become algebraic equations. However, to actually **implement** the controller on a microcontroller sending signals to motors, we need the inverse Z-transform to recover the time-domain recurrence relation or sequence. ## 2.2 The Four Standard Methods of Computing the Inverse Z-Transform ### Method 1: Long Division (Power Series Expansion) If $X(z)$ is a rational function, divide the numerator polynomial by the denominator polynomial to obtain a power series in $z^{-1}$ (for causal signals) or $z$ (for anti-causal signals). The coefficients of the series are the values of $x[n]$. **When to use:** When the function is not easily factorable, or when only the first few samples are required. ### Method 2: Partial Fraction Expansion Express $X(z)$ as a sum of simpler fractions whose inverse Z-transforms are tabulated. This is the **most commonly used method** in KTU examinations. **Procedure:** 1. Express $\frac{X(z)}{z}$ as a partial fraction (this is a standard trick to handle the $z^{-1}$ term in standard pairs). 2. Multiply each term by $z$. 3. Use the standard inverse Z-transform pairs. 4. Apply the ROC to choose between causal and anti-causal forms. ### Method 3: Residue (Contour Integration) Method Evaluate the contour integral using the **Cauchy's Residue Theorem**. This is the most rigorous method and is required when partial fractions are difficult. $$x[n] = \sum \text{Residues of } \big[ X(z) \, z^{n-1} \big] \text{ at poles inside } C$$ ### Method 4: Inspection / Direct Method For simple expressions, identify the form and match it against standard Z-transform pairs (e.g., if $X(z) = \frac{z}{z-a}$, then $x[n] = a^n u[n]$). ## 2.3 Key Properties of the Region of Convergence (ROC) | Property | Description | | :--- | :--- | | ROC is a **ring or disk** in the z-plane | Bounded by poles, never includes any pole | | Causal signal | ROC is $\vert z \vert > r_{\max}$ (exterior of outermost pole) | | Anti-causal signal | ROC is $\vert z \vert < r_{\min}$ (interior of innermost pole) | | Two-sided signal | ROC is a ring: $r_1 < \vert z \vert < r_2$ | | Stable system | ROC must include the unit circle $\vert z \vert = 1$ | | Finite-duration signal | ROC is the entire z-plane except possibly $z=0$ or $z=\infty$ | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** In robotics, ROCs help determine whether a discrete controller is **stable** (all poles inside the unit circle) and **causal** (ROC extends to infinity). This is critical for real-time robot control where non-causal systems cannot be implemented. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Z-Domain Function $X(z)$ | Time-Domain Signal $x[n]$ | ROC | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | $1$ | $\delta[n]$ | All $z$ | | 2 | $z^{-n_0}$ | $\delta[n - n_0]$ | All $z$ except possibly $z=0$ or $z=\infty$ | | 3 | $\frac{z}{z - a}$ | $a^n u[n]$ | $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$ | | 4 | $\frac{z}{z - a}$ | $-a^n u[-n-1]$ | $\vert z \vert < \vert a \vert$ | | 5 | $\frac{z}{(z-a)^2}$ | $n \, a^{n-1} u[n]$ | $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$ | | 6 | $\frac{z^2}{(z-a)^2}$ | $(n+1) \, a^n u[n]$ | $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$ | | 7 | $\frac{z}{(z-a)^k}$ | $\frac{1}{(k-1)!} n(n-1)\cdots(n-k+2) \, a^{n-k+1} u[n]$ | $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$ | | 8 | $\frac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}}$ | $a^n u[n]$ | $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$ | | 9 | $\frac{z}{(z-a)(z-b)}$ | $\frac{a^{n+1} - b^{n+1}}{a-b} u[n]$ | $\vert z \vert > \max(\vert a \vert, \vert b \vert)$ | | 10 | $\frac{1}{(1 - a z^{-1})^2}$ | $(n+1) a^n u[n]$ | $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examination Tip:** When you see $\frac{X(z)}{z}$ in the problem, always do partial fraction expansion on $\frac{X(z)}{z}$ first, then multiply back by $z$ before applying the inverse transform. This avoids confusion with $z^{-1}$ terms in standard pairs. ## 2.5 Standard Residue Formula For a simple pole at $z = p_k$: $$\text{Res}_{z=p_k} \big[ X(z) z^{n-1} \big] = \lim_{z \to p_k} \big[ (z - p_k) X(z) z^{n-1} \big]$$ For a pole of order $m$ at $z = p_k$: $$\text{Res}_{z=p_k} \big[ X(z) z^{n-1} \big] = \frac{1}{(m-1)!} \lim_{z \to p_k} \frac{d^{m-1}}{dz^{m-1}} \big[ (z - p_k)^m X(z) z^{n-1} \big]$$ ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility In robotic systems, the inverse Z-transform is used to: 1. **Convert digital filter transfer functions** into difference equations for microcontroller implementation. 2. **Recover the impulse response** of a discrete controller for stability analysis. 3. **Generate trajectory sequences** for robotic joints from Z-domain representations. 4. **Design state-space observers** for sensor fusion in autonomous robots. 5. **Implement Kalman filters** for state estimation in SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Inverse Z-Transform Formula Starting from the definition of the Z-transform: $$X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] z^{-n}$$ Multiply both sides by $z^{k-1}$ and integrate over a closed contour $C$ in the ROC: $$\oint_C X(z) z^{k-1} dz = \oint_C \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] z^{-n} z^{k-1} dz$$ Interchange the integral and summation (valid inside the ROC): $$\oint_C X(z) z^{k-1} dz = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] \oint_C z^{k-1-n} dz$$ Apply **Cauchy's Integral Theorem**, which states: $$\oint_C z^{m-1} dz = \begin{cases} 2\pi j & \text{if } m = 0 \\ 0 & \text{if } m \neq 0 \end{cases}$$ Therefore, the integral is non-zero only when $k - 1 - n = 0$, i.e., when $n = k$: $$\oint_C X(z) z^{k-1} dz = x[k] \cdot 2\pi j$$ Rearranging gives the **Inverse Z-Transform Formula**: $$x[n] = \frac{1}{2\pi j} \oint_C X(z) z^{n-1} dz$$ ## 3.2 Worked Example 1: Long Division Method **Problem:** Find the inverse Z-transform of $X(z) = \frac{1}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}}$ for $n \geq 0$. **Step 1:** Write the function in polynomial form by long division. $$X(z) = \frac{1}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}} = 1 + 0.5 z^{-1} + 0.25 z^{-2} + 0.125 z^{-3} + \cdots$$ **Step 2:** Perform the long division explicitly. ``` 1 + 0.5 z^-1 + 0.25 z^-2 + ... _______________________________________ 1 - 0.5 z^-1 | 1 1 - 0.5 z^-1 ----------- 0.5 z^-1 0.5 z^-1 - 0.25 z^-2 ------------------- 0.25 z^-2 0.25 z^-2 - 0.125 z^-3 --------------------- 0.125 z^-3 ... ``` **Step 3:** Identify the coefficients of $z^{-n}$ as $x[n]$. $$x[0] = 1, \quad x[1] = 0.5, \quad x[2] = 0.25, \quad x[3] = 0.125, \ldots$$ **Step 4:** Recognize the pattern. $$x[n] = (0.5)^n u[n]$$ ## 3.3 Worked Example 2: Partial Fraction Method (Full Derivation) **Problem:** Find the inverse Z-transform of $X(z) = \frac{z^2}{(z-1)(z-0.5)}$ with ROC $\vert z \vert > 1$. **Step 1:** Form $\frac{X(z)}{z}$ to simplify the standard pairs. $$\frac{X(z)}{z} = \frac{z}{(z-1)(z-0.5)}$$ **Step 2:** Apply partial fraction expansion. $$\frac{z}{(z-1)(z-0.5)} = \frac{A}{z-1} + \frac{B}{z-0.5}$$ **Step 3:** Solve for $A$ by covering up the $(z-1)$ term and substituting $z = 1$. $$A = \left. \frac{z}{z-0.5} \right|_{z=1} = \frac{1}{1-0.5} = \frac{1}{0.5} = 2$$ **Step 4:** Solve for $B$ by covering up the $(z-0.5)$ term and substituting $z = 0.5$. $$B = \left. \frac{z}{z-1} \right|_{z=0.5} = \frac{0.5}{0.5-1} = \frac{0.5}{-0.5} = -1$$ **Step 5:** Reconstruct $X(z)$ by multiplying by $z$. $$X(z) = \frac{2z}{z-1} - \frac{z}{z-0.5}$$ **Step 6:** Apply the inverse Z-transform using standard pairs (since ROC is $\vert z \vert > 1$, we use causal form). $$x[n] = 2(1)^n u[n] - (0.5)^n u[n]$$ $$x[n] = \big(2 - 0.5^n \big) u[n]$$ **Verification:** At $n=0$: $x[0] = 2 - 1 = 1$. At $n=1$: $x[1] = 2 - 0.5 = 1.5$. We can verify these values using long division of $X(z)$ for confirmation. ## 3.4 Worked Example 3: Residue Method (Full Derivation) **Problem:** Find the inverse Z-transform of $X(z) = \frac{z^2}{(z-1)(z-2)}$ for ROC $\vert z \vert > 2$. **Step 1:** Set up the contour integral. $$x[n] = \frac{1}{2\pi j} \oint_C \frac{z^2}{(z-1)(z-2)} z^{n-1} dz = \frac{1}{2\pi j} \oint_C \frac{z^{n+1}}{(z-1)(z-2)} dz$$ **Step 2:** Identify the poles of the integrand $F(z) = \frac{z^{n+1}}{(z-1)(z-2)}$. - Simple pole at $z = 1$ - Simple pole at $z = 2$ **Step 3:** Since ROC is $\vert z \vert > 2$, the contour $C$ encloses both poles. **Step 4:** Compute residue at $z = 1$. $$R_1 = \lim_{z \to 1} (z-1) \cdot \frac{z^{n+1}}{(z-1)(z-2)} = \frac{1^{n+1}}{1-2} = -1$$ **Step 5:** Compute residue at $z = 2$. $$R_2 = \lim_{z \to 2} (z-2) \cdot \frac{z^{n+1}}{(z-1)(z-2)} = \frac{2^{n+1}}{2-1} = 2^{n+1}$$ **Step 6:** Apply the Residue Theorem. $$x[n] = R_1 + R_2 = -1 + 2^{n+1} = 2^{n+1} - 1 \quad \text{for } n \geq 0$$ **Final Answer:** $$x[n] = (2^{n+1} - 1) u[n]$$ ## 3.5 Worked Example 4: Anti-Causal Sequence (ROC Inside) **Problem:** Find the inverse Z-transform of $X(z) = \frac{z}{z - 0.8}$ with ROC $\vert z \vert < 0.8$. **Step 1:** Note that the ROC is **inside** the pole, so the sequence is **anti-causal** (left-sided). **Step 2:** Use the anti-causal standard pair: $$\mathcal{Z}^{-1}\left\{\frac{z}{z - a}\right\} = -a^n u[-n-1] \quad \text{for } \vert z \vert < \vert a \vert$$ **Step 3:** Apply with $a = 0.8$: $$x[n] = -(0.8)^n u[-n-1]$$ **Step 4:** Expand to verify the values. $$x[-1] = -(0.8)^{-1} = -1.25, \quad x[-2] = -(0.8)^{-2} = -1.5625, \quad \ldots$$ **Step 5:** Verify using long division in powers of $z$ (anti-causal form). $$X(z) = \frac{z}{z - 0.8} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.8 z^{-1}}$$ For anti-causal expansion, we write in powers of $z$: $$X(z) = -1.25 z - 1.5625 z^2 - 1.953125 z^3 - \cdots$$ The coefficients match $x[-1], x[-2], x[-3], \ldots$ respectively. ✓ ## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Method | Method | Best Used When | Advantages | Limitations | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Long Division | Non-factorable polynomials, need only first few samples | No algebraic manipulation required, works for any form | Gives only numerical sequence, not closed form | | Partial Fraction | Rational functions with simple poles | Closed-form solution, matches standard pairs | Fails for repeated poles (needs modification) | | Residue Method | Complex functions or repeated poles | Most rigorous, handles any pole order | Computationally intensive | | Inspection | Simple forms matching standard pairs | Fastest method | Limited to standard table forms | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Pattern:** For 14-mark problems, the partial fraction method is the most commonly tested. For 3-mark problems, students are often asked to identify the ROC type (causal vs. anti-causal) or apply a single standard pair. ## 3.7 ROC Analysis for a Two-Sided Sequence **Problem:** Determine the ROC of $X(z) = \frac{z}{(z-0.5)(z-2)}$ if $x[n] = 0.5^n u[n] - 2^n u[-n-1]$. **Step 1:** The first term $0.5^n u[n]$ has ROC $\vert z \vert > 0.5$. **Step 2:** The second term $-2^n u[-n-1]$ has ROC $\vert z \vert < 2$. **Step 3:** The intersection of the two ROCs is the ROC for the combined sequence. $$\text{ROC}: 0.5 < \vert z \vert < 2$$ This is an **annular (ring-shaped) ROC**, which is characteristic of two-sided sequences. > [!NOTE] > **Stability Check:** Since the unit circle $\vert z \vert = 1$ lies within the ROC $0.5 < \vert z \vert < 2$, the system is **stable**. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Conceptual Flow: From Z-Domain to Time-Domain ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA["Input: X of z in Z-domain
with given ROC"] --> nodeB{"Choose Method"} nodeB --> nodeC["Long Division Method"] nodeB --> nodeD["Partial Fraction Method"] nodeB --> nodeE["Residue Method"] nodeB --> nodeF["Direct Inspection"] nodeC --> nodeG["Perform polynomial division
in powers of z to the power minus n"] nodeD --> nodeH["Express X of z divided by z
as sum of simple fractions"] nodeE --> nodeI["Identify poles inside contour C
Apply Cauchy Residue Theorem"] nodeF --> nodeJ["Match against standard
Z-transform table"] nodeG --> nodeK["Read coefficients
as x of n"] nodeH --> nodeL["Multiply each term by z
Apply standard inverse pairs"] nodeI --> nodeM["Sum all residues
to obtain x of n"] nodeJ --> nodeK nodeL --> nodeN["Apply ROC to select
causal or anti-causal form"] nodeN --> nodeO["Output: Discrete sequence x of n"] nodeK --> nodeO nodeM --> nodeO ``` ## 4.2 Method Selection Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD start["Given X of z and ROC"] --> check1{"Is X of z a
simple rational
function?"} check1 -->|"Yes"| check2{"Are poles simple
and distinct?"} check1 -->|"No"| longDiv["Use Long Division Method"] check2 -->|"Yes"| pfe["Use Partial Fraction Expansion"] check2 -->|"No, repeated poles"| residue["Use Residue Method"] pfe --> output["Output: Closed form x of n"] residue --> output longDiv --> output2["Output: Numerical x of n"] output --> rocCheck["Verify ROC selection:
Causal: z greater than outermost pole
Anti-causal: z less than innermost pole"] output2 --> rocCheck rocCheck --> final["Final discrete-time sequence"] ``` ## 4.3 ROC Visualization Map ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph causalROC["Causal Signal ROC: z greater than R"] r1["Pole at R"] --> region1["Exterior Region
extends to infinity"] end subgraph anticausalROC["Anti-causal Signal ROC: z less than R"] r2["Pole at R"] --> region2["Interior Region
includes origin"] end subgraph twosidedROC["Two-Sided Signal ROC: R1 less than z less than R2"] poleA["Pole at R1"] --> ring["Annular Region
between poles"] poleB["Pole at R2"] --> ring end unitCircle["Unit Circle: z equals 1
Stability boundary"] -.->|"Passes through"| causalROC unitCircle -.->|"Encloses"| anticausalROC unitCircle -.->|"Inside ring means stable"| twosidedROC ``` ## 4.4 Block Diagram: Robotic Controller Implementation Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR spec["Robot Control Specification
in Z-domain"] --> tf["Transfer Function X of z"] tf --> roc["Identify Region of Convergence"] roc --> invZT["Apply Inverse Z-Transform"] invZT --> diffEq["Difference Equation:
y of n equals sum of coefficients times y of n minus k
plus input terms"] diffEq --> code["Microcontroller Code:
C or Python on embedded board"] code --> motor["Motor Driver Signal
x of n volts at time nT"] motor --> robot["Robot Actuator Movement"] robot --> sensor["Sensor Feedback y of n"] sensor -->|"Closed loop"| spec ``` ## 4.5 Pole-Zero and ROC Relationship ```mermaid flowchart TD pzplot["Pole-Zero Plot in Z-plane"] --> p1["Cross X = Pole"] pzplot --> z1["Circle O = Zero"] p1 --> r1["Pole at radius r1"] p1 --> r2["Pole at radius r2"] r1 --> caseA["Case 1: Causal
ROC: z greater than max r1, r2
Includes z equals infinity"] r2 --> caseA r1 --> caseB["Case 2: Anti-causal
ROC: z less than min r1, r2
Includes z equals 0"] r2 --> caseB r1 --> caseC["Case 3: Two-sided
ROC: r1 less than z less than r2
Annular region"] r2 --> caseC caseA --> stabA["Stability: Requires r1 and r2 less than 1"] caseB --> stabB["Stability: Requires r1 and r2 greater than 1"] caseC --> stabC["Stability: Requires r1 less than 1 less than r2"] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define the inverse Z-transform. State the conditions under which the inverse Z-transform is unique.** **Model Answer:** The **inverse Z-transform** of $X(z)$ is the process of recovering the discrete-time sequence $x[n]$ from its Z-domain representation. It is defined by the contour integral: $$x[n] = \frac{1}{2\pi j} \oint_C X(z) z^{n-1} dz$$ **Conditions for uniqueness:** The inverse Z-transform is unique if and only if the **Region of Convergence (ROC)** is specified along with $X(z)$. Two different sequences can have identical $X(z)$ but different ROCs (e.g., a causal and anti-causal exponential with the same base value). Therefore, the ROC must be a part of the complete specification. --- ### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **State any four standard properties of the Region of Convergence (ROC) of the Z-transform.** **Model Answer:** 1. The ROC is a **ring or disk** in the z-plane, bounded by poles and never containing any pole. 2. For a **causal sequence**, the ROC extends outward from the outermost pole to infinity ($\vert z \vert > r_{\max}$). 3. For an **anti-causal sequence**, the ROC extends inward from the innermost pole to the origin ($\vert z \vert < r_{\min}$). 4. The ROC must include the **unit circle** ($\vert z \vert = 1$) for the system to be **stable**. 5. The ROC cannot include the point $z = 0$ for a causal sequence, nor $z = \infty$ for an anti-causal sequence. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** Find the inverse Z-transform of $X(z) = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.5 z^{-1})(1 - z^{-1})}$ with ROC $\vert z \vert > 1$. Use the partial fraction method. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Determine the inverse Z-transform of $X(z) = \frac{z}{(z-1)^2}$ for $\vert z \vert > 1$ using the residue method. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution for (a): **Step 1:** Form $\frac{X(z)}{z}$. $$\frac{X(z)}{z} = \frac{1}{z(1 - 0.5 z^{-1})(1 - z^{-1})}$$ **Step 2:** Convert to standard form in $z$. $$\frac{X(z)}{z} = \frac{z}{(z-0.5)(z-1) \cdot z} = \frac{1}{(z-0.5)(z-1)}$$ **Step 3:** Apply partial fraction expansion. $$\frac{1}{(z-0.5)(z-1)} = \frac{A}{z-0.5} + \frac{B}{z-1}$$ **Step 4:** Solve for $A$ by setting $z = 0.5$. $$A = \frac{1}{0.5 - 1} = \frac{1}{-0.5} = -2$$ **Step 5:** Solve for $B$ by setting $z = 1$. $$B = \frac{1}{1 - 0.5} = \frac{1}{0.5} = 2$$ **Step 6:** Reconstruct $X(z)$ by multiplying by $z$. $$X(z) = \frac{-2z}{z-0.5} + \frac{2z}{z-1}$$ **Step 7:** Apply the causal inverse Z-transform (since ROC $\vert z \vert > 1$). $$x[n] = -2(0.5)^n u[n] + 2(1)^n u[n] = 2(1 - 0.5^n) u[n]$$ **Final Answer for (a):** $$x[n] = 2(1 - 0.5^n) u[n]$$ --- #### Model Solution for (b): **Step 1:** Set up the contour integral. $$x[n] = \frac{1}{2\pi j} \oint_C \frac{z}{(z-1)^2} z^{n-1} dz = \frac{1}{2\pi j} \oint_C \frac{z^n}{(z-1)^2} dz$$ **Step 2:** Identify the poles. The function $F(z) = \frac{z^n}{(z-1)^2}$ has a **double pole** at $z = 1$. **Step 3:** Since ROC is $\vert z \vert > 1$, the contour encloses $z = 1$. Apply the residue formula for a pole of order $m = 2$. $$R = \frac{1}{(2-1)!} \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{d}{dz} \left[ (z-1)^2 \cdot \frac{z^n}{(z-1)^2} \right] = \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{d}{dz}(z^n)$$ **Step 4:** Compute the derivative. $$\frac{d}{dz}(z^n) = n z^{n-1}$$ **Step 5:** Evaluate at $z = 1$. $$R = n \cdot 1^{n-1} = n$$ **Step 6:** Apply the Residue Theorem. $$x[n] = n \cdot u[n]$$ **Final Answer for (b):** $$x[n] = n \, u[n]$$ **Verification using standard pair:** From the table, $\mathcal{Z}\{n \, u[n]\} = \frac{z}{(z-1)^2}$ for $\vert z \vert > 1$. ✓ --- ### Question B: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Using the long division method, find the first four terms of the inverse Z-transform of $X(z) = \frac{1}{1 - 0.8 z^{-1} + 0.15 z^{-2}}$ for $\vert z \vert > 0.5$. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Using the residue method, determine the inverse Z-transform of $X(z) = \frac{z^2 + z}{(z-1)(z-2)(z-3)}$ for $\vert z \vert > 3$. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution for (a): **Step 1:** Set up the long division. We need to divide the numerator (1) by the denominator $(1 - 0.8 z^{-1} + 0.15 z^{-2})$. **Step 2:** Perform the division. Multiply the divisor by $1$: $$1 \times (1 - 0.8 z^{-1} + 0.15 z^{-2}) = 1 - 0.8 z^{-1} + 0.15 z^{-2}$$ Subtract from the dividend: $$1 - (1 - 0.8 z^{-1} + 0.15 z^{-2}) = 0.8 z^{-1} - 0.15 z^{-2}$$ **Step 3:** Continue dividing. Multiply the divisor by $0.8 z^{-1}$: $$0.8 z^{-1} \times (1 - 0.8 z^{-1} + 0.15 z^{-2}) = 0.8 z^{-1} - 0.64 z^{-2} + 0.12 z^{-3}$$ Subtract: $$(0.8 z^{-1} - 0.15 z^{-2}) - (0.8 z^{-1} - 0.64 z^{-2} + 0.12 z^{-3}) = 0.49 z^{-2} - 0.12 z^{-3}$$ **Step 4:** Next term. Multiply the divisor by $0.49 z^{-2}$: $$0.49 z^{-2} \times (1 - 0.8 z^{-1} + 0.15 z^{-2}) = 0.49 z^{-2} - 0.392 z^{-3} + 0.0735 z^{-4}$$ Subtract: $$(0.49 z^{-2} - 0.12 z^{-3}) - (0.49 z^{-2} - 0.392 z^{-3} + 0.0735 z^{-4}) = 0.272 z^{-3} - 0.0735 z^{-4}$$ **Step 5:** Next term. Multiply the divisor by $0.272 z^{-3}$: $$0.272 z^{-3} \times (1 - 0.8 z^{-1}) = 0.272 z^{-3} - 0.2176 z^{-4}$$ **Step 6:** Collect the coefficients. $$X(z) = 1 + 0.8 z^{-1} + 0.49 z^{-2} + 0.272 z^{-3} + \cdots$$ **Final Answer for (a):** $$x[0] = 1, \quad x[1] = 0.8, \quad x[2] = 0.49, \quad x[3] = 0.272$$ **Note:** The recurrence relation is $x[n] = 0.8 x[n-1] - 0.15 x[n-2]$ for $n \geq 2$, with $x[0] = 1$ and $x[1] = 0.8$. --- #### Model Solution for (b): **Step 1:** Set up the contour integral. $$x[n] = \frac{1}{2\pi j} \oint_C \frac{z^2 + z}{(z-1)(z-2)(z-3)} z^{n-1} dz = \frac{1}{2\pi j} \oint_C \frac{(z^2 + z) z^{n-1}}{(z-1)(z-2)(z-3)} dz$$ **Step 2:** Identify the poles: $z = 1$, $z = 2$, $z = 3$ (all simple poles). **Step 3:** Since ROC is $\vert z \vert > 3$, the contour encloses **all three poles**. **Step 4:** Compute residue at $z = 1$. $$R_1 = \lim_{z \to 1} (z-1) \cdot \frac{(z^2 + z) z^{n-1}}{(z-1)(z-2)(z-3)} = \frac{(1 + 1) \cdot 1^{n-1}}{(1-2)(1-3)} = \frac{2}{(-1)(-3)} = \frac{2}{3}$$ **Step 5:** Compute residue at $z = 2$. $$R_2 = \lim_{z \to 2} (z-2) \cdot \frac{(z^2 + z) z^{n-1}}{(z-1)(z-2)(z-3)} = \frac{(4 + 2) \cdot 2^{n-1}}{(2-1)(2-3)} = \frac{6 \cdot 2^{n-1}}{(1)(-1)} = -6 \cdot 2^{n-1} = -3 \cdot 2^n$$ **Step 6:** Compute residue at $z = 3$. $$R_3 = \lim_{z \to 3} (z-3) \cdot \frac{(z^2 + z) z^{n-1}}{(z-1)(z-2)(z-3)} = \frac{(9 + 3) \cdot 3^{n-1}}{(3-1)(3-2)} = \frac{12 \cdot 3^{n-1}}{(2)(1)} = 6 \cdot 3^{n-1} = 2 \cdot 3^n$$ **Step 7:** Sum the residues. $$x[n] = R_1 + R_2 + R_3 = \frac{2}{3} - 3 \cdot 2^n + 2 \cdot 3^n$$ **Final Answer for (b):** $$x[n] = \left( 2 \cdot 3^n - 3 \cdot 2^n + \frac{2}{3} \right) u[n]$$ **Stability check:** The poles are at $z = 1, 2, 3$. Since the pole at $z = 3$ lies **outside** the unit circle, the system is **unstable**, which is expected for a causal sequence with $\text{ROC} = \vert z \vert > 3$. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting to multiply by $z$:** In the partial fraction method, students often forget to multiply back by $z$ after expressing $\frac{X(z)}{z}$ as a sum of fractions. This leads to incorrect answers. > 2. **Confusing causal and anti-causal pairs:** When the ROC is inside the pole, you **must** use the anti-causal pair $-a^n u[-n-1]$, not the causal $a^n u[n]$. > 3. **Missing the ROC specification:** If the ROC is not given, the answer is non-unique. Always check whether the problem specifies the ROC. > 4. **Sign errors in residue method:** When computing $R_2$ in example (b), students often forget that $(z-3) \big\vert_{z=2} = -1$, not $+1$. > 5. **Missing the $u[n]$ factor:** The unit step function $u[n]$ is **mandatory** in the final answer to indicate the sequence is causal (defined only for $n \geq 0$). > 6. **Long division arithmetic mistakes:** In long division, students frequently make sign errors during the subtraction step. Verify each subtraction carefully. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for Inverse Z-Transform** - [ ] The **Inverse Z-Transform** recovers $x[n]$ from $X(z)$ using the contour integral $x[n] = \frac{1}{2\pi j} \oint_C X(z) z^{n-1} dz$. - [ ] The **ROC is part of the specification** — without it, the inverse is not unique. - [ ] **Four methods** exist: Long Division, Partial Fraction, Residue (Contour Integration), and Direct Inspection. - [ ] **Causal ROC** extends outward from the outermost pole: $\vert z \vert > r_{\max}$. - [ ] **Anti-causal ROC** extends inward from the innermost pole: $\vert z \vert < r_{\min}$. - [ ] **Two-sided ROC** is an annular ring: $r_1 < \vert z \vert < r_2$. - [ ] A system is **stable** if and only if the ROC includes the unit circle. - [ ] In **partial fractions**, always expand $\frac{X(z)}{z}$ first, then multiply back by $z$ before applying standard pairs. - [ ] The standard pair $\frac{z}{z-a}$ corresponds to $a^n u[n]$ (causal) or $-a^n u[-n-1]$ (anti-causal). - [ ] The **residue method** uses Cauchy's Residue Theorem and handles repeated poles via the derivative formula. - [ ] **Long division** is useful when only a few samples are needed or when polynomials are not easily factorable. - [ ] The **recurrence relation** for a causal sequence can be read directly from the denominator polynomial of $X(z)$ expressed in $z^{-1}$. - [ ] In robotic control, the inverse Z-transform converts Z-domain transfer functions into **difference equations** for real-time microcontroller implementation. - [ ] **Unit step $u[n]$** must be included in causal answers; **$u[-n-1]$** for anti-causal answers. - [ ] The poles of a stable discrete system must all lie **inside the unit circle** $\vert z \vert = 1$.

topics.title: Difference Equation Representation of Discrete Time Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT402 - SIGNAL PROCESSING FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Difference Equation Representation of Discrete Time Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Discrete Time System** is a mathematical operator that maps an input sequence $x[n]$ to an output sequence $y[n]$ according to a prescribed computational rule. Such a system, when implemented using linear combinations of past and present samples, is described by a **Linear Constant Coefficient Difference Equation (LCCDE)** of the general form:

$$
\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k \, y[n-k] \;=\; \sum_{r=0}^{M} b_r \, x[n-r]
$$

where the constants $\{a_k\}$ and $\{b_r\}$ are **time-invariant real coefficients**, $N$ is the **order of the system** (the largest delay applied to the output), and $M$ is the highest delay applied to the input. By convention, $a_0 \neq 0$ and is usually normalized to **1**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The difference equation is the **time-domain equivalent of an LTI discrete system**, just as a differential equation describes continuous-time LTI systems. Any LTI discrete system can be described by exactly one such equation (under zero initial conditions for impulse response derivations).

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are a **mobile robot** rolling along a corridor. Your current position $y[n]$ depends on:

- Your **previous positions** $y[n-1], y[n-2], \dots$ (your inertia and momentum — recursive part).
- The **control commands** you receive at this and earlier instants $x[n], x[n-1], \dots$ (input forcing — non-recursive part).

The **difference equation** is essentially the discrete-time "Newton's law" that says: *"How the robot moves now is a weighted combination of how it moved before AND the commands it gets."* The weights $\{a_k, b_r\}$ are the physical parameters (mass, friction, motor gain) translated into the digital world.

### 1.3 Why Difference Equations Matter in Robotics

In PCRAT402 — Signal Processing for Robotics, the difference equation is the **fundamental computational kernel** for:

- **Encoder signal smoothing** (cascaded low-pass filtering of wheel odometry).
- **Kalman filter prediction** (state update step uses a state transition difference equation).
- **Real-time digital controllers** (PID controller output $u[n] = u[n-1] + \dots$).
- **Adaptive noise cancellation** in IMU and LiDAR signals.

### 1.4 Classification of Discrete Systems via Difference Equations

| Class | Defining Property | Engineering Equivalent |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Linear** | Superposition holds: $T\{a x_1 + b x_2\} = a y_1 + b y_2$ | Amplifier, integrator |
| **Time-Invariant (TI)** | Shift in input $\Rightarrow$ same shift in output | Constant-parameter plant |
| **Causal** | $y[n]$ depends only on $x[k]$ for $k \le n$ | All real-time systems |
| **Stable (BIBO)** | $\sum \vert h[k] \vert < \infty$ | Sensors with no DC drift |
| **Static / Memoryless** | $y[n]$ depends only on $x[n]$ | Pure gain block |
| **Dynamic** | $y[n]$ depends on past samples | All filters and controllers |

> [!NOTE]
> A system governed by an LCCDE is always **Linear** and **Time-Invariant (LTI)**. Causality and stability must be **verified separately** by analyzing the roots of the characteristic polynomial.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Canonical Forms of the Difference Equation

The general form

$$
\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k \, y[n-k] \;=\; \sum_{r=0}^{M} b_r \, x[n-r]
$$
