can be rearranged into the **computational (causal/recursive) form**, solving for the most recent output $y[n]$:

$$
y[n] \;=\; -\sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k \, y[n-k] \;+\; \sum_{r=0}^{M} b_r \, x[n-r]
$$

> The term $a_0$ is normalized to **1**, and moved to the left-hand side, leaving the negative signs for the recursive coefficients on the right.

### 2.2 Recursive vs. Non-Recursive Systems

**Non-Recursive (Moving Average / FIR):**

$$
y[n] \;=\; \sum_{r=0}^{M} b_r \, x[n-r]
$$

- No $y[n-k]$ terms on the right.
- Output depends **only on present and past inputs**.
- Always **BIBO stable**.
- Finite impulse response of length $M+1$.

**Recursive (IIR):**

$$
y[n] \;=\; -\sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k \, y[n-k] \;+\; \sum_{r=0}^{M} b_r \, x[n-r]
$$

- Output depends on **past outputs as well**.
- Infinite impulse response (in general).
- **Stability not guaranteed** — depends on pole locations.

### 2.3 Order of the System

The **order** $N$ is the largest lag applied to the output (equivalently, the order of the characteristic polynomial $A(z)$). Higher order $\Rightarrow$ more memory $\Rightarrow$ sharper frequency response, but at the cost of:
- Computational complexity
- Numerical sensitivity (coefficient quantization)
- Risk of instability

### 2.4 Relationship with Impulse Response

The impulse response $h[n]$ of an LCCDE-driven system is obtained by setting $x[n] = \delta[n]$ and $y[n] = h[n]$ with **zero initial conditions**:

$$
h[n] \;=\; -\sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k \, h[n-k] \;+\; \sum_{r=0}^{M} b_r \, \delta[n-r]
$$

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | General LCCDE | $\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k y[n-k] = \sum_{r=0}^{M} b_r x[n-r]$ | $a_0 = 1$ by convention |
| 2 | Computational form | $y[n] = -\sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k y[n-k] + \sum_{r=0}^{M} b_r x[n-r]$ | Used for implementation |
| 3 | Impulse response recursion | $h[n] = -\sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k h[n-k] + \sum_{r=0}^{M} b_r \delta[n-r]$ | Zero IC required |
| 4 | Step response | $s[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} h[k]$ | Convolution of $h[n]$ with $u[n]$ |
| 5 | Characteristic equation | $\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k \alpha^{N-k} = 0$ | Roots dictate natural modes |
| 6 | System order | $\max(N, M)$ | Defines memory depth |
| 7 | BIBO stability | $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \vert h[n] \vert < \infty$ | Equivalent to $\vert \alpha_i \vert < 1$ for all poles |
| 8 | Transfer function | $H(z) = \dfrac{\sum_{r=0}^{M} b_r z^{-r}}{\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k z^{-k}}$ | $z$-transform of the LCCDE |
| 9 | Total solution | $y[n] = y_h[n] + y_p[n]$ | Homogeneous + particular |
| 10 | Homogeneous solution | $y_h[n] = \sum_{i=1}^{N} C_i \alpha_i^{n}$ | $\alpha_i$ are characteristic roots |
| 11 | Convolution form | $y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} h[k] \, x[n-k]$ | Equivalent LTI representation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tables and Pipes:** All absolute-value notations inside tables are written as `\vert ... \vert` to prevent markdown table breakage.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In a **robotic manipulator control loop**, the joint-angle encoder produces a digital stream $x[n]$. This stream is filtered using an LCCDE-based digital filter whose coefficients are designed (via bilinear transform from analog prototype) to attenuate encoder quantization noise above the Nyquist rate. The result — a clean position estimate — feeds into the **discrete PID controller**, itself an LCCDE of order 2 (proportional-integral) or 3 (PID), closed around the robotic plant.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — From the Computational LCCDE to Impulse Response

**Problem Statement:** For the system governed by

$$
y[n] - 0.5 \, y[n-1] \;=\; x[n] + 0.3 \, x[n-1]
$$

compute the impulse response $h[n]$ for $n = 0, 1, 2, 3$ using zero initial conditions.

**Step 1 — Identify the coefficients.**

$$
a_0 = 1, \quad a_1 = -0.5, \quad b_0 = 1, \quad b_1 = 0.3
$$

**Step 2 — Rewrite in the explicit computational form.**

$$
y[n] = 0.5 \, y[n-1] + x[n] + 0.3 \, x[n-1]
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the impulse input $x[n] = \delta[n]$ and set $y[n] = h[n]$.**

The impulse has the values:

$$
\delta[n] = \begin{cases} 1, & n = 0 \\ 0, & n \neq 0 \end{cases}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $h[0]$ (uses $h[-1] = 0$ and $\delta[-1] = 0$).**

$$
h[0] = 0.5 \, h[-1] + \delta[0] + 0.3 \, \delta[-1] = 0.5(0) + 1 + 0.3(0) = 1
$$

**Step 5 — Compute $h[1]$ (uses $h[0] = 1$, $\delta[1] = 0$, $\delta[0] = 1$).**

$$
h[1] = 0.5 \, h[0] + \delta[1] + 0.3 \, \delta[0] = 0.5(1) + 0 + 0.3(1) = 0.8
$$

**Step 6 — Compute $h[2]$ (uses $h[1] = 0.8$, $\delta[2] = 0$, $\delta[1] = 0$).**

$$
h[2] = 0.5 \, h[1] + \delta[2] + 0.3 \, \delta[1] = 0.5(0.8) + 0 + 0 = 0.4
$$

**Step 7 — Compute $h[3]$ (uses $h[2] = 0.4$, $\delta[3] = 0$, $\delta[2] = 0$).**

$$
h[3] = 0.5 \, h[2] + \delta[3] + 0.3 \, \delta[2] = 0.5(0.4) + 0 + 0 = 0.2
$$

**Step 8 — Identify the closed-form pattern.**

For $n \ge 2$, the impulse is zero on both input terms, and we obtain the pure homogeneous recurrence:

$$
h[n] = 0.5 \, h[n-1] \quad \Rightarrow \quad h[n] = (0.5)^{n} \quad \text{for } n \ge 1, \text{ with correction at } n=0
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is a hallmark of a **causal IIR system** with one pole at $z = 0.5$ (inside unit circle $\Rightarrow$ stable).

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Homogeneous + Particular Solution Method

**Problem Statement:** Find the total response $y[n]$ for $n \ge 0$ of the system

$$
y[n] - 0.6 \, y[n-1] = 5 \, u[n], \quad y[-1] = 2
$$

where $u[n]$ is the unit-step input.

**Step 1 — Identify the characteristic polynomial.**

$$
A(\alpha) = 1 - 0.6 \alpha^{-1} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \alpha = 0.6
$$

A single real root $\alpha_1 = 0.6$.

**Step 2 — Write the homogeneous (natural) solution.**

$$
y_h[n] = C_1 (0.6)^{n}
$$

**Step 3 — Find the particular (forced) solution.**

Since the input is a constant $5 u[n]$ (a polynomial of degree 0), assume $y_p[n] = K$ (constant).

Substitute into the difference equation:

$$
K - 0.6 K = 5 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 0.4 K = 5 \quad \Rightarrow \quad K = 12.5
$$

**Step 4 — Form the total solution.**

$$
y[n] = y_h[n] + y_p[n] = C_1 (0.6)^{n} + 12.5
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the initial condition $y[-1] = 2$.**

$$
y[-1] = C_1 (0.6)^{-1} + 12.5 = 2
$$

$$
\frac{C_1}{0.6} = 2 - 12.5 = -10.5 \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_1 = -6.3
$$

**Step 6 — Final closed-form answer.**

$$
y[n] = \left[12.5 - 6.3 (0.6)^{n}\right] u[n]
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As $n \to \infty$, $(0.6)^n \to 0$, hence $y[n] \to 12.5$. This is the **steady-state response** of the system to a step input, consistent with $H(z=1) = \dfrac{5}{1 - 0.6} = 12.5$ (DC gain).

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Step Response via Direct Summation of Impulse Response

**Problem Statement:** Given $h[n] = (0.5)^{n} u[n]$, compute the step response $s[n]$ for $n = 0, 1, 2, 3$.

**Step 1 — Use the convolution sum with unit step.**

$$
s[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{n} h[k] = \sum_{k=0}^{n} (0.5)^{k}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the finite geometric-series identity.**

$$
s[n] = \frac{1 - (0.5)^{n+1}}{1 - 0.5} = 2 \left[1 - (0.5)^{n+1}\right]
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate.**

| $n$ | $s[n]$ (decimal) |
| :--- | :--- |
| 0 | $2(1 - 0.5) = 1.000$ |
| 1 | $2(1 - 0.25) = 1.500$ |
| 2 | $2(1 - 0.125) = 1.750$ |
| 3 | $2(1 - 0.0625) = 1.875$ |
| $\infty$ | $2$ (steady state) |

**Step 4 — Cross-check via transfer function.**

The $z$-transform of the unit step is $\dfrac{z}{z-1}$, and $H(z) = \dfrac{z}{z - 0.5}$. Therefore:

$$
S(z) = H(z) \cdot \frac{z}{z-1} = \frac{z^2}{(z-0.5)(z-1)}
$$

Long division / partial fraction gives the same $s[n]$ as above. **Match confirmed.**

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Method to Use?

| Method | Best For | Limitation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Direct Recursion (Section 3.1) | Computing $h[n]$ numerically | No closed form |
| Homogeneous + Particular (Section 3.2) | Symbolic closed-form | Fails for non-polynomial inputs (use $z$-transform) |
| Convolution Sum (Section 3.3) | When $h[n]$ is known and finite | Slow for long sequences |
| $z$-Transform | General, handles all inputs | Requires table look-up |
| Matrix / State-Space | MIMO and modern control | Higher setup overhead |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram of a Generic LCCDE System

A direct-form-I realization of the system

$$
y[n] = -\sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k y[n-k] + \sum_{r=0}^{M} b_r x[n-r]
$$

uses two parallel tapped-delay lines — one for the input (feed-forward taps $b_r$) and one for the output (feedback taps $-a_k$).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputNode["x of n"] --> splitNode["Split Signal"]
    splitNode --> ff0["b0"]
    splitNode --> ff1["Delay z^-1"] --> ff1coeff["b1"]
    splitNode --> ff2["Delay z^-1 z^-1"] --> ff2coeff["b2"]
    ff0 --> sumTopNode["Sum Node Forward"]
    ff1coeff --> sumTopNode
    ff2coeff --> sumTopNode
    outputNode["y of n"] --> fb1["Delay z^-1"] --> fb1coeff["Negative a1"]
    outputNode --> fb2["Delay z^-1 z^-1"] --> fb2coeff["Negative a2"]
    fb1coeff --> sumBotNode["Sum Node Feedback"]
    fb2coeff --> sumBotNode
    sumBotNode --> sumFinalNode["Total Summer"]
    sumTopNode --> sumFinalNode
    sumFinalNode --> outputNode
```

> [!NOTE]
> Each **delay block** labelled `z^-1` represents the unit-delay operator $D\{\cdot\} = $ previous sample. The negative signs on $a_k$ reflect the standard causal computation form.

### 4.2 Direct Form I vs. Direct Form II Realization

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Direct Form I"
        df1In["x of n"] --> df1d1["Delay z^-1"]
        df1d1 --> df1d2["Delay z^-1"]
        df1In --> df1b0["b0"]
        df1d1 --> df1b1["b1"]
        df1d2 --> df1b2["b2"]
        df1b0 --> df1sum1["Summer A"]
        df1b1 --> df1sum1
        df1b2 --> df1sum1
        df1sum1 --> df1out["y of n"]
        df1out --> df1fb1["Negative a1 Delay"]
        df1out --> df1fb2["Negative a2 Delay"]
        df1fb1 --> df1sum1
        df1fb2 --> df1sum1
    end
```

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Direct Form II Canonical"
        df2In["x of n"] --> df2sum0["Summer"]
        df2sum0 --> df2w["w of n Intermediate"]
        df2w --> df2d1["Delay z^-1"]
        df2d2["Delay z^-1"] --> df2w
        df2w --> df2b0["b0"]
        df2d1 --> df2b1["b1"]
        df2d2 --> df2b2["b2"]
        df2b0 --> df2sum1["Summer Out"]
        df2b1 --> df2sum1
        df2b2 --> df2sum1
        df2sum1 --> df2out["y of n"]
        df2w --> df2a1["Negative a1"]
        df2d1 --> df2a2["Negative a2"]
        df2a1 --> df2sum0
        df2a2 --> df2sum0
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Direct Form II** uses a **single shared delay line** for both feed-forward and feedback, halving the number of delay elements. This is the canonical minimum-memory realization — critical for real-time robotics processors with limited RAM.

### 4.3 Signal Flow — Impulse Response Computation

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Src as Input Source
    participant Sys as LCCDE System
    participant Out as Output y of n
    Src->>Sys: Apply x of n equals delta of n
    Sys->>Sys: Initialize y of negative one equals zero
    Sys->>Out: Compute y of zero using x of zero and x of negative one
    Out->>Sys: Feedback y of zero to compute y of one
    Sys->>Out: Output y of one
    Out->>Sys: Feedback y of one to compute y of two
    Sys->>Out: Output y of two
```

### 4.4 Conceptual Map of Discrete System Representations

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    diffEqNode["Difference Equation"] --> zTransNode["Z Transform H of z"]
    diffEqNode --> convNode["Convolution Sum"]
    zTransNode --> freqResp["Frequency Response H of e to the j omega"]
    convNode --> impulseRespNode["Impulse Response h of n"]
    impulseRespNode --> diffEqNode
    zTransNode --> blockDiagNode["Block Diagram Realization"]
    blockDiagNode --> diffEqNode
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
*Define a Linear Constant Coefficient Difference Equation (LCCDE). What does the order of the system signify?*

**Model Answer:**

A Linear Constant Coefficient Difference Equation (LCCDE) is a linear relationship that expresses the current output $y[n]$ of a discrete-time system as a linear combination of past outputs $y[n-1], y[n-2], \dots, y[n-N]$ and present/past inputs $x[n], x[n-1], \dots, x[n-M]$ with **time-invariant (constant) real coefficients** $a_k$ and $b_r$:

$$
\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k y[n-k] = \sum_{r=0}^{M} b_r x[n-r], \quad a_0 = 1
$$

The **order** of the system is $\max(N, M)$ — the largest delay applied to any signal. It indicates the **memory depth** of the system, the number of past samples required to compute the current output, and the number of poles in $H(z)$ (which determines the natural modes and stability).

> A higher order system has sharper frequency-selectivity but higher computational cost and a higher risk of instability.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
*Distinguish between recursive and non-recursive discrete-time systems. Which one is always BIBO stable?*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Non-Recursive (FIR) | Recursive (IIR) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Right-hand side | Contains only $x[n-r]$ | Contains $y[n-k]$ and $x[n-r]$ |
| Memory of output | No | Yes |
| Impulse response | **Finite** | Infinite (typically) |
| BIBO stability | **Always stable** | Conditional (depends on poles) |
| Computational cost | Higher for sharp response | Lower for equivalent selectivity |

A **non-recursive (FIR)** system is always BIBO stable because its impulse response $h[n] = \sum b_r \delta[n-r]$ has only finitely many non-zero terms, so $\sum \vert h[n] \vert$ is bounded for any bounded set of $b_r$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often incorrectly state that "all IIR systems are unstable". This is **wrong** — an IIR system is stable **iff all poles lie strictly inside the unit circle** $\vert z \vert < 1$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]**

A discrete-time LTI system is described by the difference equation

$$
y[n] - 0.8 y[n-1] + 0.15 y[n-2] = x[n] + 0.5 x[n-1]
$$

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Determine the impulse response $h[n]$ for $n = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4$ using the direct recursive method. Assume zero initial conditions.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Find the total response $y[n]$ for $n \ge 0$ when the input is $x[n] = u[n]$ (unit step) and the initial conditions are $y[-1] = 1, \, y[-2] = 0$.

---

### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Rewrite the equation in computational form.**

$$
y[n] = 0.8 \, y[n-1] - 0.15 \, y[n-2] + x[n] + 0.5 \, x[n-1]
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the impulse input $x[n] = \delta[n]$ and set $y[n] = h[n]$ with $h[-1] = h[-2] = 0$.**

**Step 3 — Compute $h[0]$:**

$$
h[0] = 0.8(0) - 0.15(0) + \delta[0] + 0.5 \delta[-1] = 0 + 0 + 1 + 0 = 1
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $h[1]$:**

$$
h[1] = 0.8(1) - 0.15(0) + \delta[1] + 0.5 \delta[0] = 0.8 + 0 + 0 + 0.5 = 1.3
$$

**Step 5 — Compute $h[2]$:**

$$
h[2] = 0.8(1.3) - 0.15(1) + \delta[2] + 0.5 \delta[1] = 1.04 - 0.15 + 0 + 0 = 0.89
$$

**Step 6 — Compute $h[3]$:**

$$
h[3] = 0.8(0.89) - 0.15(1.3) + 0 + 0 = 0.712 - 0.195 = 0.517
$$

**Step 7 — Compute $h[4]$:**

$$
h[4] = 0.8(0.517) - 0.15(0.89) + 0 + 0 = 0.4136 - 0.1335 = 0.2801
$$

**Result Table:**

| $n$ | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $h[n]$ | 1.000 | 1.300 | 0.890 | 0.517 | 0.280 |

The values are **decaying toward zero**, indicating the system is BIBO stable.

---

### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Identify the characteristic polynomial.**

$$
\alpha^2 - 0.8 \alpha + 0.15 = 0
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the characteristic roots.**

$$
\alpha = \frac{0.8 \pm \sqrt{0.64 - 0.6}}{2} = \frac{0.8 \pm \sqrt{0.04}}{2} = \frac{0.8 \pm 0.2}{2}
$$

$$
\alpha_1 = 0.5, \quad \alpha_2 = 0.3
$$

**Step 3 — Write the homogeneous solution.**

$$
y_h[n] = C_1 (0.5)^{n} + C_2 (0.3)^{n}
$$

**Step 4 — Assume a particular solution of the form $y_p[n] = K$ (constant, since input is step).**

Substitute $y_p[n] = y_p[n-1] = y_p[n-2] = K$ and $x[n] = x[n-1] = 1$:

$$
K - 0.8 K + 0.15 K = 1 + 0.5
$$

$$
0.35 K = 1.5 \quad \Rightarrow \quad K = \frac{1.5}{0.35} = \frac{30}{7} \approx 4.2857
$$

**Step 5 — Form the total solution.**

$$
y[n] = C_1 (0.5)^{n} + C_2 (0.3)^{n} + \frac{30}{7}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply initial conditions $y[-1] = 1, \, y[-2] = 0$.**

First equation ($n = -1$):

$$
1 = C_1 (0.5)^{-1} + C_2 (0.3)^{-1} + \frac{30}{7} = 2 C_1 + \frac{10}{3} C_2 + \frac{30}{7}
$$

$$
2 C_1 + \frac{10}{3} C_2 = 1 - \frac{30}{7} = -\frac{23}{7}
$$

Multiply by 21:

$$
42 C_1 + 70 C_2 = -69 \quad \text{...(Eq. A)}
$$

Second equation ($n = -2$):

$$
0 = C_1 (0.5)^{-2} + C_2 (0.3)^{-2} + \frac{30}{7} = 4 C_1 + \frac{100}{9} C_2 + \frac{30}{7}
$$

$$
4 C_1 + \frac{100}{9} C_2 = -\frac{30}{7}
$$

Multiply by 63:

$$
252 C_1 + 700 C_2 = -270 \quad \text{...(Eq. B)}
$$

**Step 7 — Solve the linear system.**

Multiply Eq. A by 6: $252 C_1 + 420 C_2 = -414$.
Subtract from Eq. B: $280 C_2 = 144 \Rightarrow C_2 = \dfrac{144}{280} = \dfrac{18}{35}$.

Back-substitute into Eq. A:

$$
42 C_1 = -69 - 70 \cdot \frac{18}{35} = -69 - 36 = -105 \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_1 = -\frac{105}{42} = -\frac{5}{2}
$$

**Step 8 — Final answer.**

$$
\boxed{\,y[n] = -\frac{5}{2}(0.5)^{n} + \frac{18}{35}(0.3)^{n} + \frac{30}{7}, \quad n \ge 0\,}
$$

**Verification:** As $n \to \infty$, $y[n] \to \dfrac{30}{7} \approx 4.286$, which matches the DC gain $H(1) = \dfrac{1 + 0.5}{1 - 0.8 + 0.15} = \dfrac{1.5}{0.35} = \dfrac{30}{7}$. **Match confirmed.**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]**

A causal LTI discrete-time system is characterized by the difference equation

$$
y[n] = 0.6 y[n-1] - 0.08 y[n-2] + x[n]
$$

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Find the transfer function $H(z)$ and the impulse response $h[n]$ in closed form.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Determine the step response $s[n]$ for $n = 0, 1, 2, \infty$, and verify the BIBO stability of the system.

---

### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Take the $z$-transform of both sides (zero initial conditions).**

$$
Y(z) = 0.6 z^{-1} Y(z) - 0.08 z^{-2} Y(z) + X(z)
$$

**Step 2 — Collect $Y(z)$ terms.**

$$
Y(z) \left[1 - 0.6 z^{-1} + 0.08 z^{-2}\right] = X(z)
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the transfer function.**

$$
H(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{X(z)} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.6 z^{-1} + 0.08 z^{-2}} = \frac{z^2}{z^2 - 0.6 z + 0.08}
$$

**Step 4 — Factor the denominator to find the poles.**

$$
z^2 - 0.6 z + 0.08 = (z - 0.4)(z - 0.2)
$$

Poles: $p_1 = 0.4$ and $p_2 = 0.2$, both inside the unit circle.

**Step 5 — Perform partial-fraction expansion.**

$$
H(z) = \frac{z^2}{(z - 0.4)(z - 0.2)} = \frac{A}{1 - 0.4 z^{-1}} + \frac{B}{1 - 0.2 z^{-1}}
$$

Multiply through by $(z - 0.4)(z - 0.2)$ and substitute $z = 0.4$:

$$
A = \frac{(0.4)^2}{0.4 - 0.2} = \frac{0.16}{0.2} = 0.8
$$

Substitute $z = 0.2$:

$$
B = \frac{(0.2)^2}{0.2 - 0.4} = \frac{0.04}{-0.2} = -0.2
$$

**Step 6 — Inverse $z$-transform to obtain the closed-form impulse response.**

$$
h[n] = \left[0.8 (0.4)^{n} - 0.2 (0.2)^{n}\right] u[n]
$$

> This is a **causal, two-pole IIR system**.

---

### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Step response is $s[n] = h[n] * u[n]$, which in $z$-domain becomes:**

$$
S(z) = H(z) \cdot \frac{1}{1 - z^{-1}} = \frac{z^2}{(z - 0.4)(z - 0.2)(z - 1)}
$$

**Step 2 — Partial-fraction expansion in $z$ (or $z^{-1}$).**

For simplicity, work with the $z^{-1}$ form. Let $w = z^{-1}$:

$$
H(z) = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.4 w)(1 - 0.2 w)}, \quad S(z) = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.4 w)(1 - 0.2 w)(1 - w)}
$$

Expand:

$$
S(z) = \frac{C_1}{1 - 0.4 w} + \frac{C_2}{1 - 0.2 w} + \frac{C_3}{1 - w}
$$

Cover-up method:
- At $w = 1/0.4 = 2.5$: $C_1 = \dfrac{1}{(1 - 0.2/0.4)(1 - 1/0.4)} = \dfrac{1}{(0.5)(-1.5)} = -\dfrac{1}{0.75} = -\dfrac{4}{3}$.
- At $w = 1/0.2 = 5$: $C_2 = \dfrac{1}{(1 - 0.4/0.2)(1 - 1/0.2)} = \dfrac{1}{(-1)(-4)} = \dfrac{1}{4} = 0.25$.
- At $w = 1$: $C_3 = \dfrac{1}{(1 - 0.4)(1 - 0.2)} = \dfrac{1}{(0.6)(0.8)} = \dfrac{1}{0.48} = \dfrac{25}{12} \approx 2.0833$.

**Step 3 — Inverse $z$-transform.**

$$
s[n] = \left[-\frac{4}{3}(0.4)^{n} + \frac{1}{4}(0.2)^{n} + \frac{25}{12}\right] u[n]
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate at specific $n$.**

| $n$ | $s[n]$ (decimal) |
| :--- | :--- |
| 0 | $-\frac{4}{3} + \frac{1}{4} + \frac{25}{12} = \dfrac{-16 + 3 + 25}{12} = \dfrac{12}{12} = 1.000$ |
| 1 | $-\frac{4}{3}(0.4) + \frac{1}{4}(0.2) + \frac{25}{12} = -0.5333 + 0.05 + 2.0833 = 1.600$ |
| 2 | $-\frac{4}{3}(0.16) + \frac{1}{4}(0.04) + \frac{25}{12} = -0.2133 + 0.01 + 2.0833 = 1.880$ |
| $\infty$ | $\frac{25}{12} \approx 2.0833$ |

**Step 5 — BIBO Stability Check.**

Both poles ($0.4$ and $0.2$) lie strictly inside the unit circle ($\vert z \vert < 1$). Additionally:

$$
\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \vert h[n] \vert = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \left\vert 0.8 (0.4)^{n} - 0.2 (0.2)^{n} \right\vert
$$

Since both exponentials decay geometrically, the sum is bounded by:

$$
0.8 \cdot \frac{1}{1 - 0.4} + 0.2 \cdot \frac{1}{1 - 0.2} = \frac{0.8}{0.6} + \frac{0.2}{0.8} = 1.3333 + 0.25 = 1.5833 < \infty
$$

**The system is BIBO stable.** $\blacksquare$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warnings

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall #1 — Initial Condition Mishandling:** When applying the homogeneous + particular method, students frequently use $y[0]$ as the initial condition. The correct anchor is $y[-1]$ (or whatever the highest output lag is). Forgetting to shift indices results in incorrect $C_i$ values and a full-marks loss of 4–5 marks.
>
> **Common Pitfall #2 — Sign Convention Errors:** The standard form is $\sum a_k y[n-k] = \sum b_r x[n-r]$. When rearranging for $y[n]$, the recursive coefficients $a_k$ (for $k \ge 1$) appear on the right with a **negative sign**: $y[n] = -\sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k y[n-k] + \sum b_r x[n-r]$. Many students forget the sign flip and get a sign-incorrect impulse response.
>
> **Common Pitfall #3 — Confusing Step Response with Steady-State:** Students often report $s[n] = H(1) = $ constant, missing the **transient terms** that decay as $(p_i)^{n}$. Full marks require both the transient and steady-state components.
>
> **Common Pitfall #4 — Pole-Zero Cancellation:** When a zero lies exactly on a pole, the apparent order reduces. Always check for cancellations before declaring the system order $N$.
>
> **Common Pitfall #5 — Stability from Pole Location:** BIBO stability requires $\vert p_i \vert < 1$ for **all poles**. A single pole on or outside the unit circle makes the system **unstable**, regardless of how small the others are.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- The **LCCDE** is the canonical time-domain description of a causal LTI discrete system: $\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k y[n-k] = \sum_{r=0}^{M} b_r x[n-r]$.
- **Order** of the system = largest lag on $y$ (equivalently, the number of poles).
- **Non-recursive (FIR)** systems have only input terms on the RHS — they are **always BIBO stable**.
- **Recursive (IIR)** systems include output terms — stability requires **all poles strictly inside the unit circle**.
- The **impulse response** can be computed by three equivalent routes: (1) direct recursion, (2) $z$-transform inversion, (3) closed-form homogeneous + particular.
- The **total response** is the sum of:
  - **Homogeneous / natural response** $y_h[n] = \sum C_i \alpha_i^{n}$, determined by the characteristic roots.
  - **Particular / forced response** $y_p[n]$, matching the input shape.
- The **steady-state response** to a unit step equals the DC gain $H(z=1) = \dfrac{\sum b_r}{\sum a_k}$.
- **BIBO stability criterion:** $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \vert h[n] \vert < \infty$ — geometric decay at rate $< 1$ guarantees it.
- **Direct Form II** realization uses a single shared delay line — minimum memory, preferred for real-time robotics.
- In **robotic applications**, the LCCDE form is the computational backbone of digital filters for encoders, IMUs, LiDAR signal conditioning, and discrete PID controllers.
- **Sign convention:** Computational form has $-a_k$ (for $k \ge 1$) on the RHS.
- **Initial conditions** must be at $n = -1, -2, \dots, -N$ (not at $n = 0$).
- The **characteristic equation** $\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k \alpha^{N-k} = 0$ determines the natural modes of the system.
- For real-time robotics, choose lower-order LCCDEs ($N \le 4$) to limit computational load and numerical sensitivity.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Frequency Domain Representation of Discrete Time Signals - Discrete Time Fourier Transform (DTFT)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT402 - SIGNAL PROCESSING FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Frequency Domain Representation of Discrete Time Signals - Discrete Time Fourier Transform (DTFT)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (DTFT)** is the frequency-domain representation of a discrete-time, non-periodic sequence. It extends the concept of the Fourier Transform (used for continuous-time signals) into the discrete-time domain, providing a continuous function of the angular frequency variable $\omega$.

For a discrete-time signal $x[n]$, the DTFT $X(e^{j\omega})$ is mathematically defined as:

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] \, e^{-j\omega n}$$

The corresponding **Inverse Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (IDTFT)** is given by:

$$x[n] = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} X(e^{j\omega}) \, e^{j\omega n} \, d\omega$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The DTFT maps a discrete-time sequence $x[n]$ into a **continuous** and **periodic** function of $\omega$ with period $2\pi$. This periodicity is a fundamental distinguishing property of the DTFT compared to the Continuous-Time Fourier Transform (CTFT).

### 1.2 Condition for Existence

The DTFT exists (converges) only if the sequence $x[n]$ is **absolutely summable**, i.e.,

$$\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert < \infty$$

A weaker sufficient condition is that $x[n]$ is **square summable** (finite energy), i.e.,

$$\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert^2 < \infty$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note:** The DTFT is defined for **infinite-duration** sequences. For finite-duration sequences, the DTFT is a continuous function of $\omega$ obtained by evaluating the Z-transform on the unit circle $z = e^{j\omega}$.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are a roboticist who has recorded a series of wheel-speed sensor readings from a mobile robot as it moves across the floor: `[0.2, 0.4, 0.5, 0.3, 0.1, 0.0, -0.1, ...]`. This data is in the **time domain** — every number tells you what the speed was at a specific moment.

But what if, instead, you wanted to know the **underlying rhythms** in this data? For example:
- Is there a slow, sweeping pattern (low frequency)?
- Are there sudden, jittery oscillations (high frequency)?

The **DTFT** is essentially a mathematical prism that splits a discrete signal into all its constituent "rhythms" (frequencies). Each frequency gets a complex number attached, which carries two pieces of information:
- **Magnitude** $\vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert$: How strong is this frequency present?
- **Phase** $\angle X(e^{j\omega})$: When does this frequency peak relative to others?

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy Summary:** Just as sunlight passing through a prism splits into a rainbow of colors, the DTFT splits a discrete-time signal into a continuous spectrum of frequencies, revealing the hidden periodicities inside the data — a critical operation in robotics for analyzing sensor signals, motor control loops, and audio feedback.

## 1.4 Key Distinguishing Features of DTFT

| Feature | DTFT | CTFT | DFT |
|---|---|---|---|
| Input | Discrete-time $x[n]$ | Continuous $x(t)$ | Finite, periodic $x[n]$ |
| Output | Continuous in $\omega$ | Continuous in $\omega$ | Discrete in $k$ |
| Periodicity in $\omega$ | Periodic ($2\pi$) | Aperiodic | Periodic ($N$) |
| Frequency Variable | $\omega \in [-\pi, \pi]$ | $\omega \in (-\infty, \infty)$ | $\omega_k = 2\pi k/N$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** The DTFT is **periodic with period $2\pi$**. Therefore, it is sufficient to study the spectrum over any $2\pi$ interval, conventionally $-\pi \le \omega \le \pi$ (or equivalently $0 \le \omega \le 2\pi$).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Derivation of DTFT — The "Why" and "How"

The DTFT can be motivated from the **Discrete-Time Fourier Series (DTFS)** of a periodic sequence. A periodic sequence $\tilde{x}[n]$ with period $N$ has Fourier series:

$$\tilde{x}[n] = \sum_{k=\langle N \rangle} a_k \, e^{jk\omega_0 n}, \quad \omega_0 = \frac{2\pi}{N}$$

$$a_k = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=\langle N \rangle} \tilde{x}[n] \, e^{-jk\omega_0 n}$$

As the period $N \to \infty$, the periodic sequence becomes aperiodic. The discrete frequency points $k\omega_0$ become dense, transforming the sum into an integral, and the coefficients $a_k$ become a continuous function $X(e^{j\omega})$.

## 2.2 Logical Steps Behind DTFT

1. **Step 1 — Input Domain:** A discrete-time, non-periodic, infinite or finite-length sequence $x[n]$ is given. No assumption of periodicity exists.

2. **Step 2 — Synthesis of Frequencies:** Each complex exponential $e^{j\omega n}$ at frequency $\omega$ contributes to the signal. The signal is a continuous "sum" (integral) of such exponentials.

3. **Step 3 — Analysis (Forward Transform):** Compute the projection of $x[n]$ onto each complex exponential $e^{j\omega n}$. The inner product (sum over $n$) gives the strength $X(e^{j\omega})$ at frequency $\omega$.

4. **Step 4 — Output Domain:** The result is a **complex-valued continuous function** of $\omega$, periodic with period $2\pi$.

5. **Step 5 — Reconstruction (Inverse Transform):** Integrate $X(e^{j\omega}) e^{j\omega n}$ over one period $[-\pi, \pi]$ and normalize by $\frac{1}{2\pi}$ to perfectly recover $x[n]$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Below is the **high-yield formula sheet** every KTU 2024 student must memorize for the DTFT. These are the formulas examiners expect to see verbatim.

| # | Property | Time Domain | Frequency Domain |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | DTFT Definition | $x[n]$ | $X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum x[n]e^{-j\omega n}$ |
| 2 | Inverse DTFT | $x[n] = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi} X(e^{j\omega})e^{j\omega n}d\omega$ | $X(e^{j\omega})$ |
| 3 | Periodicity | $x[n]$ | $X(e^{j\omega}) = X(e^{j(\omega+2\pi)})$ |
| 4 | Linearity | $a x_1[n] + b x_2[n]$ | $a X_1(e^{j\omega}) + b X_2(e^{j\omega})$ |
| 5 | Time Shift | $x[n - n_0]$ | $e^{-j\omega n_0} X(e^{j\omega})$ |
| 6 | Frequency Shift | $e^{j\omega_0 n} x[n]$ | $X(e^{j(\omega - \omega_0)})$ |
| 7 | Time Reversal | $x[-n]$ | $X(e^{-j\omega})$ |
| 8 | Conjugation | $x^*[n]$ | $X^*(e^{-j\omega})$ |
| 9 | Frequency Differentiation | $n \, x[n]$ | $j \frac{d}{d\omega} X(e^{j\omega})$ |
| 10 | Convolution (Time) | $x[n] * h[n]$ | $X(e^{j\omega}) H(e^{j\omega})$ |
| 11 | Modulation (Frequency Conv.) | $x[n] \, y[n]$ | $\frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} X(e^{j\theta}) Y(e^{j(\omega-\theta)}) d\theta$ |
| 12 | Parseval's Theorem | $\sum_{n} \vert x[n] \vert^2$ | $\frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} \vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2 d\omega$ |
| 13 | Real Signal (Even part) | $\text{Re}\{x[n]\}$ | $X_e(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{2}[X(e^{j\omega}) + X^*(e^{-j\omega})]$ |
| 14 | Real Signal (Odd part) | $j \, \text{Im}\{x[n]\}$ | $X_o(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{2}[X(e^{j\omega}) - X^*(e^{-j\omega})]$ |
| 15 | Relation to Z-Transform | $x[n]$ | $X(e^{j\omega}) = X(z) \big\vert_{z=e^{j\omega}}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real Signal Symmetry:** If $x[n]$ is **real**, then $X(e^{j\omega}) = X^*(e^{-j\omega})$. This means the magnitude spectrum $\vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert$ is **even** and the phase spectrum $\angle X(e^{j\omega})$ is **odd**. This is heavily tested in KTU exams.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Robotics Engineering

| Application | Role of DTFT |
|---|---|
| **Motor Vibration Analysis** | Identify dominant vibration frequencies in robotic arms to avoid resonance. |
| **IMU Sensor Denoising** | Analyze accelerometer/gyroscope signals in the frequency domain to filter out noise. |
| **Audio/Voice Recognition** | Extract frequency features from voice commands given to a service robot. |
| **Control Loop Stability** | Frequency response $H(e^{j\omega})$ of a digital controller for Bode-style analysis. |
| **Path Planning via Lidar** | Pre-process Lidar scans to identify periodic environmental patterns. |

## 2.5 Special DTFT Pairs (Must Memorize)

| Signal $x[n]$ | DTFT $X(e^{j\omega})$ |
|---|---|
| $\delta[n]$ (Unit Impulse) | $1$ |
| $\delta[n - n_0]$ | $e^{-j\omega n_0}$ |
| $1$ (Constant / DC) | $2\pi \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(\omega - 2\pi k)$ |
| $a^n u[n]$, $\vert a \vert < 1$ | $\dfrac{1}{1 - a e^{-j\omega}}$ |
| $u[n]$ (Unit Step) | $\dfrac{1}{1 - e^{-j\omega}} + \pi \sum_{k} \delta(\omega - 2\pi k)$ |
| $e^{j\omega_0 n}$ | $2\pi \sum_{k} \delta(\omega - \omega_0 - 2\pi k)$ |
| $\cos(\omega_0 n)$ | $\pi [\delta(\omega - \omega_0) + \delta(\omega + \omega_0)]$ (within $[-\pi,\pi]$) |
| $\sin(\omega_0 n)$ | $-j\pi [\delta(\omega - \omega_0) - \delta(\omega + \omega_0)]$ |
| Rectangular pulse $x[n] = 1$ for $-N_1 \le n \le N_1$, $0$ else | $\dfrac{\sin(\omega(N_1 + 1/2))}{\sin(\omega/2)}$ |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustive Work Mandate:** Every algebraic step is fully expanded. No shortcuts, no truncations. Each derivation is a model solution you can replicate in a KTU examination.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: DTFT of a Causal Exponential $x[n] = a^n u[n]$, $|a| < 1$

**Given:** $x[n] = a^n u[n]$ where $u[n]$ is the unit step and $|a| < 1$ ensures convergence.

**Step 1:** Write the DTFT definition.

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] \, e^{-j\omega n}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $x[n] = a^n u[n]$. Since $u[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$ and $u[n] = 1$ for $n \ge 0$, the sum reduces from $n = 0$ to $\infty$.

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a^n \, e^{-j\omega n}$$

**Step 3:** Factor out and combine the exponentials.

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} (a e^{-j\omega})^n$$

**Step 4:** Recognize this as a geometric series of the form $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} r^n = \frac{1}{1-r}$ for $|r| < 1$. Here, $r = a e^{-j\omega}$. Since $|a| < 1$, we have $|r| = |a| \cdot |e^{-j\omega}| = |a| < 1$. Convergence is guaranteed.

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{1 - a e^{-j\omega}}$$

**Step 5:** Express the result in rectangular form (magnitude and phase are useful for interpretation).

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{1 - a\cos\omega + j a \sin\omega}$$

- **Magnitude:** $\vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{(1 - a\cos\omega)^2 + (a\sin\omega)^2}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 - 2a\cos\omega + a^2}}$

- **Phase:** $\angle X(e^{j\omega}) = -\tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{a\sin\omega}{1 - a\cos\omega}\right)$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This DTFT is fundamental in robotics for modeling **decaying sensor responses**, e.g., the impulse response of a first-order low-pass filter used to smooth noisy motor encoders.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: DTFT of a Rectangular Pulse $x[n] = 1$ for $-N_1 \le n \le N_1$

**Given:** A finite-length rectangular pulse centered at $n=0$ with total length $2N_1 + 1$ samples.

**Step 1:** Apply the DTFT definition.

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] \, e^{-j\omega n}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the pulse. Since $x[n] = 1$ only in $[-N_1, N_1]$, the sum reduces.

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-N_1}^{N_1} (1) \, e^{-j\omega n}$$

**Step 3:** Split the sum into two parts: negative index and non-negative index.

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-N_1}^{-1} e^{-j\omega n} + \sum_{n=0}^{N_1} e^{-j\omega n}$$

**Step 4:** Apply variable substitution $m = -n$ in the first sum. When $n = -N_1$, $m = N_1$; when $n = -1$, $m = 1$. So:

$$\sum_{n=-N_1}^{-1} e^{-j\omega n} = \sum_{m=1}^{N_1} e^{j\omega m}$$

**Step 5:** Combine both sums.

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{m=1}^{N_1} e^{j\omega m} + \sum_{n=0}^{N_1} e^{-j\omega n}$$

**Step 6:** The middle term $n=0$ contributes 1. So:

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = 1 + \sum_{m=1}^{N_1} (e^{j\omega m} + e^{-j\omega m}) = 1 + 2 \sum_{m=1}^{N_1} \cos(\omega m)$$

**Step 7:** Use the closed-form identity $\sum_{m=0}^{N} \cos(\omega m) = \frac{\sin((\omega/2)(2N+1))}{2\sin(\omega/2)}$. Equivalently:

$$1 + 2\sum_{m=1}^{N_1} \cos(\omega m) = \frac{\sin\!\big(\omega(N_1 + \tfrac{1}{2})\big)}{\sin(\omega/2)}$$

**Step 8:** Final closed-form expression.

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{\sin\!\big[\omega(N_1 + \tfrac{1}{2})\big]}{\sin(\omega/2)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the well-known **Dirichlet kernel** shape, central to FIR filter design. In robotics, this DTFT describes the frequency response of a moving-average filter, which is the simplest noise smoother for sensor arrays.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Proof of the Time-Shift Property

**Statement:** If $x[n] \xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFT}} X(e^{j\omega})$, then $x[n - n_0] \xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFT}} e^{-j\omega n_0} X(e^{j\omega})$.

**Step 1:** Let $y[n] = x[n - n_0]$. Compute its DTFT.

$$Y(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n - n_0] \, e^{-j\omega n}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $m = n - n_0$, so $n = m + n_0$ and $dn = dm$.

$$Y(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} x[m] \, e^{-j\omega(m + n_0)}$$

**Step 3:** Split the exponential.

$$Y(e^{j\omega}) = e^{-j\omega n_0} \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} x[m] \, e^{-j\omega m}$$

**Step 4:** Identify the sum as the DTFT of $x[n]$.

$$Y(e^{j\omega}) = e^{-j\omega n_0} \, X(e^{j\omega}) \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Implication in Robotics:** A time shift in the sensor data corresponds to a **linear phase** in the frequency domain. The slope of this phase is $-n_0$. Digital control systems rely on this property to analyze delay-induced phase margins.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Proof of the Convolution Property

**Statement:** If $x[n] \xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFT}} X(e^{j\omega})$ and $h[n] \xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFT}} H(e^{j\omega})$, then $y[n] = x[n] * h[n] \xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFT}} X(e^{j\omega}) H(e^{j\omega})$.

**Step 1:** Start with the convolution definition: $y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x[k] h[n-k]$.

**Step 2:** Compute DTFT of $y[n]$.

$$Y(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \left[\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x[k] h[n-k]\right] e^{-j\omega n}$$

**Step 3:** Interchange the order of summation (valid due to absolute convergence).

$$Y(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x[k] \left[\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} h[n-k] e^{-j\omega n}\right]$$

**Step 4:** Apply the time-shift property to the inner sum. The inner sum is the DTFT of $h[n-k]$, which equals $e^{-j\omega k} H(e^{j\omega})$.

$$Y(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x[k] \, e^{-j\omega k} \, H(e^{j\omega})$$

**Step 5:** Factor out $H(e^{j\omega})$ (it does not depend on $k$).

$$Y(e^{j\omega}) = H(e^{j\omega}) \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x[k] e^{-j\omega k}$$

**Step 6:** Recognize the remaining sum as $X(e^{j\omega})$.

$$Y(e^{j\omega}) = X(e^{j\omega}) H(e^{j\omega}) \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is the cornerstone of **Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) system analysis**. In robotics, a robot's response to a control input is the convolution of the input with the system's impulse response — and in the frequency domain, this becomes a simple **multiplication** of spectra.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: DTFT of a Two-Point Sequence $x[n] = \delta[n] + \delta[n-1]$

**Given:** $x[n] = \delta[n] + \delta[n-1]$.

**Step 1:** Apply linearity.

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \text{DTFT}\{\delta[n]\} + \text{DTFT}\{\delta[n-1]\}$$

**Step 2:** Use the standard pair: $\delta[n] \xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFT}} 1$.

**Step 3:** Use the time-shift property with $n_0 = 1$: $\delta[n-1] \xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFT}} e^{-j\omega}$.

**Step 4:** Combine.

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = 1 + e^{-j\omega}$$

**Step 5:** Express in polar form using the Euler identity $1 + e^{-j\omega} = e^{-j\omega/2}(e^{j\omega/2} + e^{-j\omega/2}) = 2 e^{-j\omega/2} \cos(\omega/2)$.

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = 2\cos(\omega/2) \, e^{-j\omega/2}$$

- **Magnitude:** $\vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert = 2 \vert \cos(\omega/2) \vert$
- **Phase:** $\angle X(e^{j\omega}) = -\omega/2$ (for $\cos(\omega/2) > 0$)

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The magnitude spectrum is a **half-cycle cosine** peaking at $\omega = 0$ and zero at $\omega = \pm\pi$. This is a primitive **low-pass filter** response, used as a building block in robotics digital filter design.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 DTFT in the Signal Processing Pipeline

The following block diagram shows where the DTFT sits in the broader signal processing landscape and its relationship to other transforms.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Time Domain xn"] --> B["DTFT Forward"]
    B --> C["Frequency Domain X ej omega"]
    C --> D["IDTFT Inverse"]
    D --> A

    E["Z Transform Xz"] --> F["Evaluate on unit circle z e j omega"]
    F --> C

    G["Continuous Fourier Series"] --> H["Sample Frequency"]
    H --> B

    C --> I["Sample at N points omega k 2 pi k by N"]
    I --> J["DFT Xk"]
```

## 4.2 DTFT Property Dependency Map

This diagram shows how the various DTFT properties relate to one another and the core transform pair.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root["DTFT Core Pair xn to X ej omega"]

    root --> p1["Linearity"]
    root --> p2["Time Shift"]
    root --> p3["Frequency Shift"]
    root --> p4["Time Reversal"]
    root --> p5["Conjugation"]
    root --> p6["Freq Differentiation"]
    root --> p7["Convolution"]
    root --> p8["Modulation"]
    root --> p9["Parseval Theorem"]

    p2 --> app1["Linear Phase"]
    p3 --> app2["Modulated Signals"]
    p4 --> app3["Symmetry in DTFT"]
    p5 --> app3
    p7 --> app4["LTI System Analysis"]
    p9 --> app5["Energy Computation"]
```

## 4.3 DTFT Periodic Spectrum — Conceptual Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph input["INPUT: Discrete Signal"]
        s1["Sample x0 at n 0"]
        s2["Sample x1 at n 1"]
        s3["Sample x2 at n 2"]
        s4["Sample xN at n N"]
    end

    input --> proc["DTFT Computation Sum over n of xn exp j omega n"]

    proc --> out1["Output X ej omega is continuous in omega"]
    out1 --> out2["Output is periodic with period 2 pi"]
    out2 --> out3["Analysis range omega in negative pi to pi"]
```

## 4.4 DTFT Magnitude and Phase Spectrum Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Compute DTFT X ej omega"] --> rect["Obtain complex function of omega"]
    rect --> mag["Compute magnitude X ej omega equal to sqrt of real squared plus imag squared"]
    rect --> phase["Compute phase angle X ej omega equal to arctan of imag by real"]
    mag --> plot1["Plot Magnitude Spectrum vs omega"]
    phase --> plot2["Plot Phase Spectrum vs omega"]
    plot1 --> use["Applications: Filter Design, Vibration Analysis, Audio Processing"]
    plot2 --> use
```

## 4.5 DTFT vs. DFT vs. Z-Transform Relationship

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Z["Z Transform Xz of xn"] --> E["Evaluate at z e j omega"]
    E --> DTFT["DTFT X ej omega of xn"]

    DTFT --> S["Sample at omega k 2 pi k by N"]
    S --> DFT["DFT X k of xn"]

    DTFT --> INV["IDTFT xn equals 1 by 2 pi integral of X ej omega exp j omega n d omega"]
    DFT --> INV2["IDFT xn equals 1 by N sum X k exp j 2 pi kn by N"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Takeaway for Robotics:** A computer cannot directly compute the DTFT (it is continuous in $\omega$). Instead, the **DFT** is computed by sampling the DTFT at $N$ equally spaced points on the unit circle. Every modern robot's digital signal processor (DSP) chip uses FFT — a fast algorithm for the DFT.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, May 2024 Scheme]**
**Define the Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (DTFT) and state the conditions for its existence.**

**Model Answer:**

The DTFT of a discrete-time signal $x[n]$ is defined as:

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] \, e^{-j\omega n}$$

The corresponding inverse transform is:

$$x[n] = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} X(e^{j\omega}) \, e^{j\omega n} \, d\omega$$

**Conditions for existence:**
- **Sufficient condition (absolute summability):** $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert < \infty$
- **Weaker sufficient condition (square summability / finite energy):** $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert^2 < \infty$

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2023]**
**State and prove the time-shifting property of the DTFT.**

**Model Answer:**

**Statement:** If $x[n] \xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFT}} X(e^{j\omega})$, then $x[n - n_0] \xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFT}} e^{-j\omega n_0} X(e^{j\omega})$.

**Proof:** Let $y[n] = x[n - n_0]$. By definition:

$$Y(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n - n_0] \, e^{-j\omega n}$$

Substituting $m = n - n_0$:

$$Y(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} x[m] \, e^{-j\omega(m + n_0)} = e^{-j\omega n_0} \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} x[m] e^{-j\omega m} = e^{-j\omega n_0} X(e^{j\omega})$$

Hence proved. $\blacksquare$

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Part B questions carry **14 marks** and offer **internal choice** between two questions. Each question typically has sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each. Below are TWO alternative full-mark questions.

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Module 3 Internal Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme, Module 3]**

**(a)** Find the DTFT of the signal $x[n] = a^n u[n]$, where $|a| < 1$. Sketch its magnitude spectrum. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Apply the DTFT definition.

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} a^n u[n] \, e^{-j\omega n}$$

**Step 2:** Since $u[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$ and $u[n] = 1$ for $n \ge 0$:

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a^n e^{-j\omega n} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} (a e^{-j\omega})^n$$

**Step 3:** Apply the geometric series formula $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} r^n = \frac{1}{1-r}$ for $|r| < 1$:

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{1 - a e^{-j\omega}}$$

**Step 4:** Magnitude spectrum.

$$\vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 - 2a\cos\omega + a^2}}$$

For $a = 0.5$, the spectrum peaks at $\omega = 0$ with $\vert X(e^{j0}) \vert = \frac{1}{1-a} = 2$ and is minimum at $\omega = \pm\pi$ with $\vert X(e^{j\pi}) \vert = \frac{1}{1+a} = 2/3$.

**Sketch:** The magnitude spectrum is a low-pass shape, symmetric about $\omega = 0$, periodic with period $2\pi$.

**(b)** Using the time-shifting and linearity properties, find the DTFT of $x[n] = \delta[n] + \delta[n-2]$. Also find its magnitude and phase spectrum. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Apply linearity.

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \text{DTFT}\{\delta[n]\} + \text{DTFT}\{\delta[n-2]\}$$

**Step 2:** Using the standard pair $\delta[n] \xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFT}} 1$ and the time-shift property with $n_0 = 2$:

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = 1 + e^{-j2\omega}$$

**Step 3:** Express in polar form.

$$1 + e^{-j2\omega} = e^{-j\omega}(e^{j\omega} + e^{-j\omega}) = 2\cos(\omega) \, e^{-j\omega}$$

**Step 4:** Magnitude and phase.

$$\vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert = 2 \vert \cos(\omega) \vert$$

$$\angle X(e^{j\omega}) = \begin{cases} -\omega, & \cos(\omega) > 0 \\ \pi - \omega, & \cos(\omega) < 0 \end{cases}$$

The magnitude is **zero** at $\omega = \pm\pi/2, \pm 3\pi/2$, and the phase has a sawtooth-like shape.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Module 3 Internal Choice Option 2

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme, Module 3]**

**(a)** Find the DTFT of the rectangular pulse $x[n] = 1$ for $-2 \le n \le 2$ and zero elsewhere. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Apply the DTFT definition with $N_1 = 2$.

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-2}^{2} e^{-j\omega n} = e^{j2\omega} + e^{j\omega} + 1 + e^{-j\omega} + e^{-j2\omega}$$

**Step 2:** Group complex conjugate pairs.

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = 1 + 2\cos(\omega) + 2\cos(2\omega)$$

**Step 3:** Using the closed-form Dirichlet expression:

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{\sin(5\omega/2)}{\sin(\omega/2)}$$

**Verification at $\omega = 0$:** $X(e^{j0}) = 5$ (sum of 5 unit values). The closed form gives $\frac{\sin(0)}{\sin(0)}$, which is evaluated by L'Hopital's rule as $5/1 = 5$. ✓

**Verification at $\omega = \pi$:** Closed form gives $\frac{\sin(5\pi/2)}{\sin(\pi/2)} = \frac{1}{1} = 1$. The sum form gives $1 - 2 + 2 - 2 + 1 = 0$... 

**Correction:** Re-evaluate $X(e^{j\pi}) = e^{j2\pi} + e^{j\pi} + 1 + e^{-j\pi} + e^{-j2\pi} = 1 - 1 + 1 - 1 + 1 = 1$. ✓ Matches.

**(b)** State and prove the convolution property of the DTFT. Mention its significance in LTI system analysis. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Statement:** If $x[n] \xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFT}} X(e^{j\omega})$ and $h[n] \xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFT}} H(e^{j\omega})$, then:

$$y[n] = x[n] * h[n] \xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFT}} Y(e^{j\omega}) = X(e^{j\omega}) H(e^{j\omega})$$

**Proof:**

**Step 1:** Start with convolution definition: $y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x[k] h[n-k]$.

**Step 2:** Take DTFT of $y[n]$:

$$Y(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \left[\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x[k] h[n-k]\right] e^{-j\omega n}$$

**Step 3:** Interchange summation (justified by absolute convergence):

$$Y(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x[k] \left[\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} h[n-k] e^{-j\omega n}\right]$$

**Step 4:** Apply time-shift property to inner sum: $\sum h[n-k] e^{-j\omega n} = e^{-j\omega k} H(e^{j\omega})$.

$$Y(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x[k] e^{-j\omega k} H(e^{j\omega}) = H(e^{j\omega}) X(e^{j\omega})$$

Hence proved. $\blacksquare$

**Significance in LTI System Analysis:**
- A discrete LTI system with impulse response $h[n]$ transforms an input $x[n]$ into output $y[n] = x[n] * h[n]$.
- In the frequency domain, this is a simple multiplication: $Y(e^{j\omega}) = H(e^{j\omega}) X(e^{j\omega})$.
- $H(e^{j\omega})$ is the **frequency response** of the system, characterizing how each input frequency is scaled and phase-shifted.
- In robotics, this allows efficient analysis of digital controllers, sensor filters, and motor response systems.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in DTFT Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $2\pi$ normalization** in the IDTFT. Students often write $x[n] = \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} X(e^{j\omega})e^{j\omega n}d\omega$ and lose 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the periodicity of DTFT.** When sketching the spectrum, students only draw it in $[0, \pi]$ and forget that it repeats with period $2\pi$.
> 3. **Conflating DTFT and DFT.** The DTFT is continuous; the DFT is discrete (sampled). Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> 4. **Ignoring convergence conditions.** When asked to find DTFT of $x[n] = u[n]$ or a constant, students forget the impulse-train terms in the spectrum and lose 3 marks.
> 5. **Missing phase wrapping.** When computing phase, students forget the $\pm \pi$ jumps. Always state the phase as $\text{atan2}(\text{Im}, \text{Re})$ to handle all four quadrants.
> 6. **Misapplying properties.** E.g., confusing the time-shift $e^{-j\omega n_0}$ factor with the frequency-shift factor $X(e^{j(\omega-\omega_0)})$. Memorize the sign convention carefully.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist for DTFT (KTU 2024 Module 3):**

- DTFT is the discrete-time analog of the CTFT, but output is **continuous and periodic in $\omega$** with period $2\pi$.
- Forward transform: $X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]e^{-j\omega n}$ — summation, not integration.
- Inverse transform: $x[n] = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi} X(e^{j\omega})e^{j\omega n}d\omega$ — integration, normalized by $\frac{1}{2\pi}$.
- DTFT exists if $x[n]$ is **absolutely summable** or **square summable** (finite energy).
- **Periodicity in $\omega$** is a defining feature; one full period $[-\pi, \pi]$ is enough.
- **Linearity**, **time shift**, **frequency shift**, **convolution**, **modulation**, and **Parseval's theorem** are the most-tested properties.
- **Real signals** yield **Hermitian-symmetric** spectra: $X(e^{j\omega}) = X^*(e^{-j\omega})$ — even magnitude, odd phase.
- DTFT is the **Z-transform evaluated on the unit circle** $z = e^{j\omega}$: $X(e^{j\omega}) = X(z)\big\vert_{z=e^{j\omega}}$.
- DTFT of $a^n u[n]$ is $\frac{1}{1-ae^{-j\omega}}$ (key for first-order filter analysis).
- DTFT of a finite-length rectangular pulse yields the **Dirichlet kernel** $\frac{\sin(\omega(N_1+1/2))}{\sin(\omega/2)}$.
- DTFT of a constant 1 yields an **impulse train** $2\pi \sum_k \delta(\omega - 2\pi k)$.
- DTFT of $\delta[n-n_0]$ is $e^{-j\omega n_0}$ — complex exponential with unit magnitude.
- The **DFT** is the DTFT sampled at $N$ equally spaced frequencies: $\omega_k = 2\pi k/N$.
- **Convolution in time ↔ Multiplication in frequency** — the heart of LTI system analysis in robotics.
- **Parseval's theorem** equates time-domain and frequency-domain energy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** When asked "find the DTFT," always specify the conditions (e.g., $|a| < 1$) under which the transform exists. Examiners explicitly look for this awareness, and ignoring it can cost a full mark.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analysis of Discrete Time LTI Systems Using the Above Transforms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT402 - SIGNAL PROCESSING FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Analysis of Discrete Time LTI Systems Using the Above Transforms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of the KTU 2024 scheme for **PCRAT402 – Signal Processing for Robotics**, a **Discrete-Time Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) System** is a signal-processing operator $\mathcal{H}\{\cdot\}$ that satisfies both the **principle of linearity** (superposition and homogeneity) and **time-invariance** (shifting the input shifts the output by the same amount), operating on a discrete sequence $x[n] \in \mathbb{R}$ or $\mathbb{C}$ to produce an output $y[n]$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition:** A discrete-time LTI system is completely characterized in the **Z-domain** by its **system function (transfer function)** $H(z)$, which is the Z-transform of its impulse response $h[n]$:
> $$H(z) = \mathcal{Z}\{h[n]\} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} h[n]\,z^{-n}$$
> where $z \in \mathbb{C}$ is a complex variable. The output is obtained as $Y(z) = H(z)\,X(z)$.

### Formal Linearity Conditions
For scalars $a, b \in \mathbb{C}$ and inputs $x_1[n], x_2[n]$:

$$\mathcal{H}\{a\,x_1[n] + b\,x_2[n]\} = a\,\mathcal{H}\{x_1[n]\} + b\,\mathcal{H}\{x_2[n]\}$$

### Formal Time-Invariance Condition
For any integer shift $n_0 \in \mathbb{Z}$:

$$\mathcal{H}\{x[n-n_0]\} = y[n-n_0]$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Robot Sensor–Processor–Actuator" Pipeline

Imagine a small mobile robot traversing a corridor. It has three blocks:

1. **Sensor (Input)**: A LiDAR sensor produces a discrete range profile $x[n]$ at sampling instants $nT_s$ (e.g., distance readings every 10 ms).
2. **Processor (LTI System)**: The on-board digital filter smooths out noise. It does this in a way that is **linear** (scaling the input linearly scales the output) and **time-invariant** (the smoothing behavior is the same whether the robot is at second 5 or second 500 of operation).
3. **Actuator (Output)**: A cleaned signal $y[n]$ drives the wheel motors.

In the Z-domain, this entire pipeline is described by a single algebraic function $H(z) = Y(z)/X(z)$ — like a "transfer characteristic curve" of the filter — that fully captures how every frequency component of $x[n]$ is scaled and phase-shifted to produce $y[n]$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why use transforms at all?** Solving difference equations directly in the time domain is tedious because of convolution. Transforms (Z, DTFT) convert the **convolution operator** into a simple **multiplication operator** — turning a hard integral equation into high-school algebra.

## 1.3 The "Above Transforms" Referred To

The phrase *"the above transforms"* in the KTU module content refers to:

- **Z-Transform**: Comprehensive analysis (transient, steady-state, stability, causality, pole-zero).
- **Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (DTFT)**: Frequency response evaluation on the unit circle $\vert z \vert = 1$.

> [!TIP]
> **Pedagogical Bridge:** The Z-transform is the "general tool" (works for any sequence), while the DTFT is the "specialist tool" (only valid for absolutely summable/stable sequences where $\vert z \vert = 1$ lies in the ROC). The DTFT is just $H(z)$ evaluated on the unit circle: $H(e^{j\omega}) = H(z)\big\vert_{z=e^{j\omega}}$.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used Throughout This Module

- **Sampling frequency** $f_s$ in **Hz**.
- **Sampling period** $T_s = 1/f_s$ in **seconds**.
- **Normalized digital frequency** $\omega \in [-\pi, \pi]$ in **radians/sample**.
- **System order** $N$ (highest order of difference equation) — dimensionless.
- **Region of Convergence (ROC)** — a subset of the complex plane $\mathbb{C}$.
- **Unit circle**: $\vert z \vert = 1$ — boundary between stable and unstable poles.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Discrete-Time LTI System — Three Equivalent Descriptions

Any discrete-time LTI system can be equivalently described in **three domains**:

| Domain | Description | Mathematical Object | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Time | Difference equation / Convolution | $\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k\,y[n-k] = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k\,x[n-k]$ | Direct hardware implementation |
| Z-domain | System function | $H(z) = \dfrac{\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k\,z^{-k}}{\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k\,z^{-k}}$ | Pole-zero & stability analysis |
| Frequency | Frequency response | $H(e^{j\omega}) = \vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert e^{j\angle H(e^{j\omega})}$ | Magnitude & phase plot |

## 2.2 Step-by-Step System Analysis Procedure (KTU Board Method)

The standard KTU approach to *"Analyze the discrete LTI system described by …"* problems follows this canonical flow:

1. **Take the Z-transform** of the governing difference equation (using linearity and time-shift property).
2. **Algebraically solve** for the ratio $Y(z)/X(z)$ to obtain $H(z)$.
3. **Identify poles and zeros** of $H(z)$.
4. **Determine the ROC** based on the sequence type (causal, anti-causal, two-sided).
5. **Test stability** by checking whether the ROC includes the unit circle $\vert z \vert = 1$.
6. **Compute frequency response** $H(e^{j\omega})$ by substituting $z = e^{j\omega}$.
7. **Plot magnitude** $\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert$ and **phase** $\angle H(e^{j\omega})$.

## 2.3 Properties of the System Function $H(z)$

A system function $H(z) = B(z)/A(z)$ encodes all properties of the LTI system. The following table lists the high-yield relationships that KTU examiners love to test:

| Property | Condition in Z-Domain | Physical Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Causality** | ROC is exterior of the outermost pole ($\vert z \vert > r_{max}$) | System depends only on past/present inputs |
| **Stability (BIBO)** | ROC **includes** the unit circle $\vert z \vert = 1$ | Bounded input ⇒ bounded output |
| **Causal + Stable** | All poles strictly inside unit circle ($\vert p_k \vert < 1$) AND ROC: $\vert z \vert > \max_k \vert p_k \vert$ | Realizable, working filter |
| **FIR System** | $A(z) = 1$ (denominator polynomial equals 1) | No feedback, finite impulse response |
| **IIR System** | $A(z) \neq 1$ | Has feedback terms, infinite impulse response |
| **All-pass** | $\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert = 1$ for all $\omega$ | Pure phase distortion, unity gain |
| **Linear phase** | Zeros occur in mirror-image pairs about unit circle | Constant group delay |
| **Minimum phase** | All zeros inside unit circle | Minimum group delay for given magnitude |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** *"A causal stable system = poles inside, ROC outside, unit circle inside the ROC."* This is the single most-tested triplet in Part B questions.

## 2.4 The Z-Transform of the Difference Equation (Master Derivation)

For an LTI system described by the **linear constant-coefficient difference equation (LCCDE)**:

$$\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k\,y[n-k] = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k\,x[n-k]$$

Apply the Z-transform to both sides, using the **time-shift property** $\mathcal{Z}\{x[n-k]\} = z^{-k} X(z)$ (assuming zero initial conditions, the standard KTU convention):

$$\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k\,z^{-k}\,Y(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k\,z^{-k}\,X(z)$$

Therefore the system function is:

$$H(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{X(z)} = \frac{\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k\,z^{-k}}{\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k\,z^{-k}}$$

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Robotics

- **Kalman filter pre-design**: Every Kalman filter update step uses $H(z)$ to model the sensor dynamics in the Z-domain before discretization.
- **Digital PID controller**: Each of the P, I, D branches in a digital PID is a first-order IIR LTI system analyzed via $H(z)$.
- **LiDAR / IMU filtering**: Noise shaping filters are designed in the Z-domain by placing poles and zeros.
- **Motor control loops**: The discrete-time transfer function from PWM duty-cycle to motor speed is a second-order $H(z)$ whose stability is verified by the Jury/Schur-Cohn test.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $H(z) = \mathcal{Z}\{h[n]\} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} h[n]\,z^{-n}$ | Definition of system function |
| 2 | $H(z) = \dfrac{\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k\,z^{-k}}{\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k\,z^{-k}}$ | Transfer function from LCCDE |
| 3 | $Y(z) = H(z)\,X(z)$ | Convolution theorem in Z-domain |
| 4 | $h[n] = \mathcal{Z}^{-1}\{H(z)\}$ | Impulse response by inverse Z-transform |
| 5 | $H(e^{j\omega}) = H(z)\big\vert_{z=e^{j\omega}}$ | Frequency response from system function |
| 6 | $\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert$ and $\angle H(e^{j\omega})$ | Magnitude and phase spectra |
| 7 | Stability: $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h[n] \vert < \infty \iff$ ROC includes $\vert z \vert = 1$ | BIBO stability criterion |
| 8 | Causality: ROC is $\vert z \vert > r_{max}$ (exterior of outermost pole) | Realizability condition |
| 9 | Causal + Stable: All poles $\vert p_k \vert < 1$ | Working filter design |
| 10 | $H(z) = K \cdot \dfrac{\prod_{m=1}^{M}(1 - z_m z^{-1})}{\prod_{k=1}^{N}(1 - p_k z^{-1})}$ | Pole-zero factored form, gain $K = b_0/a_0$ |
| 11 | $h[n] = a^n u[n] \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{Z}} \dfrac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}}, \quad \text{ROC: }\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$ | Standard pair: causal exponential |
| 12 | $h[n] = -a^n u[-n-1] \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{Z}} \dfrac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}}, \quad \text{ROC: }\vert z \vert < \vert a \vert$ | Standard pair: anti-causal exponential |
| 13 | $u[n] \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{Z}} \dfrac{1}{1 - z^{-1}}, \quad \vert z \vert > 1$ | Unit step (causal) |
| 14 | $\delta[n] \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{Z}} 1, \quad \text{ROC: } \vert z \vert \geq 0$ | Unit impulse (all $z$) |
| 15 | $n\,a^n u[n] \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{Z}} \dfrac{a z^{-1}}{(1 - a z^{-1})^2}, \quad \vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$ | Ramp-modulated exponential |
| 16 | $\cos(\omega_0 n) u[n] \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{Z}} \dfrac{1 - z^{-1}\cos\omega_0}{1 - 2z^{-1}\cos\omega_0 + z^{-2}}$ | Resonator numerator form |
| 17 | $H_{\text{inv}}(z) = 1/H(z)$ | Inverse system (if stable and causal) |
| 18 | $\tau_g(\omega) = -\dfrac{d}{d\omega}\angle H(e^{j\omega})$ | Group delay (used in phase linearity tests) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** In problem sheets, students often forget the ROC. **Without the ROC, the inverse Z-transform is not unique.** Always specify ROC explicitly in the answer.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides complete, hand-derived analyses of the canonical KTU problems. No steps are skipped.

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Frequency Response from System Function

**Goal:** Given $H(z)$, derive the magnitude and phase as functions of $\omega$.

Starting point — the system function in pole-zero form (with $K = b_0/a_0$):

$$H(z) = K \cdot \frac{\prod_{m=1}^{M}(1 - z_m z^{-1})}{\prod_{k=1}^{N}(1 - p_k z^{-1})}$$

**Step 1 — Evaluate on the unit circle.** Substitute $z = e^{j\omega}$:

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = K \cdot \frac{\prod_{m=1}^{M}(1 - z_m e^{-j\omega})}{\prod_{k=1}^{N}(1 - p_k e^{-j\omega})}$$

**Step 2 — Convert each factor to magnitude–angle form.** For a generic complex term $(1 - \alpha e^{-j\omega})$ with $\alpha \in \mathbb{C}$:

$$1 - \alpha e^{-j\omega} = 1 - \alpha\cos\omega + j\,\alpha\sin\omega$$

Its magnitude and phase are:

$$\vert 1 - \alpha e^{-j\omega} \vert = \sqrt{(1 - \alpha\cos\omega)^2 + (\alpha\sin\omega)^2} = \sqrt{1 - 2\alpha\cos\omega + \alpha^2}$$

**Step 3 — Assemble the magnitude spectrum** (product of zero-magnitudes divided by product of pole-magnitudes):

$$\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert = \vert K \vert \cdot \frac{\prod_{m=1}^{M}\sqrt{1 - 2\,\text{Re}(z_m)\cos\omega + \vert z_m \vert^2}}{\prod_{k=1}^{N}\sqrt{1 - 2\,\text{Re}(p_k)\cos\omega + \vert p_k \vert^2}}$$

**Step 4 — Assemble the phase spectrum** (sum of zero-phases minus sum of pole-phases plus phase of $K$):

$$\angle H(e^{j\omega}) = \angle K + \sum_{m=1}^{M} \angle(1 - z_m e^{-j\omega}) - \sum_{k=1}^{N} \angle(1 - p_k e^{-j\omega})$$

where $\angle(1 - \alpha e^{-j\omega}) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{-\alpha\sin\omega}{1 - \alpha\cos\omega}\right)$ when $\alpha$ is real.

> [!NOTE]
> **Geometric interpretation:** Each term $\vert 1 - p_k e^{-j\omega} \vert$ is the **distance from the point $e^{j\omega}$ on the unit circle to the pole $p_k$**. Therefore the magnitude response is literally the *ratio of product-of-distances-to-zeros to product-of-distances-to-poles*. This is the basis of the **geometric pole-zero method** for sketching $\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Worked KTU-Style Problem (Complete Solution)

**Problem [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]:** Consider the causal LTI system described by the difference equation:

$$y[n] - \tfrac{1}{2}\,y[n-1] = x[n] + \tfrac{1}{4}\,x[n-1]$$

**(a) Find the system function $H(z)$, its poles, zeros, and ROC.**  
**(b) Determine whether the system is stable. Find the impulse response $h[n]$.**  
**(c) Compute the frequency response $H(e^{j\omega})$ and find $\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert$ at $\omega = 0$ and $\omega = \pi$.**

### Part (a) — System Function, Poles, Zeros, ROC

**Step A.1 — Apply Z-transform to both sides.** Using the time-shift property $\mathcal{Z}\{y[n-k]\} = z^{-k} Y(z)$ (zero initial conditions, causal system):

$$Y(z) - \frac{1}{2}\,z^{-1} Y(z) = X(z) + \frac{1}{4}\,z^{-1} X(z)$$

**Step A.2 — Factor each side.** Group the $Y(z)$ and $X(z)$ terms:

$$Y(z)\left(1 - \frac{1}{2} z^{-1}\right) = X(z)\left(1 + \frac{1}{4} z^{-1}\right)$$

**Step A.3 — Solve for $H(z) = Y(z)/X(z)$:**

$$H(z) = \frac{1 + \frac{1}{4} z^{-1}}{1 - \frac{1}{2} z^{-1}}$$

**Step A.4 — Convert to positive powers of $z$** (multiply numerator and denominator by $z$):

$$H(z) = \frac{z + \tfrac{1}{4}}{z - \tfrac{1}{2}}$$

**Step A.5 — Identify poles and zeros.** Setting numerator and denominator to zero:

- **Zero** (numerator): $z + \tfrac{1}{4} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; z_1 = -\tfrac{1}{4}$.
- **Pole** (denominator): $z - \tfrac{1}{2} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; p_1 = \tfrac{1}{2}$.

**Step A.6 — Determine ROC.** Since the system is **causal**, ROC is the exterior of the outermost pole:

$$\text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > \tfrac{1}{2}$$

### Part (b) — Stability and Impulse Response

**Step B.1 — Stability test.** The single pole $p_1 = \tfrac{1}{2}$ has magnitude $\vert p_1 \vert = \tfrac{1}{2} < 1$, so it lies **inside the unit circle**. The ROC ($\vert z \vert > \tfrac{1}{2}$) **includes the unit circle** $\vert z \vert = 1$. Hence the system is **BIBO stable**.

**Step B.2 — Compute $h[n]$ via inverse Z-transform.** Rewrite $H(z)$ as a sum of two simple terms using partial fractions in $z^{-1}$:

$$H(z) = \frac{1 + \frac{1}{4} z^{-1}}{1 - \frac{1}{2} z^{-1}} = \frac{1}{1 - \frac{1}{2} z^{-1}} + \frac{\frac{1}{4} z^{-1}}{1 - \frac{1}{2} z^{-1}}$$

**Step B.3 — Use the standard Z-transform pair** $a^n u[n] \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{Z}} \dfrac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}}$ with $a = \tfrac{1}{2}$:

For the first term: $\mathcal{Z}^{-1}\!\left\{\dfrac{1}{1 - \tfrac{1}{2} z^{-1}}\right\} = \left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)^n u[n]$.

For the second term, use the time-shift property $\mathcal{Z}^{-1}\{z^{-1}F(z)\} = f[n-1]$:

$$\mathcal{Z}^{-1}\!\left\{\frac{\frac{1}{4} z^{-1}}{1 - \frac{1}{2} z^{-1}}\right\} = \frac{1}{4} \left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)^{n-1} u[n-1]$$

**Step B.4 — Combine:**

$$h[n] = \left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)^n u[n] + \tfrac{1}{4} \left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)^{n-1} u[n-1]$$

Equivalently, for $n \geq 1$: $h[n] = \left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)^n + \tfrac{1}{4}\left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)^{n-1} = \left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)^n + \tfrac{1}{2}\left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)^{n} = \tfrac{3}{2}\left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)^n$. And $h[0] = 1$.

**Step B.5 — Stability check by absolute summability:**

$$\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \vert h[n] \vert = 1 + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \tfrac{3}{2}\left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)^n = 1 + \tfrac{3}{2} \cdot 1 = \tfrac{5}{2} < \infty \quad \checkmark$$

### Part (c) — Frequency Response

**Step C.1 — Substitute $z = e^{j\omega}$:**

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1 + \frac{1}{4} e^{-j\omega}}{1 - \frac{1}{2} e^{-j\omega}}$$

**Step C.2 — Magnitude at $\omega = 0$** (where $e^{-j0} = 1$):

$$H(e^{j0}) = \frac{1 + \tfrac{1}{4}}{1 - \tfrac{1}{2}} = \frac{5/4}{1/2} = \frac{5}{2}$$

So $\vert H(e^{j0}) \vert = \mathbf{2.5}$ (DC gain).

**Step C.3 — Magnitude at $\omega = \pi$** (where $e^{-j\pi} = -1$):

$$H(e^{j\pi}) = \frac{1 + \tfrac{1}{4}(-1)}{1 - \tfrac{1}{2}(-1)} = \frac{1 - \tfrac{1}{4}}{1 + \tfrac{1}{2}} = \frac{3/4}{3/2} = \frac{1}{2}$$

So $\vert H(e^{j\pi}) \vert = \mathbf{0.5}$ (high-frequency gain). This is a **low-pass filter** because the DC gain exceeds the Nyquist gain.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Stability via Pole Location (Schur–Cohn / Direct Test)

For a first-order denominator $A(z) = 1 + a_1 z^{-1}$:

- **Causal stability condition:** $\vert a_1 \vert < 1$.

For a second-order denominator $A(z) = 1 + a_1 z^{-1} + a_2 z^{-2}$:

- **Necessary and sufficient causal-stability conditions** (Schur–Cohn):

$$\vert a_2 \vert < 1$$

$$1 - a_1 + a_2 > 0$$

$$1 + a_1 + a_2 > 0$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Trick:** For the question *"Check if the system with $A(z) = 1 - 1.8 z^{-1} + 0.81 z^{-2}$ is stable"*, you must (a) verify $\vert a_2 \vert = 0.81 < 1$ ✓, (b) check $1 - (-1.8) + 0.81 = 3.61 > 0$ ✓, (c) check $1 + (-1.8) + 0.81 = 0.01 > 0$ ✓ — *just barely* stable. Poles are $0.9 e^{\pm j\theta}$ with $\vert p \vert = 0.9 < 1$. A classic KTU borderline case.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: System Properties ↔ Pole-Zero Locations

| System Property | Time-Domain Test | Z-Domain Test | Pole-Zero Test |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Memoryless** | $h[n] = K\,\delta[n]$ | $H(z) = K$ (constant) | No poles, no zeros except at origin |
| **Causal** | $h[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$ | ROC: $\vert z \vert > r_{max}$ | ROC exterior to outermost pole |
| **Stable** | $\sum \vert h[n] \vert < \infty$ | ROC includes $\vert z \vert = 1$ | All poles inside unit circle (causal case) |
| **Invertible** | $h[n] * h_i[n] = \delta[n]$ | $H(z)\,H_i(z) = 1$ | Zeros become poles of $H_i(z)$, must lie in stable ROC |
| **Real-coefficient** | $h[n] \in \mathbb{R}$ | $H(z^*) = H^*(z)$ | Complex poles/zeros in conjugate pairs |
| **All-pass** | $\vert h[n] \ast e^{j\omega n} \vert = $ const | $\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert = 1$ | Zeros are **reciprocals** of poles: $z_m = 1/p_m^*$ |
| **Minimum phase** | Smallest possible group delay | All zeros inside unit circle | All $\vert z_m \vert < 1$ |
| **Linear phase** | $h[n] = \pm h[N-n]$ | Zeros in mirror pairs | Symmetric zero locations |

For the difference equation $y[n] = -\tfrac{1}{2}\,y[n-1] + x[n] + \tfrac{1}{4}\,x[n-1]$, the **Direct Form-I** realization in robotics firmware looks like this:
